#### **PART TWO** **JOURNAL** **MINUTES OF THE FORTY-SEVENTH GENERAL** **ASSEMBLY**

**First Session – Wednesday Afternoon**

**June 26, 2019**

**47-1** **Assembly Called to Order and Opening Worship**
The Forty-seventh General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in
America gathered at 1:30 p.m. on Wednesday, June 26, 2019, at the Hilton
Anatole in Dallas, Texas, for the opening worship service and observance of
the Lord’s Supper. Outgoing Moderator TE Irwyn Ince Jr. called the
Assembly to order for worship (see Appendix X, p. 732).

Following worship, the Assembly recessed at 3:00 p.m. to reconvene
at 3:30 p.m.

**47-2** **Assembly Reconvenes – Declaration of Quorum and Enrollment**
The Moderator reconvened the Assembly at 3:00 p.m. for business.
TE Charles Knight led in prayer.

The Moderator declared a quorum present, with 407 Ruling Elders
and 1160 Teaching Elders (1567 total) enrolled (see Appendix S, p. 519 for
complete Attendance Report).

**47-3** **Election of Moderator**
The Moderator opened the floor for nominations for Moderator of
the Forty-seventh General Assembly. TE Bryan Chapell placed in nomination
RE J. Howard “Howie” Donahoe. TE Ronnie Garcia placed in nomination
RE John R. Bise. The Moderator declared nominations closed, and RE Donahoe
was elected Moderator (848-493).
RE Howard Donahoe, Moderator of the 47 [th] PCA General Assembly,
assumed the chair and addressed the court.

RE Todd Carlisle, Chairman of the Administrative Committee,
presented to retiring Moderator Ince a plaque in token of the Assembly’s
appreciation for his year of service as Moderator.

<a id="ga47-p18"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=20 printed_page=18 -->

**47-4** **Docket**

TE L. Roy Taylor, Stated Clerk, presented the Third Draft of the
Docket (p. 745)
TE Sean Lucas presented an amendment proposing that the
Assembly defer consideration of actions by Committees of Commissioners
dealing with Overtures 2 and 11 until after the report of the Overtures
Committee. The **amendment** to the Third Draft of the Docket was **adopted**
(881-356).
TE David Coffin offered a **procedural motion** regarding Overtures
Committee recommendations that have been also acted upon by any
Committees of Commissioners, that those Committees of Commissioners be
granted the right to the floor through their chairman to explain their
recommendations to the Assembly before any vote on the respective
Overtures Committee recommendations. The motion **passed** (1094-88).

**47-5** **Election of Clerks and Sound Engineers**
On nomination by the Stated Clerk, the following were elected: TEs
Paul L. Bankson, Robert S. Hornick, Kenneth McHeard, and D. Steven
Meyerhoff, recording clerks; RE William R. Stanway, timekeeper;
TE Hoochan Paul Lee computing clerk; Initial Production Group (IPG),
Production and Seminar Sound Engineers; RE Richard “Ric” Springer,
Chairman of the floor clerks; and RE Thomas H. Taylor Jr., vice-chairman
of the floor clerks.

**47-6** **Docket Adopted**
TE Sean Lucas rose to a **point of order** that the Assembly had
adopted the amendment to the Third Draft of the Docket but had not voted
on the Docket itself. The Moderator ruled the point of order **well taken**, and
the Assembly **adopted** the Third Draft of the Docket **as amended** .

**47-7** **Appointment of Assistant Parliamentarians**
RE Sam Duncan, RE John B. White Jr., and TE Stephen Tipton were
appointed assistant parliamentarians by the Moderator.

**47-8** **Appointment of Committee on Thanks**
The Moderator appointed the following men to serve as the
Committee on Thanks: TE Brannon Bowman and RE Melton Duncan.

**47-9** **Report of the Stated Clerk of the General Assembly**
TE L. Roy Taylor, Stated Clerk, presented his report (Appendix A,
p. 139).

<a id="ga47-p19"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=21 printed_page=19 -->

TE Taylor noted Communications from the National Association of
Evangelicals and the Reformed Church of Quebec, and recommended that
they be admitted to the minutes. He referred to churches added and other
statistics and reported on the referral of overtures to the appropriate
committees (see _RAO_ 11.4-11.11).
The Stated Clerk then reported on the _BCO_ amendments sent down
by the Forty-sixth General Assembly to the Presbyteries for voting
(Appendix A, p. 139), all of which had received the required approval of twothirds of the Presbyteries and were therefore before this Assembly for voting
TE Bryan Chapell rose to speak against the adoption of Item 3, the
amendment to _**BCO**_ **30-1** (Regarding Definite Suspension). The amendment
failed to receive the necessary (majority) vote by the Assembly (121-1156)
and therefore was **not adopted** .
TE Larry Hoop rose to speak against the adoption of Item 5, the
amendment to _**BCO**_ **32-19** (to Allow Counsel by Any Communing Member
in All Levels of Courts). The amendment failed to receive a majority vote
(540-728) and therefore was **not adopted** .
RE Flynt Jones rose to speak against the adoption of Item 2, the
amendment to _**BCO**_ **25-11** (to Require Thirty-Days’ Notice to Withdraw
from PCA). The amendment was **adopted** (830-408).
TE David Coffin rose to speak against the adoption of Item 4, the
amendment to _**BCO**_ **30-3** (Regarding Indefinite Suspension). The amendment
failed to receive a majority vote (508-628) and therefore was **not adopted.**
The remaining three amendments that had received the required
approval of the Presbyteries were **adopted** **in gross**, (1030-105), namely:
amendments to Item 1, _**BCO**_ **8-1 and 8-3** (Regarding Qualifications of
Elders); Item 6, _**BCO**_ **35-11** (Not to Disqualify Automatically a Member of
a court who was called as a Witness, by deleting the current 35-11 and
substituting a new **35-11** ); and Item 7, _**BCO**_ **59-3** (to grant _BCO_ 59-3, as
amended, full constitutional status)]
The Stated Clerk reminded delegates that the deadline for floor
nominations is at 5:30 p.m.

**47-10 Interchurch Relations Committee Informational and Committee**

**of Commissioner (CoC) Reports and Fraternal Greetings**
TE Roland Barnes, CoC Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer, and
presented the CoC report (see below, p. 22). (For the full report of the
Interchurch Relations Permanent Committee, see Appendix N, p. 380.)

<a id="ga47-p20"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=22 printed_page=20 -->

**Recommendations 1-4** were **adopted** .
TE Bruce O’Neil, Chairman of the Permanent Committee, introduced
visiting fraternal delegates, who brought greetings.

Rev. Bruce Backensto of the RPCNA, addressed the Assembly.
Mr. Mark Bube, of the Orthodox Presbyterian Church, addressed the
Assembly.

Rev. David Winburne, of the Associate Reformed Presbyterian Church,
addressed the Assembly.

Rev. Seung Hee Lee from the Hapdong Korean Presbyterian Church,
addressed the Assembly. He was accompanied by the Reverends Jon Hyuk
Kim, Yong Hoon Jin, Dae Bong Lee, Jong Gu Lim, and Young Gyu Park.

Rev. Richard Miller, of the URCNA addressed the Assembly.
The Very Reverend Canon Charles Camlin of the Reformed Episcopal
Church and the Anglican Church in North America addressed the Assembly.

**47-11 Assembly Recessed**
The Assembly recessed to reconvene at 9:30 a.m. on Wednesday.

**Second Session - Thursday Morning**

**June 27, 2019**

**47-12 Assembly Reconvened**
The Assembly reconvened at 9:30 a.m. on June 27, 2019, with prayer
by RE John Bise.

**47-13 Interchurch Relations Committee Informational and Committee**

**of Commissioner Reports and Fraternal Greetings (continued**
**from 47-10, p. 19)**
TE Bruce O’Neil, Chairman of the Permanent Committee on
Interchurch Relations introduced Dr. Davi Charles Gomes of the Brazilian
Presbyterian Church, who brought fraternal greetings on behalf of his
denomination and the World Reformed Fellowship.
CoC Chairman Barnes continued with the report of the Committee
of Commissioners on Interchurch Relations.
TE barnes presented as a **substitute motion** the Committee of
Commissioner’s **Recommendation 5**, to answer **Overture 23** (“Withdraw
from the National Association of Evangelicals”) **in the affirmative** . He
noted that the grounds for the recommendation (which were included in the
distributed copy) should be struck, as they had been included as part of the
recommendation in error. The CoC did not vote on the grounds.

<a id="ga47-p21"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=23 printed_page=21 -->

TE Bruce O’Neil, Chairman of the Permanent Committee, presented
the recommendation of the Permanent Committee ( _RAO_ 14-6.h), that
Overture 23 be answered in the negative
TE Carl Robbins, presented arguments on behalf of the CoC’s
substitute motion.
TE O’Neil then responded on behalf the Permanent Committee.
Floor debate ensued.
A **point of order** was raised, citing _RAO_ 19-4.d, that debate was not
being limited to three minutes per person. The Moderator **ruled** that the point
of order was **not well taken** because _RAO_ 14-9.h applies at this point.
TE Josh Owen asked for clarification on what the Assembly will be
voting on when the debate is closed. The Moderator summarized the question
upon which commissioners would be voting.
TE Allan Taha **moved** to call the question. Upon the advice of the
parliamentarians that debate shall not be limited, the Moderator **ruled** that
calling the question was not permitted at this point.
Debate continued.
TE Fred Greco made a **parliamentary inquiry** regarding the earlier
motion to move the previous question. Citing _RAO_ 14-9.e and _RONR_ (11 [th]
ed.), p. 65, ll. 14-19, he argued that moving the previous question is not a
move to limit debate, but to close debate. The Moderator referred the matter
to the parliamentarians and allowed the debate to continue.
The time for debate having expired, Permanent Committee Chairman
O’Neil yielded his time to TE Roy Taylor, Stated Clerk, who gave the final
word ( _RAO_ 19-4.b). in favor of the Permanent Committee’s recommendation
to answer Overture 23 in the negative.
The Assembly proceeded to a vote on the **CoC’s** **Recommendation 5**,
to answer Overture 23 in the affirmative. The recommendation **failed** (590845 **)**,
The Assembly then voted on **Recommendation 5 of the Permanent**
**Committee**, which was **adopted** by voice vote **,** **answering Overture 23 in**
**the negative** and thereby indicating the Assembly’s decision to remain in the
National Association of Evangelicals (NAE).
TE Per Almquist raised a **point of order** that the Assembly had not
voted on Recommendation 6 of the IRC. The Moderator ruled the point of
order **well taken,** presented IRC Recommendation 6, and seeing no
opposition, declared **Recommendation 6** **adopted** by common consent.

<a id="ga47-p22"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=24 printed_page=22 -->

**47-15 Partial Report of the Overtures Committee**
TE Sean Lucas, Chairman, opened in prayer.
The Chairman presented the following recommendations **in gross** :
that **Recommendation 6 (Overture 6** concerning memorials) be **answered**
**in the affirmative** ; that **Recommendation 12 (Overture 12** concerning
floor nominations **)** be **answered in the negative** ; that **Recommendation 18**
( **Overture 18** concerning multiple references of overtures) be **answered in**
**the affirmative as amended** ; that **Recommendation 15** (Overture 15
concerning multiple references of overtures) be answered **in reference to**
**Recommendation 18** ; and that **Recommendation 16 (Overture 16**
concerning a vice moderator).be **answered in the negative** . All were
**adopted** **in gross** by voice vote. The Moderator noted that a sufficient
quorum being present, a voice vote was appropriate.
(For the full report of the Overtures Committee, see **47-39**, p. 76; **47-**
**42**, p. 79; and **47-44**, p. 85.)

**PARTIAL REPORT OF THE OVERTURES COMMITTEE**

**TO THE FORTY-SEVENTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY**

**OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA**

**IV. Recommendations**

6. That **Overture 6** from Pacific Northwest Presbytery (“Amend _RAO_

11 and 13 to Disallow Memorials”) be answered in the **affirmative.**
_Adopted_
**Grounds:** While Memorial overtures have historical precedent in
the PCA, recent history suggests that they may be problematic. There
is no provision in the _RAO_ to prevent negative content from being
included. There is also no good mechanism for determining who
should or should not be memorialized by the Assembly.
12. That **Overture 12** from Northern New England Presbytery (“Amend

_RAO_ 8-4 regarding Floor Nominations”) be answered in the **negative.**
_Adopted_
**Grounds:** The nomination and election processes have served the
PCA well for decades. The current process is the balanced influence
of Presbyteries, a representative General Assembly committee, and
the General Assembly at large.

<a id="ga47-p25"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=27 printed_page=25 -->

15. That **Overture 15** from West Hudson Presbytery (“Amend _RAO_ 11-5

to Prevent Confusion and Possible Contradictory Actions on
Overtures by Multiple Committees of Commissioners”) be answered
**in reference to Overture 18.** _Adopted_
16. That **Overture 16** from Georgia Foothills Presbytery (“Amend the

_RAO_ to Provide for a Moderator-Elect and Vice-Moderator by
Revision of _RAO_ 1-4, Addition of _RAO_ 1-6, 1-7, and 1-8, and
Revision of _RAO_ 2-1, 2-3”) be answered in the **negative.**
_Adopted_
**Grounds:** There has never been a problem with current election
procedure. The proposed amendment would not give the Assembly
the ability to choose a Moderator based on the issues before that
Assembly.
18. That **Overture 18** from Houston Metro Presbytery (“Amend _RAO_

11-5 to Prevent Confusion and Possible Contradictory Actions on
Overtures by Multiple Committees of Commissioners”) be answered
in the **affirmative as amended** (For OC changes, ~~double strike-~~
~~through~~ for deletions, double underlining for additions; single
underlining for original additions in overture). _Adopted_

**Therefore, be it resolved**, that the General Assembly of the

Presbyterian Church in America amend _RAO_ 11-5 with the
following additions (additions are underlined):

**11-5.** Upon receipt the Stated Clerk shall refer all
overtures requesting amendment of the _Book of Church_
_Order_ or the _Rules of Assembly Operations_ to the
Committee on Constitutional Business for its advice
to the Overtures Committee. Upon receipt, the Stated
Clerk shall forward all overtures concerning presbytery
boundaries or a new presbytery to the permanent
Committee on Mission to North America. Any overture,
other than an overture proposing amendment to the
Constitution (which shall be referred to the
Committee on Constitutional Business for its advice
to the Overtures Committee), having to do with the
nature or responsibilities of a permanent Committee
or Agency shall be referred by the Clerk to the
appropriate permanent Committee or Agency or ad
interim committee. All other overtures shall be referred

to the Overtures Committee. ~~No overture shall be~~

~~referred for consideration and recommendation to~~
~~more than one Committee of Commissioners, permanent~~

<a id="ga47-p26"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=28 printed_page=26 -->

~~Committee or agency ora d interim committee; an~~
~~overture, however, may be referred for informational~~
~~purposes only to another Committee of Commissioners,~~
~~permanent committee or agency ora d interim~~
~~committee.~~ An overture proposing amendment to the
Constitution shall be referred only to the Overtures
Committee for consideration and recom-mendation;
such an overture, however, may be referred to other
Committees of Commissioners, other permanent
Committees or Agencies, or other ad interim committees
for advice only, to the Overtures Committee. All
overtures shall be published in the _Commissioner_
_Handbook_ with reference for consideration indicated.

**Grounds:** This Overture is needed to prevent contradictory
recommendations on overtures from multiple committees with
respect to Constitutional amendments.

Respectfully submitted,
TE Sean Lucas, Chairman RE Edward L. Wright, Secretary

**47-16 Review of Presbytery Records (RPR) Report**

TE Kenneth McHeard, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and
presented the report (Appendix Q, p. 444)

The Chairman **moved all General Recommendations in gross**,
with the exception of General Recommendations 20 and 21 and Presbytery
Recommendation 44. Upon request, Presbytery **Recommendations 4, 55**,
and **57** were also removed from the in gross motion **.**

All recommendations included in the in gross motion were **adopted**
by voice vote. The Moderator declared that the vote met the requisite two/thirds
of a majority of the registered commissioners required for an _RAO_ change to
be adopted ( _RAO_ XX).

**General Recommendation 20** to amend _RAO_ 16-4.a was **adopted**
by voice vote, the Moderator declaring the requisite two-thirds of a majority
of the registered Commissioners required for an _RAO_ change was met.

**General Recommendations 21** to amend _RAO_ 16-4.d was **adopted**
by voice vote, the Moderator declaring the requisite two-thirds of a majority
of the registered Commissioners required for an _RAO_ change was met.
**Presbytery Recommendation** **VI.44.e (Mississippi Valley**
**Presbytery)** was presented. TE Stephen Tipton presented the Committee’s
rationale.

<a id="ga47-p27"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=29 printed_page=27 -->

TE David Coffin **moved** the following **amendment** as a concluding
paragraph to Recommendation 44.e: “As this hearing is _de novo_, the lower
court and the representative of the report shall have the right to introduce
documentary evidence to the SJC.” The amendment was **adopted** .

TE Roger Collins **moved** a **substitute** motion to **Recommendation**
**44.e,** that would effectively conclude the matter dealt with in the
recommendation.

TE Tipton spoke for the Committee’s recommendation.
TE Per Almquist raised a **point of order** that the present vote was to
determine whether the substitute would become the main motion. The point
of order was **well taken** and the Moderator so advised the commissioners.
The **substitute motion** was **defeated** (323-802).
Moderator Donahoe yielded the chair to RE John B. White Jr.

RE Gwennap presented the CoC’s Recommendation VI.55, regarding
the minutes of Northwest Georgia Presbytery.
RE Jay Neikirk made inquiry as to whether the intent of the
committee’s recommendation was to say that presbytery is bound by an _in_

the words after the word “teaching.” The motion **passed.**
Recommendation VI.55 (p.1240) was **adopted** as amended.
Recommendation VI.57, concerning the minutes of Ohio Valley
Presbytery, was **adopted** (765-402).
CoC Chairman McHeard closed the report with prayer.

<a id="ga47-p32"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=34 printed_page=32 -->

**47-24 Committee on Constitutional Business (CCB) Report**
TE Per Almquist, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and
presented the report as information (Appendix O, p. 397). He noted that the
Committee had received no non-judicial references and no constitutional
inquiries from the General Assembly. The Committee gave advice on 22
overtures involving proposed changes to the _BCO_ and _RAO_ and advice to the
Stated Clerk on three issues. TE Almquist closed the report with prayer.

**47-25 Theological Examining Committee Report**
TE Ron Fossett, Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and presented
the Report (Appendix R, p. 514). He noted that the Committee had examined
six candidates for first- and second-level positions in PCA Committees and
Agencies. The examinations of all candidates were approved unanimously.
The report was received as information.

**47-26 Mission to the World Informational and Committee of**

**Commissioners (CoC) Reports**
TE Jeffrey Choi, CoC Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer, and
yielded to TE Lloyd Kim, Coordinator, who presented the Informational
Report. (For the full report of the MTW Permanent Committee, see
Appendix H, p. 316.)
TE Kim reported on MTW’s 1% initiative, the establishment of
regional hubs, and new RAD (Reformed and Diverse Delegate) groups. He
reviewed the work of GRACE, hired to investigate MTW’s behaviors and
culture in relation to women, and noted the follow-up efforts of MTW to
create a healthy environment.
The CoC Chairman presented the CoC report (below).
**Recommendations 1-4, 6-9** were **adopted** .
**Recommendation 5,** addressing the budget, was **deferred** to the
CoC on AC.
**Recommendation 10,** that **Overture 41** (p. 34; p. 693) be answered
in the negative, was presented.
TE David Coffin **moved** that **Recommendation 10** be recommitted
to MTW because the recommendation included the grounds for the
resolution.
TE Ewan Kennedy **moved** that the Recommendation be amended by
striking the grounds. The Moderator **ruled** the motion **out of order** because
since the CoC had voted on the grounds, striking them would be an
amendment, and CoC recommendations cannot be amended on the floor (see
_RAO_ 14-9.e). TE Kennedy challenged the Moderator’s ruling, and the chair’s
ruling was sustained.

<a id="ga47-p33"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=35 printed_page=33 -->

as a point of clarification, the Stated Clerk explained that the Assembly
was able earlier to correct the IRC recommendation editorially (p. 20) by
striking grounds because in that case the CoC had not voted on the grounds.
TE Dennis Hermerding made a parliamentary inquiry as to whether
a substitute motion could be made if the motion to recommit failed. The

Moderator answered that such a motion would be an amendment and

therefore not allowed.
The question was called, debate was ended, and the **motion to**
**recommit** **passed.** (712-526)
The Chairman closed the report with prayer.

**REPORT OF THE COMMITTEE OF COMMISSIONERS ON**

**MISSION TO THE WORLD**

**TO THE FORTY-SEVENTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY**

**OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA**

**I.** **Business Referred to the Committee**

A Review of CMTW minutes from:

March 14–15, 2018
September 26–27, 2018
B. Review of Recommendations from Permanent Committee

C. Review of finances for 2019
D. Review of Proposed 2020 CMTW Budget

**II.** **Statement of Major Issues Discussed**
A. Review of CMTW minutes from:

March 14–15, 2018
September 26–27, 2018
B. Review of Recommendations from Permanent Committee

C. Review of finances for 2019
D. Review of 2020 Proposed CMTW Budget

**III.** **Recommendations**
1. That the General Assembly urge churches to set aside the month of

November 2019 as a month of prayer for global missions, asking God
to send many more laborers into His harvest field. (MTW will offer
a 30 Days of Prayer Calendar, which your church can download from
mtw.org in the fall, as well as other prayer resources); _Adopted_
2. That the General Assembly urge churches to set aside a portion of

their giving for the suffering peoples of the world; to that end, be it
recommended that a special offering for relief and mercy (MTW
Compassion offering) be taken during 2019 and distributed by
MTW; _Adopted_

<a id="ga47-p34"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=36 printed_page=34 -->

3. That the General Assembly urge churches to set aside Sunday,

November 3, 2019, as a day of prayer for the persecuted church
worldwide; _Adopted_
4. Having performed an annual review of our coordinator, we give

thanks to our gracious Lord for His gift to the Church in the person
of Dr. Lloyd Kim, who continues to serve and lead humbly and
effectively in the glorious cause of missions around the world.
CMTW enthusiastically recommends that Dr. Kim be re-elected
coordinator. _Adopted_
5. That the proposed budget of MTW, as presented through the

Administrative Committee, be approved; _Deferred to CoC on AC_
6. That the minutes of the meeting of CMTW of March 14–15, 2018,

be accepted; and _Adopted_
7. That the minutes of the meeting of CMTW of September 26–27,

2018, be accepted; _Adopted_
8. Regarding MTW’s 2017 Financial Audit: That the Committee of

Commissioners reviewed the financial audit for calendar year ending
December 31, 2017. They also noted per CMTW’s minutes that
CMTW had accepted the audit. _Adopted_
9. That **Overture 24** from Philadelphia Metro West Presbytery “Add

Constitutional Precedence Statement to Policy Manuals” **be**
**answered with reference to the recommendation from the**
**Administrative Committee** regarding Overture 24. _Adopted_
10. That **Overture 41** from Heritage Presbytery, “Direct CMTW to

Amend its Guidelines to Implement CMTW’s ‘Statement on Valuing
Women in MTW,’ so that only Ordained Elders Will Be Allowed to
Serve in the Roles of Team Leader, Regional Director, and
International Director within MTW,” (p.153) **be answered in the**
**negative** . With the following grounds recorded as part of the motion.

**Grounds:**
In formulating and finalizing its “Statement on Valuing Women,”
CMTW carefully considered the report on the ad interim committee
to the 45 [th] General Assembly and believes that its statement on
valuing women in MTW is wholly consistent with the ad interim
report in all material respects.
Mission to World, CMTW, and the General Assembly have
approved unordained men to the roles of team leader, country
director, and regional director since 1983 and at least 3 women since
around 2006. We have identified at least 35 unordained leaders
appointed to these positions since 2003. CMTW and GA have never
required these positions be held exclusively by ordained elders.

<a id="ga47-p35"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=37 printed_page=35 -->

MTW teams vary extensively across the globe. While the
ultimate aim of all our teams is to plant and revitalize churches, not
all of our teams have ordained elders engaged directly in planting
churches. Some teams are focused on facilitative church planting
where our missionaries work alongside ordained national partners
who are leading churches and church plants. Others are focused on
university ministries (Christ’s College team in Taiwan), medical
ministries, mercy ministries (Ethiopia), education ministries, etc. to
make disciples who fill local churches and church plants. In some
cases there are a variety of different ministries all under the umbrella
of one team. The overture would unnecessarily restrict unordained
men and women from serving in roles that do not require ordination.
The overture if passed would immediately affect 19 unordained
missionaries who are serving in these roles across the globe as well
as their teams. These leaders would be removed from their positions,
receive less remuneration, and would have to find new roles on the
team. It would also require taking 19 ordained missionaries out of
their current ministry roles to take their places.
CMTW and the coordinator are committed to working with
those whose consciences are affected by women or unordained men
serving in leadership in non-ecclesial roles over (ordained) men. We
want to move forward together in a manner that does not hinder the
work of the Kingdom.
The principle that unordained leaders defer matters of the church
to ordained leaders in line authority has been MTW's practice for
many years. Women and unordained men in leadership over TE
church planters routinely defer matters related to the church to other
ordained supervisors. While this practice necessarily involves linedrawing, in practice this line-drawing has not proved to be an
unsurmountable problem, and we pledge to continue to ensure that
church matters be referred to the proper ordained authorities.
The authority given to team leaders for spiritual care of their
members does not necessitate ordination. Spiritual care can be given
by unordained believers. Team leaders can also delegate to others to
provide spiritual care as is appropriate.
Finally, while CMTW has non-voting women advisors, CMTW
is not proposing the addition of women to serve on CMTW through
this statement. _Adopted_

**III. Recommendations**

1. That the General Assembly give thanks to God for the ministry of

Covenant Theological Seminary; for its faithfulness to the
Scriptures, the Reformed faith, and the Great Commission; for its
students, graduates, faculty, staff, and trustees; and for those who
support the Seminary through their prayers and gifts. _Adopted_
2. That the General Assembly encourage the congregations of the

Presbyterian Church in America to support the ministry of Covenant
Theological Seminary by contributing the Partnership Shares
approved by the Assembly, and by recommending Covenant
Seminary to prospective students. _Adopted_
3. That the General Assembly ask the Lord to bless Covenant

Seminary’s President, Dr. Mark Dalbey, and grant him and the
Seminary’s leadership team, faculty, and Board of Trustees great
wisdom, biblical faithfulness, and clear vision as they seek to lead
the institution into a new era of ministerial fruitfulness. _Adopted_
4. That the General Assembly ask God to guide Covenant Seminary’s

ongoing efforts at recruiting new students, evaluating and
strengthening our programs, and seeking to make the Seminary a
greater resource for the church both locally and globally. _Adopted_

<a id="ga47-p39"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=41 printed_page=39 -->

5. That the General Assembly ask God’s blessing on the Seminary’s

planning and fund-raising efforts, and on its attempts to recruit a new
generation of dedicated pastor-scholars to train new generations of
leaders for Christ’s church and Kingdom. _Adopted_
6. That the General Assembly pray for the further development of

Covenant Seminary’s strategic educational partnerships—in
Nashville, Phoenix, and Cambridge; with NEXT; and with Stephanie
Hubach’s work in disability ministries—that these might be a
blessing to those preparing for ministry or seeking theological
training to better live out their faith wherever God calls
them. _Adopted_
7. That the General Assembly pray for unity among the brethren of the

PCA and ask the Lord to work in all our hearts to foster a deeper
desire to engage with one another and the world in compassionate
and gospel-centered ways, and that we might bear strong witness to
the truth and power of God’s redeeming grace. _Adopted_
8. That the General Assembly approve the minutes of the stated
and called meetings of the Seminary’s Board of Trustees and
Executive Committee of the Board of Trustees for the year as
follows:

- _Stated Board Meetings:_ April 27, 2018; September 28,
2018; January 25, 2019

- _Called Board Meetings:_ None

- _Stated Executive Committee Meetings:_ December 7, 2018;
March 1, 2019

- _Called Executive Committee Meetings:_ April 26, 2018;
August 8, 2018; August 9, 2018; September 27, 2018;
January 17, 2019; January 24, 2019
_Adopted_
9. That the financial audit for Covenant Theological Seminary for the

fiscal year ending June 30, 2018, by Capin Crouse LLC, be received.
_Adopted_
10. That the proposed 2019–20 budget of Covenant Theological

Seminary be approved. _Deferred to CoC on AC_
11. The Covenant Seminary Board of Trustees voted unanimously at its

April 26, 2019, stated meeting to recommend that **Overture 24** from
Philadelphia Metro West Presbytery, “Add Constitutional Precedence
Statement to Policy Manuals,” **be** **answered with reference to the**
**recommendation from the Administrative Committee** regarding
Overture 24 **.** _Adopted_

<a id="ga47-p40"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=42 printed_page=40 -->

12. The Covenant Seminary Board of Trustees voted unanimously at its

April 26, 2019, stated meeting to recommend that **Overture 2** from
Northwest Georgia Presbytery, “Encourage CTS toward Growth and
Release from Assembly Oversight,” **be answered in the negative**
with the following rationale. _Adopted_
Rationale:

- Covenant Seminary was shocked to hear of this overture as we
were in no way consulted on this overture.

- Covenant Seminary does not and never has desired to be
released from PCA oversight but rather delights in and values
deeply the oversight provided through the General Assemblyelected ruling and teaching elders.

- Contrary to the implication of the overture, Covenant Seminary
has every desire to continue to be under the exclusive oversight
of ordained elders as voting members of the Board of Trustees.

- Covenant Seminary believes that authoritative oversight of the
Seminary by ordained elders is particularly important and vital
for the task of training men who will be ordained pastors in the
PCA.

- Then Covenant Seminary President Dr. Will Barker stated at the
10 [th] General Assembly that “theological education should be
under the denomination’s ~~a~~ uspices and responsible to the
church. Our history has been blessed through this orientation to
the work of the church.”

- We continue to believe this, desire this, and are blessed by this
official and formal relationship.

- Between 1981 and 1982 all 25 PCA presbyteries voted to receive
the RPCES into the PCA with the understanding that Covenant
College and Covenant Seminary would be received into the PCA
and have their own Committees of Commissioners at General

Assembly.

- Elders who serve on the Board of Trustees are recommended by
presbyteries to the GA Nominating Committee, which then
recommends an annual slate of board nominees who are voted

on by the General Assembly.

- The members of the Board of Trustees elect the president,
approve faculty members, adopt the budget, and oversee the
Seminary’s doctrinal and educational work of training,
equipping, and sending graduates into the church.

<a id="ga47-p41"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=43 printed_page=41 -->

- Elected members of the Board of Trustees annually reaffirm by
signature their commitment to the system of doctrine taught in
the Westminster Standards.

- The approval of the General Assembly Theological Examining
Committee is necessary for each newly elected president to take
up the office of president.

- The Presbytery-elected Committee of Commissioners reviews
the work of the Seminary each year and brings recommendations
to the General Assembly.

- The above-named oversight and accountability provide mutual
benefit to the PCA and to Covenant Seminary by maintaining
our shared conviction to be faithfully committed to the inerrancy
of the Bible, the historic Reformed faith, and the great
commission.

- It is our deep desire and delight to continue to serve as the
denominational seminary of the PCA as we strive to “glorify the
triune God by training his servants to walk in God’s grace,
minister God’s Word, and equip God’s people—all for God’s
mission.”

<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=60 printed_page=58 -->

**III. Recommendations**

1. That the General Assembly approve the minutes of the meetings of

the Permanent Committee for the Committee on Discipleship Ministries
in September 2018 and March 2019. _Adopted_
2. That the General Assembly receive the 2018 Audit performed by

Robins, Eskew, Smith, and Jordan, and approve the same firm for
the 2019 Audit. _Adopted_
3. That the General Assembly give thanks to God and express

appreciation to the churches and individuals who contributed to the
2018 Women’s Love Gift given to benefit PCA Retirement Benefits,
Inc. (RBI). The gift totaled over $115,000. The funds will be used to
launch a ministry of care and nurture for the wives of PCA
pastors. _Adopted_
4. That the General Assembly encourage individuals, local churches,

and presbyteries to utilize the many resources available on the CDM
website (pcacdm.org), particularly those obtained from local
churches and posted on the “Ministry Toolbox,” as well as the many
books and resources offered through the PCA Bookstore
( **pcabookstore.com** ). Churches could also post links to the CDM
website on their sites. _Adopted_
5. That the General Assembly encourage individuals and churches to

attend one of the regional conferences hosted by local congregations
in connection with CDM (Established conferences for those who
work with children’s ministries or Hinge conferences for women in
2020). _Adopted_
6. That the Assembly encourage individuals and local churches to

consider and utilize the excellent curricula from Great Commission
Publications (GCP), e.g., _Show Me Jesus_ and _Kids’ Quest Catechism_
_Club_ for children, _G2R Genesis to Revelation_ Bible studies for
preteens to teens—including the new _G2R God’s Promises_ —and _So_
_What?_ Bible studies for youth. _Adopted_
7. That the General Assembly give thanks to TE Scott Barber, TE Tom

Harr, and RE John Kwasny for their faithful service as members of
the Permanent Committee and to Kathy Stair for her helpful service
a san adviser to the Committee. _Adopted_
8. That the General Assembly re-elect TE Stephen Estock to serve as

the Coordinator for the Committee on Discipleship Ministries
(CDM). _Adopted_

<a id="ga47-p59"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=61 printed_page=59 -->

9. That **Overture 11** from Fellowship Presbytery “Commend and

Distribute RPCNA’s ‘Contemporary Perspectives on Sexual
Orientation: A Theological and Pastoral Analysis’” **be answered in**
**the** **negative** . _Adopted_
Grounds:
(1) The report mentioned in the Overture was published by Crown

& Covenant Publications in 2012 under the title _The Gospel &_
_Sexual Orientation_ (Michael Lefebvre, Editor). Given that the
material is under copyright with Crown & Covenant
Publications and The Synod of the Reformed Presbyterian
Church of North America (RPCNA), the overture is improper
and out of order in that it asks the GA and CDM to do something
that would require legal permission which they do not currently
have ( _RRO_ 39, 11 [th] ed., p. 343).
(2) _The Gospel & Sexual Orientation_ is in print and available from

CDM through the PCA Bookstore (pcabookstore.com, item
#11555). It is also available publicly at the URL indicated in
the Overture.
(3) The overture directs the production of curriculum without any

consideration for the proper funding to complete the project (see
also point 1 above).
10. That **Overture 24** from Philadelphia Metro West Presbytery “Add

Constitutional Precedence Statement to Policy Manuals” be answered
by **reference to the recommendation from the Administrative**
**Committee** regarding Overture 24 (see p. 66). _Adopted_
11. That **Overture 25** from Suncoast Florida Presbytery “Support

Christian Education’” **be answered in the** **negative** . _Adopted_
Grounds:
(1) The overture is unnecessary. Scripture clearly teaches that

parents have the responsibility for the education of covenant
children (Deut. 6, Eph. 6). Church Sessions have the power “to
determine the best measures for promoting the spiritual interests
of the church and congregation” ( _BCO_ 12-5.e). The General
Assembly does not need to affirm what Scripture clearly teaches
and the Constitution already provides.
(2) The particular method(s) used by parents to fulfill their scriptural

responsibility is a matter of conscience, guided by the Holy Spirit
and informed by the Word of God and the pastoral counsel of
elders in the local church. It is inappropriate for the General
Assembly to direct parents (explicitly or implicitly) to a
particular method. The overture may (unintentionally) bind the
conscience of a parent attempting to fulfill his/her scriptural
responsibility.

<a id="ga47-p60"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=62 printed_page=60 -->

(3) The overture is vague in some of its stipulations. For example,

the overture fails to define “a wholly Christian education.”
Likewise, “financial, spiritual, and instructional assistance” that
is “helpful” is vague. The Constitution already stipulates the
office of deacon is provided for “helping one another in time of
need” ( _BCO_ 9-1) and “it is the duty of the deacons to minister to
those who are in need” ( _BCO_ 9-2).
12. That the General Assembly approve the 2020 CDM budget as

presented by the Administrative Committee.
_Deferred to the CoC on AC_
13. That the General Assembly approve the June 2018 minutes of the

CDM Permanent Committee meeting. _Adopted_

**47-36 Administrative Committee Informational and Committee of**

**Commissioners Reports**
TE Joe Grider, CoC Chairman, led the Assembly in prayer and yielded
to TE L. Roy Taylor, Stated Clerk, who presented the Informational Report.
(For the full report of the AC Permanent Committee, see Appendix C,
p. 175.)
TE Grider presented the CoC report (below).
He moved **in gross** all the CoC **recommendations with the exception**
**of Recommendations 3, 7, and 13. Recommendation 2** ( **Overture 11**,
awaiting action by the Overtures Committee) and **Recommendation 4** (upon
request) were also **removed from the in gross motion.**
**Recommendations 5-6, 8-12, and 14-36** were **adopted in gross** by
voice vote.

For action on **Recommendation 1** ( **Overture 3** ) see the AC Partial
Report 47-22, p. 28.

<a id="ga47-p64"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=66 printed_page=64 -->

**Recommendation 3** was **adopted,** answering **Overture 24,** “Add
Constitutional Precedence Statement to Policy Manuals,” **in the affirmative**
**as amended by the Permanent Committee.**
**Recommendation 7** was **adopted**, commending Dr. L. Roy Taylor and
reelecting him as Stated Clerk of the PCA for the Assembly year 2019-20.
**Recommendation 4** was **adopted, answering in the negative**
**Overture 27,** “Study Possibility of Remote Voting at General Assembly.” A
number of grounds were given (see CoC Report below).
**Recommendation 13,** regarding memorials, was **deferred** to the
Overtures Committee in reference to its consideration of Overture 6.

TE Joe Grider yielded to TE L. Roy Taylor, who announced that this
will be his last Assembly year to serve as Stated Clerk.
RE Todd Carlisle, AC Permanent Committee Chairman, then addressed
the court regarding the work of the search committee, emphasizing the
committee’s commitment to serve the entire PCA as they seek Dr. Taylor’s

successor.

The Chairman closed the report with prayer.

**REPORT OF THE COMMITTEE OF COMMISSIONERS ON**

**ADMINISTRATIVE COMMITTEE**

**TO THE FORTY-SEVENTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY**

**OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA**

**I. Business Referred to the Committee**

A. AC Permanent Committee Report to GA
B. Minutes of the 2018-2019 meetings of the AC and Board of

Directors

1. AC **–** June 13, 2018, October 4, 2018, and April 25, 2019
2. BD – June 13, 2018, October 4, 2018, and April 25, 2019
C. Budgets for the permanent Committees and Agencies
D. Overtures Referred to the AC

E. Recommendations of the AC Committee

**II. Statement of Major Issues Discussed**

A. AC Permanent Committee Report to GA
B. Minutes of the 2018-2019 meetings of the AC and Board of

Directors

1. AC **–** June 13, 2018, October 4, 2018, and April 25, 2019
2. BD – June 13, 2018, October 4, 2018, and April 25, 2019
C. Budgets for the permanent Committees and Agencies

<a id="ga47-p65"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=67 printed_page=65 -->

D. Overtures related to the AC

E. Recommendations of the AC Committee

**III. Recommendations**

1. That **Overture 3** from Northern New England Presbytery, “Amend

_RAO_ 10-3 to Promote the Exercise of Godly Prudence in Planning
and Leading Times of Corporate Prayer and Worship at General
Assembly,” **be answered in the negative** . _Adopted_
Grounds

- A protest and point of order were raised at the Forty-sixth
General Assembly regarding offenses taken at a pre-assembly
prayer meeting ( _M46GA_, 2018, p. 20, Item 46-15 Protest):

TE Dwight L. Dolby presented a formal protest
regarding the published prayer litany used at the
pre-Assembly Prayer Gathering. TE Kevin Twit
raised a point of order that the protest was out
of order for being intemperate and for calling
future Assemblies to action. On advice from the

parliamentarians, the Moderator ruled the
protest out of order _**because it was not against**_
_**an action of the Assembly,**_ [emphasis added]
but rather against a pre-Assembly function
( _BCO_ 45-3). The ruling on the protest rendered
the point of order moot.”

- _RAO_ 10-3 relates to the corporate worship services of the entire
General Assembly, not to pre-Assembly prayer meetings.
Overture 3 combines public worship services of the entire
Assembly with pre-Assembly prayer meetings.

- The primary function of the AC regarding pre-Assembly prayer
meetings is to provide a room for commissioners and guests to
pray for the General Assembly. Those involved in preAssembly prayer meetings are already expected to use godly
prudence and wisdom in conducting such prayer meetings.

- There were several rooms made available for pre-Assembly
prayer meetings in 2018. Some attendees could have taken issue
with what was said at any of those gatherings.

- A better means of dealing with the issue of potential offenses is
for leaders of prayer meetings to exercise godly wisdom in
leading prayer meetings and for attendees to refrain from making
corporate confessions of sin on matters for which they do not
individually believe themselves to be culpable.

<a id="ga47-p66"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=68 printed_page=66 -->

- For AC staff or Committee members to attend, monitor, and
report on every potential offense that could be taken by any
individual at every pre-Assembly prayer meeting would be
impractical.
2. That **Overture 11** from Fellowship Presbytery, “Commend and

Distribute the RPCNA’s ‘Contemporary Perspectives on Sexual
Orientation: A Theological and Pastoral Analysis,’” **be answered in**
**the negative** . _Adopted_
Grounds

- The report of the Cooperative Ministries Committee already
suggested to courts and members of the PCA the RPCNA report
as a useful resource ( _M44GA_, 2016, p. 334) along with other

resources.

- There could be some copyright issues involved in printing and
distributing the RPCNA report.

- The RPCNA report is already available for online access to
anyone interested as Overture 11 mentions
[http://rparchives.org/data/Minutes%20of%20Synod/](http://rparchives.org/data/Minutes%20of%20Synod/%202011%20Minutes.pdf)
[2011%20Minutes.pdf](http://rparchives.org/data/Minutes%20of%20Synod/%202011%20Minutes.pdf) .

- Ordinarily the distribution of reports and materials to
Presbyteries, Teaching Elders, and Sessions is the responsibility
of the AC. The distribution of the RPCNA report would be
another unfunded mandate to the AC to distribute the RPCNA

to approximately 1,800 churches and 4,800 ministers.
3. That **Overture 24** from Philadelphia Metro West Presbytery, “Add

Constitutional Precedence Statement to Policy Manuals,” **be**
**answered in the affirmative** _**as amended**_ as follows:

Therefore, it is resolved . . . to add the following statement as a

preface or conclusion to all policy manuals of the Permanent
Committees and Agencies: ~~“At every point, this entire~~
~~manual is subordinate to the entire Constitution of the~~

~~Presbyterian~~ ~~Church~~ ~~in~~ ~~America—the~~ ~~Westminster~~
~~Standards and the Book of Church Order. If there exists any~~
~~conflict between the PCA’s constitutional documents and~~
~~the content and stipulations of this manual, the Constitution~~
~~carries final authority.”~~ “The ecclesiastical Constitution of
the Church is defined in the Book of Church Order, Preface
III. The provisions of the Constitution shall control over any
provisions of this policy manual that may be in conflict
therewith.”

<a id="ga47-p67"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=69 printed_page=67 -->

Further resolved, that any material change in a policy manual

produced ~~or amended b~~ y any of the Permanent Committees
and Agencies shall ~~, before becoming operational,~~ be
approved by its respective committee or board and
submitted to the General Assembly ~~for ratification~~ through
the usual process of the reports of the Permanent
Committees and Agencies in accordance with _RAO_ 4-21.j
and _RAO_ 14-11.d, f, g, h.
Further resolved that each of the Permanent Committees and

Agencies ~~report back on the implementation of these actions~~
~~to~~ shall act to include a statement of Constitutional

Precedence in their respective policy manuals and record
such action in their minutes and, in this manner, report to
their respective Committee of Commissioners at the 48 [th ]

General Assembly so that the Committees of Commissioners
may examine the minutes of Committees and Agencies and
report to the General Assembly.
_Adopted_
Grounds:

- Our “Corporate Bylaws of the Presbyterian Church in America
(A Corporation), Article VIII, Section 1” has a statement of
Constitutional Precedence, “The ecclesiastical Constitution of
the Church is defined in the _Book of Church Order_, Preface III.
The provisions of the Constitution shall control over any
provisions of these Bylaws that may be in conflict therewith.”

- The above recommendation is simply an adaptation of the
Corporate Bylaws of the Presbyterian Church in America (A
Corporation), Article VIII, Section 1.

- _Robert’s Rules of Order_ prohibits actions or motions that are
contrary to an entity’s constitution, _RONR_ (11 [th] ed.) p. 343, ll.
14-17. If any such actions or motions are approved, they are
invalid. In a sense, the overture is unnecessary. Nevertheless, it
would be appropriate to include a statement regarding the
priority of the PCA Constitution.

- _BCO_ 14-1.7 ensures that Committees and Agencies do
not infringe upon the rights or powers of any church court
(Session, Presbytery, or General Assembly). “The Assembly’s
committees are to serve and not to direct any Church
judicatories. They are not to establish policy, but rather execute
policy established by the General Assembly.”

<a id="ga47-p68"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=70 printed_page=68 -->

- _BCO_ 14-1.7 has historically been understood to mean major
ecclesiastical policies, not every-day-operations policies. For
example, the MTW Cooperative Agreement with independent
missions agencies was a major issue at the Second General
Assembly ( _M2GA_, 1974, pp. 66, 68). The General Assembly,
however, has never decided every-day-operations policies such
as the office hours of a Committee or Agency, on which holidays
offices are to be closed, the salary range of staff persons other
than the Chief Administrative Officers, staff job descriptions, or
personnel vacations, etc.

- _RAO_ 4-21.j states that “An up-to-date copy of the bylaws and
manual of the Committee or Agency, if such exists, should be
kept with the minutes.” A Committee of Commissioners may
examine those bylaws and manual(s) under _RAO_ 14-11.

- _RAO_ 14-11 provides a procedure in examining the minutes of a
Committee or Agency for a Committee of Commissioners to
report to the General Assembly any alleged violations of the
PCA Constitution, a procedure for the Committee or Agency to
respond, and a procedure for the General Assembly to decide on
any exception taken to the minutes.

- Committee or Agency policies are subject to the requirements of
the civil law. That relates to corporate laws and sexual abuse,
misconduct, or harassment laws as well.

- There is a difference between employment law and ecclesiastical
discipline. Each has its own respective procedures and
requirements. They may be similar, but are not legally or
ecclesiastically required to be identical. For example, a
Committee or Agency could terminate an employee for a failure
adequately to carry out one’s responsibilities, which could not
necessarily be a matter of ecclesiastical discipline. if it were a
possible matter for ecclesiastical discipline, the Committee or
Agency should report the matter to the ecclesiastical court of
original jurisdiction for the ecclesiastical court to make a
determination in accordance with the _Book of Church Order_,
Part II, The Rules of Discipline.

- Committees and Agencies would be well advised to make a
distinction between policy manuals and handbooks. For
example, Covenant College could have handbooks that stipulate
what a minimum or maximum teaching load fora professor
would be or what expectations of student conduct are, without
the necessity of getting the General Assembly’s approval.

<a id="ga47-p69"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=71 printed_page=69 -->

4. That **Overture 27** from Westminster Presbytery, “Study Possibility

of Remote Voting at General Assembly,” **be answered in the**
**negative.** ~~**with the proviso**~~ ~~that the AC report its finding and~~
~~positive or negative recommendation regarding the feasibility to the~~
~~Forty-eighth General Assembly in 2020.~~ _Adopted_
Grounds:

- Both creation and redemption highlight the value of humans
being embodied creatures. The historic practice at GA of singing
Psalm 133 with its distinctively physical images, as well as _RAO_
1-1 which indicates that the GA begin with a worship service
including prayer, a sermon, and the Lord’s Supper, recognize
the church’s physical presence together in one place as being
constitutive of the Assembly.

- Because a technology exists does not mean it should be
implemented. As indicated by our cultural moment,
conversation on weighty matters involving deeply held
theological and religious convictions are best discussed face to
face. Engaging important issues in the same room is
fundamentally different than over a screen.

- Implementing Distance Voting runs the risk of communicating
to TEs and REs (and to congregants) that being physical present
for the work of the church is not essential.

- As indicated in the Theological Ground, Christian worship is
physical in nature. The work of the General Assembly and our
church courts goes beyond voting on substantive issues, but
glorifying God and edifying one another as the body of Christ.

- The primary concern of Overture 27 is the involvement of REs
in the work of the GA. However, no supportive data is given that
indicates that proxy voting would increase RE involvement.

- The overture does not have a specific cost and cap of
implementing a new technology.

- Currently the AC does not have appropriate cyber security
policies in place for implementation of a distributive service (i.e.
cloud service).

13. Memorial resolutions were ruled out of order at the Forty-sixth

General Assembly. The parliamentarians were asked by the
Moderator to make a recommendation through the AC regarding
memorials. The AC recommends that _RAO_ 13-6 be amended by
adding the following sentence to the end of the paragraph, All such
overtures must conform to _Robert’s Rules of Order_ regarding
Decorum in Debate. Further, that the AC’s recommendation be
referred to the Overtures Committee so that the OC may consider
the AC’s recommendation as they consider a response to Overture 6
from Pacific Northwest Presbytery to eliminate all memorial
resolutions. _Adopted_
14. That the AC recommend to the General Assembly that that the Forty
eighth General Assembly in 2020 convene on Tuesday Evening at
7:30 p.m. for worship, the election of a Moderator and preliminary
business, and adjourn no later than Friday at noon. ~~on Thursday night~~
~~by midnight.~~ _Adopted_
Grounds:

- Concerns were expressed at the October 4, 2018, AC meeting
that the shortened Assembly schedule was presenting difficulties.

- The shortened Assembly has not achieved the goal of greater
Ruling Elder participation.

- Based on past experience, the Assembly prefers having a
Thursday night business session to a Friday morning business
session. However, Assembly reserves the right to have a Friday
morning business session, if needed.

- Commissioners who prefer to spend Wednesday and Thursday
only in business sessions could still do so under the revised
schedule, missing committee of commissioners meetings,
seminars, one worship service, and the election of the Moderator.

- Committee and Agencies, as well as other entities that have meal
events and gatherings report that the shortened schedule has
been a hindrance.

- Exhibitors prefer having more visitors to the exhibits on
Tuesday.

- We need a year’s lead time to change the contracts for
convention centers.

<a id="ga47-p72"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=74 printed_page=72 -->

- NOTE: _RAO_ 8-4.i would need to be changed back to floor
nominations being due on the second day of business and the
Nominating Committee’s reporting the third day of business.

**47-39 Overtures Committee Report**
TE Bruce O’Neil made a procedural motion that the Assembly take
up Overture 42 first in the Overture Committee Report.
Overtures Committee Chairman TE Sean Lucas explained the
Committee’s rationale for the order in which they were presenting the

overtures.
After a short debate, a **motion** to call the question **passed** .
The **procedural motion failed** (355- 880)
Chairman Lucas led the Assembly in prayer and presented the report
(see p. 86).
TE Lucas brought before the Assembly the overtures dealing with
changes to the _BCO,_ explaining the Committee’s rationale for each
recommendation. He then moved in gross Recommendations **17, 36, 1, 5, 9,**
and **34** related to the corresponding Overtures. Upon request, Overture 9 was
removed from the in gross motion.
**Recommendations** **17, 36, 1, 5,** and **34**, regarding the corresponding
overtures, were **adopted in gross.** by voice vote.
**Recommendation 9** to answer Overture 9 in the affirmative as
amended by the CoC was **adopted** by voice vote.
**Recommendation 33** to answer **Overture 33** in the negative **was**
**adopted** by voice vote.
Chairman Lucas proceeded to consideration of the overtures regarding
human sexuality ( **Overtures 4, 22, 11, 35, 39, 42, 30, 44, 45, 37, and 28** ).
RE Mel Duncan presented the Committee’s **Recommendation 4**
that Overture 4 (to declare the “‘Nashville Statement’ on Biblical Sexuality
as a Biblically Faithful Declaration”) be answered in the affirmative.
A **Minority Report** for Recommendation 4 (see p. 112), presented
by RE Jim Wert, offered a **substitute motion** that Overture 4 be recommitted
to the Overtures Committee of the 48 [th] General Assembly.
RE Duncan responded on behalf of the OC.
Debate on the issue ensued.
RE Duncan gave the final word for the Committee’s
recommendation.( _RAO_ 19-4.b).
The **Minority Report** calling for recommittal of Overture 4 **failed**
(572-792).
**Recommendation 4** that Overture 4 be **answered in affirmative**

was **adopted** (803-541).

<a id="ga47-p77"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=79 printed_page=77 -->

**Recommendation 22** that Overture 22 be answered with reference
to answer to Overture 4 was **adopted** by voice vote.
Chairman Lucas presented **Recommendation 11,** that Overture 11
be answered in the affirmative as amended by the CoC.
According to a previous motion of the Assembly, the Moderator
offered the privilege of the floor to representatives of AC, CDM, CC, and
RUF, to whom the Overture had also been referred. As no representative came
forward to speak, the Moderator called for a vote, and **Recommendation 11**
was **adopted** by voice vote.
**Recommendation 35,** that Overture 35 be ruled **out of order** by the
Moderator ( _RONR_ (11 [th] ed.) p. 104 ll. 24-31), was **so ruled** on the grounds
given in the OC Report (p. 103).
**Recommendation 39**, that Overture 39 be ruled **out of order** by the
Moderator ( _RONR_ (11 [th] ed.) p. 104 ll. 24-31), was **so ruled** on the grounds
given in the OC Report (p. 691).
Chairman Lucas presented **Recommendation 42,** that Overture 42,
“Establish Study Committee on Sexuality,” be answered in the affirmative
as amended by the CoC.

[Clerk’s Note: The Assembly **adopted** the Administrative Committee
CoC changes to the overture recommending that the budget for the Study
Committee be raised from $15,000 to $25,000, provided solely by designated
gifts to the AC (p. 70).]
After a short debate, a **motion** to call the question **passed** (1216-85).
**Recommendation 42** was **adopted** by voice vote.
**Recommendations 30, 44, and 45**, that Overtures 30, 44, and 45 be
answered with reference to overture 42, were moved **in gross** . Seeing no
opposition, the Moderator declared them **adopted** by common consent **.**
**Recommendation 37,** that Overture 37, “Assembly Statement on
Same-sex Attraction,” be answered with reference to Assembly’s answer
to Overture 4, was presented.
A **Minority Report** for Recommendation 37 (see p. 134) offered a
**substitute motion** that Overture 37, as amended by the Minority, be
answered in the affirmative.
In accordance with _RAO_ 15-8 g (1), TE Sean Lucas, Chairman,
presented the committee’s rationale. TE Daniel Shrock presented the Minority
Report. TE Sean Lucas then gave a response.
Debate on the floor ensued.
A **motion** **to call the question passed** by hand vote.
The **substitute motion** of the Minority **failed** (492-724)
**Recommendation 37,** that Overture 37 be answered with reference
to Overture 4, was **adopted** by common consent.

<a id="ga47-p78"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=80 printed_page=78 -->

**Recommendation 28,** that Overture 28, “Affirmations and Denials
regarding Homosexuality,” be answered with reference to Overture 4, was
presented.
A **Minority Report** for Recommendation 28 (see p. 120) offered as
a **substitute motion** that the Assembly adopt an amended version of
Overture 28 with 12 articles affirming the Bible’s teaching about
homosexuality.
TE Sean Lucas, Chairman, presented the committee’s rationale for
its recommendation.

TE Steve Warhurst presented the Minority Report.
TE David Richter rose to a **point of order** that the Minority Report
was not appropriate because an argument being made was not germane to the
overture. The Moderator **ruled** that the point of order was **well taken** and
that the line of argument was not germane to the overture.
TE Warhurst challenged the ruling of the chair.
A **point of order** was raised by TE Ewan Kennedy that the entire
Minority Report was not germane to the overture because it addressed issues
related to Revoice. The Moderator **ruled** the point of order was **not well**
**taken** because the Revoice conference was mentioned in the overture, and
he allowed te war hurst to continue.
TE Sean Lucas spoke again for the Committee’s recommendation.
Debate on the floor ensued.
The question was called, and the **substitute motion** of the Minority
**failed** (210-967)
Hearing no objection, the Moderator declared that **Recommendation 28**
was **adopted** by common consent, thereby answering Overture 28 with
reference to Overture 4.
A procedural **motion** made to amend the docket **failed** .
The Stated Clerk made announcements.

**47-40** **Assembly Recessed**
The Assembly recessed at 12:05 a.m. to reconvene at 8:00 a.m. for
worship.

**Fifth Session - Friday Morning**

**June 28, 2019**

**47-41 Assembly Reconvened**
The Assembly reconvened at 8:50 a.m. following the Worship
Service. RE Jim Pocta opened with prayer.

<a id="ga47-p79"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=81 printed_page=79 -->

**47-42 Report of the Overtures Committee (continued from 47-39)**
Chairman Sean Lucas continued the Overtures report, bringing
before the Assembly the overtures dealing with domestic abuse and sexual
assault (Overtures 7, 10, 13, 20, 26, 31, 38, 43, and 47).
**Recommendation 7,** that Overture 7, “Form an Ad Interim Committee
to Address Matters Related to Domestic Abuse, Domestic Oppression, and
Sexual Assault” be answered in the affirmative as amended, was **adopted** by
hand vote **.**

[Clerk’s Note: The Assembly **adopted** the Administrative Committee
CoC changes to the overture recommending that the budget for the Study
Committee be raised from $15,000 to $25,000, provided solely by designated
gifts to the AC (see p. 69).]
teed wright raised a **point of order** that speakers in debate are
allowed five minutes, not three. The Moderator **ruled** the point of order **not**
**well taken** on the grounds that the five-minute rule applies to debates on
minority reports, whereas only three minutes per speaker are allowed in
debates on other motions.

Chairman Lucas moved **in gross** that **Recommendations 10, 13, 20,**
**26, 31, 38, 43,** and **47**, regarding the corresponding Overtures, **be answered**
**in reference to Overture 7** . The motion was **adopted** by voice vote.
Chairman Lucas brought before the Assembly the overtures dealing
with changes to allow non-ordained persons to serve on Committees and
Boards (Overtures 8, 14, 19, 21, and 32).
Chairman Lucas moved **Recommendation 8,** that Overture 8, “Amend
_BCO_ 14-1.10, 14-1.11, and 14-1.12 to Allow Non-ordained Persons to serve
on Committees and Boards” be answered in the negative.
A **Minority Report** for Recommendation 8 offered a **substitute**
**motion** to answer Overture 8 in the affirmative as amended (see p. 113)
Chairman Sean Lucas, Chairman, presented the committee’s
rationale for its recommendation.

RE E.J. Nusbaum presented the Minority Report.
TE Lucas responded for the Committee.
Debate on the floor of the Assembly ensued.
TE Irwyn Ince **moved** to recommit Overture 8 to the Overtures
Committee of the 48 [th] GA. The motion **failed** . (442-471)
TE Daniel Jarstfer raised a **point of order** that the Moderator, in
seeking to alternate between speakers for and against motions, was allowing
debate to be cut off when the question is called, and commissioners waiting
at the microphone were losing the opportunity to speak.

<a id="ga47-p80"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=82 printed_page=80 -->

The Moderator declared that there was no breach of the rules of order
and accepted the motion to call the question ( _RONR_ [11th ed] pp. 197-202;
p.386, ll. 27-29).
TE Daniel Jartsfer again raised a **point of order** concerning the order
in which commissioners at the microphone are recognized.
The Moderator **ruled** that alternating between speeches has long
been the procedure followed and, according to Robert’s Rules, a procedure
that is customary ( _RONR_ [11 [th] ed.] p. 31, ll. 8-17). Therefore, **calling the**
**question would be putt oa vote** ( _RONR_ [11 [th] ed.] p. 31, ll. 8-17).
The question was called, and the Assembly ended debate by voice

vote.
Chairman Sean Lucas had the final word ( _RAO_ 19-4 b).
The Minority Report for Recommendation 8 **failed to be adopted** .
(274-639)
**Recommendation 8** to **answer Overture 8 in the negative** was
**adopted** (618-260).

[See 47-39 below for action on Overtures 14, 19, 21, and 32.]

**47-43 Protest**
TE Kevin Twit arose to file a formal **protest** (see below) against
perceived intemperate language in a speech given in support of the Minority
Report on Overture 28, presented at the Thursday evening session ( _RONR_

[11 [th] ed.] p. 43, ll. 18-20; p. 392, ll. 12-25).
TE Ric Appleton raised an objection to entering the protest into the
record because of the perceived intemperate language of the protest. The
Assembly voted by hand vote against the objection. The hand vote was
challenged, and on a vote the objection to the protest **failed** (408-450).
The Moderator indicated the procedure for commissioners who
desired to sign the protest. RE Christian Madsen requested that the Moderator
rule the **protest out of order** on the basis that the protest was not against an
action of the court ( _BCO_ 45-3) but against a person. The Moderator **ruled**
that the protest would stand ( _BCO_ 45-5).
TE David Boxerman **challenged** the chair’s ruling that the protest
was in order. The Chair was **sustained** by a hand vote.

**47-44 Report of the Overtures Committee (continued from 47-42)**
Chairman Lucas brought before the Assembly **Recommendation 2,**
that Overture 2 be answered in the negative.
TE Lucas reminded the Assembly that when the docket was
changed, CTS action on Overture 2 was deferred until after the OC acted.
TE Dr. Mark Dalbey, President of CTS, spoke briefly from the floor,
emphasizing the delight of CTS to be under the authority of the PCA and
recommending that the Assembly approve the OC action.
**Recommendation 2**, that Overture 2, “Encourage CTS toward
Growth and Release from Assembly Oversight,” be answered in the negative,
was **adopted** (681-164).
Chairman Lucas presented **in gross** **Recommendations 14, 19 21,**
**32**, that the corresponding overtures dealing with changes to allow nonordained persons to serve on Committees and Boards be answered with
reference to Overture 8. Seeing no objection, the Moderator declared
**Recommendations 14, 19, 21**, and **32** **adopted** by common consent.
**Recommendation 46,** that Overture 46 be ruled **out of order** by the
Moderator because it is reaffirming a position previously taken by the
Assembly, was **so ruled** by the Moderator ( _RONR_ [11 [th] ed.] p. 104 ll. 24-31).
**Recommendation 40.** that Overture 40, “…for Sessions to
Acknowledge and Support Women Leaders without Delay or More
Divisiveness,” be answered in the negative, was presented. Seeing no
objection, the Moderator declared **Recommendation 40 adopted** by common

consent..

<a id="ga47-p86"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=88 printed_page=86 -->

**Recommendation 48,** that Overture 48, “Make a Stronger Statement
regarding Abortion” be answered in the negative, was presented. Seeing no
objection, the Moderator declared **Recommendation 48 adopted** by common

consent.

**REPORT OF THE OVERTURES COMMITTEE**

**TO THE FORTY-SEVENTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY**

**OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA**

**I. Business Referred to the Committee** - 41 Overtures: 1-2, 4-22, 26,

28, 30-40, 42-48
Thirteen overtures (7, 10, 13, 20, 26, 30, 31, 38, 42, 43, 44, 45, and 47)
were referred to the AC as well as to the OC. One overture (11) was
referred to AC, CDM, CC, and RUF as well as to the OC. Five overtures,
referred exclusively to other Committees or Agencies, were not
considered by the OC (3 and 27 to AC; 23 to IRC; 25 to CDM; and 24
to AC, CDM, MNA, MTW, RUF, CC, CTS, PCAF, PCA-RBI, and RH).

**II. Statement of Major Issues Discussed**

Each overture was discussed and recommendations were made. If the

OC did not recommend any amendment, then the overture is not
reprinted, and we have included only the Clerk’s Summary Title. In
instances where the OC proposed amendments, the Presbytery’s
proposed action is reprinted, noting the changes proposed by OC.

The full texts of the Overtures, as originally submitted, are found on
pp. 581-728. OC Recommendation numbers in this report correspond to
the Overture numbers.

**III.** **Summary of Recommendations**

Overture Presbytery and Description Disposition Vote
1. Northwest Georgia – Amend _BCO_ 14-2 Negative 101-27-1
2. Northwest Georgia – Amend _BCO_ 14-1.12 Negative 79-39-5
4. Calvary – Affirm “Nashville Statement” Affirmative 74-53-1
5. Central Indiana – Amend _BCO_ 23-1 Refer back 108-16-3

6. Pacific NW – Amend _RAO_ 11 and 13 Affirmative 120-7-2

7. Pacific NW – Form Ad Interim Committee

Affirmative/Amended 104-20-0

8. Ohio Valley – Amend _BCO_ 14-1.10-12 Negative 84-38-0
9. Platte Valley – Amend _BCO_ 42-4; 43-2; 43-3 Affirm./Amended 116-3-7
10. N. New England – Appoint Ad Interim Committee Ref. to O-7 122-1-0

<a id="ga47-p87"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=89 printed_page=87 -->

Affirmative/Amended 97-24-4

14. S. New England – Amend _BCO_ 14.1.10-12 Ref. to O-8 111-4-4

Add _RAO_ 1-6, 1-7, 1-8 Negative 106-19-3

18. Houston Metro – Amend _RAO_ 11-5 Affirmative/Amended 127-0-0

Homosexuality Ref. to O-4 91-34-0

Ref. to O-4 71-52-2

Sanctity of Life Out of order 115-2-1
