**2020 PARTNERSHIP SHARES STATEMENT**

**FOR THE PCA GENERAL ASSEMBLY MINISTRIES**

**PREFACE**

The working definition under which the 2020 Partnership Share Budgets have
been calculated is as follows.

_**As a general statement, “Partnership Shares” describes the**_
_**amount of money needed to cover the anticipated total expenses of**_
_**a ministry minus earned income and minus funds designated to**_
_**specific individuals who are missionaries, church planters, campus**_
_**ministers, and staff (unless the ministry also guarantees the full**_
_**compensation of the employee), as well as specific capital funds or**_
_**similar designated monies. This portion of the approved expense**_
_**budget is dependent on contributions from the PCA churches and**_
_**individuals. In every case the “Partnership Share” is permitted to**_
_**be at least the General Administrative and Overhead portion of the**_
_**particular ministry’s total budget.**_

Two important numbers for each participating ministry are provided by the
Partnership Share and Ministry Ask calculations. First, the numbers located
in the column labeled “Per Capita Calculation” are obtained by a per capita
giving formula, which divides the Partnership Share Fund amount for each
General Assembly Ministry by the total number of communicant members
last reported to and accumulated by the Office of the Stated Clerk.

A second set of numbers under the column labeled “Ministry Ask” is
provided for churches. The “Ministry Ask” is the amount of money each
Committee or Agency is asking the churches of the PCA to give if the church
would like to give to PCA Ministries on a “per member” basis. The amount
listed in this column is generally an estimate of what each Committee and
Agency needs to receive from each _**donor**_ church per member in order for
the Committee or Agency to raise their full budget approved by the PCA
General Assembly.

**These two numbers provide churches and individuals with important**
**factors as they seek to decide how to give to the PCA General Assembly**
**Committees and Agencies. All PCA Ministries struggle to raise**
**Partnership Share funds, and none of the PCA ministries would be**
**sustained without generous donors who give far beyond the Partnership**
**Share. Please assist as generously as you are able.**

In short, the Partnership Shares calculation is based on the inaccurate
assumption that all churches have the same giving capacity per member and
that all churches will give to all Committees and Agencies. The Ministry Ask
is a more realistic figure.

<a id="ga47-p263"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=265 printed_page=263 -->

**2020 Budgeted Partnership Shares and Ministry Asks**

**of PCA Ministry Partners**
**by the Participating General Assembly Ministries**

**Ministry**

**Asks**

The total number of Communicant Members according to the PCA Administrative Committee

was 300,424 as of December 31, 2018.

**GENERAL NOTE**

Gifts designated “spread per Partnership Shares” (or some equivalent) and the totally
undesignated gifts (which amount to less than $3,000 a year) will be spread
according to the “Ministry Ask” column (by percentages of the total).

**SPECIFIC COMMITTEE AND AGENCY NOTES**

1 - The PCA Administrative Committee requests that you contribute on the basis of

0.35% of total tithes and offerings (excepting contribution to capital
campaigns for such efforts as new buildings). Please support us in this way
if you are able to do so.
2 – By giving $10 per member to Covenant College, churches qualify for the Church

Scholarship Promise program at Covenant College.
3 – The portion of RUF's budget that General Assembly approves is $15,707,891.

The rest of this amount comes from budgets of churches and presbyteries
that sponsor RUF works.

<a id="ga47-p264"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=266 printed_page=264 -->

#### **APPENDIX D** **REPORT OF** **THE COMMITTEE ON DISCIPLESHIP MINISTRIES** **TO THE FORTY-SEVENTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY** **OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA**

“... but just as we have been approved by God to be entrusted with the gospel,
so we speak, not to please man, but to please God who tests our hearts. So,
being affectionately desirous of you, we were ready to share with you not
only the gospel of God but also our own selves, because you had become
dear to us.”

(1 Thessalonians 2:4, 8 ESV)

Biblical discipleship is a presentation of the God-given truth of salvation that
flows from a God-given affection for those he has placed in your life.
Discipleship presents the life-transforming truth of God’s Word in the
context of relationships. The Committee on Discipleship Ministries (CDM)
exists to strengthen the local church by connecting leaders to the people and
resources they need to make disciples in the ministry to children, youth, and
adults. We pursue that mission through connection: connecting people to
people, and people to resources. Churches in the PCA share the same
commitment to Scripture and the Westminster Standards, but we do ministry
in very different contexts. The various expressions of gospel ministry can
bring blessing. Together, we are stronger.

**Connecting People to People**
CDM staff members and consultants nurture relational connections primarily
through individual contact, local training events, and regional conferences.
Ministry coordinators make daily contact with local church leaders through
e-mail, telephone calls, and face-to-face conversations. The questions span
the spectrum of discipleship ministry from the youngest children to the oldest
child of God. Questions range from “Can you help with ...?” to “Who can I
talk to about ...?” to “Where can I go for ...?” The contact information for
them is on the CDM website ( **pcacdm.org/about-cdm/cdm-staff** ).

Local training events include seminars on women’s ministry, children’s
ministry, parenting, and leadership development. The training is conducted
by CDM staff members or members of our ministry teams for children,

<a id="ga47-p265"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=267 printed_page=265 -->

youth, or women. If you would like to schedule an event, contact the CDM
office through our website at **pcacdm.org/contact-cdm** .
In 2018, CDM worked with local churches to host a series of regional
training opportunities. ONE conferences for women were held in Jackson,
MS, Annapolis, MD, Chattanooga, TN, and St. Louis, MO. Plenary sessions
centered on the theme of unity in the Body of Christ according to Jesus’
prayer in John 17. Workshop sessions were tailored to contextualize biblical
truth according to the needs and issues facing the region of the host church.

Due to the generosity of a donor, CDM continued to partner with host
churches to offer five Established conferences for those who serve in
children’s ministry. Events were held in Naples, FL, Rochester, MN, Olathe,
KS, Boston, MA, and Indianapolis, IN. The theme centered on Psalm 78 and
the ways covenant theology shapes ministry to children. CDM staff members
worked with the host churches to design sessions to meet the needs of the
region.

|Col1|Attendees|Churches<br>involved|
|---|---|---|
|**Connections through regional events**|**Connections through regional events**|**Connections through regional events**|
|4 **ONE** conferences for women|2,104|300|
|5 **Established** conferences for children’s workers|300|77|
|The**Legacy** conference for older adult ministry|50|19|
|**Connections through national training events**|**Connections through national training events**|**Connections through national training events**|
|Women’s Ministry Leadership Training|350|50|
|Children’s Ministry Leadership Training|100|50|
|Directors of Women’s Ministry Training|25|17|

In September 2018, CDM partnered with Covenant PC in Chattanooga, TN,
to host the Legacy Conference. This was an initial attempt to address a
growing need in the PCA to equip and mobilize older adults to serve the
kingdom in a different season of life. A parallel theme was equipping church
members to care for aging adults. A biblical commitment to multi-cultural
ministry includes developing a multi-ethnic and multi-generational approach
to discipleship. CDM is looking to partner with other churches in the future
to continue addressing this need.

Relational connections were also made through annual national training
events. The Women’s Leadership Training (WMLT) is held in February. The
2019 theme was “Life-giving Leadership,” featuring a new book by Karen
Hodge and Susan Hunt. CDM again partnered with Ridge Haven to provide
an annual retreat and training conference for staff and volunteers in
children’s ministry (CMLT). In April 2019, the featured speakers were David

<a id="ga47-p266"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=268 printed_page=266 -->

and Nancy Guthrie and the pastor for the week was Aaron Messner from
Westminster PC, in Atlanta, GA. In October 2018, CDM partnered with
Perimeter Church in Atlanta, GA to provide a time of encouragement and
professional training for those who are part-time and full-time staff members
in women’s ministry. Debbe Mays spoke on care and counseling for women
in the church. A recurring theme in the evaluations from these events is the
benefit of gathering with others who share similar ministries from a shared
theological commitment, yet in different cultural and ministry contexts.
Together, we are stronger.

As CDM looks forward to the coming year, we will again provide training
for directors of women’s ministry (October) and leaders (February for
women’s ministry and April for children’s ministry). We will also continue
the Established conferences in Colorado Springs, CO (October 2019),
Atlanta, GA (February 2020), and Fullerton, CA (March 2020). Additionally,
we will begin another series of regional conferences for women. The
“Hinged” conferences will be in Birmingham, AL, and Charlotte, NC in
January 2020, and San Antonio, TX, and Chicago, IL in April 2020. For more
information, go to **pcacdm.org/cdm-events** .

**Connecting People to Resources**
CDM seeks to connect people to resources through the website
( **pcacdm.org** ), conferences, training events, blogs, and social media. The
Facebook pages for women (Connect PCA Women’s Ministry) and children
(Connect PCA Children’s Ministry), along with the PCA Discipleship
Ministries page, have become important ways PCA members around the
denomination ask questions and share ideas about discipleship ministry. The
_enCourage_ blog ( **encourage.pcacdm.org** ) continues to grow and now has
over 900 subscribers and 166,385 visitors. This is a means for women authors
in the PCA to challenge and encourage those who share a commitment to
Scripture and the Westminster Standards.

The weekly _enCourage_ podcast has also grown to almost 44,000 downloads
and an average of about 830 downloads per episode. Listeners span the globe
and extend well beyond the PCA. In April 2019, CDM launched a new
website and podcast for those in children’s ministry. The GROW site will be
a place for information and resources so staff and volunteers will be “Leading
Children’s Ministry with Purpose.”

CDM continues to serve the PCA and the broader church as a “niche
publisher,” specializing in resources for Bible and small group studies,
particularly those by PCA authors. In response to action of the 46 [th] General

<a id="ga47-p267"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=269 printed_page=267 -->

Assembly (Recommendation 2, _M46GA2018_, p. 628), CDM published _The_
_Pursuit of Gospel Unity—The PCA Papers on Racism and Racial_
_Reconciliation_ . Copies are available from the PCA Bookstore (item #14375).

Other publications were designed to train leaders ( _Life-giving Leadership_ by
Karen Hodge and Susan Hunt, along with a leader’s guide) and parents ( _The_
_Bible Is NOT Boring_ by Lisa Updike). _The Good Shepherd’s Pasture_ by
Elizabeth Harwell is intended to help parents understand and teach the
meaning of covenant baptism. _Beyond the Roles_ (Melanie Cogdill, General
Editor) is a collection of essays by a diverse group of female authors on how
women can exercise their gifts to further Christ’s kingdom bless his Church.
CDM is also partnering with other ministries (Third Mil) and churches (Oak
Mountain PC in Birmingham, AL) to publish resources for the PCA
community. Together, we are stronger.

In January 2019, CDM launched a new certification program for children’s
ministry leaders. In order to maximize the learning experience, CDM capped
the inaugural class at 36 students, which led to a waiting list for the next
class. The program incorporates web-based video instruction, along with the
encouragement of local and national mentors to provide a foundation for
ministry in the context of the PCA. In April 2019, CDM began accepting
additional applications for the January 2020 class. Thus far the program has
been very well-received by the students, their mentors, and leaders in the
churches they represent.

**PCA Bookstore (www.pcabookstore.com)**
The PCA Bookstore serves as an important avenue to connect PCA
individuals and churches to resources that agree with our doctrinal
convictions. A CDM staff member reviews the items listed in the PCA

Bookstore and considers if the resource(s) might be used by people serving
in the PCA. Some customers have expressed gratitude for a place where they
can focus their search for resources and escape the somewhat overwhelming
results from searches on the Internet. Additionally, the PCA Bookstore offers
greater discounts for group orders (5 or more books), along with free
shipping for orders over $30.

The bookstore is designed to operate on a “break-even” basis (i.e., the cost
of inventory, staff salaries, floor space, postage, etc., is covered by sales
revenue). In 2018, bookstore sales increased by 6%, which covered expenses
and provided over $40,000 to CDM’s general fund.

<a id="ga47-p268"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=270 printed_page=268 -->

**Financial Review**

The ministry of CDM is funded through a combination of revenue sources:
1) giving from churches, 2) giving from individuals, 3) revenue from the sale
of resources, and 4) revenue from conference fees. CDM ended 2018 with a
nominal operating deficit of -$10,992 (or 0.7%). Ironically, the loss was
primarily attributable to the abrupt downturn in the stock market in the fourth
quarter of 2018, which caused CDM’s investments to decline -$20,000.
Thankfully, the market rebounded in January 2019 and approximately half
of the year-end loss was quickly recovered. CDM continues to have an
operating reserve greater than the goal of six months of contribution income.
CDM’s financial success in 2018 was primarily due to surpluses realized
through fees for the regional conferences and the large donation given to
underwrite the Established Conferences. This is the third straight year that
God has provided for CDM through conference proceeds and/or
extraordinarily large gifts from individuals.

Since the beginning of the denomination, CDM’s primary source of income
has been support from PCA congregations ( _BCO_ 14-1). However, only 29%
of PCA particular churches contribute financially to the ministry of CDM.
This reality is discouraging to the members of the staff and permanent
committee, as CDM has been hindered in providing training and resources
for ministry to youth, men, and older adults. Together, we could be stronger.

<a id="ga47-p269"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=271 printed_page=269 -->

**Conclusion to the CDM Report**
The Committee on Discipleship Ministries strengthens the church by
connecting leaders to the people and resources they need for discipleship
ministry. We give thanks for what God has done through the ministry of
CDM. By his grace, we strive to be stewards of his truth and the relationships
he creates through his Spirit. There are a variety of gifts, service, and
activities, but the same God who empowers them all in everyone (1
Corinthians 12:4-6). Together, we are stronger.

**Recommendations**

1. That the General Assembly approve the minutes of the meetings of the

Permanent Committee for the Committee on Discipleship Ministries in
September 2018 and March 2019.
2. That the General Assembly receive the 2018 Audit performed by Robins,

Eskew, Smith, and Jordan, and approve the same firm for the 2019 Audit.
3. That the General Assembly give thanks to God and express appreciation

to the churches and individuals who contributed to the 2018 Women’s

Love Gift given to benefit PCA Retirement Benefits, Inc. (RBI). The gift
totaled over $115,000. The funds will be used to launch a ministry of
care and nurture for the wives of PCA pastors.
4. That the General Assembly encourage individuals, local churches, and

presbyteries to utilize the many resources available on the CDM website
(pcacdm.org), particularly those obtained from local churches and posted
on the “Ministry Toolbox,” as well as the many books and resources
offered through the PCA Bookstore ( **pcabookstore.com** ). Churches
could also post links to the CDM website on their sites.
5. That the General Assembly encourage individuals and churches to attend

one of the regional conferences hosted by local congregations in
connection with CDM (Established conferences for those who work with
children’s ministries or Hinge conferences for women in 2020).
6. That the Assembly encourage individuals and local churches to consider

and utilize the excellent curricula from Great Commission Publications
(GCP), e.g., _Show Me Jesus_ and _Kids’ Quest Catechism Club_ for
children, _G2R Genesis to Revelation_ Bible studies for preteens to teens—
including the new _G2R God’s Promises_ —and _So What?_ Bible studies
for youth.
7. That the General Assembly give thanks to TE Scott Barber, TE Tom

Harr, and RE John Kwasny for their faithful service as members of the
Permanent Committee and to Kathy Stair for her helpful service as an
adviser to the Committee.

<a id="ga47-p270"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=272 printed_page=270 -->

8. That the General Assembly re-elect TE Stephen Estock to serve as the

Coordinator for the Committee on Discipleship Ministries (CDM).
9. That Overture 11 from Fellowship Presbytery “Commend and Distribute

RPCNA’s ‘Contemporary Perspectives on Sexual Orientation: A
Theological and Pastoral Analysis’” be answered in the **negative** .
Grounds:

(1) The report mentioned in the Overture was published by Crown &

Covenant Publications in 2012 under the title _The Gospel & Sexual_
_Orientation_ (Michael Lefebvre, Editor). Given that the material is
under copyright with Crown & Covenant Publications and The
Synod of the Reformed Presbyterian Church of North America
(RPCNA), the overture is improper and out of order in that it asks
the GA and CDM to do something that would require legal
permission which they do not currently have ( _RRO_ 39, 11 [th] ed., p.
343).
(2) _The Gospel & Sexual Orientation_ is in print and available from

CDM through the PCA Bookstore (pcabookstore.com, item
#11555). It is also available publicly at the URL indicated in the
Overture.

(3) The overture directs the production of curriculum without any

consideration for the proper funding to complete the project (see also
point 1 above).
10. That Overture 24 from Philadelphia Metro West Presbytery “Add

Constitutional Precedence Statement to Policy Manuals” be answered by
**reference** to the recommendation from the Administrative Committee

regarding Overture 24.
11. That Overture 25 from Suncoast Florida Presbytery “Support Christian

Education’” be answered in the **negative** .
Grounds:

(1) The overture is unnecessary. Scripture clearly teaches that parents

have the responsibility for the education of covenant children (Deut.
6, Eph. 6). Church Sessions have the power “to determine the best
measures for promoting the spiritual interests of the church and
congregation” ( _BCO_ 12-5.e). The General Assembly does not need
to affirm what Scripture clearly teaches and the Constitution already
provides.
(2) The particular method(s) used by parents to fulfill their scriptural

responsibility is a matter of conscience, guided by the Holy Spirit
and informed by the Word of God and the pastoral counsel of elders
in the local church. It is inappropriate for the General Assembly to
direct parents (explicitly or implicitly) to a particular method. The

<a id="ga47-p271"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=273 printed_page=271 -->

overture may (unintentionally) bind the conscience of a parent
attempting to fulfill his/her scriptural responsibility.
(3) The overture is vague in some of its stipulations. For example, the

overture fails to define “a wholly Christian education.” Likewise,
“financial, spiritual, and instructional assistance” that is “helpful” is
vague. The Constitution already stipulates the office of deacon is
provided for “helping one another in time of need” ( _BCO_ 9-1) and
“it is the duty of the deacons to minister to those who are in need”
( _BCO_ 9-2).
12. That the General Assembly approve the 2020 CDM budget as presented

by the Administrative Committee.

<a id="ga47-p272"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=274 printed_page=272 -->

**Attachment 1**

**REPORT FROM THE PUBLISHING MINISTRY OF**

**GREAT COMMISSION PUBLICATIONS (GCP)**

Great Commission Publications (GCP) is the official publishing ministry of
the PCA and the Orthodox Presbyterian Church (OPC). This joint publishing
ministry was established by the respective General Assemblies in 1975. GCP
publishes high-quality Reformed and covenantal curriculum for all ages.
This full-orbed system of curriculum is based solely upon the **Bible as the**
**Word of God** and conforms to the **Westminster Standards** .

gc pis governed by a twelve-member board of trustees, all teaching or ruling
elders. Six are elected by the PCA CDM and six by the OPC Committee on
Christian Education (CCE). Additionally, the PCA CDM Coordinator and
the OPC CCE General Secretary serve as ex-officio, non-voting trustees.
Currently the GCP Executive Director and the Director of Publishing are
PCA teaching elders. The GCP Director of Business Operations is a PCA
ruling elder. The President of the board of trustees for 2019 is OPC teaching
elder Dr. Alan Strange.

Our newest curriculum is “G2R—God’s Promises,” written to teach
covenant theology to grades 7-9. (G2R is an abbreviation for “Genesis to
Revelation.”) “G2R—God’s Promises” completes the G2R curriculum,
which begins with Bible surveys of the Old Testament and the New
Testament. This survey of all the Old and New Testament books helps
students integrate the “stories” of the Bible they have heard into a
comprehensive understanding of God’s single story of his redemptive plan
throughout Scripture. Both departments allow churches great flexibility in
how to group grades 7-9. Check out **www.gcp.org** to see how to use these
studies. Upon completion of the G2R curriculum, students should be well
grounded not only in the entire Bible, but with the essence of covenant
theology.

GCP also publishes _**Trinity Hymnal**_, a resource that enriches worship with
its collection of over 700 hymns. It includes the Westminster Confession of
Faith, as well the Creeds. Orchestrations are available for most of the hymns.

**Show Me Jesus** (Toddler through fourth grade) is the core upon which all
the rest of GCP’s curriculum is built. Further, **Kids’ Quest** is a club program
that is a catechetical curriculum ( _**First Catechism**_ ) suited for use in many

<a id="ga47-p273"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=275 printed_page=273 -->

settings of church life. GCP also has a pocket edition of _First Catechism_ for
use by all ages, as well as digital editions of _First Catechism_ and the Shorter
Catechism. **G2R** follows **Show Me Jesus**, and is followed by **So What?** for
high school students

**So What?** Bible studies for youth continue to grow in popularity. There are
ten studies available in a modular format, which makes them flexible for
youth groups, small group Bible studies, homeschools, and Christian day
schools. Visit the **So What?** website at **www.sowhatstudies.org** for
samples, video previews, and more.

GCP publishes a child’s curriculum based on _**The Pilgrim’s Progress**_ . It
features a new child’s edition of the book, a dramatic reading, and a selection
of original songs, as well as lessons. For sample lessons, please visit
**www.childrenspilgrimsprogress.org.**

Please visit our website at **www.gcp.org** to order or call **800-695-3387** to
speak to a curriculum specialist.

<a id="ga47-p274"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=276 printed_page=274 -->

#### **APPENDIX E** **REPORT OF COVENANT COLLEGE** **TO THE FORTY-SEVENTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY** **OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA**

Greetings in the name of Jesus Christ,

On behalf of the Board of Trustees and the Covenant College community, I
offer this annual report on the 2017-2018 academic year.

**Academics**

We appointed five full-time faculty members, who were installed at our
opening convocation in the fall of 2017:

- Dr. Chris Cyr, Assistant Professor of Mathematics; with a Ph.D. in

mathematics from the University of Florida

- Dr. Heath Garris, Assistant Professor of Biology; with a Ph.D. in

integrated bioscience, University of Akron

- Mr. Brian Hecker, Reference Librarian; with an MLIS (Master of

Library and Information Science) from the University of Wisconsin
at Milwaukee

- Dr. David Saiki, Associate Professor of Chemistry; with a Ph.D. in

physical chemistry from the University of California Davis

- Mr. Mark Slavovsky, Assistant Professor of Accounting; with an

MTAX (Master of Taxation) from the University of Tulsa in
Oklahoma

Selected faculty publications for the 2017-2018 academic year:

- Dr. Bill Davis, professor of philosophy and coordinator of faculty

development authored _Departing in Peace: Biblical Decision-_
_Making at the End of Life_ .

- Dr. Bill Dennison, professor of interdisciplinary studies, authored

_Karl Marx: Great Thinker_ s.

- Dr. Jeff Dryden, professor of biblical studies, authored _A_

_Hermeneutic of Wisdom: Recovering the Formative Agency of_
_Scripture_ .

- Dr. Scott Finch, professor of music, composed “Praise God for Joy

of Sabbath Blest” for the _Trinity Psalter Hymnal_ .

- Dr. Kelly Kapic, professor of theological studies, authored

_Embodied Hope: A Theological Meditation on Pain and Suffering_ .

<a id="ga47-p275"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=277 printed_page=275 -->

**Admissions & Marketing**
In the fall of 2017, we welcomed 296 new students who came from 36 states
and 11 countries. Throughout the year we welcomed 1,577 prospective
student visitors to campus from 44 states.

**Campus & Facilities**
In the year 2017-2018, we began the first phase of renovations to a building
generously gifted to Covenant College from neighboring Grace Presbyterian
Church (PCA). This facility, renamed The Kirk, continues to be beneficial to
Covenant providing much needed office, meeting, and multipurpose space.
Other campus initiatives include building system improvements as well as
an investigation to address water infiltration at the Dora Maclellan Brown
Memorial Chapel.

**Development & Finances**
Donors generously gave over $9.8 million to Covenant during the 2017-2018
fiscal year.

Covenant saw positive operating results again this year, with an increase in
unrestricted net assets due to the funding received for the Carter Hall
renovation project, of about $5.6M. This renovation project is now complete
and we are grateful to report that 100% of the funds required have been
received or pledged.

The number of churches that gave to Covenant decreased slightly to 495, and
220 churches participated at a level to qualify for our Church Scholarship
Promise program.

**Strategic Plan**
The 2017-2018 academic year was spent gathering input from the various
constituents across campus and planning the College’s next strategic plan.
Dr. Scott Quatro was retained as strategic planning consultant for the
purposes of assessing and codifying the institutional strategy of the College,
at the direction of the President and Board Chair. This included Dr. Quatro’s
facilitation of Cabinet strategic planning sessions, recommendations, and
deliverables related to the mission, values, and vision of the College.

In the end, the Cabinet presented a 7-year strategic plan that includes a
simplified mission statement, “We challenge one another to explore and
express Christ’s preeminence in all things” and a 5-point 2025 vision for the
College:

<a id="ga47-p276"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=278 printed_page=276 -->

I. 1,100 Strong on the Mountain
At Covenant College, we have capacity for 1,100 traditional
undergraduate students on our Lookout Mountain campus. For the sake
of populating communities around the globe with thoughtful Christian
men and women, we will fill our campus with well-qualified students.
II. $100 Million in the Endowment

We will, through the generosity of our supporters, build an endowment
that provides for the long-term sustainability of Covenant College and
makes a Covenant College education affordable for qualified students.
III. Raise our Voice

We will contribute to larger conversations about what it means to live
faithfully as Christians in this day and age, and will be known as a place
that generates first-rate, insightful, biblically-grounded Christian
thinking and thinkers/doers.
IV. Cultivate our Community

We can only be as strong as our community is healthy. Giving thanks for
the rich blessings of community we already enjoy here at Covenant, we
will make our community one that is marked by the flourishing of
students and faculty and staff.
V. Offer One-of-a-Kind Education

By leveraging both the strengths of our existing program and our unique
location, we will very intentionally make the educational experience we
offer at Covenant College even more distinctive and more valuable.

**Student Life**
The Student Athletic Advisory Committee set a new Covenant record for
"Cans Across the Conference" producing over 600 canned goods. All items
were donated to the Chattanooga-area Food Bank. The Committee has also
implemented new student-athlete specific events such as worship nights and
SAAC-sponsored spikeball tournaments to raise money for conference wide

events.

The CC Lead Program enjoyed its second full year as a cohesive leadership
program for all student leaders. The CC Lead Conference, held in April,
began training for over 120 newly hired student leaders for the 2018-2019
school year. Aaron Damiani was the keynote speaker and his topic was
“Becoming an Unanxious Leader.”

Covenant launched Handshake to our student body in an effort to give our
students a leading-edge web-based platform designed to help them better

<a id="ga47-p277"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=279 printed_page=277 -->

connect with employers. Nearly 1,000 Handshake employers are advertising
some 2,600 jobs/internships specifically to Covenant students and alumni.
Speakers in chapel included Chaplain Grant Lowe preaching on Fridays in
the fall and spring; PCA pastors and members Rev. Erik McDaniel, Rev.
Aaron Tolson, Dr. Dan Doriani, Vaneetha Risner, Rev. Irwyn Ince, Dr. Carl
Ellis, Rev. Randy Nabors, Rev. Paul Billy Arnold; additional guest speakers
included Stephan and Belinda Bauman, Rev. Thabiti Anyabwile, Rev.
George Robertson, Dr. Jock McGregor, Dr. Richard Pratt, Nicole Bromley,
Rev. Timothy George, Dr. Bill Edgar; and a number of faculty lectures and
student led chapels _._

**Recognition**
_U.S. News & World Report_ again ranked Covenant in the top tier of national
liberal arts colleges.

College Consensus ranked Covenant #8 of the Best Colleges & Universities in

- Georgia in 2018. (https://www.collegeconsensus.com/rankings/best colleges ga/)

**Conclusion**
We are grateful for the oversight of the PCA as we seek to remain faithful to
our mission to explore and express the preeminence of Jesus Christ in all
things. Thank you for your continued partnership in the work God is doing
through Covenant College.

Yours in Christ,
J. Derek Halvorson (’93), Ph.D. President

**Recommendations**

1. That the General Assembly thank and praise God for the excellent work

and faithfulness of the Board of Trustees, faculty, and staff of Covenant
College in serving the Presbyterian Church in America by shaping
students for lives of service in the Kingdom of God.
2. That the General Assembly encourage congregations of the PCA to

support the ministry of Covenant College through encouraging
prospective students to attend, through contributing the Partnership
Shares approved by the General Assembly, and through prayer.
3. That the General Assembly approve the budget for 2019-2020, as

submitted through the Administrative Committee.
4. That the General Assembly adopt “The Covenant College and

Supporting Foundation Consolidated Financial Statements” dated June
30, 2018, and prepared by Capin Crouse LLP.

<a id="ga47-p278"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=280 printed_page=278 -->

5. That the General Assembly approve the minutes of the meetings of the

Board of Trustees for October 18-19, 2018, and March 14-15, 2019, with
notations.

6. That the General Assembly receive as information the foregoing Annual

Report, recognizing God’s gracious and abundant blessing and
commending the College in its desire to continue pursuing excellence in
higher education for the glory of God.
7. That the General Assembly designate a Sunday in the month of October

in the coming year on which churches of the denomination are
encouraged to highlight the ministry of Covenant College and to pray
specifically for the College in its mission and ministry.
8. That Overture 11 be answered with reference to the Administrative

Committee's answer to that overture.

9. That Overture 24 be answered with reference to the Administrative

Committee's answer to that overture.

<a id="ga47-p279"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=281 printed_page=279 -->

#### **APPENDIX F** **REPORT OF** **COVENANT THEOLOGICAL SEMINARY** **TO THE FORTY-SEVENTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY** **OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA** **JUNE 2019**

**God’s Word for God’s World:**
**Building on Biblical Foundations for Training Impactful Gospel**

**Leaders**

**Executive Summary**
Last year, we reported on several important changes as the Seminary
launched new degrees and degree formats. We introduced a new church
planting track and 30 percent church-match scholarships to better serve
the needs of the PCA. We grew our online offerings to meet the changing
educational needs of today’s world. We are thankful for the way the Lord
has blessed these efforts so that the church can receive the biblically
trained and qualified leaders it needs and deserves. This year, we built on
these initiatives in several significant ways that included:

- Continued and growing interest in our new MABTS, MAM, and
hybrid-format MDiv programs, especially their online aspects, as
students seek more flexible ways to achieve their education goals.
Our residential programs, including the MDiv and MAC, remain
strong and vital.

- Strategic partnerships with NEXT, Christian Heritage Cambridge,
the Missional Training Center—Phoenix, and disability ministry
expert Stephanie Hubach that further strengthened our training
resources and capabilities.

- Promotions to positions of greater responsibility for several longserving, faithful members of our deeply biblical and Reformed
faculty, with key additions to our adjunct faculty.

- Publication of several major works by faculty and the launch of
a faculty blog called _Orthodoxy and Orthopraxy_ focused on
theological issues and reflections from a biblical and Reformed
perspective.

<a id="ga47-p280"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=282 printed_page=280 -->

The beginning of a year-long celebration of the 30 [th] Anniversary of the
Francis Schaeffer Institute at Covenant Seminary with a variety of events
on campus and other venues to mark the occasion.

- The inaugural Professional Counselor Training Series to provide
professional counselors licensed in the state of Missouri with
quality continuing education units in which Covenant counseling
faculty help Christian counselors think Christianly about
important topics facing counselors, their clients, and the church.

- The launch of redesigned Covenant Seminary branding to better
reflect our historic heritage and unchanging mission in a
changing world.

- The Lord’s provision in bringing us to a place of greater financial
stability after several challenging years, for which we are
immensely grateful.

- Great generosity from our friends and supporters who have given
faithfully to our _Hope for the Future_ Capital Campaign, which is
now reaching its final stage toward our $40 million goal.

These and other initiatives and activities detailed more fully in this report are
evidence of God’s ongoing grace as we seek to glorify him by preparing
biblical and Christ-centered pastors, counselors, youth and campus ministers,
and other ministry leaders for service to his church.

Sadly, this year also brought much contention as we continually had to
defend Covenant Seminary against unfair and inaccurate accusations
made mostly through social media and public blogs. Many of these are
clear violations of the ninth commandment as explained in the
Westminster Larger Catechism questions #143–145. Most of these
attacks have centered around issues related to last year’s Revoice
conference and another event that a St. Louis church was to host. Neither
of these events were affiliated with Covenant Seminary. The accusations
made against us with regard to these two events, coming from a highly
vocal minority within and especially outside the PCA, have, in most
cases, been made without anyone from the originating organizations
attempting to contact our administration to talk about the matters at hand.

In addition, Overture 2 from North Georgia Presbytery has been put forth
to General Assembly and gives the impression that Covenant Seminary
no longer wishes to be part of the denomination. Again, no one from the
Presbytery spoke with any Seminary representatives prior to this overture

<a id="ga47-p281"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=283 printed_page=281 -->

being drafted and submitted to GA, and the impression it gives of a desire
on our part to no longer be under the oversight of the PCA is incorrect
and unfounded.

We have responded to these challenges in Christian love, both publicly
and privately. We invite those who have issues with our institution to
discuss their concerns with us openly and honestly in a spirit of unity
before making public pronouncements or accusations.

**God’s Word for God’s World:**

**Building on Biblical Foundations for Training Impactful Gospel Leaders**

_The purpose of Covenant Theological Seminary is to glorify the triune God_
_by training his servants to walk in God’s grace, minister God’s Word, and_
_equip God’s people—all for God’s mission._ Last year, we reported on several
important changes in how we carry out that purpose: we launched some new
degrees, restructured others, introduced a new church planting track and 30
percent church-match scholarships, and grew our online offerings to meet the
changing educational needs of today’s world. We are thankful for the way
the Lord has blessed these efforts so that the church can receive more of the

biblically trained and qualified leaders it needs and deserves. This year, we
built on these initiatives in several significant ways.

**New Degrees, New Flexible Ways to Learn**
To better meet the growing need in the church for training that is biblical,
accessible, and flexible, Covenant Seminary last fall introduced two new
degree programs. These are designed to help students get the training they
need for their unique ministry or life callings without necessarily relocating
to St. Louis. The degrees also enabled us to offer **a new hybrid-format**
**Master of Divinity** in addition to our traditional residential MDiv program.

- **The Master of Arts (Biblical and Theological Studies)**
**(MABTS)—** available both on-campus and fully online—is ideal for
ministry leaders who do not have the opportunity to complete a
degree in residence or who simply desire more robust biblical
training than most online programs provide. It is also a good option
for those who feel called to teach in the church or academy. The
degree also enables students to **complete two-thirds of an MDiv**
**online** . The MABTS:

<a id="ga47-p282"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=284 printed_page=282 -->

`o` Requires study of **at least one biblical language** (Greek or

Hebrew)—though students may take both if they desire—
and can be done completely online.
`o` Offers an **optional thesis** for those who wish to prepare for

a PhD.
`o` Allows for **easy transfer into the MDiv program** for those

who wish to complete a final third of the MDiv through
week-long visits to campus for intensive courses.
`o` Requires **66 total credit hours** and may be completed in 2

– 4 years.

- **The Master of Arts in Ministry (MAM)—** available on campus or
in a hybrid online/on-campus format—is perfect for people who
sense a call to ministry but need a season of more immersive
preparation. It is also a good choice for busy ministry leaders unable
to relocate to complete their training but who desire a biblical and
practical degree to better equip them for the work to which the Lord
has already called them. The MAM:

`o` Offers **great flexibility** for students to tailor their degree to

their particular needs. All MAM students take substantial
core courses providing foundations in Bible and theology,
with a wide range of electives to spread across classes in
biblical studies and applied theology.
`o` Provides students the opportunity to **focus electives into a**

**concentration area** to better equip them for their unique
ministry contexts or goals. The **MAM General Track** may
be done ina hybrid (online/on-campus) format with as few
as 4 one-week visits to campus for intensive courses. In
addition, the program offers five potential concentration
tracks:

- **Biblical Studies and Christian Ministry —** Ideal
for the ministry leader who wants to handle and
communicate God’s Word and care for his people.
This track may be done in a hybrid format with as
few as 5 one-week visits to campus for intensive

courses.

- **City Ministry —** Ideal for those called to minister
in an urban context, whether in the church or outside
of it. This track tracks require several semester-long

on-campus courses.

<a id="ga47-p283"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=285 printed_page=283 -->

- **Counseling Ministries —** Ideal for those not
seeking professional licensure but who are involved
in counseling ministries such as recovery groups,
diaconal ministry, mercy ministry, etc. This track
may be done in a hybrid format with as few as 5 oneweek visits to campus for intensive courses.

- **Cultural Apologetics and Communication —**
Ideal for those who want to be equipped to address
the issues of our day in a biblical, thoughtful and
winsome way. This track tracks require several
semester-long on-campus courses.

- **Educational Ministries —** Ideal for ministry
directors (women, youth, children’s, etc.) or anyone
called to make disciples in a non-ordained setting.
This track tracks require several semester-long on
campus courses.

`o` Requires 200 hours of **supervised field education work** in

an approved ministry setting.
`o` Requires **54 total credit hours** and may be completed in 2

– 4 years.

- **The Master of Divinity (MDiv) can now also be completed in a**
**hybrid format.** Two-thirds of the degree may be completed online
with the rest being completed in **as few as 6 one-week trips to**
**campus** . While we remain as devoted as ever to residential-based
mentored ministry training, our students now have even greater
flexibility in how they choose to study.

The MABTS online and MAM hybrid options also have **built-in community**
**learning components** so that students not physically on campus can still
experience the benefits of learning and growing together with their peers and
professors.

**The MDiv Church Planting Track: Pathway to Church Planting**
The Seminary last year inaugurated a Church Planting Track for the MDiv
program. The track is designed to meet the increasing need for church
planters in the PCA. Students interested in and gifted for church planting
now have **a way to concentrate their studies for deeper preparedness**
upon graduation. Students may pursue the Church Planting Track through

<a id="ga47-p284"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=286 printed_page=284 -->

**on-campus residential study** or through **the hybrid MDiv format**, using a
combination of online and on-campus intensive (week-long or weekend)
courses to complete the degree. The track offers:

- Classes focused specifically on church planting and related issues.

- Multiple professional assessments of gifting and readiness to be a
church planter.

- Mentored oversight and regular evaluation of developing church
planters.

- Church planting internship and apprenticeship opportunities that
fulfill Field Education and Presbytery internship requirements.

- Training in fundraising and related issues.

- A limited number of Church Planting Scholarships each year for
qualified students.

**New DMin Cohort on Cultural Apologetics and Communication**
Our Doctor of Ministry team this spring launched a **new Cultural**
**Apologetics and Communication cohort,** led by **[Professors Mark Ryan,](https://www.covenantseminary.edu/faculty/mark-ryan/)**
**[Tasha Chapman,](https://www.covenantseminary.edu/faculty/tasha-chapman/)** and **Zack Eswine.** The cohort draws on the ethos and
expertise of the Francis A. Schaeffer Institute to help pastors and others in
ministry learn how to engage people effectively and winsomely with the
gospel in a world where “truth” changes almost daily. Instructors include
Covenant Seminary’s own **Prof. Jerram Barrs, Dr. Michael Williams,** and
**Dr. Michael Goheen,** as well as guest lecturers such as Grace DC Institute
for Cross-Cultural Mission’s Executive Director **Rev. Dr. Irwyn Ince.** The
current iteration of the cohort begins meeting this June and is closed for new
registrations, but our Admissions team is eager to talk with interested parties
about future editions.

**30% Church-Match Scholarships for All Degree Programs**
Churches now have a powerful way to partner with Covenant Seminary in
helping potential pastors and church leaders get the training they need to
minister effectively. The Seminary now offers a Church-Match Scholarship
program for students receiving seminary funding from their churches. **The**
**scholarship matches up to 30% of a student’s tuition cost, totaling a 60%**
**tuition reduction for qualifying students.**

The Church-Match Scholarship is **available for students in all of**
**Covenant’s degree programs and all learning formats** (except for those
through the Missional Training Center–Phoenix), which includes those
studying full-time, part-time, on campus, online, or through one of our new
hybrid-format options.

<a id="ga47-p285"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=287 printed_page=285 -->

Along with the new scholarship, we have also retooled our scholarship
structure in general to offer **even more benefits to students in our online**
**and hybrid degree programs**, including higher scholarship amounts and
longer-term scholarship availability.

- All **hybrid-format Master of Divinity (MDiv) scholarships** will
**last for six years**, allowing students in that program to take courses
part-time.

- **Online Master of Arts (Biblical and Theological Studies)**
**(MABTS)** students are eligible for scholarships **lasting for five-**
**year terms** .

- **Online Master of Arts (Theological Studies) (MATS)** and hybridformat **Master of Arts in Ministry (MAM) scholarships** are
**available for four-year terms** .

**Covenant + NEXT Partnership = Expanded Training for Next-**
**Generation Ministry**
In 2018 we formed **a partnership with** **NEXT (formerly Global Youth**
**and Family Ministries)** that will give Covenant’s ministry-leaders-intraining greater access to the knowledge and expertise of NEXT’s
experienced staff and provide NEXT with a new hub of operations for its
global ministry. The distinctive partnership enables both organizations to
better provide strategic training for shaping a new generation of local church
leaders with a global mindset. Founded by Covenant Seminary alumnus **Dr.**
**Eric Larsen,** NEXT (a ministry of MTW) is focused on equipping
missionaries, national leaders, and indigenous churches around the world to
reach the youth in their communities with the gospel. Larsen holds a
doctorate in youth, family, and culture, and is the author of _MissionNext:_
_How the Church Can Reach a Global Generation_ (Doulos Resources, 2017).
He has taught courses on next-generation ministry at Covenant and other
Christian institutions for many years. Under the partnership, he continues to
teach key courses in Covenant’s youth ministry curriculum and consults
regularly with Seminary faculty, staff, and alumni on best practices for
reaching and equipping emerging generations for ministry.

**Edinburgh Summer School: Transformative Learning in a Unique Setting**
Covenant Seminary continues to partner with the **Chalmers Institute in**
**St. Andrews** to deliver **a week-long summer school** combining the best
theological resources with application to the church’s discipleship and
mission. Previously held in Cambridge, England, this year’s edition of our
summer school program offers **a unique opportunity for pastors, scholars,**

<a id="ga47-p286"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=288 printed_page=286 -->

**and others to deepen their theological learning** and insight while studying
in the beautiful location of Edinburgh, Scotland. Students will gain a deeper
understanding of who God is, what he has called us to do as his people, how
we can understand the Bible, and how to engage our neighbors with his lifegiving message. The July 2019 edition features **Dr. Jack Collins** teaching a
track on the Psalms and Wisdom Literature, while **Prof. Jerram Barrs** and
**Dr. Mark Stirling** (director of the Chalmers Institute) lead a track on
Engaging Contemporary Challenges to Christianity. You can find more at
_www.covenantseminary.edu/ academics/edinburgh/._

**New Faculty Blog:** _**Orthodoxy and Orthopraxy**_
How we understand theology affects how we live that theology out. That’s
why Covenant Seminary’s faculty came together to produce a new theology
blog on our website called _Orthodoxy and Orthopraxy_ . In regular posts,
faculty address thoughtfully and biblically a variety of theological topics and
issues of our day, and provide insight for Christian living and compassionate
gospel witness in our confused and hurting world. Check out _Orthodoxy and_
_Orthopraxy_ on our homepage at _www.covenantseminary.edu_ .

**Same Mission, New Logo**
In 2018, we rolled out a new version of the Covenant Seminary logo with
updates to our website and print publications to reflect the new look and
style. This was the first complete logo and branding redesign for the
institution in nearly 20 years. The goal for the redesign was **to build on the**
**historical imagery of earlier versions** of our logo while crafting **a look that**
**feels more contemporary** . While our mission and theological commitments
remain the same as always, we wanted our new logo to **better reflect both**
**our long heritage of biblical, Reformed theological education and our**
**continuing dedication to preparing church leaders** and Kingdom workers
who can communicate God’s gospel of grace and truth in powerful and
relevant ways in today’s world.

**Celebrating 30 Years of the Francis Schaeffer Institute**
This year marks three decades since the Francis Schaeffer Institute (FSI) was
founded at Covenant Seminary to extend and embody the legacy of
renowned pastor and apologist Francis A. Schaeffer. Established in 1989 by
Professor Jerram Barrs, a student and coworker of Dr. Schaeffer and longtime director of the English branch of L’Abri Fellowship, FSI has had a
profound impact on the formation of countless church and Kingdom leaders.

<a id="ga47-p287"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=289 printed_page=287 -->

We’ve been celebrating FSI throughout 2019 with events and activities
**highlighting the Institute’s purpose of helping students to engage**
**graciously with the people and issues of our day** while communicating the
“true truth” of the gospel of Jesus Christ in culturally relevant ways.

- In February, **Dr. W. Brian Shelton,** provost of Toccoa Falls College
and author of _Quest for the Historical Apostles: Tracing Their Lives_
_and Legacies_, joined us and event co-sponsor Fontbonne University
to discuss his book and related themes.

- In March, FSI joined with Covenant Presbyterian Church in St.
Louis—of which Francis Schaeffer was one of the early pastors—to
celebrate the church’s 80 [th] anniversary. The weekend-long event
entitled _**Speaking the Truth in Love: The Enduring Legacy of**_
_**Francis Schaeffer**_ featured guests **Dr. Bryan Follis,** author of _Truth_
_with Love: The Apologetics of Francis Schaeffer_ ; **Dr. Thurman**
**Williams,** pastor of New City Fellowship–West End, St. Louis;
**Prof. Jerram Barrs,** professor of Christian studies and contemporary
culture; and **Prof. Mark Ryan,** director of FSI—with a special
appearance by recording artist **Matt Epp** at one of Covenant
Church’s evening worship services. Epp also performed a free
concert at Main Street Church in historic Old St. Charles, jointly
sponsored by FSI and Main Street Church.

- Also in March, FSI and Covenant Seminary joined forces to host
**Small Town, Big Gospel, Hope for the Country,** a special
weekend conference/elective course focused on the special
evangelistic/missionary challenges and opportunities of rural church
planting. Featured speakers for the event included **Donnie Griggs**,
pastor of One Harbor Church in Morehead City, North Carolina, and
author of _Small Town Jesus_ ; **Mark Fugitt**, pastor of Mountain
Grove Family Church in Mountain Grove, Missouri; **Belafae**
**Johnson**, pastor of Purposed Church in Mascoutah, Illinois; **Duane**
**Otto,** pastor of Christ Community Church in Chenoa, Illinois, and
founder of Ithaka Fellowship; and **Jeanette Brown,** LPC, with
Magellan Healthcare in St. Louis, MO.

- April saw a visit to Covenant by **Dr. John Dickson,** author of _The_
_Doubter's Guide to the Bible_, _The Doubter’s Guide to Jesus_, and
other similar apologetic works. Dr. Dickson joined us to discuss his
new documentary series _For the Love of God: Why the Church is_
_Better and Worse Than You Ever Imagined_ .

- In October, the annual **Francis Schaeffer Lecture Series** will
feature **Dr. John Dickson** again, along with various other speakers

<a id="ga47-p288"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=290 printed_page=288 -->

still to be announced. The event will take place **October 4–5** . Look
for more information soon.

Audio from these and other FSI events is available at

_www.covenantseminary.edu/ resources_ .

**Covenant Seminary Preaching Lectures: Scriptural Wisdom for**
**Communicating God’s Word**
Preaching is key to the mission and ministry of the church. Preparing good
preachers is one of our main goals at Covenant Seminary. In addition to our
Christ-centered, grace-focused, and apologetically oriented preaching
curriculum, the Seminary each year invites recognized leaders in the field of
homiletics to speak on topics relevant to communicating God’s Word
effectively in today’s world. **The 2018 Preaching Lectures** featured our
own **Dr. Zack Eswine,** director of homiletics and scholar-in-residence with
FSI, speaking on the theme _Sage Voice: How the Wisdom Literature Equips_
_Us to Communicate Jesus in the 21st Century_ . Dr. Eswine presented insights
from the biblical Wisdom Literature to help preachers find points of contact
with the swirling cultural voices of our generation and address wisely the
concerns and questions that raise doubts and suspicions about the gospel.
Audio from the Preaching Lectures is available at
_www.covenantseminary.edu/resources._

**The Austin Harrington Counseling Lectures:** _**The Science of Virtue**_
The Austin Harrington Counseling Lectures, sponsored by the Seminary’s
Counseling Department, each year brings in notable authorities in the field
of Christian counseling to provide additional instruction and resources for
those training to serve God’s people as pastors and counselors. For 2019, the
theme was _**The Science of Virtue: Positive Psychology, Counseling, and**_
_**the Church**_ . Featured speaker **Dr. Mark McMinn,** professor of psychology
and faith integration with George Fox University’s Graduate Department of
Clinical Psychology, showed how virtues that Christians have considered essential
for centuries are now being explored in social science laboratories all over
the world—though the faith element inherent in those virtues often is not. Dr.
McMinn urged that dialogue between psychology, theology, and counseling is
essential to building bridges that benefit both the church and science.
Audio is online at _www.covenantseminary.edu/resources._

**The Professional Counselor Training Series: Continuing Education for**
**Practicing Counselors**
The inaugural Professional Counselor Training Series to provide
professional counselors licensed in the state of Missouri with quality
continuing education units in which Covenant counseling faculty **help**

<a id="ga47-p289"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=291 printed_page=289 -->

**Christian counselors think Christianly about important topics** facing
counselors, their clients, and the church. Each lecture session met for 1.5
hours on a Friday afternoon, with 30 minutes for networking beforehand.
Topics for the inaugural series included:

- “Trauma + Sexuality”

- “Trauma + Lament”

- “Trauma + Attachment”

**Faculty Publications and Kingdom Service**
The Lord has blessed Covenant Seminary with a distinctive and
distinguished faculty whose academic excellence and pastoral focus make
them uniquely qualified to prepare the next generation of leaders for Christ’s
church and Kingdom. Our faculty’s influence reaches far beyond the
classroom: professors mentor and disciple students, participate in the life of
local congregations, share their teaching and preaching abilities with the
larger church in the U.S. and around the world, and engage with our culture
and the pressing issues of our day from a biblically sound and gospelcentered perspective. Here is a sampling of their recent publications and
activities.

- **Prof. Jerram Barrs,** Professor of Christian Studies and Contemporary
Culture and Senior Scholar-in-Residence for the Francis Schaeffer

Institute
`o` Published Spanish translation of his earlier _The Heart of_

_Evangelism_ .
`o` Preached and taught at the St. Louis Chinese Gospel Church

monthly for the entire year, and at the International Presbyterian
Church in Liss, UK, last June.

- **Prof. Suzanne Bates,** Associate Dean of Students and Assistant
Professor of Counseling
`o` With Prof. Jerram Barrs, presented in chapel on Lamenting

Sexual Abuse and participated in student discussions on this topic.
`o` Served on several forums addressing issues of race and violence,

and spoke on reconciliation and justice.

- **Dr. Hans Bayer,** Professor of New Testament
`o` Wrote a commentary on the Gospel of Mark for ESV Bible

Expository Commentary (Crossway, forthcoming).
`o` Taught on New Testament theology at Ukraine Biblical Seminary

in Kiev, Ukraine, and on “Missiologische Schwerpunkte” at
AWM Korntal/Stuttgart, Germany.

<a id="ga47-p290"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=292 printed_page=290 -->

- **Dr. David W. Chapman,** Professor of New Testament and Archaeology
`o` Served as New Testament editor for the _ESV Archaeology Study_

_Bible_ (Crossway, 2018) and wrote notes for the Gospel of John
and the General Epistles.
`o` Wrote _1 and 2 Thessalonians_ for the ESV Bible Expository

Commentary series (Crossway, 2018).
`o` Served as board member for Near East Archaeological Society

and as board member and vice president of Presbyterian Mission
International.

- **Dr. Tasha Chapman,** Professor of Educational Ministries
`o` Published (with Bob Burns and Donald Guthrie) _The Politics of_

_Ministry: Navigating Power Dynamics and Negotiating Interests_
(IVP, 2019).
`o` Served as a prayer leader for the Committee on Discipleship

Ministries’ National Women’s Leadership Training Conference
in Atlanta.
`o` Taught two workshops on “Stewardship or Selfishness?” at PCA

Women’s Ministry Regional Conference in St. Louis.

- **Dr. C. John “Jack” Collins,** Professor of Old Testament
`o` Published _Reading Genesis Well: Navigating History, Poetry,_

`o` Working on commentaries on Numbers and Psalms 89–120.

`o` Published _Work: Its Purpose, Dignity, and Transformation_

`o` Completed work on _Basics of Ethics_ (P&R, forthcoming in

`o` Published a chapter titled “I’m Under the Fire of Criticism” in

`o` Published trending blog posts for The Gospel Coalition and the

Christian jargon.

for Seminary website.
`o` Served as board member for The Gospel Coalition and worked

<a id="ga47-p291"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=293 printed_page=291 -->

`o` Served as Theologian in Residence at Kirk of the Hills

Presbyterian Church in St. Louis and helped launch a Center for

`o` Served on advisory board for a proposed Christian college in

`o` Had multiple speaking engagements locally and nationally

- **Dr. Zack Eswine,** Director of Homiletics and Scholar-in-Residence

`o` Created the short video series _The Listening Preacher_, which

- **Dr. Dan Zink,** Director of Counseling and Professor of Counseling
