**Faculty / Staff Updates**
The Lord’s greatest gift to Covenant Seminary is the people who serve here
as faculty, staff, or trustees. We have been particularly blessed in this regard,
as the following updates on our personnel and their Kingdom service
indicate.

- **Prof. Suzanne Bates,** who has taught as adjunct professor of applied
Theology (counseling) since 2010, was **recently promoted to**
**assistant professor of counseling** . Prof. Bates holds an MAC from
Covenant Seminary and is currently pursuing a PhD in counseling
education at the University of Missouri–St. Louis. She is a licensed
professional counselor who serves as a staff counselor and ministry
director at New City Fellowship (PCA) in St. Louis, and has been a
case worker in the St. Louis County Department of Justice Services
and a counselor for the Community Action Agency of St. Louis,
where she worked with the prison population. She also serves as the
Seminary’s associate dean of students for women and is a regular
speaker at conferences and women’s retreats.

- **Dr. Tasha Chapman,** formerly director of educational studies and
adjunct associate professor of educational ministries, was **promoted**
**to** **professor of educational ministries** . She holds a PhD in
educational studies from Trinity Evangelical Divinity School and

<a id="ga47-p294"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=296 printed_page=294 -->

has taught at Covenant in various capacities since 2004. Her current
consulting and research focuses on leadership and self-stewardship
in ministry, cross-cultural learning, and instructional and curriculum
design. She is the co-author (with Bob Burns and Donald Guthrie)
of _Resilient Ministry: What Pastors Told Us About Surviving and_
_Thriving_ (IVP, 2013) and its follow-up _The Politics of Ministry:_
_Navigating Power Dynamics and Negotiating Interests_ (IVP, 2018).
Prior to Covenant, Dr. Chapman worked in campus ministry and as
director of women’s ministry in a local church.

- **Dr. Zack Eswine,** director of homiletics and adjunct professor of
homiletics, and his wife, **Jessica Eswine,** a trained lawyer and
Christian apologist, **joined the staff of the** **Francis Schaeffer**
**Institute (FSI)** . Dr. Eswine will now also serve as **scholar-in-**
**residence for FSI** . Mrs. Eswine will serve as **visiting instructor of**
**integrated apologetics** . Dr. Eswine will join FSI Director **Prof.**
**Mark Ryan** and FSI founder and Senior Scholar-in-Residence **Prof.**
**Jerram Barrs** in offering new MA and DMin degree tracks in
communication and cultural apologetics. He will continue to teach
for and direct the homiletics program and to serve as pastor at
Riverside Community Church in Webster Groves, Missouri, where
he has been since 2008. Mrs. Eswine will also teach apologetics
courses for FSI.

- **Dr. Michael W. Goheen,** director of theological education for the
Missional Training Center (MTC)–Phoenix, joined the faculty of
Covenant Theological Seminary as **professor of missional theology**
in July 2018. In addition to his ongoing directorial and teaching work
with MTC—which became an extension site of Covenant in 2017—

Dr. Goheen will teach occasional courses on mission-related topics
at the Seminary. A pastor, church planter, scholar, and recognized
authority on mission in today’s world, Dr. Goheen’s work with MTC
provides deeper theological training in a non-traditional, missionoriented educational setting for those currently serving in ministry.
He also serves as scholar-in-residence for Surge Network, a
Phoenix-based church-leadership training network. Dr. Goheen has
authored, co-authored, or edited several books, including _A Light to_
_the Nations_ (IVP, 2014), _Introducing Christian Mission Today_
(Baker, 2011), and _The Church and Its Vocation:_ (Baker, 2018).

- **Prof. Aaron Goldstein** was recently **appointed associate director**
**of online learning and adjunct professor of Old Testament** . Prof.
Goldstein began teaching at Covenant in 2015 as a visiting instructor
in Old Testament. In January 2018, he took on additional

<a id="ga47-p295"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=297 printed_page=295 -->

responsibilities as the Seminary’s interim assistant director of online
learning before being named associate director of online learning
and adjunct professor of Old Testament later that year. Prior to
coming aboard full time at Covenant, Prof. Goldstein served for
eight years as a pastor at Cornerstone Church (EFCA) in St. Louis,
Missouri, and taught as an adjunct professor of religion at
Lindenwood University. He is currently pursuing a PhD (ABD
status) in biblical studies at Concordia Seminary in St. Louis. Prof.
Goldstein is a member of Trinity Church Kirkwood (PCA).

- **Dr. Joel Hathaway,** director of alumni and career services, took on
the additional and complementary role of **director of the Doctor of**
**Ministry program** in 2018. a st hes ono fa pastor, Dr. Hathaway
knows firsthand how the ups and downs of ministry can impact a
minister, his family, and the larger church community. As a graduate
of Covenant’s DMin program, he also knows the value of this degree
for further strengthening and equipping church leaders for long-term
ministry success and sustainability. Joel also has previous
experience with Mission to the World, is certified in the Meyers
Briggs Type Indicator (MBTI), and is the author of _Finding a_
_Pastor: A Handbook for Ministerial Search Committees_ .

- **Stephanie O. Hubach,** noted author, speaker, and respected authority
on disability ministry, joined Covenant Seminary as **research fellow**
**in disability ministries.** Ms. Hubach, whose youngest son has Down
syndrome, has extensive experience in disability ministry in a variety
of contexts. She was the founding director of MNA Special Needs
Ministries, where she served from 2007 to 2016. A long-time visiting
instructor of educational ministries at the Seminary, Hubach
continues to teach as a guest ina variety of courses and conferences
and lead special events to help the campus and area churches engage
in disability ministry. She is the author of _Same Lake, Different Boat:_
_Coming Alongside People Touched by Disability_ (P&R, 2006) and
_All Things Possible: Calling Your Church Leadership to Embrace_
_Disability Ministry_ (Joni and Friends, 2007).

- **Dr. Murray Lee,** a Covenant graduate and current member of the
Seminary’s advisory board, began serving the Seminary as **interim**
**director of the Church Planting Track** in the MDiv program in
2018. Dr. Lee, senior pastor of Cahaba Park Church (PCA) in
Birmingham, Alabama, is a seasoned church planter and
organizational leader who brings years of valuable experience in
these areas to his new role. He will continue to serve at Cahaba Park
while stepping in to help the Seminary prepare students interested in
and gifted for planting churches after they graduate from seminary.

<a id="ga47-p296"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=298 printed_page=296 -->

- **Dr. Dan Zink,** professor of counseling, was named **director of the**
**Seminary’s Master of Arts in Counseling (MAC) program** in
2018. Dr. Zink had been serving as interim director of the program
since the retirement of founding director Dr. Richard Winter (now
professor emeritus of counseling) two years ago. Zink joined the
full-time faculty in 1995 after five years as adjunct professor of
counseling and director of student services. He also spent eleven
years as a family counselor, case-worker, and supervisor of public
children’s services. He was the founding director and a five-year
head of New Hope Counseling Services, a ministry of nearby
Chesterfield Presbyterian Church (PCA). His doctoral research
focused on enduring marriages of adult children of divorce.

**Board / Advisory Board Updates**

- Board members **Rev. John Haralson,** senior pastor, Grace Church
Seattle, Seattle, Washington; **Mr. Paul Stoll,** president, Armin Tool
and Manufacturing Company, Elgin, Illinois; and **Mr. Gif Thornton,**
managing partner, Adams and Reese, Nashville, Tennessee, **each**
**completed a second consecutive term** of service and entered a
mandatory roll-off year for 2018–2019, during which they served on
the Seminary’s Advisory Board.

- New Board members for the 2018–2019 year were **Dr. John Plating,**
director of the Center for Calling and Career at Covenant College; and
**Rev. Doug Serven,** pastor of City Presbyterian Church, Oklahoma
City, Oklahoma. **Rev. Ryan Laughlin,** senior pastor, Covenant
Presbyterian Church, St. Louis, Missouri, returned for a second term.

- Also joining our Advisory Board for 2018–2019 were **Rev.** **Tyler**
**Johnson,** lead pastor, Redemption Church Arizona, Gilbert, Arizona;
**Rev.** **Chris Gonzalez,** lead pastor, _Missio Dei Communities_ (Church),
Tempe, Arizona; and **Rev.** **Jim Mullins,** pastor and director of faith,
work, and rest, Redemption Church Arizona, Gilbert, Arizona.

**Financial Growth and Stability**
In line with our audited financials, the Seminary finished last year with
$9,815,147 in total revenues, which included $2,762,836 from net tuition;
$6,217,697 in total giving; and $841,482 from auxiliary enterprises revenue.
Additionally, the endowment gained $1,321,148 in investment income from
endowment assets, and $1,059,211 was released from the endowment into the
operating revenues. Gifts received were at a six-year high due in large part
to the success of the current capital campaign. Net tuition has stabilized due to
increased enrollments in a number of our newly launched degree programs.

<a id="ga47-p297"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=299 printed_page=297 -->

**Conclusion**
We are grateful to be the denominational seminary for the PCA and value
greatly the partnerships we have in ministry with our sister PCA agencies
and churches. it is a joy and a blessing to serve our denomination with them.
Our sincere desire is to glorify the Lord together as we all seek to bless
Christ’s people, grow Christ’s church, and expand Christ’s Kingdom—all
for Christ’s glory.

Thank you for your support and prayers, and thank you for sharing with us
in the exciting things the Lord is doing in and through the PCA and beyond.

Respectfully submitted,

_**Dr. Mark Dalbey**_
President

**Recommendations**
1. That the General Assembly give thanks to God for the ministry of

Covenant Theological Seminary; for its faithfulness to the Scriptures, the
Reformed faith, and the Great Commission; for its students, graduates,
faculty, staff, and trustees; and for those who support the Seminary
through their prayers and gifts.
2. That the General Assembly encourage the congregations of the

Presbyterian Church in America to support the ministry of Covenant
Theological Seminary by contributing the Partnership Shares approved
by the Assembly, and by recommending Covenant Seminary to
prospective students.
3. That the General Assembly ask the Lord to bless Covenant Seminary’s

President, Dr. Mark Dalbey, and grant him and the Seminary’s leadership
team, faculty, and Board of Trustees great wisdom, biblical faithfulness,
and clear vision as they seek to lead the institution into a new era of
ministerial fruitfulness.
4. That the General Assembly ask God to guide Covenant Seminary’s

ongoing efforts at recruiting new students, evaluating and strengthening
our programs, and seeking to make the Seminary a greater resource for
the church both locally and globally.
5. That the General Assembly ask God’s blessing on the Seminary’s

planning and fundraising efforts, and on its attempts to recruit a new
generation of dedicated pastor-scholars to train new generations of
leaders for Christ’s church and Kingdom.
6. That the General Assembly pray for the further development of

Covenant Seminary’s strategic educational partnerships—in Nashville,
Phoenix, and Cambridge; with NEXT; and with Stephanie Hubach’s
work in disability ministries—that these might be a blessing to those

<a id="ga47-p298"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=300 printed_page=298 -->

preparing for ministry or seeking theological training to better live out
their faith wherever God calls them.
7. That the General Assembly pray for unity among the brethren of the PCA

and ask the Lord to work in all our hearts to foster a deeper desire to
engage with one another and the world in compassionate and gospelcentered ways, and that we might bear strong witness to the truth and
power of God’s redeeming grace.
8. That the General Assembly approve the minutes of the stated and called

meetings of the Seminary’s Board of Trustees and Executive Committee
of the Board of Trustees for the year as follows:

- _Stated Board Meetings:_ April 27, 2018; September 28, 2018;
January 25, 2019

- _Called Board Meetings:_ None

- _Stated Executive Committee Meetings:_ December 7, 2018; March 1, 2019

- _Called Executive Committee Meetings:_ April 26, 2018; August 8,
2018; August 9, 2018; September 27, 2018; January 17, 2019;
January 24, 2019
9. That the financial audit for Covenant Theological Seminary for the fiscal

year ending June 30, 2018, by Capin Crouse LLC, be received.
10. That the proposed 2019–20 budget of Covenant Theological Seminary

be approved.
11. The Covenant Seminary Board of Trustees voted unanimously at its

April 26, 2019, stated meeting to recommend that Overture 24 from
Philadelphia Metro West Presbytery, “Add Constitutional Precedence
Statement to Policy Manuals,” be answered with reference to the
recommendation from the Administrative Committee regarding
Overture 24 **.**
12. The Covenant Seminary Board of Trustees voted unanimously at its

April 26, 2019, stated meeting to recommend that Overture 2 from
Northwest Georgia Presbytery, “Encourage CTS toward Growth and
Release from Assembly Oversight,” be answered in the negative with the
following rationale.
Rationale:

- Covenant Seminary was shocked to hear of this overture as we were
in no way consulted on this overture.

- Covenant Seminary does not and never has desired to be released
from PCA oversight but rather delights in and values deeply the
oversight provided through the General Assembly-elected ruling and
teaching elders.

- Contrary to the implication of the overture, Covenant Seminary has
every desire to continue to be under the exclusive oversight of
ordained elders as voting members of the Board of Trustees.

<a id="ga47-p299"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=301 printed_page=299 -->

- Covenant Seminary believes that authoritative oversight of the
Seminary by ordained elders is particularly important and vital for
the task of training men who will be ordained pastors in the PCA.

- Then Covenant Seminary President Dr. Will Barker stated at the 10 [th]
General Assembly that “theological education should be under the
denomination’s ~~a~~ uspices and responsible to the church. Our history
has been blessed through this orientation to the work of the church.”

- We continue to believe this, desire this, and are blessed by this
official and formal relationship.

- Between 1981 and 1982 all 25 PCA presbyteries voted to receive the
RPCES into the PCA with the understanding that Covenant College
and Covenant Seminary would be received into the PCA and have
their own Committees of Commissioners at General Assembly.

- Elders who serve on the Board of Trustees are recommended by
presbyteries to the GA Nominating Committee, which then
recommends an annual slate of board nominees who are voted on by
the General Assembly.

- The members of the Board of Trustees elect the president, approve
faculty members, adopt the budget, and oversee the Seminary’s
doctrinal and educational work of training, equipping, and sending
graduates into the church.

- Elected members of the Board of Trustees annually reaffirm by
signature their commitment to the system of doctrine taught in the
Westminster Standards.

- The approval of the General Assembly Theological Examining
Committee is necessary for each newly elected president to take up
the office of president.

- The Presbytery-elected Committee of Commissioners reviews the
work of the Seminary each year and brings recommendations to the
General Assembly.

- The above-named oversight and accountability provide mutual
benefit to the PCA and to Covenant Seminary by maintaining our
shared conviction to be faithfully committed to the inerrancy of the
Bible, the historic Reformed faith, and the great commission.

- It is our deep desire and delight to continue to serve as the

denominational seminary of the PCA as we strive to “glorify the
triune God by training his servants to walk in God’s grace, minister
God’s Word, and equip God’s people—all for God’s mission.”

<a id="ga47-p300"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=302 printed_page=300 -->

#### **APPENDIX G** **REPORT OF THE COMMITTEE ON** **MISSION TO NORTH AMERICA** **TO THE FORTY-SEVENTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY** **OFTHE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA**

_[...Standing firm in one spirit, with one mind striving side by side for the faith](https://www.esv.org/Pp2.2%3B1Co1.10)_
_of the gospel..._
Philippians 1:27

**MNA Calling and Vision:** The calling of Mission to North America (MNA)
is to serve Presbyterian Church in America (PCA) members, congregations,
presbyteries, networks, and the assembly as a whole as a catalyst for the
progress of the gospel, down deeper into our lives and out wider into the
communities of the United States and Canada. Catalysts in nature enable
chemical reactions to take place more efficiently and bountifully; MNA
seeks to be a catalyst for the vibrancy and extension of the church throughout
North America. Therefore, MNA exists to celebrate and share the gospel of
Jesus Christ in the United States and Canada by coming alongside the local
and regional church in church planting, church renewal, and missional
partnerships.

**MNA Thanksgivings:** Please join with me in rejoicing in God’s gracious
provision for MNA, as we not only saw major funding pass through our
stewardship for designated gifts to support church planting, disaster
response, chaplain ministries, and other efforts in mission and renewal, but
we also finished 2018 with a surplus in MNA’s Ministry Support Fund. The
MSF has been renamed the MNA General Fund, because it supports and
undergirds all MNA does in the field. Developing sufficient income for the
General Fund, most of which is provided through PCA church giving in
response to the Askings, continues to be one of our greatest challenges. We
praise God for his provision of sufficient support again in 2018. Thanks be
to God!

**MNA Next Steps in Church Planting:** With expectation of Christ’s
kingdom coming in greater fullness among us by the advance of his gospel
in depth and breadth throughout North America, MNA is taking a major new
step in serving churches, networks, and presbyteries as we strive together
side by side for the progress of the gospel through **church planting** .

<a id="ga47-p301"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=303 printed_page=301 -->

Experience demonstrates that church planting expands and flourishes when
a church planting “ecosystem” (When we say ecosystem, think in term sofa
healthy, thriving lake, farm, forest, or ocean bay.) is in place and thriving in
these seven phases:

- Developing and recruiting church planters and church planting teams

**(Develop and Recruit)**

- Assessing church planter candidates and their spouses **(Assess)**

- Training and assisting church planters in their fundraising

**(Fundraise)**

- Seeking God’s guidance in placing the church planter **(Placement)**

- Providing church planter training **(Train)**

- Supporting and encouraging the church planter through coaching

and mentoring **(Coach and Mentor)**

- Celebration and Renewal, reflecting on God’s great work thus far

and looking to him for greater works in the future. **(Celebrate and**
**Renew)**

In the early days of the PCA, MNA focused primarily on welcoming
churches transferring into the PCA from other denominations. In its next
generation, MNA assumed direct responsibility for providing in a centralized
fashion the services required for the seven phases of church planting
ecosystems. In recent years, MNA has moved steadily toward a resourcing
and training role as churches and presbyteries assume a growing ownership
and have achieved ever-greater skills in developing local and regional
ecosystems for church planting. Thus, MNA serves as catalyst for church
planting.

God has blessed the PCA with a strong sense of calling to church planting.
**2018 saw God’s blessing in sending 50 church planters to the field to**
**begin a new mission church, continuing since 2005 an average of one**
**PCA church planter being placed on the field each week across North**
**America.** (See Attachment 1 for the list of the church planters placed in
2018.) while this is a good number fora denomination the size of the PCA,
we trust God for more. MNA is moving toward an even greater focus on
serving churches, presbyteries, and networks in their development of church
planting ecosystems. As a next step, MNA will begin highlighting the seven
phases above by providing a growing number of events and identified
resource people and organizations to provide growing services in each of the
seven phases. In short, if we are to see church planting multiply, it is time for
us to pursue new and best pathways for multiplication, so that local and
regional systems of church planting begin to reproduce radically.

<a id="ga47-p302"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=304 printed_page=302 -->

As we make these changes, MNA church planting services will no longer be
coordinated by just one staff member and his assistant. Church Planting
Coordinator Ted Powers and Church Planting Assistant Ann Powers have
stepped away from their MNA roles after 17 and 21 years, respectively, of
faithful service. This change brings sadness in what it means personally for
the many who have worked alongside Ted and Ann, and at the same time it
comes with great thanksgiving for the many ways in which God has led and
worked through them. In this new approach, our goal is to multiply the
services they performed while serving with MNA. Our prayers are with them
as we all continue in the journey of following Jesus and serving in his
kingdom. As one of the core services that MNA provides, we expect to
continue conducting centralized Church Planter Assessment Centers through
the remainder of 2019. In 2020 and beyond, we intend to multiply our
assessments into regional, and even local, expressions.

**MNA 2018 Highlights:** Beyond the fiscal blessings we enjoyed in 2018, we
have welcomed to the MNA staff these new key leaders of ministry:

- MNA Korean Ministries Coordinator TE Bill Sim. Bill will focus

primarily on working with first generation PCA Korean language
church pastors. He will work alongside the KALI (Korean American
Leadership Initiative) leaders already serving in leadership with a
focus on the second generation. KALI includes RE Alex Jun, TE Mike
Park, and TE Owen Lee.

- Don Baret has joined us to lead MNA Media Service, leading in the

development of media services for MNA as an entire organization,
as well as individual MNA ministries. Don also serves as assistant

director to MNA ESL Director Nancy Booher.

- Chris Bolton has joined our staff as MNA Operations Director. He

comes to us with extensive experience in both for-profit and nonprofit organization management, along with lay leadership in a
number of mission churches.

We also trust God that the new steps we are taking in providing church
planting services will provide greater platforms and opportunities for growth
in **church renewal** resourcing also. We are continuing to develop our
overarching approach to church renewal. We will keep in touch with
churches, networks, and presbyteries as we continue to develop **church**
**renewal resources**, partnerships, and strategies.

<a id="ga47-p303"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=305 printed_page=303 -->

In addition to **church planting** and **church renewal,** we continue to see
great progress in the services provided by MNA **missional partnerships.**
Here are some highlights:

- Engaging Disability comes alongside families touched by disability

and encourages churches to welcome these families and fully
integrate them into the body of Christ, so that none will be left out.
Free online training sessions averaged 25 participants, including
pastors, elders, deacons, volunteers, parents, caregivers, and people
with disabilities.

- Metanoia Prison Ministries facilitates churches in providing

discipleship and mentoring to men in prison and assisting them in
acclimating to civilian life on release. In 2018 they added 50 new
mentors and began a new ministry at Phillips State Prison in
Duluth, GA.

- MNA Disaster Response coordinated help to those affected by the

Paradise, CA, Fire, Hurricane Michael, and Hurricane Florence,
along with many other regional disaster events. In October 2018, The
Charles H. Jones Family Disaster Response Center was dedicated as
the home of the Mission to North America (MNA) Disaster
Response Warehouse and Disaster Readiness Training Center in
Rome, GA.

- MNA Chaplain Ministries added a record-setting number of 25 new

chaplains and chaplain candidates in 2018, increasing the number
who minister among those serving in our military, police forces, and
other institutions. (See Attachment 2.)

- The PCA Unity Fund provided $48,300 in financial assistance to

qualified minority students in 2018 who were pursuing studies
toward gospel ministry. We request churches and individual church
members to prayerfully consider making significant contributions to
this fund. Contact The PCA Unity Fund Coordinator TE Scott
Bridges <sbridges@pcanet.org or 301-974-7935> for further
information on The PCA Unity Fund.

For a fuller list of MNA’s missional partnerships, as well as to gain more
information on our church planting systems and church renewal resources,
please visit our website, www.pcamna.org. Please consider a gift to one of
our ministries, to The PCA Unity Fund, or to the MNA General Fund. To
give online, go to www.pcamna.org and select the “Support MNA” tab. You
may also mail donations to: MNA, PO Box 890233, Charlotte NC 282890233.

<a id="ga47-p304"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=306 printed_page=304 -->

We at MNA ask your prayers for God’s leading and blessing in our ongoing
**church planting** efforts, our **missional partnerships** and our pursuit of
**church renewal**, all flowing from the gospel. May the gates of hell not stand
against the advance of Christ’s church! May his kingdom come in greater
fullness! Amen.

**MNA Stewardship and Finances: 2018 Progress**

**A.** **Ministry Ask/Askings Giving:**

MNA was supported in 2018 by 1,601 churches giving $4,403,084 and
3,729 individual donors giving $2,395,556. The MNA Partnership Fund
budget includes designated support for MNA program staff members.
100% of all designated gifts to MNA go to their intended project; MNA
receives no administrative support from designated income. MNA
requests that churches give the Ministry Ask of $26 per member, if giving
on a _per capita_ basis. If all churches gave $26 per member, all projects
would be funded without individual fundraising by project leaders.

MNA requests that churches give to all PCA Committees and Agencies
at the Ministry Ask level. Because many churches do not contribute at
the Ministry Ask level, MNA senior staff members seek designated
support for their personal support and programs. Churches have
responded generously to these additional requests for support, providing
significantly greater resources for ministry. Contact MNA Associate
Coordinator TE Fred Marsh <fmarsh@pcanet.org or 404-307-8266> or
MNA Church Relations Director RE Stephen Lutz <slutz@pcanet.org or
828-242-1440> for further information on financial support for MNA.

**B.** **Church Planting Projects and Other Funding:**

- All church planters are supported by gifts designated for their

particular projects. No administrative fees are taken from project
support for any project coordinated by MNA. All designated gifts to
MNA are used fully for the purpose designated.

- Church planters who do not have a strong personal PCA network

require a special priority for project support as we trust God for much
greater ministry among the many people groups of North America.
MNA strongly encourages churches to give a high priority to church
planters who do not have a background in the PCA and who thus
lack a strong personal network through which to raise support.

<a id="ga47-p305"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=307 printed_page=305 -->

- Five Million Fund for Church Buildings: providing interest-free

loans of up to $100,000, this fund continues to be a helpful source
for churches as they put together funding packages for their initial
building programs. This is a revolving fund, supported by loan
repayments, a swell as by donations.

C. **Thanksgiving Offering:** MNA is grateful to the Lord for more than

$44,100 given to the 2018 Thanksgiving Offering, and commends to
PCA churches the opportunity to support, through the annual MNA
Thanksgiving Offering, the training of men and women for leadership in
ministry among the ethnic groups of our communities.

TE Paul Hahn

MNA Coordinator

according to the General Assembly guidelines, the MNA Committee
commends TE Paul Hahn for his excellent leadership, with thanks to the
Lord for the good results in MNA Ministry during 2018 and recommends
his re-election as MNA Coordinator for another year. Attachment 3
provides a complete list of MNA staff; see Attachment 4 for the list of

2. That the General Assembly adopt the 2020 MNA Budget and commend

4. That TE James Cotton Pakala and TE Paul Riley Wrigley be appointed

to serve as PCA members of the Presbyterian and Reformed Commission
on Chaplains and Military Personnel (PRCC) for the Class of 2023.
5. That **Overture 24** be answered by reference to the action of the General

Assembly on the PCA Administrative Committee recommendation on

##### Overture 24.

<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=308 printed_page=306 -->

**Attachment 2**

**MNA CHAPLAIN MINISTRIES REPORT ON 2018 ACTIVITIES**

**THANKSGIVING AND PRAISE**
We are thankful for the generosity of God’s people as our 2018 operating
expenses were covered with gifts from churches, presbyteries, individuals,
and PRCC denominations. We are also thankful for the generosity of MNA’s
administration in managing databases, salaries, printing support, and much
more. Their devotion and dedication has made a significant impact in
empowering our Chaplain Ministry.

As of December 31, 2018, the PCA had a total of 209 endorsed chaplains
(145 Military Chaplains and Seminary Candidates, and 64 Civilian
Chaplains). The PCA continues to endorse military and civilian chaplains in
a variety of settings including those of the additional 6 denominations in the
Presbyterian and Reformed Commission on Chaplains (PRCC): Associate
Reformed Presbyterian Church (ARPC), Korean American Presbyterian
Church (KAPC), Korean Presbyterian Church in America (KPCA), Orthodox
Presbyterian Church (OPC), The Reformed Presbyterian Church in North
America (RPCNA), and the United Churches of North America (URCNA).
I serve as the Endorser for these denominations as well as the PCA. The PRCC
endorses and supports a total of 285 chaplains in all member denominations.

**2018 HIGHLIGHTS**
June 2018 Chaplains Training Conference was hosted by Smyrna PCA
Church. We had over 65 Chaplains and many spouses and children attend
our annual training, which was led by Kevin DeYoung and Peter Lillback.
It was an amazing time of study and spiritual renewal for our Chaplains and
their family members.

Civilian Chaplain Population: We are blessed to see our Civilian Chaplain
ministry continue to grow. Whether they be Hospital, Bureau of Prisons,
Retirement, Hospice, or Corporate Chaplains, we are encouraged to see our
gracious Lord growing this ministry and Teaching Elders entering this
special calling.

Chaplain Recruitment: The goal in 2018 was to add 20 Chaplains or
Chaplain Candidates and we were able to add 23 Chaplains to serve our Lord
within the PRCC.

<a id="ga47-p310"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=312 printed_page=310 -->

PRCC Staff: It is a blessing to serve with two other godly Teaching Elders
and one godly Ruling Elder who make up the Chaplain Staff. TE Mack
Griffith, TE Del Farris and RE Gary Hitzfeld are a true blessing and make
this ministry so fulfilling. These men are outstanding servant leaders and
make significant contributions to the pastoral care of our chaplains.

**CHURCH AND PRESBYTERY SUPPORT PROGRAMS**
Congregational Sponsorship: It is our goal to enlist three sponsoring
congregations for every full-time military and civilian chaplain. The primary
purpose of the Sponsorship Program is to enlist prayer support for the
chaplain, his ministry, his family, and for those he serves. The sponsoring
chaplain, in turn, will provide at least three update reports per year with
prayer requests to the congregation or presbytery.

Missions Conferences: More and more congregations are asking us to preach
and speak at numerous mission conferences, and this has been a blessing.
Several of our chaplains have also been invited to speak or preach. We
encourage our ministers and congregations to ask our chaplains to come and
speak. The Chaplaincy is about fulfilling the Great Commission and we
deeply desire to spread the gospel of Christ.

**PLEASE JOIN IN PRAYER FOR THESE CURRENT REQUESTS**
For Ministry Boldness: Pray that our Chaplains will continue to boldly
represent our faith. Our Chaplains have the First Amendment liberty to
boldly proclaim the gospel and we are thankful that they are standing firm.
They are able to cooperate with other groups but without compromise. Our
Chaplains have an excellent reputation among their peers and leaders and are
superb at walking through the pluralistic minefields within the Chaplaincy.
Just as Daniel was able to navigate the waters in the Court of Darius, so our
Chaplains navigate our secular culture as they live for Christ and bring glory
to His name. However, prayer is needed for them to continue to do so.

For Spiritual Strength: Please pray for our Chaplains as many are deploying;
pray, too, for family members left behind. Also, pray for our Civilian
Chaplains who pour themselves out in service to the King and face much
pressure to conform to the spirit of the world.

For Increased Giving: As our ministry grows and we expand our number of
Chaplains, it is critical that we increase our budget to sustain the pastoral care
to our Chaplains. Our budget needs to be above $500,000 in order to

<a id="ga47-p311"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=313 printed_page=311 -->

properly care for this growing ministry. This will fund our necessary travel
and help us recruit more godly ministers to serve as Chaplains.
For more information on ministry opportunities with the MNA Chaplain
Ministries, please contact TE James R. Carter at jcarter@pcanet.org or 954850-2448 or RE gary hitzfeld at chaplainministries@pcanet.org or 678-8251251.

TE. James R. Carter

MNA Chaplain Ministries Coordinator
Executive Director, Presbyterian and Reformed Commission on Chaplains
(PRCC)

www.prcc.co

#### **APPENDIX H** **REPORT OF** **THE COMMITTEE ON MISSION TO THE WORLD** **TO THE FORTY-SEVENTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY** **PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA**

**Vision:** _the gospel of the kingdom advancing throughout the world_ ∼

We want the gospel to spread throughout the world, the Church to
grow, Satan’s kingdom destroyed, and Christ’s reign extended to the
ends of the earth.

**Mission:** _making disciples among all nations_ ∼

We are called to be obedient to the Great Commission by teaching
people to follow Jesus as Lord and Savior, to be baptized, and to
obey all that Jesus commands.
**Values:**

- _Church_ ∼

The establishment, growth, and maturity of the Church in all our
ministry efforts.

- _Grace-based_ ∼

Community life and ministry shaped by God’s grace for us in His
Son, Jesus Christ.

- ∼
_Reformed and Covenantal_

A ministry that is guided, inspired, and shaped by our theology.

- ∼
_Mercy, Justice, and the Love of God_

A love for God that is demonstrated through acts of mercy and justice.

In anticipation of God answering our prayer to raise up at least 1% of the
PCA’s adult members for longer-term missions for kingdom advancement,
this year has been marked by intentional strategic changes:

**Leadership Structure** **—** In order to better facilitate communication and
coordinate change across the organization, we have changed our leadership
structure. Previously, the assistant coordinator and the international directors
were the primary positions reporting to the coordinator. Moving forward, an
Executive Leadership Team (ELT) of three will report to the coordinator.
These positions are the chief administrative officer, senior director of field
operations, and senior director of U.S. operations. Please pray for the newlyappointed leaders and that this new structure would serve our missionaries
well.

<a id="ga47-p317"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=319 printed_page=317 -->

**Software Implementation** **—** We gave significant time and resources to
implement new financial and customer relationship management systems.
From beginning to end this project was managed very capably by Kendra
Jeffreys, senior director for U.S. operations, and her team. These new
systems will support the growth we are anticipating. Please give thanks with
us for the successful implementation of these much-needed systems.

**Regional Hubs** **—** MTW West Coast and MTW Midwest have been
progressing in their service to their respective regions. Of the 100 churches
that have signed up or pledged involvement in the 1% initiative, a
disproportionate number have come from the West and Midwest. This is
clear evidence of the fruit of the hubs’ work in engaging with local
congregations. MTW South Central has launched with the appointment of
Jerry Gibson as director. Jerry is the former senior director of missions at
Park Cities Presbyterian Church, Dallas. We are excited about his transition
into this role. Lastly, a core group has formed in the Northeast/Mid-Atlantic
and we anticipate a new hub in that region to launch this year. Please continue
to pray for our existing hubs and those in development.

In addition to the preparations for growth, we have worked on issues related
to our organizational culture and values:

We have initiated a new Reformed and Diverse Delegates group (RADD),
led by Omar and Kate Ortiz, focusing on Hispanic and Latino-Americans.
Our African-American RADD group has been very active in promoting
missions and mentoring potential missionary candidates. We pray for the
same fruit from this new Hispanic/Latino-American RADD group. Please
pray for these RADD groups, and the development of an Asian-American
RADD group.

We have also given much thought to valuing women in MTW. In keeping
with the recommendation that was passed at the 45 [th] General Assembly of
the PCA stating, “That sessions, presbyteries and the General Assembly
strive to develop, recognize, and utilize the gifts, skills, knowledge, and
_”_ [1]
wisdom of godly women in the local, regional, and national church…,
CMTW drafted this statement:

1 Lines 31-33, page 2459; Report of the Ad Interim Committee on Women Serving in the
Ministry of the Church to the Forty-fifth General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in
America.

<a id="ga47-p318"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=320 printed_page=318 -->

MTW values all women as created in the image of God.
Under Scripture’s authority and in our doctrinal view of

ordination, we affirm that female disciples of Christ are coheirs of the kingdom, co-laborers in the work of the gospel,
gifted by the Spirit as members of the body of Christ and
called to use these gifts for the building up of the Church
and for the extension of the Kingdom of God throughout the
world.

Furthermore, the MTW community deeply needs the gifts
and glory of God uniquely expressed in women. Therefore,
MTW is committed to supporting and equipping our sisters
in Christ as they exercise their gifts and abilities throughout
MTW's global ministry as together we show and tell the
gospel of God's grace.

In keeping with this statement, we affirm that women may
serve in leadership roles within MTW, recognizing that only
ordained elders exercise ecclesiastical authority over church
plants or churches.

In addition, we asked Godly Response to Abuse in a Christian Environment
(GRACE) to conduct an independent assessment of our policies and practices
related to preventing and responding to behavioral misconduct in MTW,
focusing especially on how women are treated. They completed their
assessment and have passed on their recommendations to MTW. We are in
the process of implementing those recommendations, bringing greater
consistency to CMTW’s statement on valuing women and our organizational
culture. Please pray that we would continue to grow in demonstrating value
to the many women serving with MTW.

We give thanks to God for your partnership in the gospel and count it a great
privilege to participate with you in fulfilling the Great Commission. Soli Deo
Gloria!

Lloyd Kim, Coordinator

<a id="ga47-p319"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=321 printed_page=319 -->

**2018 GLOBAL MINISTRY HIGHLIGHTS**

**A** **SIA** **-P** **ACIFIC**
**East Asia —** WC* Covenant Theological Seminary just held its second
annual graduation. MTW missionary and academic dean Tim M* looked out
upon the graduates with both a sense of pride and of concern. Pride because
of what the seminary has accomplished through God’s grace, but concern for
the trials they would face ahead. New government regulations concerning
religious gatherings have been the source of personal trials for some MTW
personnel. Tim recently had his visa revoked, was forced out of his previous
apartment, and has been harassed by the police. All these, however, are rather
mild compared to what these graduates face. The week prior to graduation
Tim spoke with an American brother who comes to the seminary every year
to teach a class. Since this man travels around East Asia and works with

different groups, he shared how his experience at the seminary compared to
other places and what was unique. He replied that he really appreciated the
theological rigor and the moral courage that the leaders in at the seminary
are instilling in the next generation. As Tim looked out upon the faces of the
graduates he got a bit choked up during his prayer for them. “We don’t know
what will happen tomorrow,” he prayed, “but we know our future is in His
hands.” Since then the church in this East Asia country has faced extensive
persecution with church property destroyed, members harassed, and many
imprisoned. Please pray for missionaries and national pastors in East Asia to
be strengthened in their trust in the Lord and in His plans for the future.
*name withheld due to sensitive ministry location

**Cambodia —** Khmer Christian Church (KCC) has been praying for three
years for a Khmer missionary to go to Prey Thom village to minister to the
families there. God heard those prayers and has answered them. Pecht and
his wife, Soknee, have been called by God to serve in Prey Thom and KCC
has confirmed that calling. Currently Pecht is preparing to live in Prey Thom
by attending classes taught by the MTW team as well as partnering with
Family Clinic to start a clinic in Prey Thom. Please pray for these ongoing
preparations, for God to bring much fruit from ministry in Prey Thom village.

**Perth, Australia —** As the local band the Hand Me Downs took the stage at
Clancy’s, MTW missionary and band member Andy Shadburne looked out
into the crowd to see MTW teammates, church mates, musician mates, and
the mates of all those mates gathered to hear the inaugural gig of this unique
band. This is where those who are in Christ are interacting with those who

<a id="ga47-p320"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=322 printed_page=320 -->

do not yet know they are in need of a savior. This is where the sweet aroma
of Christ and life soars above the smell of pub food and beer. This is where
people who might not ever connect begin to connect because of a common
friend, where conversations began to form and where gospel seeds are
planted and begin to germinate. The team looks forward to seeing God
continue to grow those relationships and is thankful for the opportunity to
connect with so many individuals. Please pray that those who are getting a
whiff of the aroma of life would be compelled to seek after Christ.

**Taiwan —** Although she has been a Christian since childhood, J struggled
for years with forgiveness issues. Studying the writing of Jonathan Edwards
in a Spiritual Classics course at Christ’s College, j discovered anew
perspective. She wrote in a journal, “To forgive shows how much you can
trust God, and it is a gift to bless us. That’s what I should learn and improve
over time until I can trust him through forgiveness.” Please pray for the
ministry of Christ’s College as they seek to provide Christian education and
discipleship to Taiwanese and Chinese college students.

**E** **UROPE**
**Ongoing Refugee Ministry —** Over the past three years a significant
ministry focus for Europe, as in other parts of the world, has been ministering
to the needs of refugees. In the summer of 2016, millions of people were
displaced from a variety of countries in the Middle East due to political
turmoil and persecution. Most headed to Europe. While some European
countries closed their borders, others welcomed refugees with open arms.
The needs were great, the stories told by those who had been displaced were
often heart-wrenching, and gospel opportunities abounded. God brought
many people into His kingdom. What was initially deemed a refugee “crisis”
has ushered in an important movement of the Holy Spirit.

Fast forward two years to 2018: Although the stream of refugees into Europe
slowed to a trickle, the needs of those who have come and remain are still
abundant. In that initial year, the focus was on meeting daily needs and
helping displaced people simply survive their harrowing ordeal. Many
governments were not set up to deal with a massive influx of people. Now
many cases are being reviewed to determine whether or not these people truly
meet the definition of “refugee,” and thus gain legal asylum status. Many are
being returned to their country of origin, where they may face persecution
and even death due to having entered into a relationship with Jesus Christ
during their time of sojourn on European soil. Others are given the green
light to remain and enter the next phase of social integration.

<a id="ga47-p321"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=323 printed_page=321 -->

This all changes the face of ministry. Field personnel now attend court
hearings, testifying to the new-found faith of their refugee friends, pleading
for the government to recognize the dangers that lie ahead should our
brothers and sisters be returned to the land of their birth. The refugees are
weary of living in limbo. Workers are weary from dealing with trauma.
Therefore, ministry has shifted from meeting the basic needs of home,
clothing, and food to providing care on a deeper level. Our workers in
Athens, Greece, have opened integration houses, where refugees learn
needed skills to become a part of Greek society. Two trauma care trainings
were held in Europe during 2018, teaching workers how to identify not only
trauma suffered by others, but also how to deal with the trauma they
themselves have experienced in the process. Opportunities still abound.

**Moving Leadership to “The Next Level” —** With MTW’s goal of
mobilizing an additional 1% of the PCA’s membership to world missions, at
the field level we are grappling with what is needed to receive and adequately
care for an expanding workforce. Trusting that God will answer our prayers
for more laborers to the field, the regional leadership team has taken the
following steps in 2018:

- Created the position of Europe mobilization coordinator to help the fields
think creatively and dream of how God’s kingdom in Europe could grow
with an expanded work force. What are the opportunities? Needs?
Interests? The sky’s the limit!

- Created the position of women’s ministry coordinator to meet the needs
of the women who are serving in Europe. Over half of our field personnel
are women, so this is a strategic position to ensure our personnel are
reaching their potential in all areas of life.

- Created the position of Europe prayer coordinator to deepen the prayer
life of our field personnel. Without prayerful dependence on God in all
our endeavors, the work of our hands is doomed to fail.

- Raising up leaders: In Europe we are facing a number of significant
retirements in the next two to three years. All regional directors and team
leaders have been asked to actively prepare the people they oversee to
eventually move into leadership positions, either in their current area or
to lead new works elsewhere. During 2018 one couple was assessed for
a regional leadership position, and two couples were assessed as
potential team leaders. The list is growing for those who will be assessed
in 2019. This is an exciting step in preparing for the future.

- Developing leaders: Not only do we want to appoint leaders, we also
want to ensure their success as they transition into new responsibilities.

<a id="ga47-p322"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=324 printed_page=322 -->

A new leadership development program is being designed to assist new
team leaders during the first year in their position. This program will also
be open to all field personnel, as each person is a leader in his or her own
ministry area.

- Europe Leadership Forums: Fifty people attended the MTW-Europe
Leadership Forum (ELF), where the training focus was on gospel
coaching. Tools were made available not only to those who have a
leadership title, but also to site leaders and team members who lead
ministries at the local level. The desire is to continue strengthening this
training component in the future. The 2019 ELF will focus on providing
church-planting training that is specific to the European context.

Please be in prayer that God would raise up laborers to join what He is doing
in Europe, and that He would raise up leaders and national partners who will
move this work forward in the decades to come.

**S** **UB** **-S** **AHARAN** **A** **FRICA**
**International Director —** The close of 2018 marked the first year of
transition for our new international director, Victor Nakah. Victor has begun
a major emphasis of planting covenantal, Reformed, and Presbyterian
churches and presbyteries throughout Sub-Saharan Africa (SSA). This
emphasis includes related ministry priorities that will bring intentionality to
how we go about doing Reformed campus ministry, developing leaders, and
promoting integral mission in communities we serve. He has networked with
fellow African Reformed church leaders where we hope to expand churchplanting initiatives in the Congo, Malawi, and Sierra Leone. Victor has begun
an initiative that we hope will make it possible to have a missionary who is
focused on reaching Muslims join each of the Sub-Saharan Africa teams.

**Southern Africa Reformed Mission (SARM) —** February 8–9 we launched
the SARM as a platform to intentionally bring resources to support MTW
Southern Africa ministry priorities by creating a board comprised of
missionaries and PCA church leaders committed to funding these priorities.
The SARM Board will be a resource-finding board with the potential that the
resourcing could also happen in both directions as functional gospel
partnerships are established.

**Madagascar —** A short-term couple, Caleb and Whitney Wiley, joined our
long-term missionary family, Bryan and Rebe McReynolds. Caleb has been
developing the music ministry of their recording studio, helping Malagasy
nationals put Scripture to song, then be recorded and played over regional

<a id="ga47-p323"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=325 printed_page=323 -->

and national radio. Whitney has been very capably home schooling one of
the McReynolds’ daughters, while also assisting in the women's ministry.
Bryan and Rebe have started two fledgling church plants, one in the small
village of Nosy Be where the missionary couples live, and one in a jungle
setting. They know of no Reformed, covenantal, and Presbyterian church
throughout the country of Madagascar. Please pray with us for this urgent
need.

**Uganda —** MTW’s Ben and Kim Church have seen fruit of their churchplanting efforts over the past several years. On December 30, the first church
service was held at the newly-built Christ our Redeemer Presbyterian
Church. Ben is working alongside several national partners in joint
leadership at the church. At their first service, they had approximately 150
adults with 120 children in attendance. The ground-breaking service was a
time of great rejoicing at our Lord's faithfulness in providing national
partners, potential congregants, and financial provision for the church
facilities’ construction.

**Ethiopia —** Ethiopia ACT, the ministry started by MTW missionaries to
plant churches and serve the poorest residents of Addis Ababa, continued to
grow and reach more families in extreme poverty. In 2018 the ministry
served more than 600 families with health care, children’s education,
spiritual counseling, and assistance in becoming self-sustaining. Of the 1,233
families that have been part of the project since it began, 1,129 are now able
to support themselves. The team’s church plant in the Suki community has
grown steadily this year with 10 people making professions of faith and five
baptisms. In 2019 the project is expanding into a new community, Akaki
Kality, and will add 150 families to the ministry and launch a second church
plant.

**South Africa —** In 2018 the McGintys completed their second year with
REACH (Reformed Evangelical Anglican Church of South Africa) student
ministry at the University of Cape Town (UCT). Since this ministry was only
formed recently, the McGintys’ focus has been on laying the foundation for
future ministry—establishing relationships, investing in student leadership,
and, of course, sharing the gospel. REACH has also launched a church plant
connected to the student ministry at UCT. This church grew numerically in
2018, capitalizing on synergy from the student ministry. Pamela McGinty
was also involved in another new and necessary ministry that started in 2018:
a biblical counseling ministry with national scope. Biblical Counseling South

<a id="ga47-p324"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=326 printed_page=324 -->

Africa was started in partnership with REACH S.A. and U.S.-based CCEF
(Christian Counseling & Educational Foundation). The purpose is to equip
lay and pastoral counselors for the churches in South Africa..

**A** **MERICAS**

—
**Seminario Biblico Reformado – Arequipa** We are grateful to see
growth at the Seminario Biblico Reformado in Arequipa, Peru. The seminary
just concluded its first academic year and registered its hundredth student,
praise the Lord! The first group of students has completed two thirds of their
program, and we have recently launched the newest extension of the
seminary in Spain. Unfortunately, one of our church plants, La Roca, had to
close its doors in the fall, but God provided amidst the change. Since the
closing of the church, we have been able to serve multiple churches
represented in the seminary. Our team participated in marriage conferences,
church anniversary services, funerals, and regular preaching. We are thankful
that Seminario Biblico Reformado has already touched the lives of over 100
students in Peru, Venezuela, and Spain. We look forward to seeing how the
Lord continues to work through the seminary.

**RUF – Bogota** — MTW missionary Peter Dishman continues to see progress
and traction in the RUF ministry at the University of Colombia in Bogota. In
August, Peter and his team handed out 6,000 academic calendars. They had
1,086 students sign up for more information on English clubs and 271 for
more information on Bible studies. They continue to have weekly large group
meetings and small group studies covering a range of topics from the _Jesus_
_Storybook Bible_ to Theology Club. Three of their students in leadership grew
significantly this past year by leading small groups and caring for the group
members. We are grateful for the work the Lord continues to do in this
ministry, and we pray this is only the beginning of His work.

**Global Distance Education** — Our Global Education team continued to

flourish throughout 2018. MTW missionary Carla Stevens developed
courses in Christian Education and traveled throughout the world to teach
seminars. Missionaries Quentin and Karen Nantz worked alongside Carla,
helping develop the courses and assisting in the establishment of Christian
schools. The Nantzes are also focusing on developing an online course,
which would enable MTW to reach even more students. We are grateful that
they are receiving positive feedback from the courses. Please pray that their
roles in education would impact many for the kingdom

<a id="ga47-p325"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=327 printed_page=325 -->

**Recommendations:**
1. That the General Assembly urge churches to set aside the month of

November 2019 as a month of prayer for global missions, asking God to
send many more laborers into His harvest field. (MTW will offer a 30
Days of Prayer Calendar, which your church can download from
mtw.org in the fall, as well as other prayer resources);
2. That the General Assembly urge churches to set aside a portion of their

giving for the suffering peoples of the world; to that end, be it
recommended that a special offering for relief and mercy (MTW
Compassion offering) be taken during 2019 and distributed by MTW;
3. That the General Assembly urge churches to set aside Sunday, November 3,

2019, as a day of prayer for the persecuted church worldwide;
4. Having performed an annual review of our coordinator, we give thanks

to our gracious Lord for His gift to the Church in the person of Dr. Lloyd
Kim, who continues to serve and lead humbly and effectively in the
glorious cause of missions around the world. CMTW enthusiastically
recommends that Dr. Kim be re-elected coordinator.
5. That the proposed budget of MTW, as presented through the Administrative

Committee, be approved;
6. That the minutes of the meeting of CMTW of March 14–15, 2018, be

accepted; and
7. That the minutes of the meeting of CMTW of September 26–27, 2018,

be accepted;
8. Regarding MTW’s 2017 Financial Audit: That the Committee of

Commissioners reviewed the financial audit for calendar year ending
December 31, 2017. They also noted per CMTW’s minutes that CMTW
had accepted the audit.
9. That **Overture 24** from Philadelphia Metro West Presbytery “Add

Constitutional Precedence Statement to Policy Manuals” **be answered**
**with reference** **to the recommendation from the Administrative**
**Committee regarding Overture 24** .
10. That **Overture 41** from Heritage Presbytery, “Direct CMTW to Amend

its Guidelines to Implement CMTW’s ‘Statement on Valuing Women in
MTW,’ so that only Ordained Elders Will Be Allowed to Serve in the
Roles of Team Leader, Regional Director, and International Director
within MTW,” (p. 693) **be answered in the negative.** With the following
grounds recorded as part of the motion.

Grounds:
In formulating and finalizing its “Statement on Valuing Women,” CMTW
carefully considered the report on the ad interim committee to the 45 [th]

<a id="ga47-p331"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=333 printed_page=331 -->

General Assembly and believes that its statement on valuing women in MTW
is wholly consistent with the ad interim report in all material respects.

Mission to World, CMTW, and the General Assembly have approved
unordained men to the roles of team leader, country director, and regional
director since 1983 and at least 3 women since around 2006. We have
identified at least 35 unordained leaders appointed to these positions since
2003. CMTW and GA have never required these positions be held
exclusively by ordained elders.

MTW teams vary extensively across the globe. While the ultimate aim of all
our teams is to plant and revitalize churches, not all of our teams have
ordained elders engaged directly in planting churches. Some teams are
focused on facilitative church planting where our missionaries work
alongside ordained national partners who are leading churches and church
plants. Others are focused on university ministries (Christ’s College team in
Taiwan), medical ministries, mercy ministries (Ethiopia), education
ministries, etc. to make disciples who fill local churches and church plants.
In some cases there are a variety of different ministries all under the umbrella
of one team. The overture would unnecessarily restrict unordained men and
women from serving in roles that do not require ordination.

The overture if passed would immediately affect 19 unordained missionaries
who are serving in these roles across the globe as well as their teams. These
leaders would be removed from their positions, receive less remuneration,
and would have to find new roles on the team. It would also require taking
19 ordained missionaries out of their current ministry roles to take their
places.

CMTW and the coordinator are committed to working with those whose
consciences are affected by women or unordained men serving in leadership
in non-ecclesial roles over (ordained) men. We want to move forward
together in a manner that does not hinder the work of the Kingdom.

The principle that unordained leaders defer matters of the church to ordained
leaders in line authority has been MTW's practice for many years. Women
and unordained men in leadership over TE church planters routinely defer
matters related to the church to other ordained supervisors. While this
practice necessarily involves line-drawing, in practice this line-drawing has

<a id="ga47-p332"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=334 printed_page=332 -->

not proved to be an unsurmountable problem, and we pledge to continue to
ensure that church matters be referred to the proper ordained authorities.

The authority given to team leaders for spiritual care of their members does
not necessitate ordination. Spiritual care can be given by unordained
believers. Team leaders can also delegate to others to provide spiritual care
as is appropriate.

Finally, while CMTW has non-voting women advisors, CMTW is not
proposing the addition of women to serve on CMTW through this statement.

Respectfully submitted,
TE Mark Bates, Chairman
Committee on Mission to the World

<a id="ga47-p333"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=335 printed_page=333 -->

#### **APPENDIX I** **REPORT OF THE** **PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA** **FOUNDATION, INC.** **TO THE FORTY-SEVENTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY** **OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA**

The PCA Foundation is pleased to report that, by God’s grace, the PCA
Foundation’s ministry was once again blessed during 2018. We are pleased
to see how the Lord continues to help the PCA Foundation facilitate
generosity to advance God’s Kingdom.

Total gifts to the PCA Foundation during 2018 were $11.8 million.

We are pleased to report that the PCA Foundation distributed, or granted to
ministry, $14.4 million during 2018. Distributions to PCA churches were
$4.7 million, distributions to PCA Committees and Agencies were $2.1
million, and distributions to other Christian ministries were $7.6 million.

We continue to look for opportunities to work with PCA churches and their
members, and are desirous of helping individuals and their families fulfill
their stewardship responsibilities and carry out their charitable desires.

The 2018 distributions and grants to ministry by the PCA Foundation were
as follows:

Mission to the World $ 458,000
Mission to North America 290,000
Committee on Discipleship Ministries 68,000
Administrative Committee 20,000
RBI-Ministerial Relief 195,000
Reformed University Ministries 279,000
Covenant College 307,000
Covenant Theological Seminary 172,000
PCA Foundation 143,000
Ridge Haven 123,000
_Total Committees & Agencies_ 2,055,000

PCA Churches 4,708,000
Other Christian Ministries 7,674,000
_Total_ $14,437,000

<a id="ga47-p338"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=340 printed_page=338 -->

The PCA Foundation’s total assets were $86.4 million as of December 31,
2018. This compares to $93.1 million as of December 31, 2017. The primary
reasons that total assets declined $6.7 million during 2018 were grant
distributions exceeding gifts to the PCA Foundation to fund accounts by $2.6
million and asset valuation decreases of $3.9 million due to the 2018 yearend financial market declines. Much of what the Foundation does results in

gifts coming into the Foundation and going right back out as distributions
and grants to ministries within a relatively short period of time – often within
the same or the following year. Therefore, the PCA Foundation may realize
significant amounts as both gifts and distributions in a given year, and total
assets may stay about the same, or experience substantial increases or
decreases from year to year.

The PCA Foundation has and continues to make significant progress in
serving members and friends of the PCA. Over the last 20 years, total assets
have grown from $20.0 million to $86.4 million. This total asset balance
provides a sound base for future financial support of PCA Churches, PCA
Committees and Agencies, and other Christian ministries.

Coincidental with the growth of its assets over the last 20 years, the PCA
Foundation received $207.9 million in gifts, and made distributions to
ministries totaling $151.9 million.

During 2018 the PCA Foundation continued its increased intentional
marketing to individuals and families, churches, presbyteries, and ministries.
The PCA Foundation rebranded its look with a new logo, website, and
marketing materials in 2016, during which time it also began using various
social networking platforms to introduce its services to individuals and
churches.

The donor portal, which went live in 2016, continues in its increasing
popularity with donors. The donor portal allows PCA Foundation donors to
access their account information online, from which they can also
recommend grant distributions from their accounts.

The PCA Foundation became fully self-supported in 2000. The Foundation
does not participate in the PCA’s Partnership Shares or Ministry Ask
Programs, nor does it rely on the financial support of churches to help
underwrite its operating budget. Rather, its operations are funded primarily
by fees and earnings on accounts, and by some charitable contributions from
a small number of individuals and families, including current and former

<a id="ga47-p339"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=341 printed_page=339 -->

PCA Foundation Board Members. Prior to becoming self-supported, a
significant amount of its operating expenses were underwritten by some of
the PCA Committees and Agencies.

Because the main focus of the PCA Foundation is not on raising funds for its
own operations, or for any other particular ministry, it has a unique
opportunity and niche within the PCA. Our ministry is providing charitable
financial services and vehicles to help Christians carry out their stewardship
responsibilities and charitable desires. We facilitate generosity to advance
God’s kingdom. Our most popular service is the Advise & Consult Fund (a
donor-advised fund). We also offer endowments, charitable trusts, bequest
processing and estate design to individuals and families, as well as
Designated Funds for Churches and Presbyteries.

Since the PCA Foundation is self-supported it is “donor driven,” which
means that we work on the donor’s agenda, not our own, or that of any other
ministry. Therefore, the timing and amounts of distributions and to which
ministry are determined by the donors themselves, not the PCA Foundation.
We provide charitable services to individuals without pressuring them to give
to the PCA Foundation for its own operations, or to any other particular
ministry.

The PCA Foundation will continue to strive to effectively meet the needs of
its present and future donors, as well as those of the PCA: its churches,
presbyteries, Committees, and Agencies. By God’s grace, the PCA
Foundation will be able to do so.

In July 2018, I informed the PCA Foundation Board of my intention to retire
on June 30, 2019, after more than 21 years as president. I am thankful for
the opportunity to have been able to serve the Lord, the PCA, and our donors
since 1998.

On March 1, 2019, the PCA Foundation Board elected Timothy W.
townsend to succeed me as president, effective July 1, 2019. Please pray
that he will be successful as the next president, and will find joy in serving
in his new calling.
Also, please pray for the Board and Staff of the Foundation as they seek to
continue leading the PCA Foundation successfully into the future. The PCA
Foundation is financially sound, and is postured well for continued growth.

<a id="ga47-p340"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=342 printed_page=340 -->

**Recommendations:**

1. That the financial audit for the PCA Foundation, Inc. for the calendar

year ended December 31, 2018, by Capin Crouse, LLP be adopted.
2. That the General Assembly approve the proposed 2020 Budget of the

PCA Foundation, Inc. with the understanding that it is a spending plan
and will be modified as necessary by the PCA Foundation’s Board of
Directors to accommodate changing circumstances during the year.
3. That the Minutes of Board meetings of August 3, 2018, and February28
March 1, 2019, be approved.
4. That Overture 24 be answered by reference to the Administrative

Committee Permanent Committee’s Recommendation on Overture 24.

Respectfully Submitted,
Randel N. Stair

President
