#### **APPENDIX J** **REPORT OF THE BOARD OF DIRECTORS OF** **PCA RETIREMENT & BENEFITS** **TO THE FORTY-SEVENTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY** **OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA**

We are pleased to present the 2018 Annual Report on behalf of the Board of
Directors and Staff of PCA Retirement & Benefits.

As I begin writing this, my _twelfth_ President’s Annual Report, i do so with
mixed feelings. Some of you may have heard that PCA Retirement &
Benefits (RBI) recently announced the beginning of a transition process to
find the next President. After consultations with RBI’s Board of Directors,
we determined the search process should begin immediately. I have given
your Board my commitment to remain in this position until it is clear that
God’s leader for this organization has been named and is prepared to assume
the position of President.

You should know my love for ministry partners who serve in the PCA
ministries and my vision for this work have not diminished. It’s not unusual
for those who consider retirement to do so because the former “fire in the
belly” started smoldering. I can assure you this is _not_ how I feel about the
ministry of RBI! why then would i want to leave a ministry i loves o much?

There are several reasons why I believe entering this period of change is good
and necessary. First, i haven o intention of disengaging from a ministry of
love and care for those who serve the church as our pastors and employees.
I’m confident God will surely guide my path into work that reflects the
desires of my heart! Second, entering this period of leadership transition is
simply good stewardship. Often, senior leaders remain in their positions too
long. I believe an example of good stewardship is to properly hand the job
to the next incumbent at the right time with excellence. So, “just as a good
man leaves an inheritance to his children’s children” (Proverbs 13:22), a
good leader should steward his transition with supreme care. Finally, I
believe denominational leaders have an awesome responsibility to prepare
the next generation for important positions of leadership in the church, such
as RBI’s presidency. Every participant in any of the PCA’s benefit plans
deserves our very best and we will do all that is possible to deliver that
standard.

<a id="ga47-p343"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=345 printed_page=343 -->

There is one more area of change I’d like to mention in this report, which is
the retirement of TE Bob Clarke. Bob has served RBI for 13 years as a
member of the Board of Directors and for the past 10 years as the Director
of Ministerial Relief. During Bob’s tenure with RBI he has faithfully served
the widows of our pastors, overseen the development of the Retirement
Readiness survey, and grown Ministerial Relief by adding needed new
ministries such as ServantCare and Cherish. Bob has been a faithful partner
in ministry and he will be greatly missed. We expect to announce his
replacement in early April of 2019. Please pray that God would continue to
bless the work of our hands as we navigate this important transitional period.

Given all of the changes mentioned above, let me conclude this section of
the annual report by stating what will _not_ change. When RBI’s Board of
Directors formed the President Search Committee, it was done with the
expectation that this committee’s primary goal would be to find a candidate
who would preserve and grow RBI’s stated Mission, Vision and Core
Values. Clearly, finding a candidate with the requisite technical skills is very
important, but preserving the relational and ministry-minded culture of this
organization is vital. I am thankful and happy to say the Board of Directors
and members of RBI’s staff all fully support this objective. Praise the Lord!!

**Financial Market Update**
If you thought making money in the stock market looked easy after 2017,
this past year (2018) was a reminder that risk over the short run remains an
enduring challenge. Stocks (as measured by the S&P 500) experienced a
near ceaseless experience of continuing new highs in 2017. The new year
was quickly interrupted by news of tariff wars and stocks didn’t really find
their stride until the beginning of the second quarter. Just when it seemed
like we were experiencing the second leg of our prior years’ experience
(insert sounds of champagne corks popping), pain and misery became our
new friend as the market unmercifully began a swan dive at the end of the
third quarter. By the end of the year most markets posted negative returns
which was very unsettling.

As investors who are _very_ interested in achieving reasonable returns, how do
we understand this post Great Recession recovery? For what it’s worth, here
are my thoughts at this time…

- First, the U.S economy has finally achieved the technical status of
“full employment”. This has long been the Fed’s objective and
there are many implications for this status which include worries
about wage inflation and a rising quit rate (resigning employees
seeking higher pay).

<a id="ga47-p344"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=346 printed_page=344 -->

- The Fed has been regularly increasing short term interest rates. This
monetary policy together with the reversal of the QE (Quantitative
Easing) program is having a tightening impact on the economy.
Recently the Fed has declared they are maintaining a more neutral
policy with respect to higher interest rates and I believe lower
interest rates are the new normal.

- Growth rates in the U.S. are likely to slow modestly. Contributing
factors are the economic slowdown in China, stagnant growth in
Europe, the Brexit disaster in the U.K., the ebbing favorable impact
of the U.S. corporate tax cut, and the divided U.S. Congress which
will neutralize fiscal policy impacts.

All this is to say the biggest growth we’ve recently experienced is behind us.
This recovery is now 10 years old, having produced an average annual return
of 13.12% for the S&P 500. We should experience more moderate growth
in the years ahead given the factors presented above, and there seems to be
no material evidence of a potential recession yet on the horizon.

Finally, I’m excited to report that in partnership with Women’s Ministry of
PCA Committee on Discipleship Ministries, RBI will be introducing a new
counseling ministry for pastors’ wives called Cherish. God has
tremendously blessed us with the necessary resources to begin this vital
ministry. It is our greatest hope that God will use this ministry to bring hope
and healing to hurting Teaching Elder families across the PCA.
Thank you for your support of PCA Retirement & Benefits. Through your
participation in the Retirement Plan, Group Insurance products and
Ministerial Relief, we are able to sustain programs that encourage and
support our PCA churches and employees.

**Summary of 2018 Operations**
Total PCA Retirement Plan assets under management decreased by 3.6%
from $576,949,747 to $556,375,106. This decrease can be attributed to
outflows (withdrawals and distributions) and comparative market
performance over the prior year. The PCA Retirement Plan continues to be
the employee benefit plan with the largest amount of participation with over
7500 retirement accounts at year-end 2018.

Participation results for the various PCA insurance benefit plans offered by
RBI remained strong with an over growth of 2% in insurance participation.
For the year, participation and volume increased in the PCA Long Term
Disability Plan and the combined PCA Life Insurance Plans, with little change
in participation in both the PCA Vision Plan and the PCA Dental Plan.

<a id="ga47-p345"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=347 printed_page=345 -->

PCA Group Long Term Disability plans experienced no plan design changes
in 2018. Offered through Unum, the PCA Long Term Disability Plan
provides elements of top-tier group disability benefits, such as retirement
income protection, cost of living adjustments and claims payment to Social
Security normal retirement age.

PCA Group Life Insurance plans experienced no plan design changes in
2018. Offered through MetLife, the PCA Life Insurance Plans, include such
features as Will Preparation, Portability, Estate Services, Accelerated Benefit
Offerings, and limited or no medical underwriting for new employees.

RBI has recommended a Property and Liability insurance carrier for PCA
organizations since 2000. Since January 1, 2017, Brotherhood Mutual has
been recommended to PCA churches, schools and ministries. Brotherhood
Mutual specializes in providing insurance to Christian organizations and
seeks to provide PCA organizations special group-affiliated pricing.
Brotherhood Mutual also provides commercial auto, worker’s compensation,
mission travel insurance, legal assistance and payroll processing.

The 2017–2018 Relief Offering and other donations to Ministerial Relief
totaled $775,254. During 2018, fifteen teaching elders, six wives of teaching
elders, and five widows were called home to Glory.

Throughout the year, there were sixty relief recipients who received relief
funding; and, eighteen families received Survivor Assistance. Monthly,
short-term, or emergency supplemental income assistance was provided to
those retired pastors, disabled pastors, pastors without call, missionaries,
active pastors facing emergencies, lay workers, their widows (by death or
abandonment), and dependent children who qualified according to need
under guidelines established by the Relief Committee of the RBI Board of
Directors.

Please assist us in the stewardship of our God-given resources and our
ministry to “the least of these” by directing those in need to the Ministerial
Relief section of our website for the applications for Ministerial Relief and
Health Insurance Assistance (for pastors without call).

We would appreciate your prayers that God would give us discernment and
wisdom as we consider the needs of His servants in the U.S. and throughout
the world, that He may be glorified in all things.

<a id="ga47-p346"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=348 printed_page=346 -->

More information about the PCA Retirement Plans, PCA Group Insurance
Plans, Ministerial Relief, and other services provided through RBI can be
found on our website at www.pcarbi.org.

**Regulatory Changes**
In October of 2018, the Internal Revenue Service announced retirement plan
contribution limits for 2019. The list below references maximum
contribution amounts for elective deferrals (employee contributions),
defined contributions (employee and employer contributions), and catch-up
contributions (employee contributions for participants who are 50 and older).

402(g) Elective Deferral Maximum: $19,000
415(c) Defined Contribution Maximum: $56,000
414(v) Catch-up (Age 50 or older) Contribution Limit: $6,000

**Staff**
The RBI staff is thankful to the Lord for His faithfulness and everlasting love
to His Church this past year and eagerly awaits the opportunities and
challenges in store for our future. We believe that God will continue to bless
our ministry to others as we remain faithful to Him. We welcome the prayers
and partnership of participants and churches this year and into the future. It
is our privilege to serve those who minister in the Presbyterian Church in
America.

#### **APPENDIX K** **REPORT OF** **REFORMED UNIVERSITY FELLOWSHIP** **TO THE FORTY-SEVENTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY** **OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA**

**INTRODUCTION**
Ministry to colleges represents an important part of the mission of the
Presbyterian Church in America. The college years have increasingly
become a time for questioning authority, scrutinizing absolutes, throwing out
old premises, and reinventing the self. Students must learn to navigate the
milieu of converging thought, and Reformed University Fellowship is
thankful to be part of this unfolding campus narrative to reach students for
Christ and equip them to serve. The passion and vigor of college students
have proved, over the last 200 years, to affect the church globally,
significantly engaging the world with mission and purpose. The story of
redemption playing out is bigger than any story that we can imagine.

Reformed University Fellowship goes to the campus with a fixed theology
(The Westminster Standards) and a flexible methodology that allows us to
contextualize in order to suit various campus personalities and
demographics. We are not limited in how and where we preach the Gospel,
and we know that a large percentage of people come to faith in Jesus between
the age of 18 and 25.

To engage the current academic culture, Reformed University Fellowship
sends ordained PCA ministers to serve on the college campus, preach the
gospel of Christ, build Christ’s Church, and ultimately prepare students to
live all of life under the Lordship of Christ. This is a concrete expression of
our commitment to our covenant children and our obedience to the Great

Commission, to reach students for Christ and equip them to serve.

The Permanent Committee for Reformed University Fellowship wishes to
thank all of our churches, presbyteries, and the General Assembly for their
oversight, financial support, prayers, and encouragement for our campus
ministers, staff, and interns, who have served on 163 campuses across
America.

<a id="ga47-p349"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=351 printed_page=349 -->

**REFORMED UNIVERSITY FELLOWSHIP**
Reformed University Fellowship (RUF) offers the truth of God’s Word to
students who are searching. By working within the context of the Church,
we follow Christ’s leadership as He builds His Kingdom. Students are
instructed in Evangelism and Missions, Growth in Grace, Fellowship and
Service, and a Biblical World-and-Life View. An ordained PCA minister
leads each RUF, actively working to accomplish goals in these four major
areas. RUF strengthens the Church by reaching students who may not know
Christ, as well as equipping those who know Him to serve, and to love Jesus
for a lifetime. (See Attachment 1 for a list of RUF, RUF-I, and Global
campus ministers, campuses served, and Presbytery committees.)

**REFORMED UNIVERSITY FELLOWSHIP INTERNATIONAL**
We began the semester with RUF-I Training in Dallas, TX. RUF-I staff are
with all other RUF staff at July Training, with a separate training in the
winter, which is RUF-I specific. We had approximately 30 RUF-I staff
together for 3 days of fellowship and training. Dr. Denis LaClare, from
Bridges International, spoke on Engaging International Student Cultures and
Engaging and Understanding Honor-Shame Cultures. It was exciting to have
Chad Brewer and his family there with us in order for the RUF-I staff to
spend time getting to know them. Chad will assume his new role as the
Assistant Coordinator for RUF-I/G on June 1. Currently we have 16 RUF-I
ministries and we ask that you pray for Chad’s transition as well as new
opportunities to see this ministry expand. We were able to assess two RUFI candidates, one of whom will be filling the role as RUF-I Campus Minister
at Auburn University, which is has been vacant this year.

**REFORMED UNIVERSITY FELLOWSHIP GLOBAL**
Four of our six RUF-Global Campus Ministers (affiliations with MTW and
SERGE) were able to attend RUF Staff Training in December. Currently, we
have staff in Malaysia, Ukraine, Czech Republic, Senegal, Mexico City, and
Colombia. Chad Brewer and John Pearson visited RUF-G staff in Bogota,
Colombia, in January and spent two days teaching over 30 South American
Pastors and Campus Staff RUF’s Philosophy of Ministry. It was an exciting
time to see how God is using RUF in the lives of so many Colombian college
students. Conversations are currently happening with teams across the globe,
who have expressed interest in affiliating with RUF in hopes of more
effectively reaching college students.

**MINISTRY DISTINCTIVES**
Weekly large group, small groups, and one-on-one staff-student meetings
provide the structure for campus ministry. Each kind of meeting is essential

<a id="ga47-p350"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=352 printed_page=350 -->

in ministering to college students. In large group meetings, the truth is taught
through preaching the good news of Jesus and corporate worship. Small
groups focus on study, prayer, and fellowship, and many are led by junior
and senior students, under the direction of the campus minister and interns.
One-on-one meetings between students and staff members offer in-depth
discipleship, evangelistic encounters, and accountability in trust-confidence
relationships, as well as counseling. RUF emphasizes the development of a
biblical world-and-life view. As students learn to think biblically, they will
make a lasting difference in the Church and the world. A key distinctive of
RUF is its connection to the Church. Through exhortation by their campus
minister, attendance with friends at local churches, involvement in campus
community, and exposition of Biblical truth, college students learn to love
the Church and develop a lifelong commitment to involvement with God’s
people. RUF provides a bridge maintaining (or establishing) connection to
the Church as students make the transitions from home to college to work
and family life. RUF does not exist for the purpose of perpetuating a campus
ministry, but in order to grow the church.

**CAMPUS INTERNS AND STAFF**
Launched in 1980, the Intern Program has trained over 700 interns. In the
last 28 years the program has grown at a rate of 13% per year. Nearly 161
young men and women (all recent college graduates) are currently working
directly with a campus minister to receive on-the-job training in evangelism,
small group leadership, and one-on-one ministry. While interns minister to
college students, they also participate in a study program focusing on biblical
and theological training. After their internship with Reformed University
Fellowship, interns move into both vocational ministry and the broader
marketplace with a deepened understanding of God’s Word and experience
in His service. The campus intern, as well as campus staff, is equipped to be
“an instrument for noble purposes, made holy, useful to the Master and
prepared to do any good work” (II Timothy 2:21). (See Attachment 2.)

The Campus Staff program within RUF exists to provide a long-term
vocational ministerial position for women who desire to work on the college
campus. While most of our Campus Staff come directly from serving two
years as an RUF intern, we have begun expanding outside of that channel to
bring in qualified candidates in an effort to meet the growing demand on
campus. (See Attachment 2.)

In 2018, the Campus Staff department hired six new full-time staff, and three
part-time staff to bring the overall number of staff to 28.

<a id="ga47-p351"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=353 printed_page=351 -->

**SUMMER CONFERENCE**
**Summer Conference 2018 –** In May 2018, Reformed University Fellowship
hosted its 38 [th] RUF Summer Conference at Laguna Beach Christian Retreat
Center in Panama City Beach, Florida. In our three weeks of conferences this
year, we hosted 2,158 students and 461 staff and their families. Last year
Summer Conference large group speakers addressed the topic of
Justification, one of the principles of RUF Philosophy of Ministry. Week
One Dates: May 7–12, Speaker: Rev James Forsyth, Senior Pastor, McLean
Presbyterian Church, McLean, VA. Week Two Dates: May 14–19, Speaker:
Rev Chad Scruggs, Associate Pastor, Park Cities Presbyterian Church,
Dallas, TX. Week Three Dates: May 21-26, Speaker: Rev John Stone,
Former Assistant Coordinator, RUF, Knoxville, TN. The Summer
Conference schedule included seminars in the morning, afternoon fellowship
and recreation, and large group in the evening followed by small group
discussions and fellowship. Total income including scholarships granted was
$843,429. Expenses totaled $837,949 including scholarship refund.

**Summer Conference 2019 –** May 2019 registration opened March 1, and
over 2,500 students are projected to attend this year. The 2019 cost per
student is $375. Large group speakers will cover the topic of Sanctification.
The format for this summer schedule will be similar to last year: seminars in
the morning, afternoon recreation, and evening large group, followed small
groups and fellowship. Week One Dates: May 6-11, Speaker Rev Hunter
Bailey, Planting Pastor, Christ Community Church, Fayetteville, AR. Week
Two Dates: May 13-18, Speaker Rev Shawn Slate, Senior Pastor, Redeemer
Presbyterian Church, Knoxville, TN. Week Three Dates: May 19-24, Rev
Russ Whitfield, Pastor, Grace Mosaic, Washington, DC, and Director of
Cross-Cultural Ministry, RUF.

**WIVES RETREAT**
Ninety-one RUF Campus Minister wives descended on the city of New
Orleans the weekend of January 17-20. It is the largest attended Wives
Weekend to date. RUF Wives enjoyed the sites and sounds of New Orleans,
and of course the food. Paige Brown and Rev. Reyn Cabinte were this year’s
weekend speakers. Wives also enjoyed time together in Prayer Groups and
Area dinners. We are extremely grateful for the role wives play in RUF’s
ministry to college students, and Wives Weekend plays a vital role in
encouraging them in their marriages and ministry.

<a id="ga47-p352"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=354 printed_page=352 -->

**STAFF TRAINING**
In 2018, the three full weeks of training for field staff included orientation
for new interns and new campus ministers. This in-depth training is a
distinctive of the ministry and provides philosophical, practical, and
reflective instruction to RUF campus ministers, interns, and staff. We have
Dr. Sandy Willson teaching on leadership at 2019 July Staff Training.

**RUF CAMPUS MINISTER ASSESSMENT**
In December of 2006, RUF held its first Campus Minister Assessment. Since
that time, Assessment has been held twice a year, in February and July.
During Assessment, candidates are interviewed by former and senior RUF
ministers and some of their wives. The prospective campus ministers
complete a personality profile, preach and demonstrate small group
leadership, and engage with assessors in a marriage and family interview,
along with other activities designed to help RUF evaluate each applicant. We
assess approximately 25 candidates a year.

**GROWTH**
2018 was another year of growth as RUF partnered with presbyteries to start
four new campus; University of Texas-Dallas RUF-I, RUF-I at Johns Hopkins,
RUF-I at Columbia University, RUF at Indiana University of Pennsylvania.
This growth placed RUF ministries over 163 campuses in 38 states and in 60
Presbyteries.

Reformed University Fellowship continues to grow with 7 new ministries to
begin in 2019.

**HEADCOUNT GROWTH** **OVERALL MINISTRY**

<a id="ga47-p353"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=355 printed_page=353 -->

**INCOME GROWTH vs. EXPENSES YEAR TO YEAR**

**OVERALL MINISTRY**

**RUF’S VISION FOR THE CHURCH**
RUF continues to be a pipeline for leadership in the PCA. Currently over 90
former RUF Campus Ministers are serving our church as church planters,
pastors, associate pastors, assistant pastors, and denominational staff.
Thousands of RUF Alumni are serving in the church enforcing the fact that
RUF is not just about perpetuating campus ministry but about enriching the
Church. We continue to assist in the planting of churches through our church
planting initiative program.

**CONCLUSION**
God is at work through the ministry of RUF. RUF strives to engage culture
and carry out the kingdom priorities of the Church. God brings together
students and ministers from many different walks of life to accomplish His
purposes. Each person influenced by Reformed University Fellowship will
in turn influence many other people in the course of his or her life. The
Church is strengthened as students learn to love and seek out the Church, and
are trained to serve as future church leaders.

**Recommendations** _**:**_
1. That the General Assembly approve the minutes of the meetings of the

Committee on Reformed University Fellowship for October 2, 2018, and

<a id="ga47-p354"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=356 printed_page=354 -->

March 5, 2019, and the Permanent Committee Conference call on
March 21, 2019.
2. That the General Assembly adopt the financial audit for Reformed

University Fellowship for the fiscal year ending December 31, 2018,
by Carr, Riggs & Ingram, LLP.
3. That the General Assembly approve the 2020 budget of Reformed

University Fellowship.
4. That Attachments 1, 2, 3, and 4 be received as information.

(Attachment 4 can be found in the 2019 Commissioner Handbook,
Report of Reformed University Fellowship to the Forty-seventh
General Assembly, pcaga.sharefile.com.)
5. That RUF **answer Overture 11 in the negative** .
6. That RUF **answer Overture 24 by referring to the Administration**

**Committee’s recommendation on Overture 24.**
7. That the General Assembly re-elect TE Rod Mays as interim

National Coordinator of Reformed University Fellowship from July
1, 2019, until December 31, 2019, and warmly commend him with
thanksgiving for his faithful service.
8. That the General Assembly elect RE Will Huss as National Coordinator

of Reformed University Fellowship, effective January 1, 2020.

<a id="ga47-p370"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=372 printed_page=370 -->

#### **APPENDIX L** **RIDGE HAVEN** **Brevard, NC – Cono, IA** **REPORT** **TO THE FORTY-SEVENTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY** **OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA**

As mentioned in our Budget Report, it has been an amazing year capping off
nine straight years of wonderful ministry, and phenomenal growth. We are
blessed and humbled that, this year alone, over 100 churches and 30 college
ministries have come to Ridge Haven. For the first time we hosted over 5,000
guests this summer. Adding to that encouragement, we had even more attend
our year-round programs. We finished the year with the total guest tally of
over 10,000. In addition, we hosted almost 200 at our new RH Cono campus
in Iowa. What a year indeed.

Each year we consider the possibility that Ridge Haven’s growth may begin
to plateau. Yet to our astonishment, the Lord, through our churches, seems
to say, “keep going, keep growing.” In the past several years we have
completed a number of expansion projects to accommodate our growth. First,
we remodeled Robeson meeting hall to host 275-300 guests. Then we built
additional lodging facilities to accommodate up to 700. This resulted in a
pressing need to build a dining hall that was able to hold this drastically
increased number. Thankfully, the Lord blessed us with our new Jolly Dining
Hall. The old dining hall building was then reconfigured to provide another
meeting space for 200 guests. Then we believed that once Jolly Dining Hall
was completed, we could take a breather from new projects i nb reva rd fora
while. But it became apparent this past year that we are not done growing yet.

Now that our Brevard campus can lodge and feed 700+ guests, we need more
facilities for indoor meeting space. This will balance out the capacities of our
campus, and allow us to continue to grow. We love our large outdoor Barnes
Activity Pavilion that can host 600-700 in the spring, summer and early fall.
But as one group that used it this November commented, “it is just too cold
in November, put us on the wait list for October.” Sadly, this past year we
had to turn away two large groups in October and November for lack of
indoor meeting space.

<a id="ga47-p371"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=373 printed_page=371 -->

We are thrilled that we have already hosted over 2,000 visitors at Ridge
Haven Brevard just during this year’s winter season. Ironically, it was not
too long ago that we questioned the value of hosting events year-round. Most
summer camps function seasonally, and we wrestled with the idea of
following that model of ministry. But the Lord was clear at the time that
there was value in our off season retreat efforts. And even if the numbers

were small, that we should partner with our PCA churches to support
relational ministry growth through retreats. Additionally, we saw the value
and evangelical potential to host other Christian organizations. To our
delight, our “off-season” has become just as busy as the summer camp
season. What a special thrill it is to see groups of college students and young
adults, the next generation of leadership in the PCA, gathering to learn, to
worship, and to relax together in the beauty of God’s creation.

To give a picture of what the retreat season now looks like: while writing this
report on Saturday March 23, there are two college groups on campus (one
of which is a ministry to international students), a PCA church’s youth group,
and a PCA men’s retreat group. The sun is out, it is avery comfortable 55
degrees and it is beautiful. What a blast. But, just a month ago we had
multiple church groups here and it rained the whole weekend. Don’t get me
wrong, the groups had a great time. We received some of our best surveys.
One attendee left the note, “ _RH is a beautiful campus. I enjoyed my stay even_
_with the rainy weather. The paths were well maintained despite the wet_
_season.”_ Though we are grateful for the gracious attitudes of our guests,
when the high is 45 degrees and the rain is relentless, everyone on campus
needs at least a few hours inside, at the very least for their meeting times.
Unfortunately, when we have many groups on campus, meeting space is a
premium and we end up forced to use outdoor space such as the Barnes
Center. As mentioned, we have had groups recently opt to be put on a wait
list for the sake of reserving some indoor meeting space.

Thus, we have enthusiastically started planning and raising funds to build an
indoor multipurpose building that can be used as a gym and large meeting
space, and eventually will include our camp store, a coffee shop, and smaller
meeting rooms. We feel confident that the right next step is for us to build
upon the growing year-round ministry in Brevard. We are trusting that the
Lord will bless this new building project as He has so abundantly provided
with our others building efforts.

As you know, through the generosity of our churches in Iowa, we expanded
our ministry to Cono, Iowa, last year. We ran two weeks of summer camp at

<a id="ga47-p372"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=374 printed_page=372 -->

Cono and had phenomenally positive feedback from the children, youth, and
adult leaders that attended. In fact, many of last year’s attendees have signed
up to return this year. There are so many small PCA churches in the Midwest
and our goal is to provide a space where these children and youth can
convene and be encouraged by the testimony of staff and by connecting with
other students. One PCA pastor from Missouri validated this effort by telling
us, _“We feel like a complete island out here. Our kids are used to doing things_
_on their own; it would be so encouraging for them to interact with other kids_
_in the denomination!”_ We have four weeks of camp planned for 2019. While
we are thankful to extend our camp offerings this year, we have a
tremendously long way to go before we can fully utilize the Cono facility
that God has so wonderfully provided.

We hope that Cono can be like Brevard, a year-round operation. To get our
summer camp ministry started at Cono in 2018, we had to quickly construct
six camper cabins to hold a total of 72 guests each week. In order to expand
our camp capacity and to include spring and fall retreat services, we have
two large and expensive mandatory maintenance projects to complete. First,
we must install sprinkler systems in the existing lodging facilities, a statemandated requirement. Once installed, we will be able to handle over 300
campers each week. Secondly, we need extensive repair work for the sewage
system. Humanly speaking, both projects feel like huge hurdles, but we are
trusting the Lord as we seek to raise the two million dollars in necessary
funds.

In our increasingly secular culture, we praise the Lord that there are many
camps on mission to grow gospel truths in the hearts and minds of the next
generation. While we share a common goal with these organizations
nationwide, we also “do ministry” quite differently from many. Ridge Haven
programs place emphasis on mentoring through its staff and group activities,
and not just through entertaining activities. We want children, youth, and
adults to interact with the Lord and with each other, and truly grow in their
spiritual walk.

We center our activities and schedules around authentic relationship
building. The games and activities we choose to facilitate focus on group
interaction, rather than simply personal enjoyment. One of the reasons we
discontinued running our zipline was that it was too individually focused.
There is a time and place for personal challenge, but in the limited time we
have with campers over the course of a week, we want them to leave camp
inspired and equipped to walk with Jesus. Research continues to show that
foundational beliefs take root most through relationship building.

<a id="ga47-p373"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=375 printed_page=373 -->

Studies also show that 80% of the decisions that are made for the Lord come

during the formative years of childhood and youth. What a God given
opportunity we have – to meet, mentor, and minister to our covenant children
each camping season. And not to be forgotten is the ministry training and
experience that is being provided to the nearly 100 interns and counselors
that work as year-round and summer staff to support our programs. Trained
and mentored themselves through our programs, work experience, and
campus pastors, these sharp young adults are the future.

It is worth noting that our year-round expansion is not aimed at financial
gain, although our growth has helped us continue to keep our camp costs low.
As mentioned, many camping facilities operate seasonally because the cost
of year-round operation often exceeds their income. Yet, Ridge Haven’s
ability to partner with our churches – many of whom are too small to have
youth staff – is too important of a calling to dismiss. Indeed, due to the
generosity of our churches and donors, we also offer camp scholarships, and
even free camp for some inner-city ministries.

God has provided us with a place for year-round spiritual growth and
development, and so we should continue to grow. Thankfully, the Lord has
also given us the confidence that He wants us to keep it up with the incredibly
increasing numbers He has brought us. And so, we continue in faith, and with
your prayers and blessings.

**Recommendations:**

1. That the Ridge Haven 2020 Budget as presented through the AC

Budget Review Committee be approved.
2. That the 2017 audit dated July 30, 2018, performed by Robins, Smith

& Jordan, be received.
3. That the minutes of the Board of Directors of Ridge Haven be

approved: March 5-7, 2018; Executive Board Minutes, September 9,
2018; and October 1-3, 2018.
4. That February 23, 2020, be a day for our churches to pray for the

ministries of Ridge Haven.
5. That **Overture 24** from Philadelphia Metro West Presbytery “Add

Constitutional Precedence Statement to Policy Manuals” be
**answered by reference to the recommendation from the**
**Administrative Committee regarding Overture 24**

Wallace Anderson

Executive Director

<a id="ga47-p374"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=376 printed_page=374 -->

#### **APPENDIX M** **REPORT OF THE** **COOPERATIVE MINISTRIES COMMITTEE** **TO THE FORTY-SEVENTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY** **OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA**

**T** **HE** **F** **UNCTIONS OF THE** **C** **OOPERATIVE** **M** **INISTRIES** **C** **OMMITTEE**
The CMC is unique in that all ten of the chairmen of the General Assembly
Permanent Committees and Agency Boards, along with the Chief
Administrative Officers of the General Assembly Permanent Committees
and Agencies are all voting members. Also the present Moderator and five
immediate past Moderators of the General Assembly are advisory members.
The Cooperative Ministries Committee was formed by the 34 [th] General
Assembly in 2006, as a result of the Strategic Planning process, to serve as a
forum for advancing inter-agency cooperation, collaboration, or conflict
resolution ( _RAO_ 7-3.f, h), to monitor or evaluate the standards of
effectiveness and efficiency of Permanent Committees and Agencies ( _RAO_
7-3.g), and to engage in long-range planning ( _RAO_ 7-3.c).

The CMC is a consultative body within the PCA structure. It cannot make
recommendations directly to the General Assembly. From 2006 to 2017 the
CMC could make recommendations to Committees and Agencies, which
would consider, approve, amend, or negate such recommendations for matters
that required Assembly action. In 2017 the Forty-fifth General Assembly
amended _RAO_ 7-3.c to remove the CMC’s ability even to recommend to
Committees and Agencies matters that require Assembly action. _RAO_ 7-3.c
now requires that any matters dealt with in the CMC that necessitate
Assembly action can only come to the Assembly via overtures from
Presbyteries ( _M45GA_, 2017, p. 51).

Over the past years, the CMC has worked to “facilitate integrated long-range
planning that supports progress toward the overall mission and ministry of
the PCA.”

**M** **EETINGS OF THE** **C** **OOPERATIVE** **M** **INISTRIES** **C** **OMMITTEE**
The Cooperative Ministries Committee met twice since the last General
Assembly, on September 10, 2018, 9:00 a.m. to 3:00 p.m. and on January 23,
2019, 9:00 a.m. to 2:15 p.m., both at the Crowne Plaza near the Atlanta
Airport. A fellowship dinner was held the night before each meeting.

<a id="ga47-p375"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=377 printed_page=375 -->

September 10, 2018 Meeting
At the September 10, 2018, meeting the Moderator of the Forty-sixth General
Assembly, Dr. Irwyn Ince, presided and gave a devotional address from
Hebrews 11:30-40.

Most of the voting members were present except for the chairman of RBI
and the chairman of RUF. The Moderator of the Forty-sixth General
Assembly and the Moderators of the five previous General Assemblies were
present as advisory members.

Visitors included RE Richard Doster, Editor, _byFaith_ Magazine; Ms. Angela
Nantz, AC Operations Manager; and TE John Robertson, PCA Business
Administrator.

The Stated Clerk presented the annual John Calvin Award to RUF for the
most creative report to the Forty-sixth General Assembly. The Stated Clerk
informed the CMC that the Assembly-wide Seminar at the 2019 Assembly
would be on Civil Conversations on Controversial Issues: Speaking the Truth
in Love.

RE Gary Campbell, President of RBI, reported on the status of the Rising
Generations working group and led a discussion on the issue.

A discussion was held on the recent and upcoming leadership transitions
among the General Assembly Committees and Agencies. The PCA Foundation
Board and Reformed University Fellowship reported on the stages of
transition planning in which they are involved. A period of prayer was held
for the transitions in Committee and Agency leadership.

TE Paul Hahn, Coordinator of MNA, reported on the progress of the Unity
Fund and other means of advancing minority leadership. The Committee
prayed for the advancement of minority leadership in the PCA.

The Committee discussed the possibility of having a Vice-Moderator or
Moderator-in-Nomination at the General Assembly. Since the revision of
_RAO_ 7-3.c, that would have to be brought by overture from a Presbytery.

No items of inter-agency conflict were discussed. The committee noted that
the General Assembly Committees and Agencies are cooperating well. No
issues of efficiency of Permanent Committees or Agencies were discussed.

<a id="ga47-p376"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=378 printed_page=376 -->

The Committee also discussed the shortened schedule for General Assembly
in Atlanta. The consensus was that the present schedule is too short and that
the goal of shortening the Assembly to increase Ruling Elder participation
was not being accomplished. The AC is the committee that makes
recommendations regarding the General Assembly; it could take several
years to implement changes because contracts for hotels and convention
centers are made several years in advance.

January 23, 2019 Meeting
At the January 23, 2019, meeting the Moderator of the Forty-sixth General
Assembly, Dr. Irwyn Ince, presided and gave a devotional address from
Mark 5:21.

Most of the voting members were present except for the chairman of the
Covenant College Board. The Moderator of the Forty-sixth General Assembly
and three Moderators of the previous General Assemblies were present as
advisory members. The Moderators of the 42 [nd] and 44 [th] General Assemblies
were absent.

Visitors included RE Richard Doster, Editor, _byFaith_ Magazine; Ms. Angela
Nantz, AC Operations Manager; Mrs Karen Hodge CDM Women’s Ministry
Coordinator; and TE John Robertson, PCA Business Administrator.

RE Gary Campbell, President of RBI, reported on the Rising Generations
working group.

TE Paul Hahn reported on Practicing Diversity Well in the PCA. He reported
that the unity fund is doing well with a goal of a $5,000,000 endowment
and a $200,000 budget for 2019. He reported that of all of the 4,700+
Teaching Elders of the PCA, only several hundred are Korean Americans,
fifty-five are African Americans, forty-five are Latino, and one is Native
American.

TE Stephen Estock, Coordinator of CDM, reported that CDM is readying the
PCA papers on racial reconciliation for publication, a project which was a
recommendation of the Ad Interim Committee on Racial and Ethnic

Reconciliation. Dr. Estock also reported on the completion of a document
titled “Principles of Coordinator Unity” (see Attachment, p. 380).

Mrs. Karen Hodge, CDM Women’s Ministry Coordinator, gave an update
on that ministry.

<a id="ga47-p377"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=379 printed_page=377 -->

The Committee prayed for the progress of diversity, the future of the PCA,
and women’s ministry within the PCA.

The Stated Clerk led a discussion on current issues facing the PCA.

The Committee gave thanks for the work and ministry of departing members
RE Gary Campbell, RE Randy Stair, and RE Bruce Terrell.

**C** **ONCLUSION**
The CMC only gives informational reports. The CMC has no recommendations
for action to the General Assembly because, as noted above, under _RAO_ 7-3.c,
the CMC cannot make recommendations directly to the General Assembly.

<a id="ga47-p378"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=380 printed_page=378 -->

**Attachment**

**Principles of Coordinator Unity**

“Behold, how good and pleasant it is for brothers to dwell in unity.” Psalm
133:1 ESV)

As Coordinators and Presidents, we believe our mutual fellowship should
represent the reality of our union with the Lord Jesus Christ (John 17:20-23).
as we serve in positions of leadership, we hope the priority of our deep and
abiding relationships with each other will be a visible reminder of gospel
oneness, a display to all who observe the reality of the love of the Lord Jesus
for his people.

In order to pursue this goal and move forward in love, we pledge ourselves
to the following. Our desire is that gospel and covenantal unity among the
elected leaders of the denomination will contribute to a greater gospel and
covenantal unity among the elders of the denomination.

“. . .Walk in a manner worthy of the calling to which you have been called,
with all humility and gentleness, with patience, bearing with one another in
love, eager to maintain the unity of the Spirit in the bond of peace”
(Ephesians 4:1-3 ESV).

“Let no corrupting talk come out of your mouths, but only such as is good
for building up, as fits the occasion, that it may give grace to those who hear”
(Ephesians 4:29 ESV).

- We pledge to assist each other in pursuing a deeper understanding of
Christ in the gospel. Gospel unity rejoices in and promotes the truth
as revealed by God in his Word. Unity is not sameness. Though we
are united by common ordination vows, our ministry experiences
and priorities are often different.

- We pledge to pray regularly for each other and the ministries we
represent.

- We pledge to communicate regularly with each other regarding the
blessings we enjoy and the challenges we face in the lives of our
families and ministries.

- We pledge to make our corporate gatherings a priority. When
circumstances prevent us from attending, we will inform the group
and ask to be excused. We will later seek to know what happened in
our absence.

<a id="ga47-p379"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=381 printed_page=379 -->

- We pledge to avoid a root of bitterness by communicating to each
other quickly and directly when we are offended.

We pledge to help each other understand better God’s Word and
promote the doctrine contained in the Word as summarized in the
Westminster Standards. We will seek to understand fully the views
of another before we seek to correct what we believe to be error.

- We pledge to help each other follow the principles of church
government explained in _The Book of Church Order_ ( _BCO_ ) and
_Rules of Assembly Operations_ ( _RAO_ ), as these are the guidelines the
Assembly has given to promote purity and peace.

- We pledge to seek and grant forgiveness, working together to pursue
a path that promotes the mutual good. When disagreements exist, we will
submit the situation to the group for input and guidance. If there is still
no resolution, we will seek the guidance of the Cooperative Ministries
Committee (CMC) and submit to their direction ( _RAO_ 7-3h).

- We pledge to cooperate in shared ministry and cooperative projects.
Rather than competing through duplicate ministries, we will seek
ways to leverage abilities and opportunities for the good of the whole
denomination.

- We pledge to respect the spheres of ministry responsibility God has
given to each Committee and Agency through the General Assembly.
When circumstances lead to an overlap of spheres (perceived or
real), we will seek to pursue mutual benefit rather than individual
ministry expansion and control.

- We pledge to protect the reputation of each other and the ministries
we represent. This protection involves believing the best and being
slow to believe negative reports about each other (1 Corinthians 13:7).
Protection also involves confronting false information and encouraging
critics to raise issues directly with the leader of a ministry.

- We pledge to give and receive constructive criticism regarding our
ministries with kindness and patience. Our desire is to help each
other serve the Church well by responding to the needs and desires
of the members.

The unity we seek, the unity that will lead the world to believe God sent Jesus
(John 17:21), is a unity created and sustained by the Holy Spirit. To that end,
we seek his blessing:

“May the God of endurance and encouragement grant [us] to live in such
harmony with one another, in accord with Christ Jesus, that together [we]
may with one voice glorify the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ”
(Romans 15:5-6 ESV).

<a id="ga47-p380"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=382 printed_page=380 -->

#### **APPENDIX N** **REPORT OF** **THE INTERCHURCH RELATIONS COMMITTEE** **TO THE FORTY-SEVENTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY** **OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA**

**Membership**

TE Marvin Padgett, Alternate RE Paul Richardson
TE Kevin Rogers RE James D. Walters Jr., Alternate
