TE Robert Cartee Bales, MTW Advisory
TE Billy Park, Advisory on Korean Relations

**Meetings**

- October 4, 2018, via conference call

- April 16, 2019, via conference call

**IRC Officers for 2019-2020 Assembly Year**

The committee elected as officers:

TE Bruce O’Neil – chairman
TE Marvin Padgett – vice-chairman (contingent upon re-election)
RE James C. Richardson – secretary

**The Role of the Interchurch Relations Committee**

- The Interchurch Relations Committee is a Special Committee,
functioning under the _Rules of Assembly Operations_ 8-1, through
which the Presbyterian Church in America relates to other branches
and entities of the Church Universal.

- The IRC is funded by the Administrative Committee of the General
Assembly.

- The committee is composed of six principal members and two
alternates, elected by the General Assembly.

- The Stated Clerk is a member of the IRC by virtue of his office ( _RAO_
3-2.j). He is expected to communicate with other Reformed
denominations ( _RAO_ 3-5.e), have an appreciation of the whole
Church of the Lord Jesus Christ, and to be able to work with the
leaders of all branches of the Church ( _RAO_ 3-5.h).

<a id="ga47-p381"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=383 printed_page=381 -->

The committee also is advised by TE Cartee Bales of MTW, since some of
the work of the IRC relates to churches and Christians abroad, and TE by
Billy Park on relations with Korean churches.

- Through the IRC, the PCA relates to other Reformed and
Presbyterian denominations in North America by participation in the
North American Presbyterian and Reformed Council (NAPARC),
which meets annually.

- Through the IRC the PCA relates to Presbyterian and Reformed
denominations abroad based in such countries as the Netherlands,
the United Kingdom, South Korea, South Africa, Brazil, and Mexico.

The PCA is a member of the World Reformed Fellowship, a fellowship of
evangelical Reformed Churches, congregations, institutions, ministries, and
individuals, across the spectrum of the evangelical Reformed faith. So the
WRF includes Anglicans and Reformed Baptists at the ends of the spectrum,
as well as Presbyterians, churches with continental Reformed roots, and
Congregationalists in the middle of the continuum. WRF has a General
Assembly every four years.

- The PCA has been a member of the National Association of
Evangelicals since 1986. The NAE is composed of evangelical
denominations, congregations, institutions, ministries, and individuals
in the USA. The PCA Stated Clerk has served as Chairman of the

Board since 2006.

- With the approval and encouragement of the IRC, the Stated Clerk
has had contacts since 2009 with evangelical, conservative, orthodox,
and traditional renewal groups and newly formed denominations
through what is now named the Common Ground Christian Network,
formally organized in 2014. “The Common Ground Christian Network
is a group of denominations, organizations, congregations, and
individuals who believe 21st century Christians need to stand
together for the cause of Christ. The common ground upon which
we stand is the affirmation of classical orthodox Christianity.”
Member denominations include the Evangelical Presbyterian Church,
the Anglican Church in North America, the North American Lutheran
Church, the Evangelical Covenant Order of Presbyterians (ECO),
and the Evangelical Association of Reformed and Congregational
Churches. The PCA is not a member presently. The PCA and the
Lutheran Church-Missouri Synod send observers.

<a id="ga47-p382"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=384 printed_page=382 -->

**The North American Presbyterian and Reformed Council (NAPARC)**
The PCA delegation to the annual meeting of the North American
Presbyterian and Reformed Council consisted of TE Bruce O’Neil, RE James
C. Richardson, TE Marvin Padgett, and TE L. Roy Taylor. The three-day
meeting was hosted by the Korean American Presbyterian Church
denomination. Fifty-nine attended the meeting, including observers.

The officers for 2019-2020 elected are:

- The Reverend David Kim, KPCA (Kosin) – Chairman

- Dr. Anthony Curto, OPC – Vice-chairman

- The Reverend Ralph Pontier, URCNA – Secretary

- The Reverend Todd De Rooy, URCNA – Treasurer

The 2019 NAPARC meeting will be in Anaheim, CA, hosted by the KPCA
(Kosin) November12-14, 2019. The Presbyterian Church of Mexico, the
largest Presbyterian denomination in North America, was invited by the ad
interim committee to send observers to the 2019 meeting upon
recommendation of the PCA delegation. It was not a unanimous action.

Member Denominations
Associate Reformed Presbyterian Church (ARPC), joined 1982
Canadian Reformed Churches (CanRC), joined 2008
Evangelical Reformed Church of Quebec (ERQ), joined 2003
Free Reformed Churches of North America (FRCNA), joined 2006
Heritage Reformed Churches (HRC), joined 2007
Korean American Presbyterian Church (KAPC), joined 1982
Korean Presbyterian Church in America, Kosin (KPCA Kosin), joined 2014
Orthodox Presbyterian Church (OPC), founding member 1975
Presbyterian Church in America (PCA), founding member 1975
Presbyterian Reformed Church (PRC), joined 2009
Reformed Church in the United States (RCUS), joined 1995
Reformed Presbyterian Church in North America (RPCNA), founding member

1975
United Reformed Churches in North America (URCNA), joined 2005

Observer Denominations
Bible Presbyterian Church (BPC)
Protestant Reformed Churches of America (PRCA)

NAPARC was founded in 1975 with five denominations as members, the
CRCNA, OPC, PCA, RPCES, and RPCNA. The two-fold purpose is 1) to
advise, counsel, and cooperate with one another, and 2) to hold out the

<a id="ga47-p383"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=385 printed_page=383 -->

desirability and need for organic union. in the early years there was a multi year attempt to have a five-way merger of all the member denominations. At
that time the CRCNA was the largest denomination in NAPARC. The only
actual merger was the Joining and Receiving of the Reformed Presbyterian
Church Evangelical Synod and the PCA in 1986.

**NAPARC Membership**

|Denomination|Churches|Mission<br>Churches|Communicant<br>Members|%of<br>NAPARC<br>Membership*|
|---|---|---|---|---|
|ARPC|220|48|29,434|6.39|
|CanRC|57|17|11,372|2.46|
|ERQ|5|0|363|0.08|
|FRCNA|20|0|2,849|0.62|
|HRC|9|0|1,271|0.27|
|KAPC|650|NA|70,000|15.20|
|KPCA Kosin|131|18|5,500|1.20|
|OPC|281|40|23,032|5.00|
|**PCA**|**1,568**|**344**|**292,450**|**63.50**|
|PRC|6|0|109|0.02|
|RCUS|41|7|2,815|0.61|
|RPCNA|90|9|5,122|1.11|
|URCNA|114|12|16,253|3.52|

*Percentages rounded off

<a id="ga47-p384"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=386 printed_page=384 -->

The composition of NAPARC has changed over the years. The Christian
Reformed Church of North America was suspended (1998) and then expelled
(2002) from membership for declaring a portion of their constitution
“inoperative” by action of one synod rather than going through the proper
process of amending their constitution. The presenting issue was the opening
of ordained offices to women. The underlying issue was a 1972 Report 44
approved by the General Synod regarding hermeneutics. Since the expulsion
of the CRCNA, seven denominations have joined. There are now thirteen
member denominations in NAPARC. The PCA comprises 63.5% of the
entire membership of NAPARC.

The PCA Assembly in 2006 requested that there be another discussion of
organic union. a committee was appointed to come up with a definition of
“organic union.” Discussion ensued among affinity denominations. Since
1982 there have been no organic unions of NAPARC denominations. It
appears unlikely that any of the twelve other NAPARC member
denominations will merge with the PCA in the foreseeable future because of
the size of the PCA, the ethnic history and constituency of some denominations
(ex. Korean, Dutch, French-Canadian), differences in polity (though all are
in the Presbyterian polity family), and the desire to maintain certain
denominational distinctives.

At present, the second purpose of NAPARC, “to hold out the desirability and
need for organic union,” is not the primary raison d’être of the council. The
first purpose, “to advise, counsel, and cooperate with one another,” seems to
be taking precedence. The ARPC and RPCNA have had some conversations
and simultaneous General Synod meetings, but are not likely to merge. Four
denominations of Dutch heritage have been having church union discussions,
for example, the CanRC with the URCNA and the FRCNA with the HRC.
They have some distinctive theological views, which may be impediments to
church union though.

The BPC split off from the OPC one year after the OPC’s origin in 1936 over
the BPC’s dispensational pre-millennialism, total abstinence from tobacco
and alcohol, and secondary separation. After the BPC’s founder, Carl
McIntire’s death in 2002, the BPC has had reunion discussions with the OPC
in recent years.

Much of the plenary meeting time was taken up with denominational reports
of and prayer for the member churches and observer churches.

<a id="ga47-p385"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=387 printed_page=385 -->

The OPC discussed two matters in plenary sessions: 1) the updating of the
language in the Westminster Standards, and 2) the OPC’s diaconal ministry
in which they invited other NAPARC denominations to participate.

The PCA delegation had bilateral meetings with delegations of the OPC,
ERQ, ARPC, and URCNA. The OPC wanted to hear the PCA delegation’s
comments on the Revoice Conference held by the Revoice organization at a
PCA church in St. Louis in July of 2018. The PCA delegation pointed out that:
1) Revoice is not a PCA organization; 2) Revoice’s mission is “Supporting,
encouraging, and empowering gay, lesbian, same-sex attracted, and other
gender and sexual minority Christians, so they can flourish while observing
the historic, Christian doctrine of marriage and sexuality”; 3) a CTS OT
professor led a seminar in which he affirmed that the biblical prohibitions
(OT and NT) against homosexual lusts and homosexual actions are still in
force; and 4) the Revoice conference was not a PCA conference, though it
was held at a PCA church site.

The meetings with the ERQ, ARPC, and URCNA delegations were of a
general nature and were very cordial, respectful, and appreciative.

**World Reformed Fellowship**

- The World Reformed Fellowship is a fellowship or network of
Reformed denominations (ranging from Anglican, continental
Reformed, Presbyterian, Congregationalist, and Baptist),
congregations, educational institutions, mission agencies, and
individuals. It represents a range of evangelical Reformed thought,
polities, ministries, and emphases.

- On March 31, 2011, the Board of Directors of the World Reformed
Fellowship completed work on a Statement of Faith that was five
years in the making. Why a new Statement of Faith? There were
three guiding motives behind our work: 1) We wished to express
accurately the common elements of the historic Reformed
Confessions which we have used to define ourselves theologically The Gallican Confession, The Belgic Confession, The Heidelberg
Catechism, The Thirty-Nine Articles, The Second Helvetic
Confession, The Canons of Dort, The Westminster Confession of
Faith, the London Confession of 1689, and the Savoy Declaration;
2) We wished to bring that historic Reformed orthodoxy to bear on
some of the critical issues facing the church of the 21st century abortion, genetic manipulation, the nature of marriage, poverty,
suffering, and oppression; and 3) We wish to include the voices of

<a id="ga47-p386"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=388 printed_page=386 -->

evangelical Reformed Christians from the entire world, in light of
the fact that all of the historic Reformed confessions were written

in Western Europe or Great Britain. It has never been and is not now
our intention that our Statement of Faith _**replace**_ any of the historic
confessions. However, at the Fourth General Assembly of the WRF
in Sao Paulo, Brazil, on March 26, 2015, the members of the WRF
determined that the WRF Statement of Faith may stand alongside the
historic confessions as one of those to which prospective members
may subscribe. Versions of the Statement in the following languages
are available as attachments to this article: Afrikaans, French,
German, Italian, Korean, Portuguese, Russian, Spanish, and
Ukrainian.

- The WRF has three commissions; 1) Evangelism and Missions, 2)
Theological Education, and 3) Theology.

- The WRF has regional boards in Africa, Asia, Australia/New
Zealand, Europe, Latin America, and North America.

- The WRF has a General Assembly once every four years. At General
Assemblies, we seek to do two primary things: 1) conduct the
necessary business of the Assembly (such as considering any
changes to our doctrinal standards or our purpose statements and
electing the Board of Directors) and 2) addressing major issues
facing the global Reformed and evangelical church. The first
General Assembly of the WRF was held in Orlando, Florida, in
2000; the second Assembly was held in Johannesburg, South Africa,
in 2006; the third Assembly was held in Edinburgh, Scotland, in
2010; and the Fourth General Assembly was held in March of 2015,
in Sao Paulo, Brazil.

- The next General Assembly, The WRF General Assembly 2019, will
be held in the city of Jakarta, Indonesia, August 8-12, 2019. The
meetings will be held at the facilities of the Reformed Evangelical
Church in Indonesia (GRII). Under Article V. E. of the WRF Bylaws
the PCA is entitled to five representatives in the bicameral Assembly.
Several PCA members will be speakers at the WRF General
Assembly; Drs. Ligon Duncan, Richard Pratt, and Peter Jones will
be Plenary Speakers. Dr. Kathleen Nielson, of the Gospel Coalition’s
Women’s Ministry, will be a Workshop Leader. The voting
representatives to the WRF Assembly will be TE Bruce O’Neil, and
RE Paul Richardson, plus three other PCA members who will be
attending.

<a id="ga47-p387"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=389 printed_page=387 -->

**National Association of Evangelicals (NAE)**

- Under the present rules of the NAE, denominational representatives
at the NAE are the primary administrative officers of denomination
(or their designees in case of absence) depending on the polity of the
denomination. So among the Presbyterian-Reformed denominations,
the Stated Clerks of the PCA and EPC, the Synod Executive of the
Evangelical Covenant Order of Presbyterians, and the General
Secretary of the Christian Reformed Church of North America, are
members of the Board representing their respective PresbyterianReformed denominations.

- The PCA Stated Clerk has been chairman of the NAE Board since

2006. He chaired a meeting of the Board of Directors of the National
Association of Evangelicals October 11, 2018, and March 7, 2019.

- There is nothing to report to the Assembly regarding the NAE that
is contrary to the positions of the PCA.

- The Stated Clerk provides the IRC members a copy of each edition
of the “NAE Update,” the electronic newsletter to the IRC
summarizing the actions of the NAE.

- Several online articles and posts inaccurately reported that the NAE
at its October 11, 2018 meeting endorsed “Fairness for All Legislation,”
a.k.a. “the Utah Compromise.” The Utah Compromise was a law
enacted in Utah that seeks to protect both religious freedom and the
basic civil rights of LGBT persons (housing, employment, medical
access, etc.) after aboard members hared a portion of uncorrected,
unapproved minutes. In fact, no specific legislation was endorsed
by the NAE.

- After the October 11, 2018 meeting, the NAE Office received less
than fifteen inquiries regarding the Board’s action on the issue of
religious liberty and civil rights. That included emails, phone calls,
and requests for interviews. a sizeable portion were from a few PCA
members, one in particular who made repeated inquiries.

- Some possible legislation has been discussed among members of the
US Congress. On the one hand, The Equality Act would amend the
1964 Civil Rights Act to make sexual orientation and gender
identification (SOGI) a protected class but would not offer any
protections of religious freedoms. On the other hand, The First
Amendment Defense Act would strengthen religious freedoms but
does not protect the civil rights LBGT persons already have. Some
believe that both religious freedoms and civil rights ought to be
protected in a both/and approach rather than an either/or approach.

<a id="ga47-p388"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=390 printed_page=388 -->

- The NAE is on record as affirming that biblical marriage is between
one man and one woman and that any sexual relations between
people (of opposite sex or the same sex) outside of biblical
heterosexual marriage is contrary to biblical teaching. The NAE is
on record as affirming religious liberty as guaranteed to every
American under the First Amendment of our Constitution. The NAE
is on record as affirming that the basic religious and human rights
should be protected even though we as evangelicals may disagree
with the practices or beliefs of our fellow citizens.

- The NAE has been, is, and will continue to be committed to biblical
marriage and religious liberty. Evangelicals are not of one opinion
on the best strategies for protecting our religious liberties under the
First Amendment in relation to the civil rights of people whose
sexual practices and beliefs we believe to be erroneous and harmful.
For example, whether we should be reactive or proactive, or whether
the best solutions are influencing public opinion, political activities,
legislative action, presidential executive orders, or judicial cases.
These are areas where continued discernment is needed. With the
increasing secularization of our culture and growing hostility to
biblical morality, civil conversation and gracious but persistent advocacy
of religious liberties are needed as we seek the best way(s) forward.

- Overtures have been submitted from Central Carolina Presbytery
(2011-12 & 2019-23) seeking for the PCA to withdraw from the
NAE. The IRC recommends that Overture 23, “Withdraw from the
NAE” be answered in the negative by the Forty-seventh General
Assembly. See recommendation 5 below.

**Common Ground Christian Network**

- With the approval and encouragement of the IRC, the Stated Clerk
began in 2009 to attend the Association for Church Renewal, an
association of renewal groups that were devoted to renewal in the
mainline denominations. The ACR evolved into the Common Ground
Christian Network in 2014, with emerging evangelical denominations
from the mainline denominations: the Anglican Church of North
America (ACNA), the North American Lutheran Church (NALC),
the Evangelical Presbyterian Church, the Evangelical Covenant
Order of Presbyterians (ECO), and the Evangelical Association of
Reformed and Congregational Churches. The Lutheran ChurchMissouri Synod and the PCA send observers. All these groups are
stressing the same things, i.e. biblical fidelity, evangelism,
discipleship, church planting, and mercy ministries.

<a id="ga47-p389"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=391 printed_page=389 -->

- As our culture continues to secularize and religious liberties are
increasingly threatened, there will be a need to build alliances with
other evangelicals beyond our Presbyterian-Reformed circles so that
we have an Apostles’ Creed circle of friends, allies, and fellow
laborers not limited to Presbyterian-Reformed Christians or, as
Frances Schaeffer advocated, joining with other co-belligerents to
resist the secularized culture and to stand together for righteous

causes.

**Presbyterian Heritage Center, Montreat, NC**

- The Presbyterian Heritage Center (PHC) is an independent interPresbyterian organization that seeks to serve PCUSA evangelicals,
the PCA, ARPC, EPC, OPC, and ECO. Their website is
[www.phcmontreat.org. The PHC was formed as an independent](http://www.phcmontreat.org/)
organization after the PCUSA moved the archives to Philadelphia
after the reunion of the PCUS and PC(USA) decades ago. The PHC
board met December 11, 2018, in Montreat, North Carolina, and at
the PCA Offices May 13, 2019.

- PHC hosted a symposium on The History of Presbyterian Missions
to China at North Avenue Presbyterian Church Atlanta, Georgia, on
March 31, 2019.

- The PHC will have an exhibit on Presbyterian Missions to Central
America and the Caribbean at the center in Montreat, May 1, 2019,
through January 15, 2020.

- The Presbyterian Heritage Center will host an International Symposium
on C. S. Lewis: Influence & Relevance Today November 6-8, 2019,
in Montreat, NC.

- The director of the Presbyterian Heritage Center is working with
RE Wayne Sparkman, the Director of the PCA Historical Center.

- The Presbyterian Heritage Center has invited some PCA ministers in
the Montreat and Atlanta areas to attend board meetings and other
events. The PCA Stated Clerk serves on the Board.

**Fraternal and Corresponding Relations**

- Each year we receive greetings from representatives of denominations
with whom we have fraternal or corresponding relations, as well as
organizations of which the PCA is a member.

- The IRC is aware that fraternal greetings sometimes take extended
time on the floor of the Assembly. The committee is seeking ways
of dealing with that effectively.

<a id="ga47-p390"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=392 printed_page=390 -->

- At this Assembly, due to our shortened schedule, the IRC has asked
fewer of our ecumenical guests (fraternal delegates, corresponding
delegates, and ecclesiastical observers) to address the Assembly.
Ecumenical guests will be introduced to the Assembly.

- The IRC sends representatives to the assemblies of other denominations
with whom we have fraternal or corresponding relations.

**Recommendations to the General Assembly**
1. That Fraternal Delegates, Corresponding Delegates, and Ecclesiastical

Observers be welcomed and that the Assembly hear their greetings.
2. That visiting ministers be introduced to the General Assembly ( _BCO_ 13-3).
3. That the minutes of October 4, 2018, be approved without exception.
4. That the minutes of April 16, 2019, be approved without exception.
5. That the Assembly **answer in the negative Overture 23** from Central

Carolina Presbytery, “Withdraw from the National Association of
Evangelicals.”
**Grounds** :

1) Overture 2019-23 repeats many of the same arguments of Overture

2011-12 presented to the Thirty-ninth General Assembly and adds a
few new arguments.
2) The Thirty-ninth General Assembly invited Sessions and Presbyteries

to comment on the overture before acting on Overture 20011-12 in
2012. Of the 1,455 Sessions and eighty-one Presbyteries at the time,
not a single session or pres byte ry submitted a comment.
3) The Fortieth General Assembly answered Overture 2011-12 in the

negative.
4) Overture 2019-23 seems to argue that _Westminster Confession of_

_Faith_ 31-4 and _Book of Church Order_ 3-3 prohibit the Church from
speaking out on issues dealing with faith and life that are either
directly addressed in the Scriptures or by good and necessary
consequence may be deduced from Scriptures ( _WCF_ -31-6). This is
an argument that was advanced in Overture 2011-12. There are
differences among us on the present application of the doctrine of
“the Spirituality of the Church.” Some of our forbearers used that
doctrine for the Southern Church not to speak as a body on the issue
of chattel slavery on the grounds that it was a political issue. Others
of previous generations argued that chattel slavery was a biblical and
moral issue on which the Church must speak. The same is true of
human trafficking (sexual, economic [slave labor], or child soldiers)
and other issues.

<a id="ga47-p391"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=393 printed_page=391 -->

5) The NAE does not “intermeddle” (“unduly interfere with the affairs

of others”) with the civil government; it bears Christian witness on
matters affecting the common good. The NAE’s presence in
Washington and other venues champions such concerns as the
defense of marriage as being between one man and one woman,
religious liberty in light of increasing threats, the liberty of evangelical
military chaplains freely to preach, teach, and practice the Gospel
and biblical truth, the liberty of evangelical campus ministries, not
only speaking out against abortion but actually reducing the number
of abortions in America, seeking to reduce international sexual
trafficking of women and children, promoting religious liberty in
areas where Christians are persecuted, imprisoned or enslaved.
Surely, such issues are not off limits for discussion or actions in PCA
church courts. Indeed they are matters to which our General Assembly
has spoken and, in some instances, petitioned governments.
6) The NAE does not claim “to speak for all its members” any more

than our General Assembly speaks for every single member of the
PCA. No resolution or action of the NAE represents the unanimous
opinion of all its member denominations or individual members any
more than the majority vote or every action of our General Assembly
represents the unanimous opinions of all of the commissioners to the
General Assembly, all of its churches, all of its officers, or all of its
members. The NAE, like the PCA, reflects a constituency not a
monolith.

7) Overture 2019-23 repeats the argument of Overture 2011-12 alleging

that the “NAE has frequently intermeddled in civil affairs, by
publicly pushing for action on the environment, immigration, and
has changed its original stance on the death penalty,” while also
stating that “the Scriptures clearly teach that Christians should be
stewards of God’s creation, care for sojourners and aliens, and
promote matters of civil justice, and members of the PCA may have
legitimate differences of opinion on the most effective political
policies to address these matters.”
a. All Christians should be stewards of God’s creation. The

position of the NAE on Creation Care is found in the revised
“For the Health of the Nation” document and is written in such
away to accommodate a range of opinions. [1]
b. All Christians are to care for sojourners and aliens. The position

of the NAE on immigration is found in the revised “For the

1 _For the Health of the Nation_ . 2018 ed., pp. 48-52.

<a id="ga47-p392"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=394 printed_page=392 -->

Health of the Nation” document and is written in such a way to
accommodate a range of opinions. [2]
8) Overture 2019-23 repeats the argument of Overture 2011-12 that

because the RPCNA withdrew from the NAE in 2009, the PCA
should follow suit. The RPCNA’s withdrawal from the NAE needs

to be understood in its context, as a motion made from the floor; it
was _not_ a studied motion from their Interchurch Relations
Committee. [3]

2 _For the Health of the Nation_, 2018 ed., pp. 32-36.
3 The RPCNA’s withdrawal from the NAE needs to be understood in its context.

- The RPCNA was a member of the NAE since 1949. Dr. Jack White of the RPCNA was
Secretary of the Board and Dr. D. Howard Elliott of the RPCNA was President for a
time.

- RPCNA concerns were expressed to the NAE Board Chairman that a statement on
Creation Care could be misunderstood as radical environmentalism. The Creation Care
portion of the Health of the Nation document was carefully crafted (with PCA
representative input) so that it would be a biblical and responsible statement and did not
endorse a radical position, either right or left. Moreover the NAE staff was instructed by
the board not to go beyond the NAE’s adopted position.

- The President (Leith Anderson) and Board Chairman (Roy Taylor) met with
representatives of the RPCNA at an NAE Board meeting in Orlando in March, 2008 and
discussed RPCNA concerns.

- The RPCNA delegation asked the advice of NAPARC regarding participation in the NAE
at the November, 2008 meeting. The PCA delegation (Craig Higgins and Roy Taylor)
discussed the matter with the RPCNA brothers.

- The RPCNA’s Interchurch Relations Committee did _not_ recommend to the General
Synod that the RPCNA withdraw from the NAE; rather they recommended that the
Synod discuss the matter. After fifteen minutes of discussion a motion from the floor
was made that the RPCNA withdraw. The motion secured a majority vote ( _Minutes of_
_the RPNCA General Synod,_ June 24, 2009, pp. 120-121). No grounds for the action were
specified in the Minutes.

- The RPCNA reported to NAPARC at the November 2009 meeting, “We wish to thank
the Council for the discussion held one year ago on membership in the National
Association of Evangelicals (NAE). We found it helpful but our committee was still not
persuaded one way or another, therefore we asked our Synod for a discussion of the
matter to see whether the delegates would give us a clear indication on whether to
continue our membership. Instead, a motion was offered from the floor calling for an end
to this membership and the motion passed” (RPCNA Report to NAPARC, Fall, 2009).

- _In August 26, 2010, over one year after the RPCNA General Synod’s action_, a member
of the RPCNA Interchurch Relations Committee informed the NAE President and Board
Chairman of the Synod’s 2009 action and mentioned NAE President Dr. Leith
Anderson’s participation ina conference of Christians, Jews, and Muslims held at Yale
University, (July 2008) and the document entitled “Loving God and Neighbor
Together” (November, 2007) that was the basis of the conference. The letter mentioned
other unspecified concerns. The letter concluded by saying “We are not closing the door
to future cooperation and/or membership with the NAE, but we pray that our action may

<a id="ga47-p393"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=395 printed_page=393 -->

9) Overture 2019-23 advances a new argument not found in Overture

2011-12, namely that “the term ‘evangelical’ has come to designate
political distinctives as much or more than theological commitments
and distinctives.” The extension of that argument would be that,
since the NAE has “Evangelical” in its name that the PCA should not
be associated with the term “evangelical.” Mainline denominations
that use the terms “Presbyterian” and “Reformed” that are to the
theological left of the PCA do not cause us to abandon those terms.
We gladly call ourselves Presbyterian in polity and Reformed in
theology. The theologically accepted definition of evangelicalism is
derived from David W. Bebbington’s 1989 classic study _Evangelicalism_
_in Modern Britain: A History from the 1730s to the 1980s_ . Bebbington
identifies four main qualities which are to be used in defining
evangelical convictions and attitudes with which most in the PCA
would agree:

- **Biblicism**, a particular regard for the [Bible](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bible) (e.g. all essential
spiritual truth is to be found in its pages)

- **Crucicentrism**, a focus on the [atoning](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Atonement_in_Christianity) work of [Christ](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jesus_Christ) on the

cross

- **[Conversionism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Religious_conversion)**, the belief that human beings need to be
converted

- **Activism**, the belief that the gospel needs to be expressed in
effort.

We should not abandon the use of the term evangelical any more
than we should abandon the terms Presbyterian and Reformed,
simply because some people misunderstand or misidentify them.
10) There is certainly a spectrum of political opinions on numerous

issues within the PCA and within the constituency of the NAE. Most
of the time the resolutions adopted by the NAE Board deal with
broad biblical principles that reflect an evangelical consensus (not
unanimity) applied to public life, and very rarely with specific bills

be a means that God will use to help to encourage the NAE to be faithful to its
commitment to ‘cooperate without compromise.’”

- On September 13, 2009, the President of the NAE responded to the RPCNA Interchurch
Committee. He stated that the Yale Conference of 2007 was not mentioned by the
RPCNA delegation in the meeting with them and the NAE President and Chairman in
2008. Dr. Anderson also mentioned that at the Yale Conference he had clearly given a
straightforward explanation of biblical evangelical doctrine on God and salvation only
through the Lord Jesus Christ. So clear was the NAE president’s explanation that a
Jewish rabbi, strongly objected.

<a id="ga47-p394"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=396 printed_page=394 -->

in Congress. The NAE does not claim “to speak for all its members”
any more than our General Assembly speaks for every member of
the PCA.

11) Overture 2019-23 makes a new argument not found in Overture

2011-12 that, since other denominations are not members of the
NAE, the PCA should withdraw. This argument is also a _non_
_sequitur_ that unless other theologically conservative denominations,
NAPARC denominations, mainline denominations, the Roman
Catholic Church, Eastern Orthodox Churches, and African-American
Churches have not joined the NAE that the PCA should leave the
NAE. Each denomination has its own ethos and reasons why it
chooses to join associations. Some would not agree to the NAE
Statement of Faith.
12) Overture 2019-23’s asserting, as a new argument, that the NAE “has

changed its original stance on the death penalty” could be understood
as the NAE’s going on record as opposing the death penalty in every
instance. _That interpretation is factually incorrect_ . The Washington
Post article referenced in the overture acknowledges that the NAE’s
2015 position is more nuanced than its 1973 position. But the 2015
resolution _did not_ say that there are no circumstances in which the
death penalty is appropriate. The article simply pointed out that with
the advances in DNA technology to prevent innocent persons from
being executed, the facts that African-American, Hispanic, and lowincome prisoners are more likely to be executed, and the
generational differences in perspectives make the death penalty a
more complex issue today than it was in 1973. Our confessional
position is that the state has the power of the sword to punish
evildoers, which may include capital punishment ( _WCF_ 23-1).
Neither the NAE 1973, nor 2015 resolutions are contrary to our
confessional position that the state has the power of the sword.
13) Overture 2019-23 gives as a new argument, namely, the assertion

that “the NAE has recently passed a motion entitled “Fairness for
All” which, in advocating for a political compromise regarding
sexual orientation, gender identity, and religious freedom, goes
beyond Chapter 31 of the _Westminster Confession of Faith_ .” That
assertion is, in certain senses, inaccurate and incomplete at best. In
communications with the author of the overture, the Stated Clerk
(who is also the chairman of the NAE Board) relied on memory,
which was incomplete, and was limited by the NAE Code of Ethics
to which he and all other Board members had agreed that he would
not disclose minutes until the minutes had been approved and that he

<a id="ga47-p395"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=397 printed_page=395 -->

would quote from approved minutes only with permission. The
Stated Clerk’s memory was that the board had not taken final action
because the issue is ongoing. His first memory was not totally
correct. The Board did act on a motion, but the issue is ongoing. In
October of 2018 the NAE Executive Committee and Board dealt

with the issue of religious liberty and civil rights. A board member
leaked information of unapproved, uncorrected minutes contrary to
the Code of Ethics. The NAE Board at its March 2019 meeting
approved the minutes of October 2018. The Stated Clerk has
secured, in accordance with the Code of Ethics to which he has
agreed, permission to quote from the approved October 2018
minutes. The approved minutes of October 2018 state, under the
heading of “Religious Liberty and Civil Rights,” **Motion** **(2018-11)**
to support principles calling on Congress to consider federal
legislation:

- We believe that God created human beings in his image as male

or female and that sexual relations be reserved for the marriage
of one man and one woman.

- We support long-standing civil rights laws and First Amendment

guarantees that protect free religious exercise.

- No one should face violence, harassment, or unjust discrimination

on the basis of sex, sexual orientation, or gender identity.

Several things should be noted:

- All three principles need to be taken as a unit, not one to the
exclusion of the other two.

- The principles advanced do not relate to any specific legislation
now under consideration.

- The NAE has not endorsed “Fairness for All” legislation, either
past (as in the so-called “Utah Compromise”) or proposed in the
US Congress. In fact, there is no Fairness for All legislation
pending in Congress.

- There is a difference of opinion on the best course(s) of action
among evangelical Christians and others concerned about the
relationship of religious liberty and the civil rights of people
whose views and practices we regard as unbiblical.

- There are at least five approaches to dealing with the issues of
religious liberty and civil rights: 1) presidential executive orders,
2) legislation, 3) political (electing sympathetic legislators,

<a id="ga47-p396"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=398 printed_page=396 -->

seeking to influence legislators’ decisions, passing laws, etc.), 4)
seeking to change public opinion, and 5) legal action (filing
lawsuits filing amicus curiae briefs, securing court rulings, etc.).
There are advantages and disadvantages to all five strategies.

- A legislative solution is one of several options; it is not the only
option. Including religious exemptions in legislation is a
practice that has been used to the advantage of evangelicals and
others in several key legislations previously.

- The NAE’s advocating for religious liberty is not contrary to the
PCA version of the _Westminster Confession of Faith_, Chapter
31, “Of Synods and Councils,” Chapter 23-3, “Of the Civil
Magistrate,” or the _Book of Church Order_, Chapter 3, “The
Nature and Extent of Church Power.”

- The NAE is a voluntary association of denominations,
congregations, institutions, and individuals; it is not a denomination
of the Church Universal, a synod ora Council.
14) In light of the increasing secularization of our culture and the

increasing threats to religious liberties, the PCA needs all the friend
and allies we can muster not only among Reformed Christians, but
also among evangelical Christians, and Apostles’-Creed-adhering
Christians in order to advance the Gospel, be salt and light to our
culture, and protect our religious liberties.

<a id="ga47-p397"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=399 printed_page=397 -->

#### **APPENDIX O** **REPORT OF THE** **COMMITTEE ON CONSTITUTIONAL BUSINESS** **TO THE FORTY-SEVENTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY** **OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA**

**I. Introduction**

The Committee on Constitutional Business (CCB) met once during the
46 [th] General Assembly in Atlanta, twice prior to the 47 [th] General
Assembly on February 28, 2019, by conference call, and on May 6, 2019,
in the PCA Administrative offices in Lawrenceville, GA.

Attendance at the June 13, 2018, meeting was as follows:

Teaching Elders Ruling Elders
Per Almquist, chairman – Present Mark Anderson III – Present
Josh Anderson – Absent Richard Dolan – Absent
Robert Browning – Present Tom Harley – Absent
Roger Collins (Alt) – Present Flynt Jones, secretary – Present
Chris Florence – Present John Ward Weiss (Alt) – Absent

TE Roger Collins was seated as a voting member.

Attendance at the February 28, 2019, meeting was as follows:

Teaching Elders Ruling Elders
Per Almquist, chairman – Present Mark Anderson III – Present
Josh Anderson – Present Tom Harley – Present
Chris Florence – Present Flynt Jones, secretary – Present
Larry Hoop – Present Fredric Marcinak (Alt) – Absent
Joel St. Clair II (Alt) – Present Edward Wright – Present

Attendance at the May 6, 2019, meeting was as follows:

Teaching Elders Ruling Elders
Per Almquist, chairman – Present Mark Anderson III – Present
Josh Anderson – Present Tom Harley – Present
Chris Florence – Present Flynt Jones, secretary – Present
Larry Hoop – Present Fredric Marcinak (Alt) – Present
Joel St. Clair II (Alt) – Present Edward Wright – Present

<a id="ga47-p398"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=400 printed_page=398 -->

**II. Advice on Overtures**

The Stated Clerk referred the following overtures to the Committee:

**A** . Overture 1 from Northwest Georgia Presbytery: “Revise _BCO_ 14-2

to Permit Additional Ruling Elder Representation at the General
Assembly”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 1 is **not in conflict** with other
parts of the Constitution. _Adopted by the CCB_

**B** . Overture 2 from Northwest Georgia Presbytery: “Encourage CTS

toward Growth and Release from Assembly Oversight”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 2 **creates conflict** with the
Constitution in that the passage of this Overture requires unspecified
subsequent independent actions to amend the _RAO_ and _Corporate_
_By-Laws_ .

Although the Overture does state what is required in the _RAO_ and
_Corporate By-Laws_ that needs to be passed, this Overture does not
contain any proposed contingent amendments for those sections.
_Adopted by the CCB_

**C** . Overture 3 from Northern New England Presbytery: “Amend _RAO_

10-3 to Promote the Exercise of Godly Prudence in Planning and
Leading Times of Corporate Prayer and Worship at General
Assembly”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 3 is **not in conflict** with other
parts of the Constitution. _Adopted by the CCB_

**D** . Overture 5 from Central Indiana Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 23-1 on

Procedures for Dissolving Various Pastoral Relations”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 5 is **not in conflict** with other
parts of the Constitution. CCB notes the following constitutional

consequences:

- It removes the explicit requirement for a congregational meeting
when the minister resigns.

- It removes the reference that the meeting is “called and
conducted in the same manner as the call of the pastor” ( _BCO_
20-3, 4) with requirements regarding who presides, how votes
are counted, the method of voting, etc. _Adopted by the CCB_

<a id="ga47-p399"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=401 printed_page=399 -->

**E** . Overture 6 from Pacific Northwest Presbytery: “Amend _RAO_ 11 and

13 to Disallow Memorials”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 6 is **not in conflict** with other
parts of the Constitution. _Adopted by the CCB_

**F** . Overture 7 from Pacific Northwest Presbytery (Revised): “Form an

Ad Interim Committee to Address Matters Related to Domestic
Abuse, Domestic Oppression, and Sexual Assault”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 7 **is in conflict** with other parts
of the Constitution. _RAO_ 9-3 requires ad interim committees be
established with “financing provided from outside the Administrative
Committee budget.” _Adopted by the CCB_

**G** . Overture 8 from Ohio Valley Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 14-1.10, 14
1.11, and 14-1.12 to Allow Non-ordained Persons to serve on
Committees and Boards”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overtures 8, 14, 19, 21, and 32 **are in**
**conflict** with _RAO_ 5-2, as the Overtures establish additional
requirements for the Chairman. It should also be noted that _RAO_ 421.c.6 does not explicitly mention non-ordained members.
Additionally, _RAO_ 4-5 would need to be changed editorially to
properly reference the newly numbered sections in _BCO_ 14-1 upon
passage and adoption of the Overtures. _Adopted by the CCB_

**H** . Overture 9 from Platte Valley Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 42-4, 43-2,

and 43-3 regarding Method and Deadlines of Filing Cases”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 9 is **not in conflict** with other
parts of the Constitution. While both Overtures 9 and 34 are not in
conflict with the Constitution, they are in conflict with each other
and only one can be adopted. _Adopted by the CCB_

**I** . Overture 12 from Northern New England Presbytery: “Amend _RAO_

8-4 regarding Floor Nominations”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 12 is **not in conflict** with other
parts of the Constitution. _Adopted by the CCB_

<a id="ga47-p400"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=402 printed_page=400 -->

**J** . Overture 14 from Southern New England Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_

14-1.10, 14-1.11, and 14-1.12 to Allow Non-ordained Persons to
serve on Committees and Boards” (Version 2)

In the opinion of the CCB, Overtures 8, 14, 19, 21, and 32 **are in**
**conflict** with _RAO_ 5-2 as the Overtures establish additional
requirements for the Chairman. It should also be noted that _RAO_ 421.c.6 does not explicitly mention non-ordained members.
Additionally, _RAO_ 4-5 would need to be changed editorially to
properly reference the newly numbered sections in _BCO_ 14-1 upon
passage and adoption of the Overtures. _Adopted by the CCB_

**K** . Overture 15 from West Hudson Presbytery: “Amend _RAO_ 11-5 to

Prevent Confusion and Possible Contradictory Actions on Overtures
by Multiple Committees of Commissioners”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overtures 15 and 18 **create an internal**
**conflict** in _RAO_ 11-5. The Overtures do not specify how to refer
overtures that require consideration by more than one Committee or
Agency. _Adopted by the CCB_

**L** . Overture 16 from Georgia Foothills Presbytery: “Amend the _RAO_ to

Provide for a Moderator-Elect and Vice-Moderator by Revision of
_RAO_ 1-4, Addition of _RAO_ 1-6, 1-7, and 1-8, and Revision of _RAO_
2-1, 2-3”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 16 is **not in conflict** with other
parts of the Constitution. _Adopted by the CCB_

**M** . Overture 17 from Houston Metro Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 32-8 to

Allow for Video Testimony by Witnesses”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 17 **is in conflict** with other parts
of the Constitution. _BCO_ 35-10 deals with the same subject matter
but is not amended under this proposed Overture. _BCO_ 32-13
requires that witnesses “be examined in the presence of the accused.”
_Adopted by the CCB_

**N** . Overture 18 from Houston Metro Presbytery: “Amend _RAO_ 11-5 to

Prevent Confusion and Possible Contradictory Actions on Overtures
by Multiple Committees of Commissioners”

<a id="ga47-p401"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=403 printed_page=401 -->

In the opinion of the CCB, Overtures 15 and 18 **create an internal**
**conflict** in _RAO_ 11-5. The Overtures do not specify how to refer
overtures that require consideration by more than one Committee or
Agency. _Adopted by the CCB_

**O** . Overture 19 from Rocky Mountain Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 14-1.10,

14-1.11, and 14-1.12 to Allow Non-ordained Persons to serve on
Committees and Boards”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overtures 8, 14, 19, 21, and 32 **are in**
**conflict** with _RAO_ 5-2 as the Overtures establish additional
requirements for the Chairman. It should also be noted that _RAO_ 421.c.6 does not explicitly mention non-ordained members.
Additionally, _RAO_ 4-5 would need to be changed editorially to
properly reference the newly numbered sections in _BCO_ 14-1 upon
passage and adoption of the Overtures. _Adopted by the CCB_

**P** . Overture 21 from Nashville Presbytery: “Amend _Book of Church_

_Order_ 14-1.10, 14-1.11, and 14-1.12” (Version 3)

In the opinion of the CCB, Overtures 8, 14, 19, 21, and 32 **are in**
**conflict** with _RAO_ 5-2 as the Overtures establish additional
requirements for the Chairman. It should also be noted that _RAO_ 421.c.6 does not explicitly mention non-ordained members.
Additionally, _RAO_ 4-5 would need to be changed editorially to
properly reference the newly numbered sections in _BCO_ 14-1 upon
passage and adoption of the Overtures. _Adopted by the CCB_

**Q** . Overture 29 from Metro New York Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 5-9.c,

5-9.i (1), 7-2,
9-3, 17-3, 24, and Add a Chapter 25 In Order to Allow Local
Sessions to Decide Whether Women Are Allowed to Serve as

Deacons”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 29 is **not in conflict** with other
parts of the Constitution. _Adopted by the CCB_

**R** . Overture 32 from Tennessee Valley Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 14
1.10, 14-1.11, and 14-1.12 to Allow Non-ordained Persons to Serve
on Committees and Boards” (Version 4)

<a id="ga47-p402"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=404 printed_page=402 -->

In the opinion of the CCB, Overtures 8, 14, 19, 21, and 32 **are in**
**conflict** with _RAO_ 5-2 as the Overtures establish additional
requirements for the Chairman. It should also be noted that _RAO_ 421.c.6 does not explicitly mention non-ordained members.
Additionally, _RAO_ 4-5 would need to be changed editorially to
properly reference the newly numbered sections in _BCO_ 14-1 upon
passage and adoption of the Overtures. _Adopted by the CCB_

**S** . Overture 33 from Tennessee Valley Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 57-5

to Add Vow Regarding Trinity”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 33 is **not in conflict** with other
parts of the Constitution. _Adopted by the CCB_

**T** . Overture 34 from Philadelphia Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 42-4, 43-2,

and 43-3 as well as _OMSJC_ 18.10.b to Clarify Filing Requirements
and Allow Electronic Filings”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 34 is **not in conflict** with other
parts of the Constitution. While both Overtures 9 and 34 are not in
conflict with the Constitution, they are in conflict with each other
and only one can be adopted. _Adopted by the CCB_

**U** . Overture 36 from Savannah River Presbytery: “Amend _BCO_ 40-4

and 40-5 to Clarify the Meaning of ‘Credible Report’”

In the opinion of the CCB, Overture 36 is **not in conflict** with other
parts of the Constitution. _Adopted by the CCB_

**V** . One overture was received after the deadline for submitting

overtures that require CCB review ( _RAO_ 11-6). The CCB took no
action on this overture.

**III. Advice to Stated Clerk**

The CCB met by conference call on February 28, 2019, to advise Stated
Clerk Dr. Roy Taylor on three issues. Per _BCO_ 8-2.a.1, “Such advice
shall be for information only and without binding authority.”

**A** . Is a member of the General Assembly’s Standing Judicial

Commission (SJC) eligible for concurrent service on the Board of a
General Assembly Agency? Yes. _Adopted by the CCB_

<a id="ga47-p403"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=405 printed_page=403 -->

Rationale: _RAO_ 4 identifies three types of bodies through which the
General Assembly conducts its “affairs and programs”: permanent
Committees, Agencies, and Special Committees. _BCO_ 15-4
specifically prohibits SJC members from serving on “ _permanent_
Committees,” a term that is distinct from Agencies ( _BCO_ 14-1.12,
_RAO_ 4-5, 4-6, 4-10, 4-11, 4-12, 4-13, 4-14, 4-16, 4-19, 4-20, 4-21)
or their Board ( _RAO_ 4-15). Although _RAO_ 4-3 states “The relationship
of the Agencies to the Assembly remains as a Committee . . .” (cf.
_BCO_ 15-1), it does not specify their relationship as being that of a
_permanent_ Committee; therefore, the prohibition does not apply to
serving on an Agency Board.

**B** . is a member of a general assembly permanent committee who has

served a full term, eligible for consecutive service on a _different_
permanent Committee or Agency Board without taking a sabbatical
year? No. _Adopted by the CCB_

Rationale: _BCO_ 14-1.12 states, “Persons who have served for a full
term… on _one of_ the Assembly’s permanent committees or agencies
shall not be eligible for re-election to _an_ Assembly committee until
one year has elapse. . . .” The inclusiveness of the first italicized
word, combined with the fact that the second is an indefinite article
suggests that membership on any Assembly Committee/Board
makes one ineligible for election to _any other_ Committee until a
sabbatical year has passed. This has been the long-standing
interpretation of the Assembly’s Nominating Committee. However,
exceptions may be permitted in Agency bylaws approved by the
Assembly.

**C** . Questions on the Effect of Presbytery Division upon Advice and

Consent
1. If a presbytery gives its “advice and consent” to proposed _BCO_

amendments prior to its division, is the newly erected presbytery
eligible to give its “advice and consent” on the same
amendments? Yes.
2. After reception into a new presbytery, must an individual

presbyter abstain from voting a second time on a proposed _BCO_
amendment? No. _Adopted by the CCB_

Rationale:
1. _BCO_ 26 does not limit advice and consent to Presbyteries in

existence when amendments are proposed. F.P. Ramsay appears

<a id="ga47-p404"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=406 printed_page=404 -->

to be of this opinion: Section 142 “The enacting Assembly may
be any Assembly after a _majority of the Presbyteries existing at_
_the time of the enactment_ have given their advice and consent.”
( _Exposition of the Book of Church Order_, 1898, p. 168). Of
course, the PCA requires a 2/3 majority, and also imposes a time
limit regarding the enacting Assembly.
2. There is no basis in the constitution to exclude the vote of a

presbyter who is a member in good standing of a pres byte ry at
the time a matter is voted on.

**IV. Non-Judicial References**

No non-judicial references were received.

**V. Advice to the Overtures Committee**

The CCB met on June 13, 2018, at the 46 [th] General Assembly to respond
to a request for advice from the Overtures Committee on the question of
the referral of overtures.

The Question:
Does the Overtures Committee have exclusive property over overtures
requesting amendment to the Constitution? Yes. _Adopted by the CCB_

Rationale: The phrase “other than” in _RAO_ 11-5 excludes “an overture
proposing amendment to the Constitution” from the set of Overtures to
be referred to permanent Committees and Agencies. The phrase “other
than” is used throughout the _BCO_ and _RAO_ as a term of exclusion.

**VI. Minutes of the Standing Judicial Commission**

The CCB examined the minutes of the Standing Judicial Commission
meetings on May 17, 2018; October 18, 2018; and February 7, 2019; and
also examined the minutes of the meetings of the SJC officers on June 29,
2018; July 12, 2018; August 14, 2018; August 30, 2018; October 5,
2018; and December 5, 2018. The minutes were found to be in order
without exception and notations were reported to the SJC.

_Adopted by the CCB_

**VI. Election of Officers for 2019-2020**

The following were elected as officers of the Committee for 2019-2020:

Chairman – TE Per Almquist
Secretary – TE Chris Florence

Submitted by:
TE Per Almquist, Chairman RE Flynt Jones, Secretary

<a id="ga47-p405"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=407 printed_page=405 -->

#### **APPENDIX P** **MINUTES OF THE NOMINATING COMMITTEE** **OF THE GENERAL ASSEMBLY OF THE** **PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA** **March 23, 2019**

The Nominating Committee of the General Assembly convened in Atlanta,
Georgia, at the Crowne Plaza Atlanta Airport on Saturday, March 23, 2019.
The Chairman, TE Joel St. Clair, called the meeting to order at 9:00 a.m. and
led the Committee in a devotional on Habakkuk 2. TE Paul Lee opened the
meeting in prayer.

A quorum was declared. A roll was distributed for attendance. The
Chairman welcomed the Committee and recognized two guests from the
PCA Administrative Committee Office –TE L. Roy Taylor, Stated Clerk, and
Ms. Angela Nantz, Operations Manager. Fifty-eight committee members
were in attendance as follows and 9 additional members submitted
preliminary ballots.

Preliminary vote tallies were discussed by the Committee. The Committee
approved a slate of nominees for each of the Standing Committees, Agencies,
and Commission to be presented to the General Assembly.

Nominations were entertained for Chairman and Secretary of the 2019-2020
Nominating Committee. The Committee elected TE Fred Greco, Houston
Metro Presbytery, to serve as Chairman and TE Matt Bradley, Nashville
Presbytery, as Secretary.

The Chairman announced that the next meeting of the Nominating
Committee will be at General Assembly in Dallas, TX, on Wednesday, June
26, 2019, at 5:00 p.m., after the conclusion of the Floor Nominations. The
2020 meeting will be on Saturday, March 28, 2020.

**MSP** that the Committee adjourn. Chairman St. Clair adjourned the meeting
at 12:05 p.m., and closed in prayer.

**D. Biographical Sketches:**

**TE Robert F. Brunson:** _Metro Atlanta_ . BA in Communications, University

of Alabama (1979); M.Div., Reformed Theological Seminary (1986). TE
Brunson is the Executive Pastor at Christ Church Presbyterian, Atlanta,
GA, and has 34 years of ministry experience. This experience includes
the following: moderator in four presbyteries; member of Christian
Education Committee (Grace and Warrior, chair); MNA Committee
(Warrior); Credentials Committee & RUM (Rocky Mountain);
Administrative Committee (MVP & Suncoast Florida); member of Ridge
Haven Board of Directors 10 years (1991-2001; President, 1994-2001);
member of Permanent Administrative Committee (2008-12; 2013-17;
alternate, 2018, chairman 2011/12; 2016/17).

**TE Todd Baucum** : _Southeast Alabama_ . D.Min. Trinity School for Ministry

(2004); Th.M., Puritan Reformed Theological Seminary (2019). TE
Baucum has pastored Fist Presbyterian in Enterprise, AL, since 2004. He
oversaw the remodeling of the church in 2010, initiated a missional
neighborhood outreach (VBS; tornado relief), and helped to build houses
for needy families with Habitat for Humanity. He moderated Southeast
Alabama Presbytery in 2015, and was chaplain to the Kansas House of
Representatives (Feb. 1993-April 1994), and to Memphis State University
(1988-1990). He has taught Systematic Theology, Church History,
Missions, and Apologetics at Birmingham Theological Seminary.

**RE Frank Cohee:** _Providence_ . Serves as an Elder at Cornerstone

Presbyterian, in Huntsville, AL, and served as the presbytery’s
moderator in 2017. He attended the Universities of Connecticut and

<a id="ga47-p409"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=411 printed_page=409 -->

Alabama (Huntsville). He is Chief Security Officer and Cyber Security
Architect for Internet of Things, specializing in cyber security
operations, product development, and secure technology solutions. A
former naval intelligence specialist, he worked with the Defense
Intelligence Agency, US Army, Joint Special Operations Command,
Missile Defense Agency, Department of Energy, and others. General
Assembly service includes: Nominating Committee (2016-present;
2018, chair), CoC for CDM (2017), AC (2016, 2018), and MTW (2019).

**RE James A. "Pat" Hodge:** _Calvary_ . BS in Economics, Mars Hill College.

Serves New Hope Presbyterian Church in Abbeville, SC. He has
management experience as funeral director at Harris Funeral Home
(2008-present), owner-operator at Hodge Frozen Food (1975-2008),
Milliken & Company (1972-75), and USAF (1968-72). He has served
twice on the Administration Committee, and on Calvary Presbytery’s
administration, MNA, MTW, & Shepherding committees. He has also
served in the following capacities: chairman of the board of Abbeville
First Bank; Abbeville Chamber of Commerce; Abbeville Habitat for
Humanity; Gideon’s International; Abbeville City Council.

**RE Richard Dolan:** _Georgia Foothills_ . BA Furman University; MDiv

Beeson Divinity School; PhD from Georgia State University. Clerk of
the Session at Restoration Presbyterian Church in Buford, GA, where he
also teaches Sunday School. He is a SharePoint Engineer at Jackson
Healthcare and is responsible for SharePoint strategy and implementation
along with other enterprise applications. He has worked as a youth pastor,
grant writer, and taught at Georgia State University, Johannes Gutenberg
University, and the _Deutsche Universitat fur Verwaltungswissenschaften_ .
He was Moderator of Georgia Foothills (2013), and has served on the
PCA’s RUF CoC (chairman), OC, NC, and RPR.

on the PCA Administrative Committee. Marty attends Chestnut
Mountain Presbyterian Church, where he serves on the session and

have two grown children and four granddaughters.

Springs in Coral Springs, Florida. Ryan has been involved in the PCA
for over 35 years and three of his four children graduated from Covenant

**RE Stephen E. Sligh:** _Southwest Florida._ Steve is a graduate of Covenant

College and the National College of Chiropractic in 1976 and has been
in private chiropractic practice for over 40 years. Steve is a chiropractic
physician in Lakeland, Florida, and he and his wife, Annette, attend
Covenant Presbyterian Church. They have two adult children who both
attended Covenant and six grandchildren.

**D. Biographical Sketches**

**Class of 2020**
**TE John Haralson** : _Pacific Northwest._ B.S. Engineering, U.S. Air Force

Academy; M.Div., Covenant Theological Seminary. Senior Pastor, Grace
Church Seattle, Seattle, Washington. Previously served as an Associate
Pastor at City Church, San Francisco, California and Redeemer Presbyterian
Church in New York City. Previously served as Presbytery Moderator,
as well as on various Presbytery committees and commissions, and on
the Northwest Church Planting Network. First joined Covenant’s Board
in 2010.

**Class of 2023**
**TE Hugh Barlett** : _Missouri_ . B.A. Wheaton College, M.Div and D.Min.

Covenant Theological Seminary, St. Louis, MO. Senior Pastor,
Chesterfield PC, Chesterfield, MO since April 1992. Previously served

<a id="ga47-p418"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=420 printed_page=418 -->

Covenant PC, as campus minister, ruling elder, Senior Pastor. In
Heartland Presbytery, serve dona number of committees: MNA,
Candidates, and the Care of Churches (chair). In Missouri Presbytery,
chaired and served on the MNA Committee and various commissions.
Adjunct professor at Covenant Seminary for 15 years, and a member of
the American Theological Schools accreditation team for the Seminary.
First elected to the Seminary Board in 2015.

**RE Bill Bennett** : _Evangel_ . B.A. Furman University, Greenville, SC;

M.B.A. Samford University, Birmingham, AL. Graduated from the
School of Mortgage Banking, has 28 years of experience and currently
serves as Senior Vice President of Cadence Bank. Member of Covenant

Presbyterian Church in Homewood, AL, since 1992 where he is a ruling
Elder, has served on various committees and chaired the Senior Pastor
Search Committee. Currently serves Evangel Presbytery on the
Administrative Committee and the Credentials Committee, and
previously served on the Church and Pastoral Care Committee. Also has
served on the MNA Committee of Commissioners. Joined the Covenant
Seminary Board in 2015.

**TE Jonathan Seda** : _Heritage._ B.A. Houghton College, Houghton, NY;

M.Div. Biblical Theological Seminary, Hatfield, PA; D.Min. Covenant
Theological Seminary, St. Louis, MO. Senior Pastor Grace Presbyterian
Church, Dover, DE, since 1983. Previously served on Heritage
Presbytery’s RUF Committee, the Presbytery Racial Reconciliation
Committee, the Board of Directors of ARISE (Delaware Community
Development Ministry) and the GA Study Committee on Racial
Reconciliation. Also served as a trustee for Chesapeake Theological
Seminary, Mosaix Global Network and Young Life Delaware. First
Elected to the Seminary Board in 2006, and has a daughter and son-inlaw who graduated from Covenant.

**RE Paul Stoll** : _Chicago Metro_ . B.S. Welding Engineering, LeTourneau

College, Longview, TX. Employed by Armin Tool and Manufacturing
Company since 1973 and has served as President since 1987. Has led the
company in a variety of manufacturing processes for products that we
take for granted today. A 40-year member of Westminster Presbyterian
Church, Elgin, Illinois, Paul is currently serving his sixth term on the
Session. First elected to Covenant’s Board in 2006.

<a id="ga47-p419"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=421 printed_page=419 -->

**RE Gif Thornton** : _Nashville Presbytery_ . B.S. Vanderbilt University,

Nashville, TN; J.D. Vanderbilt School of Law, Nashville, TN. Managing
Partner at Adams and Reese, LLP, Nashville, TN. Ruling Elder at Christ
Presbyterian Church, Nashville, Tennessee and active member of
Session’s Administrative Committee. Serves the Nashville as Chairman
on Judicial Business. Gil’s wife, Anna, served on the Advisory Board
of the Francis A. Schaeffer Institute of Covenant Theological Seminary.
Gil was first elected to the Covenant Seminary in June 2010.

**TE C.R. Wiley** : _Southern New England_ . B.A. Eastern Nazarene College;

M.A. Nazarene Theological Seminary; post graduate studies Gordon
Conwell Seminary, Harvard Divinity School. Associate Pastor, Cambridge
Church of the Nazarene. Senior Pastor, Presbyterian Church of Manchester,
Manchester, CT. One of the Founders of The Boston Education Collaborative,
Boston, MA. Writer, Modern Reformation, Touchstone, and First Things.
Instructor, Eastern Nazarene College.

**D. Biographical Sketches**

**TE Marvin Padgett:** _Nashville_ . BS, Aerospace Engineering, University of

Alabama. Serves as Director/CEO of Great Commission Publications.

Founding board chairman of Christ Presbyterian Academy. Has served
as RUM Coordinator (1996-1997), fifteen years on CEP/CDM, and
Covenant College Board for two terms. Served as PCA Fraternal
Delegate to OPC GA and URCNA General Synod. Served four terms as
Moderator of Presbytery. He and his wife Jean have 3 children and 11
grandchildren.

**TE Scott Reiber:** _Mississippi Valley._ BS, Community and Regional Planning;

M. Div., Reformed Theological Seminary. Pastor, Westminster
Presbyterian Church, Vicksburg, MS. Formerly pastor Providence
Presbyterian Church, Savannah, GA and Calvary Presbyterian Church,
Mize, MS. Former moderator of Presbytery of Mississippi Valley. Has
served on presbytery committees for credentials, shepherding,
MNA/church planting in Georgia and Mississippi. Served on GA
Committees of Commissioners for RPR, MNA, IRC, and on a Judicial
Commission.

**RE James D. Walters, Jr:** _Calvary._ Professional forester, retired from the

South Carolina Forestry Commission as Program Coordinator for Urban
and Community Forestry. Served presbytery on committees for
Examination (twice), MTW, Nominations (twice). Served two terms as
Moderator of Calvary Presbytery. Previously served on ICR (19962000), which included attending NAPARC meeting, serving on IRC
Commission to visit with CRC officials, and bringing greetings to ARP
General Synod (three times). Married with two daughters and five
grandchildren.

Lanier Community Church (PCA). Served as member and chairman of

planting internationally (Nicaragua).

**TE Robert A. Willetts:** _Tidewater_ . BS Civil Engineering, University of

Maryland, 1994; M. Div. Westminster Theological Seminary, 2002.
Pastor, Grace Presbyterian Church, Chesapeake, VA. Planted Grace
Presbyterian Church. Training as a church planter and Gospel Coach.

RUF, MTW) for Hills and Plains Presbytery and Vice-Chairman of
Southwest Church Planting Network. Coaches church planters. Married
with four children.

and Trust (Prattville, AL). Has served First Presbyterian Church of

Committee member and chairman (present). Served as Moderator of
Southeast Alabama Presbytery. Served on MNA Committee of South
Alabama Presbytery (2011 to present), including participation in the
Alabama Church Planting Network and pastoral search committee for a
church plant in Auburn, AL.

University, 1989; M.Div., Reformed Theological Seminary, 1992.

missionaries oversees. Chief Operating Officer, investment management
firm with experience in strategic planning and implementation,
personnel management, and financial management.

**DE Theodore J. Dankovich:** _Calvary_ . B.S. Business Accounting, Indiana

University. Since 2002, DE Dankovich has been the director of finance
& operations at Mitchell Road Presbyterian Church in Greenville, SC.
Previously he was CFO/Controller at UTI Inc. (2002), financial & business
cconsultant at Operations Associates (1998-2001), and regional controller
IKON Office Solutions (1994-1998). He has served on the PCA-RBI
board in the following capacities: director of class 2019 (partial term);

to this, he served at Trinity Presbyterian in Charlottesville, VA (201116), Grace Presbyterian in Palo Alto, CA (2000-11), and Redeemer
Presbyterian, NY, NY (1995-2000). He has been a church planter, senior
pastor, executive pastor, and been involved in missions. He was on the
board of CCEF (chair), and has served in numerous roles at the
presbytery and General Assembly levels.

**RE Bruce W. Jenkins:** _Rocky Mountain_ . BS, Business Administration,

Drake University (1973). RE Jenkins serves at Grace Church
Presbyterian, Fort Collins, CO. He is a mortgage consultant with Excel
Financial Group, LLC (2011-present). Prior to this he was principal
consultant at Hewitt Associates (2007-10), and director & consulting
actuary at Buck Consultants (2000-2007). He also serves on the board of
directors of Restoration Now. He served a partial term on the PCA-RBI’s
class of 2015, and currently serves the PCA-RBI’s class of 2019. While
serving on the PCA-RBI, RE Jenkins has served on committees dealing
with both audit and investment.

**D. Biographical Sketches**

**TE Robert E. Hamby Jr.:** _North Texas._ BS, Business Administration from

The Citadel, MDiv from Covenant Theological Seminary (2000).
Currently, Church Planter/Pastor for Mercy Presbyterian church in
Dallas, Texas. Former Acting CFO and Executive Vice-President, Focus
on the Family, Previously served as Chairman of the Nominations
Committee, Member of the MNA Committee and the Theological
Examinations Committee, Campus Ministries Committee. Was Campus
Minister (RUF) at Texas Christian University (2006-11) and was first
Campus Minister at Furman (2000-06). Married to Lauren since 1995.
Has son and daughter who is involved with RUF at Ole Miss.

**TE Josh Martin:** _Calvary_ . BS, USC-Spartanburg; MDiv, GPTS. Senior

Pastor, Palmetto Hills PCA in Simpsonville, SC. Previously served as
RUF-CM at Delta State and LSU and asst pastor at South Baton Rouge
PCA. Previously served as Moderator, RUF, MNA, and Credentials
committee chairman of Southern Louisiana Presbytery, RUF Summer
Conference Coordinator, and Campus Minister Representative to the
RUF Permanent Committee.

<a id="ga47-p431"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=433 printed_page=431 -->

**TE David Osborne:** _Eastern Carolina._ Ordained August 2003, Senior

Pastor of Emmanuel Presbyterian September 2001-07; RUF Committee
Chairman of Western Carolina Presbytery 2004-07; Exam Committee of
Western Carolina Presbytery 2007-12. Various Commissions of Western
Carolina Presbytery. Study Committee on NPP. Administrative Committee
of Western Carolina Presbytery. RUF Campus Minister 2008-12. Pastor
of Christ PC, 2013-Present. Admin Committee of Eastern Carolina
Presbytery 2014-17; Exam Committee of Eastern Carolina Presbytery
2017-Present. RUF Permanent Committee 2013-2018, including service
on Executive Committee. Chairman of Affiliations Sub-Committee of

RUF Permanent Committee.

**RE Charles Powell:** _Evangel._ RUF (Alabama) under Billy Joseph. Married

– Emily, 23 years; daughters (2): sophomore at Alabama and high school
senior to attend Alabama. Owner commercial real-estate brokerage and
investment company. RUF-involvement - 11 years, chairing state committee,
serving sub-committees to hire campus ministers (Samford, BSC, Alabama,
UAB, JSU). Briarwood member (1994) - Sunday School teacher, former
men's small-group and Sunday School leader - 2 years with 62 families. As
Elder- Discipline Committee (9 years) chairing twice; Nominations
Committee; Vice Chairman - "Special Committee" reviewing all church
ministries, policies, and relationship to school. Class Shepherd - Sunday
School.

**RE David Hall:** _Hills and Plains_ **.** MD Vanderbilt University, Nashville, TN.

works as a physician in Tulsa, OK. Introduced to Reformed theology
while at Vanderbilt RUF during medical school. Completed medical
residency in Dallas and continued to serve in connection to RUF and
Park Cities PC. When called to work as a physician in Tulsa, OK, worked
to launch a new PCA church and subsequently to support/ develop campuses
across the state of Oklahoma. Daughter is part of the RUF ministry. Also
serves on the Candidates and Credentials Committee at Presbytery.

#### **SUPPLEMENTAL REPORT OF THE** **NOMINATING COMMITTEE** **TO THE FORTY-SIXTH GENERAL ASSEMBLY** **OF THE PRESBYTERIAN CHURCH IN AMERICA**

The Nominating Committee of the General Assembly convened in Dallas,
Texas, at the Hilton Anatole Hotel, on Wednesday, June 26, 2019. Chairman
TE Joel St. Clair called the meeting to order at 5:45 p.m. with prayer.

**TE Robert F. Brunson:** _Metro Atlanta_ . BA in Communications, University

of Alabama (1979); M.Div., Reformed Theological Seminary (1986). TE
Brunson is the Executive Pastor at Christ Church Presbyterian, Atlanta,
GA, and has 34 years of ministry experience. This experience includes
the following: moderator in four presbyteries; member of Christian
Education Committee (Grace and Warrior, chair); MNA Committee
(Warrior); Credentials Committee & RUM (Rocky Mountain); Administrative
Committee (MVP & Suncoast Florida); member of Ridge Haven Board
of Directors 10 years (1991-2001; President, 1994-2001); member of
Permanent Administrative Committee (2008-12; 2013-17; alternate,
2018, chairman 2011/12; 2016/17).

**TE Randy Lovelace:** _Chesapeake_ . Covenant Seminary (1997). Assistant

pastor in Cape Coral, Florida (1997-2001), church planter Redeemer
Montclair, Montclair New Jersey (2001-2011), Sr. Pastor Columbia
Presbyterian Church (2011-Present). Chair of Metro New York Presbytery
Admin. Committee (2007-2011). Member of the Administrative Committee
(2008-2010).

**Alternate – Teaching Elder**

**Nominating Committee Nominee** **Floor Nominee**
TE Todd Baucum, Southeast Alabama TE Rod Mays, Calvary

<a id="ga47-p438"></a>

**TE Todd Baucum** : _Southeast Alabama_ . D.Min. Trinity School for Ministry

(2004); Th.M., Puritan Reformed Theological Seminary (2019). TE
Baucum has pastored First Presbyterian in Enterprise, Alabama, since
2004. He oversaw the remodeling of the church in 2010, initiated a
missional neighborhood outreach (VBS; tornado relief), and helped to
build houses for needy families with Habitat for Humanity. He moderated
Southeast Alabama Presbytery in 2015 and was chaplain to the Kansas ·
House of Representatives (Feb. 1993-April 1994), and to Memphis State
University (1988-1990). He has taught Systematic Theology, Church
History, Missions, and Apologetics at Birmingham Theological Seminary.

**TE Rod Mays:** _Calvary_ . Served as TE since 1977. Pastored churches in WV,

MS, and SC. National Coordinator for RUF for 17 years. Has served the
Permanent Committees of CDM and AC. Served as Executive Pastor,
Mitchell Road PC, Greenville, SC while also directing the Greenville
Fellows Program. Has held positions as Adjunct Professor of Practical
Theology at Reformed Theological Seminary and Westminster
Theological Seminary. Served Calvary Presbytery on Examinations and
Shepherding Committees and as Moderator. Presently serves on the AC
transition committee for a new Stated Clerk in 2020

**RE Dan Barber** . _Central Indiana_ . B.S. Psychology, Georgia College

(2011). Currently Product Specialist and Technology Evangelist for

in Biblical Theology,” P&R, 2012.

currently serves as the Head of School at Kirk of the Hills (PCA) and is
the President of the Christian School Association of St. Louis. He is also

the former Assistant Head of School at The Covenant School in
Nashville, TN, which is a PCA Ministry School. He is passionate about
students learning that all truth points back to the Creator and that they are
loved.

on Theology (2017).

at Twin Oaks Presbyterian Church, St. Louis, Missouri. Prior to that
time he served on the General Assembly Ad Interim Study Committee
on Divorce and Remarriage. After his time in the Marine Corp (19721974), he graduated from the University of Missouri in 1977 with a B.A.
in History and went on to receive his J.D. from Washington University
in 1981. Terry has served various family related ministries to include:
Fresh Start Seminars, Inc., Crossroads Christian Counseling and Westminster
Biblical Missions.

Mission to Iowa Committee Chairman. GA Service: Interchurch

Relations Committee of Commissioners, Administrative Committee of

of Commissioners (Convener & Moderator), Overtures Committee of

(multiple years).
