_Commentaries on the Four Last Books of Moses_, Vol. 4, 406
(Grand Rapids: Baker, 1979), on Deut. 34:6: “So Moses the
servant of the Lord died there in the land of Moab, according
to the word of the Lord, and he buried him in the valley in the
land of Moab opposite Beth-peor; but no one knows the place
of his burial to this day.” (ESV)

_Adopted by Pacific Northwest Presbytery at its stated meeting, January 25, 2019_
_Attested by /s/ RE Brett Chase, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 7 from Pacific Northwest Presbytery (to CCB, OC, AC)

“Form an Ad Interim Committee to Address Matters Related to
Domestic Abuse, Domestic Oppression, and Sexual Assault”

**Be it resolved:**

1. That the 47th General Assembly create a seven-man Study Committee

on Domestic Abuse and Sexual Assault tasked to accomplish the
following.
a. The Committee shall prepare an annotated bibliography of resources

the Committee endorses on topics related to child abuse and sexual
assault, domestic abuse and sexual assault, and domestic oppression. [1]
The annotated bibliography should also include pastoral resources
for the care of victims of these sins, as well as ministry and counsel
for those overtaken by these sins.
b. The Committee shall report regarding best practices and guidelines

that could be helpful for elders, Sessions, Presbyteries, and agencies

1 Rev. Warren Lamb (Th.M., MS, MA) Vancouver Bible Institute & Truth in Love Biblical

Counseling Center
"Domestic oppression is an ongoing pattern of intimidating and domineering behavior

employed by one family member to control one or more other family members. A
superior/inferior, hierarchical power differential is established, maintained, and increased
more and more as time goes on...."
"Domestic oppressors systematically tyrannize, intimidate, threaten, emotionally coerce,

dehumanize, objectify, demean, degrade, manipulate, and bully at least one other person
within the family home - usually their spouse."
[https://pastorlamb.com/2018/05/08/domestic-oppression-time-to-talk-about-i](https://pastorlamb.com/2018/05/08/domestic-oppression-time-to-talk-about-i)
[https://www.vancouverbible.com/our-staff.html](https://www.vancouverbible.com/our-staff.html) &
[https://www.tilbcc.com/biblical-counseling-team1.html](https://www.tilbcc.com/biblical-counseling-team1.html)

<a id="ga47-p597"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=599 printed_page=597 -->

for protecting against these sins and for responding to them.
However, no practice, policy, or guideline will be proposed for
adoption or approval. It is simply information, which shall not be
binding or obligatory in any sense.
c. The Committee may recommend to the 48th GA (Birmingham 2020)

any statement(s) it believes would be prudent and warranted for the
Assembly to adopt.
d. The Committee may recommend to the 48th GA any revisions or

additions to _BCO_ Rules of Discipline that might help prevent these
sins, protect and care for victims, procure justice, and lead offenders
to repentance. Reporting procedure is governed by _RAO_ 12-1.

_RAO_ 12-1: ...All recommendations [from permanent
Committees and Agencies, special committees, and ad
interim committees of the GA] proposing amendment to the
constitution shall be referred to the Overtures Committee

for their review and recommendation to the General

Assembly under the rules governing a committee of
commissioners as applicable ( _RAO_ 14-6.d.-k.; 14-7.c.-d.;
14-9.d.-h.) [Underlining added. See also _RAO_ 15-1.]

Any proposed constitutional amendments shall also be referred to
the Committee on Constitutional Business for advice. ( _RAO_ 8-2.b.3
& b.4)

2. The 47th GA authorizes the GA Moderator to appoint the seven voting

members of this Committee, per _RAO_ 9-4 and _RAO_ 19-1 (Robert's Rules,
RONR, 11th ed., pp. 174-175, pp. 495-496, and p. 579).
a. These members shall be PCA ministers or PCA ruling elders, and

the Committee shall include at least two of each. (Anyone may
suggest names to the Moderator.)
b. The 47th GA authorizes the Moderator to make his appointments

after the 47th GA adjourns.
3. The 47th GA authorizes the Moderator to appoint others to serve the

Committee as non-voting advisors, regardless of denominational
affiliation or gender. The 47th GA recommends the Moderator consider
appointing, as advisors, at least two subject-matter experts and at least

two women.
4. Committee budget shall be $15,000. This expense shall be included in

the Administrative Committee budget. Presbyteries, churches, and
individuals are highly encouraged to contribute funds to the AC,

<a id="ga47-p598"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=600 printed_page=598 -->

designated for this work. (The Pacific NW Presbytery has approved
$1,000 to that end.)

**Rationale for the Need** :

A. The sins of domestic abuse and sexual assault are expressly forbidden by

Scripture and the Westminster Standards. (For example, see 2 Timothy
3:2-5; Jude 7; Exodus 21:15; Deuteronomy 22:25-27; _WLC_ 135 and 139.)
B. These sins are especially heinous as they are “against the express letter

of the law,” “break many commandments,” “admit of no reparation,” and
often involve various other aggravations ( _WLC_ 151).
C. Sadly, these sins are prevalent in our society [2], and, to some extent, even

appear in the Church. [3]

D. These sins often produce in the victims a complex response of denial

along with immense fear and shame that persist with varying severity
throughout life (e.g., 2 Samuel 13:1-22).
E. It is the responsibility of the elders of the Church to exercise their

authority, severally and jointly, to shepherd those who are under their
care by guarding against such sinful behavior as much as possible, by
wisely caring for those victimized by the sins of others, and by
appropriately disciplining those guilty of such sins, with an aim toward
a full repentance.
F In 2014, the 42nd General Assembly (Houston, TX) exhorted the PCA
and its courts to respond boldly against one form of these sins, namely
the sexual abuse of children. ( _M42GA_, pp. 58-60) The OC, by a vote of
89-0, recommended the Houston GA adopt a revised version of Georgia
Foothills Overture 6, and the GA adopted it. Twenty-three other
Presbyteries had filed similar child-protection overtures, and all were
answered by reference to the action on Overture 6.)

2 For example, the National Domestic Violence Hotline reports: “More than 1 in 3 women

(35.6%) and more than 1 in 4 men (28.5%) in the United States have experienced rape,
physical violence and/or stalking by an intimate partner in their lifetime.” (Available at
www.thehotline.org/resources/statistics/, accessed 6/15/18). For further statistics on sexual
violence see the National Center for Injury Prevention and Controls Report titled _Sexual_
_Violence Surveillance: Uniform Definitions and Recommended Data Elements_ (Atlanta: CDC,

2014; available at [www.cdc.gov/violenceprevention/pdf/sv_surveillance_definitionsl](http://www.cdc.gov/violenceprevention/pdf/sv_surveillance_definitionsl-2009-a.pdf)
[2009-a.pdf](http://www.cdc.gov/violenceprevention/pdf/sv_surveillance_definitionsl-2009-a.pdf) ).
3 [https://www.houstonchronicle.com/news/investigations/article/Southern-Baptist-sexual-](https://www.houstonchronicle.com/news/investigations/article/Southern-Baptist-sexual-abuse-spreads-as-leaders-13588038.php)
[abuse-spreads-as-leaders-13588038.php](https://www.houstonchronicle.com/news/investigations/article/Southern-Baptist-sexual-abuse-spreads-as-leaders-13588038.php)

<a id="ga47-p599"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=601 printed_page=599 -->

G Many PCA ministers, ruling elders, and candidates report a desire to be

resourced in order to provide better pastoral care on these sins and their
resulting complex consequences.
H. Many PCA ministers, ruling elders, and candidates report a desire to

learn of "best practices" in these matters. For example, some ministers
and elders are unaware of the different levels of background checks that
can be utilized in screening. Some do not know what their State's laws
are regarding mandatory reporters, or in what situations those laws may
or may not apply.
I. Our _BCO_ Part II Rules of Discipline might benefit from additions or
revisions that address these matters. The following _BCO_ provisions are
examples of some that _might_ benefit from review and revision (at least,
for example, in the context of a minor who accuses an adult of abuse or
sexual misconduct):

_BCO_ 32-20. Process, in case of scandal, shall commence within
the space of one year after the offense was committed, unless
it has recently become flagrant.

_BCO_ 32-13. In order that the trial may be fair and impartial, the
witnesses shall be examined in the presence of the accused,
...Witnesses may be cross-examined by both parties,...

_BCO_ 35-4. no witness afterwards to be examined, unless a
member of the court, shall be present during the examination
of another witness on the same case, if either party object.

**Review of** _**RAO**_ **Procedures on Proposed Constitutional Amendments**

While ad interim and study committees ordinarily report recommendations
directly to the GA floor, if they propose constitutional amendments, _RAO_ 12-1
requires them to be referred to the OC. But in such instances, the OC would
act in many regards like a committee of commissioners.

_RAO_ 12-1. The permanent Committees and Agencies, special
committees, and ad interim committees of the Assembly shall make
annual reports, which shall be transmitted to the Stated Clerk by at
least ninety (90) days prior to the opening of the General Assembly.
The Stated Clerk shall refer these reports to the relevant committee
of commissioners for their review and recommendation to the
General Assembly (cf. 14-6; 14-7). The Nominating Committee,
the Committee on Review of Presbytery Records, the Standing
Judicial Commission, the Committee on Constitutional Business,

<a id="ga47-p600"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=602 printed_page=600 -->

and ad interim committees shall report directly to the General
Assembly without reference to a committee of commissioners.
However, all recommendations proposing amendment to the
Constitution shall be referred to the Overtures Committee for their

review and recommendation to the General Assembly under the rules
governing a committee of commissioners _as applicable_ ( _RAO_ 14-6.d-k;
14-7.c-d; 14-9.d-h). [Emphasis added. See also _RAO_ 15-1. [4] ]

The OC would function under certain _RAO_ 14 rules, instead of entirely under
_RAO_ 15 rules (i.e., the fifteen paragraphs from _RAO_ 14 cited in the
parenthesis). Thus, if any of those fifteen provisions stipulate something
different than what is stipulated in _RAO_ Chapter 15 (Overtures Committee),
the _RAO_ 14 (Committee of Commissioners) provision governs. One
difference, for example, regards the allowance or disallowance of an OC
minority report.

_RAO_ 15-6.s allows minority reports from the OC when the OC is acting in
its ordinary _RAO_ 15 capacity. But _RAO_ 14-6.h prohibits minority reports
from committees of commissioners. And since _RAO_ 14-6.h is included in
the parenthetical list in _RAO_ 12-1, that prohibition would govern the OC
when it reports a recommendation on a constitutional amendment proposed
by any study committee.

Presumably, this is because a substitute motion from a committee of
Commissioners is like a minority report when it proposes something different
from what is proposed by a Permanent Committee. Likewise, a substitute
recommendation from the oc would beak into a minority report on a GA appointed study committee recommendation.

in the event there was a minority report from a study committee on a
proposed constitutional amendment, the OC would also consider it. The OC
would report recommendations on each to the GA.

and if the oc recommends a substitute fora constitutional amendment

proposed by the study committee, the procedures and time limits for debate
are the same as those for handling an OC minority report on an ordinary OC
recommendation (i.e., _RAO_ 14.9.h.1-3 and _RAO_ 15-8.g.1-3).

4 _RAO_ 15-1 Overtures Committee shall consider and make recommendation upon all overtures

or recommendations (cf. _RAO_ 12-1) proposing amendment to the Constitution, and all other
overtures referred by the Stated Clerk.

<a id="ga47-p601"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=603 printed_page=601 -->

_RAO_ 14.9.h. if a committee of commissioners [OC in this case]
offers a substitute fora recommendation of a permanent
Committee or Agency [or Study Committee in this case], the
substitute shall be presented to the Assembly as follows ...

Regardless of whether the OC recommended approval or a substitute, _RAO_
14-9.e would apply to the debate on the GA floor. (The same rule applied to
recommendations that ordinariliy come from OC, that is, _RAO_ 15-8.c.)

_RAO_ 14-9.e. A recommendation [from a committee of commissioners]
shall be considered under the standard rules governing debate, but
Subsidiary Motions ( _RRO_ VI) to _Postpone Indefinitely,_ to _Amend,_
to _Commit_, and to _Limit Debate;_ and Incidental Motions ( _RRO_ VIII)
to _Divide a Question,_ to _Consider Seriatim,_ and _Constitutional_
_Inquiries,_ shall not be permitted. A motion to _Recommit_ shall be
permitted, but a motion to _Recommit with Instructions_ shall not be
permitted. (Italics original)

_Adopted by Pacific NW Presbytery at its stated meeting on January 25, 2019_
_Revised by the Overture Revision Commission of Pacific NW Presbytery on_
_April 17, 2019_
_Attested by /s/ RE Brett Chase, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 8 from Ohio Valley Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 14-1.10, 14-1.11, and 14-1.12 to Allow Non-ordained
Persons to serve on Committees and Boards”

**Whereas**, the Presbyterian Church in America believes Christ has called his

church to be a “spiritual commonwealth” ( _BCO_ 3-1), in which members
elect godly men to be ordained as elders who are given the power of
jurisdiction as they act jointly in the courts of the church ( _BCO_ 3-2); and

**Whereas**, a robustly complementarian perspective affirms the oversight of

these elders and the proper, vital, and full expression of the gifts given
by Christ to men and women for his service; and

**Whereas**, vigorous participation of women in the life of the early church is

attested by the New Testament: they helped select (Acts 1:14) and train
(Acts 18:26) leaders of the church, prophesied (Acts 21:8-9), served the
church through deeds of mercy in vital ways (Acts 9:36–41), were
instrumental in the planting of churches (Acts 16:12–15; 17:4,12) and

<a id="ga47-p602"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=604 printed_page=602 -->

other missionary endeavors (Acts 18:1f), and were acknowledged as
important leaders for the church (Rom. 16:3-4); and

**Whereas**, as part of the calling of church leaders to “equip the saints for the

work of ministry for building up the body of Christ” (Eph. 4:12), sessions
throughout the PCA identify gifted men and women to serve on
committees of the church, such as missions, building, and pastoral search
committees; and

**Whereas**, in contrast with a commission in the PCA, which is "authorized to

deliberate and conclude the business referred to it," committees are
appointed by courts “to examine, consider and report” to their
constituting court concerning the matters that court delegates to them
( _BCO_ 15-1) and thus do not exercise governing authority; and

**Whereas**, in accordance with this principle, Permanent Committees

established by the General Assembly are “to serve and not to direct any
Church judicatories. They are not to establish policy, but rather execute
policy established by the General Assembly” ( _BCO_ 14-1.7); and

**Whereas**, these considerations suggest there is no impediment to an

unordained member in good standing in the PCA serving on a Permanent
Committee of the General Assembly established in _BCO_ 14-1.12; and

**Whereas**, the 45th General Assembly adopted the recommendation of The

Ad Interim Committee on Women Serving in the Ministry of the Church
that stated, “sessions, presbyteries and the General Assembly strive to
develop, recognize, and utilize the gifts, skills, knowledge, and wisdom
of godly women in the local, regional, and national church, and
particularly consider overtures that would allow qualified women to
serve on appropriate committees and agencies within the church”;

**Therefore be it resolved** to amend _The Book of Church Order_ by deleting

14-1.10; by amending 14-1.11 as follows (and renumbering as 14-1.10);
by adding a new 14-1.11; and by amending 14-1.12 as follows:

[Proposed additions underlined and deletions noted by strike out.]

_**BCO**_ **14-1**

10. ~~The committees are to be established on the basis of an~~

~~equal number between teaching and ruling elders.~~

~~11.~~ 10. A Nominating Committee shall be comprised of
one representative elected by each Presbytery in the
following manner. Each Presbytery shall be assigned to a
class by the stated clerk based on its date of formation. The
members shall serve in classes of three year terms,

<a id="ga47-p603"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=605 printed_page=603 -->

alternating between ruling and teaching elders. When
necessary, unexpired terms shall be filled by an elder of the
same class, teaching or ruling.
This committee is to present all nominations for which it is
responsible to the next meeting of the Assembly from a
slate of ~~men~~ candidates nominated by the Presbyteries.
Presbyteries shall utilize the nominating forms provided by
the stated clerk for their nominations. Each presbytery may
present one teaching elder, ~~and~~ one ruling elder, and, when
applicable, one candidate who is not an elder to fill
openings that are designated for members at large for each

committee or agency. Any non ordained person
nominated must meet the qualifications outlined in _BCO_
14-1.11.

In addition to nominees for expired terms, the
Committee shall nominate for each permanent Committee
one ruling, ~~and~~ one teaching elder and a member-at-large
candidate as alternates to fill any vacancies that may occur
during the year. Each alternate should attend each meeting
and fill any vacancy necessary to meet a quorum. In
addition to the new nominees from the Presbyteries,
alternates not assuming any vacancies during a year will be
automatically considered by the Nominating Committee as
candidates for nomination to that same committee.

11. To be qualified for nomination and election to serve on a

Permanent Committee or Agency as a member at large, a
non-ordained candidate must be a member in good
standing of a Church in the Presbyterian Church in
America and must receive an endorsement from their

Session. The endorsement will consist of testimony
regarding the candidate’s Christian character, knowledge of
the Constitution, and promise of faithful service to the
General Assembly.

12. The Assembly permanent committees are the
Administrative Committee of the General Assembly,
Committee on Discipleship Ministries, Committee on
Mission to North America, Committee on Mission to the
World, and Committee on Reformed University
Fellowship.

<a id="ga47-p604"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=606 printed_page=604 -->

The Administrative Committee of the General

Assembly (AC) shall consist of twenty (20) members:
a. Eleven members, six elders and five members who are

not elders, shall be in classes elected through the
standard nomination and election procedure,
b. One member from each of the following program

committees or agencies:

1. Committee on Discipleship Ministries (CDM)
2. Covenant College (CC)
3. Covenant Theological Seminary (CTS)
4. Mission to North America (MNA)
5. Mission to the World (MTW)
6. PCA Foundation (PCAF)
7. PCA Retirement & Benefits, Inc. (RBI)
8. Reformed University Fellowship (RUF)
9. Ridge Haven Conference Center (RH)
The eleven elected members ~~at large~~ shall serve a
term of four years. The chairman of the
Administrative Committee shall be one of its elected

ordained members ~~at large.~~
Each program committee and agency shall
designate its member each year at the last meeting of
the committee or board before the meeting of the
General Assembly. The chief administrative officers
of the program committees and agencies may attend
any meeting of the Administrative Committee. They
shall be entitled to the privilege of the floor but shall
not have a vote and must be excluded when an

executive session is called.

Committee on Discipleship Ministries, Committee
on Mission to North America, Committee on Mission
to the World, and Committee on Reformed University
Fellowship shall consist of fifteen (15) ~~men~~ members
divided into five classes of three ~~men~~ members ~~each,~~
~~with two men Being TEs and one RE or two men being~~
~~REs and one TE on alternate years.~~ Three classes will
have one TE, one RE and one member at large. One
class shall have one TE and two members at large and
one class shall have one RE and two members at

large. Each class shall be elected to serve five-year
terms. Committee on Discipleship Ministries, Committee

<a id="ga47-p605"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=607 printed_page=605 -->

on Mission to North America, Committee on Mission
to the World, and Committee on Reformed University
Fellowship shall have one ruling elder, and one
teaching elder and one member at large as alternates to
fill any vacancy that may occur during the year. A
quorum for each Committees shall be eight (8) members,
with a majority of those present being elders. The
Chairman of each Committee shall be an elder.

Persons who have served for a full term, or for at
least two years of a partial term, on one of the
Assembly’s permanent committees or agencies shall
not be eligible for re-election to an Assembly
committee until one year has lapsed. (Exceptions may
be permitted in agency bylaws approved by the
Assembly).

So that _BCO_ 14-1.10, 14-1.11, and 14-1.12 as amended would read:

10. A Nominating Committee shall be comprised of one

representative elected by each Presbytery in the following
manner. Each Presbytery shall be assigned to a class by the
stated clerk based on its date of formation. The members

shall serve in classes of three year terms, alternating
between ruling and teaching elders. When necessary,
unexpired terms shall be filled by an elder of the same
class, teaching or ruling.
This committee is to present all nominations for which
it is responsible to the next meeting of the Assembly from
a slate of candidates nominated by the Presbyteries.
Presbyteries shall utilize the nominating forms provided by
the stated clerk for their nominations. Each presbytery may
present one teaching elder, one ruling elder, and, when
applicable, one candidate who is not an elder to fill
openings that are designated for members at large for each
committee or agency. Any non-ordained person nominated
must meet the qualifications outlined in _BCO_ 14-1.11.
In addition to nominees for expired terms, the
Committee shall nominate for each permanent Committee
one ruling, one teaching elder and a member-at largecandidate as alternates to fill any vacancies that may occur
during the year. Each alternate should attend each meeting

<a id="ga47-p606"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=608 printed_page=606 -->

and fill any vacancy necessary to meet a quorum. In
addition to the new nominees from the Presbyteries,
alternates not assuming any vacancies during a year will be
automatically considered by the Nominating Committee as
candidates for nomination to that same committee.

11. To be qualified for nomination and election to serve on a

Permanent Committee or Agency as a member at large, a
non-ordained candidate must be a member in good
standing of a Church in the Presbyterian Church in
America and must receive an endorsement from their

Session. The endorsement will consist of testimony
regarding the candidate’s Christian character, knowledge of
the Constitution, and promise of faithful service to the
General Assembly.

12. The Assembly permanent committees are the Administrative

Committee of the General Assembly, Committee on
Discipleship Ministries, Committee on Mission to North
America, Committee on Mission to the World, and
Committee on Reformed University Fellowship.
The Administrative Committee of the General

Assembly (AC) shall consist of twenty (20) members:
a. Eleven members, six elders and five members who are

not elders, shall be in classes elected through the
standard nomination and election procedure,
b. One member from each of the following program

committees or agencies:

1. Committee on Discipleship Ministries (CDM)
2. Covenant College (CC)
3. Covenant Theological Seminary (CTS)
4. Mission to North America (MNA)
5. Mission to the World (MTW)
6. PCA Foundation (PCAF)
7. PCA Retirement & Benefits, Inc. (RBI)
8. Reformed University Fellowship (RUF)
9. Ridge Haven Conference Center (RH)
The eleven elected members shall serve a term of

four years. The chairman of the Administrative
Committee shall be one of its elected ordained

members.

<a id="ga47-p607"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=609 printed_page=607 -->

Each program committee and agency shall
designate its member each year at the last meeting of
the committee or board before the meeting of the
General Assembly. The chief administrative officers
of the program committees and agencies may attend
any meeting of the Administrative Committee. They
shall be entitled to the privilege of the floor but shall
not have a vote and must be excluded when an

executive session is called.
Committee on Discipleship Ministries, Committee
on Mission to North America, Committee on Mission
to the World, and Committee on Reformed University
Fellowship shall consist of fifteen (15) members
divided into five classes of three members. Three
classes will have one TE, one RE and one member at
large. One class shall have one TE and two members
at large and one class shall have one RE and two
members at large. Each class shall be elected to serve
five-year terms. Committee on Discipleship Ministries,
Committee on Mission to North America, Committee
on Mission to the World, and Committee on Reformed
University Fellowship shall have one ruling elder, one
teaching elder and one member at large as alternates to
fill any vacancy that may occur during the year. A
quorum for each Committees shall be eight (8)
members, with a majority of those present being
elders. The Chairman of each Committee shall be an

elder.
Persons who have served for a full term, or for at
least two years of a partial term, on one of the
Assembly’s permanent committees or agencies shall
not be eligible for re-election to an Assembly
committee until one year has lapsed. (Exceptions may
be permitted in agency bylaws approved by the
Assembly).

**Further, be it resolved** that the Stated Clerk shall be responsible for

developing the nomination forms necessary to implement this process
and shall be responsible for converting the composition of each class as
each class term expires.

_Adopted by Ohio Valley Presbytery at its stated meeting, January 26, 2019_
_Attested by TE Larry C. Hoop, stated clerk._

<a id="ga47-p608"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=610 printed_page=608 -->

##### OVERTURE 9 from Platte Valley Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 42-4; 43-2; 43-3 regarding Method and Deadlines
of Filing Cases”

**Whereas**, the Standing Judicial Commission ruled in Case 2016-02

( _M45GA_, 2017, pp. 510–14), that notice of complaints must be made by
hard copy, and that electronic filing of complaints are administratively
out of order; and

**Whereas**, the language of both _BCO_ 42-4 (filing appeals) and _BCO_ 43-3

(filing complaints) confusingly refer to two kinds of notification: the
notification of the lower court’s original decision, and the notification of
appeal/complaint by the appellant/ complainant; and

**Whereas** _,_ _BCO_ 42-4 and _BCO_ 43-3 explicitly provide that the “notification”

(that is, the notification of the lower court’s original decision) may be
made electronically, but do not explicitly forbid notification of appeals
and complaints from being made electronically; and

**Whereas**, _BCO_ 43-2 does not describe multiple types of “notification,” but

still does not explicitly define that complainants may not file a complaint
electronically; and

**Whereas** _,_ the _Operating Manual_ (18.10.b) of the Standing Judicial

Commission much more clearly defines proper, timely filing:

Documents required or permitted to be filed by a party shall be
filed with the Stated Clerk. Such filing shall not be timely
unless the documents are received in the office of the Stated

Clerk within the time fixed for such filing, except that papers
shall be deemed filed on the day of mailing if certified,
registered or express mail of the United States Postal Service
or any private service where verifying receipt is utilized.
Neither facsimiles nor Email will be allowed for purposes of
filing. Interested parties should be aware that responsibility for
such filings rests with them and that delays in delivery or nondelivery are the sole responsibility of the filing party. and

**Whereas**, this language would also help Sessions and Presbyteries clarify

proper, timely filing of appeals and complaints if included in the _BCO_ ;
and

**Whereas** _,_ the authors of the SJC Concurring Opinion on Case 2016-02

acknowledged this confusion of language, writing, “Accordingly we
believe that _BCO_ 43-3 should be clarified by amendment offered through
an Overture” ( _M45GA_, 2017, p. 514);

<a id="ga47-p609"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=611 printed_page=609 -->

**Therefore** _**,**_ **Platte Valley Presbytery overtures** to amend _BCO_ 42-4 to read

( ~~strikethrough~~ for deletions; underlining for new words):

**42-4** . Notice of appeal may be given the court before its
adjournment. Written notice of appeal, with supporting
reasons, shall be filed by the appellant with both the clerk of
the lower court and the clerk of the higher court ~~, within thirty~~
~~(30) days of notification of the last court’s decision.~~ Such filing
shall not be timely unless the documents are received by the
clerk of the lower court and the clerk of the higher court within
thirty (30) days of notification of the last court’s decision,
except that papers shall be deemed filed on the day of mailing
if certified, registered or express mail of the United States
Postal Service or any private service where verifying receipt is
utilized. Neither facsimiles nor e-mail will be allowed for

purposes of filing. Interested parties should be aware that
responsibility for such filings rests with them and that delays

in delivery or non delivery are the sole responsibility of the
filing party.
Notification of the last court’s decision shall be deemed to have

occurred on the day of mailing (if certified, registered or
express mail of a national postal service or any private service
where verifying receipt is utilized), the day of hand delivery,
or the day of confirmed receipt in the case of e-mail or
facsimile. Furthermore, compliance with such requirements
shall be deemed to have been fulfilled if a party cannot be
located after diligent inquiry or if a party refuses to accept
delivery. No attempt should be made to circularize the courts
to which appeal is being made by either party before the case
is heard.

So that it reads:

**42-4** . Notice of appeal may be given the court before its
adjournment. Written notice of appeal, with supporting
reasons, shall be filed by the appellant with both the clerk of
the lower court and the clerk of the higher court. Such filing
shall not be timely unless the documents are received by the
clerk of the lower court and the clerk of the higher court within
thirty (30) days of notification of the last court’s decision,
except that papers shall be deemed filed on the day of mailing
if certified, registered or express mail of the United States

<a id="ga47-p610"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=612 printed_page=610 -->

Postal Service or any private service where verifying receipt is
utilized. Neither facsimiles nor e-mail will be allowed for

purposes of filing. Interested parties should be aware that
responsibility for such filings rests with them and that delays
in delivery or non-delivery are the sole responsibility of the
filing party.
Notification of the last court’s decision shall be deemed to

have occurred on the day of mailing (if certified, registered or
express mail of a national postal service or any private service
where verifying receipt is utilized), the day of hand delivery,
or the day of confirmed receipt in the case of e-mail or
facsimile. Furthermore, compliance with such requirements
shall be deemed to have been fulfilled if a party cannot be
located after diligent inquiry or if a party refuses to accept
delivery. No attempt should be made to circularize the courts
to which appeal is being made by either party before the case
is heard.

**And, therefore** _**,**_ **Platte Valley Presbytery further overtures** to amend _BCO_

43-2 to read ~~(strikethrough~~ for deletions; underlining for new words):

43-2. A complaint shall first be made to the court whose act or
decision is alleged to be in error. Written notice of complaint,
with supporting reasons, shall be filed with the clerk of the
court ~~within sixty (60) days following the meeting of the court~~ .
Such filing shall not be timely unless the documents are
received in the office of the Stated Clerk within sixty (60) days
following the meeting of the court, except that papers shall be
deemed filed on the day of mailing if certified, registered or
express mail of the United States Postal Service or any private
service where verifying receipt is utilized. Neither facsimiles
nor e-mail will be allowed for purposes of filing. In regard to
filing a complaint with a court, as outlined in this section, or in
taking that complaint to a higher court, as outlined in _BCO_ 433, interested parties should be aware that responsibility for such
filings rests with them and that delays in delivery or
nondelivery are the sole responsibility of the filing party. The
court shall consider the complaint at its next stated meeting, or

<a id="ga47-p611"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=613 printed_page=611 -->

at a called meeting prior to its next stated meeting. No attempt
should be made to circularize the court to which complaint is
being made by either party.

So that it reads:

**43-2** . A complaint shall first be made to the court whose act or
decision is alleged to be in error. Written notice of complaint,
with supporting reasons, shall be filed with the clerk of the
court. Such filing shall not be timely unless the documents are
received in the office of the Stated Clerk within sixty (60) days
following the meeting of the court, except that papers shall be
deemed filed on the day of mailing if certified, registered or
express mail of the United States Postal Service or any private
service where verifying receipt is utilized. Neither facsimiles
nor e-mail will be allowed for purposes of filing. In regard to
filing a complaint with a court, as outlined in this section, or in
taking that complaint to a higher court, as outlined in _BCO_ 433, interested parties should be aware that responsibility for such
filings rests with them and that delays in delivery or nondelivery are the sole responsibility of the filing party. The court
shall consider the complaint at its next stated meeting, or at a
called meeting prior to its next stated meeting. No attempt
should be made to circularize the court to which complaint is
being made by either party.

**And, therefore**, Platte Valley Presbytery further overtures to amend _BCO_

43-3 to read ( ~~strikethrough~~ for deletions; underlining for new words):

**43-3** . If, after considering a complaint, the court alleged to be
delinquent or in error is of the opinion that it has not erred, and
denies the complaint, the complainant may take that complaint
to the next higher court. If the lower court fails to consider the
complaint against it by or at its next stated meeting, the
complainant may take that complaint to the next higher court.
Written notice thereof shall be filed with both the clerk of the
lower court and the clerk of the higher court ~~within thirty (30)~~
~~days of notification of the last court’s decision.~~ Such filing
shall not be timely unless the documents are received by the
clerk of the lower court and the clerk of the higher court within
thirty (30) days of notification of the last court’s decision,
except that papers shall be deemed filed on the day of mailing

<a id="ga47-p612"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=614 printed_page=612 -->

if certified, registered or express mail of the United States
Postal Service or any private service where verifying receipt is
utilized. Neither facsimiles nor e-mail will be allowed for
purposes of filing.
Notification of the last court’s decision shall be deemed to
have occurred on the day of mailing (if certified, registered or
express mail of a national postal service or any private service
where verifying receipt is utilized), the day of hand delivery,
or the day of confirmed receipt in the case of e-mail or
facsimile. Furthermore, compliance with such requirements
shall be deemed to have been fulfilled if a party cannot be
located after diligent inquiry or if a party refuses to accept
delivery.

So that it reads:

**43-3** . If, after considering a complaint, the court alleged to be
delinquent or in error is of the opinion that it has not erred, and
denies the complaint, the complainant may take that complaint
to the next higher court. If the lower court fails to consider the
complaint against it by or at its next stated meeting, the
complainant may take that complaint to the next higher court.
Written notice thereof shall be filed with both the clerk of the
lower court and the clerk of the higher court. Such filing shall
not be timely unless the documents are received by the clerk of
the lower court and the clerk of the higher court within thirty
(30) days of notification of the last court’s decision, except that
papers shall be deemed filed on the day of mailing if certified,
registered or express mail of the United States Postal Service
or any private service where verifying receipt is utilized. Neither
facsimiles nor e-mail will be allowed for purposes of filing.
Notification of the last court’s decision shall be deemed to
have occurred on the day of mailing (if certified, registered or
express mail of a national postal service or any private service
where verifying receipt is utilized), the day of hand delivery,
or the day of confirmed receipt in the case of e-mail or
facsimile. Furthermore, compliance with such requirements shall
be deemed to have been fulfilled if a party cannot be located
after diligent inquiry or if a party refuses to accept delivery.

_Adopted by Platte Valley Presbytery at its stated meeting, February 2, 2019_
_Attested by TE Jacob Gerber, stated clerk_

<a id="ga47-p613"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=615 printed_page=613 -->

##### OVERTURE 10 from Northern New England Presbytery (to OC, AC)

“Appoint Ad Interim Committee on Domestic Abuse and Sexual
Assault” (Version 2)

**Whereas**, the sins of domestic abuse and sexual assault are expressly

forbidden (2 Timothy 3:2-5; Jude 7; Exodus 21:15; Deuteronomy 22:2527; also _WLC_ 135 and 139); and

**Whereas**, these sins are especially heinous because they are “against the

express letter of the law,” “break many commandments,” “admit of no
reparation,” and often involve various other aggravations ( _WLC_ 151);
and

**Whereas**, these sins are grievously prevalent in our society; [5] and

**Whereas**, these sins often produce in the victims a complex response of

denial along with immense fear and shame that persist with varying
severity throughout life (e.g., 2 Samuel 13:1-22); and

**Whereas**, it is the responsibility of the elders of the church to exercise their

authority, severally and jointly, to shepherd those who are under their
care by guarding against such sinful behavior as much as possible, by
wisely caring for those victimized by the sins of others, and by
appropriately disciplining those guilty of such sins toward a full
repentance; and

**Whereas,** these sins have been a concerning subject of attention not only in

other denominations but even in the proceedings of the 46 [th] General
Assembly; and

**Whereas**, the 42 [nd] General Assembly previously exhorted the PCA and its

courts to respond boldly against one form of these sins, namely the
sexual abuse of children; and

**Whereas**, no standardized resources for pastoral care on these sins and their

complex consequences exist for officers, church courts, and agencies in
the PCA; and

**Whereas**, no clear guidelines for policy or practice with regard to these sins

exist for the officers, church courts, and agencies in the PCA that would
protect individuals and congregations; and

5 For example, the National Domestic Violence Hotline reports: “More than 1 in 3 women

(35.6%) and more than 1 in 4 men (28.5%) in the United States have experienced rape,
physical violence and/or stalking by an intimate partner in their lifetime” (available at
http://www.thehotline.org/resources/statistics/ accessed 6/15/18). For further sobering
statistics on sexual violence see the National Center for Injury Prevention and Controls
report entitled _Sexual Violence Surveillance: Uniform Definitions and Recommended Data_
_Elements_ (Atlanta: Center for Disease Control, 2014; available online at
[http://www.cdc.gov/violenceprevention/pdf/sv_surveillance_definitionsl-2009-a.pdf](http://www.cdc.gov/violenceprevention/pdf/sv_surveillance_definitionsl-2009-a.pdf) )

<a id="ga47-p614"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=616 printed_page=614 -->

**Whereas**, the Rules of Discipline protect against unjust accusation ( _BCO_ 31
8), they also make no consideration for the unique heinousness of the
sins and their practical effect on the victims;

**Therefore be it resolved** that Northern New England Presbytery hereby

overtures the 47th General Assembly of the PCA to form a committee to
study:
(1) the nature and causes of domestic abuse and sexual assault;
(2) the pastoral resources available for the care of survivors of these sins

as well as ministry and counsel for those overtaken by these sins;
(3) the best practices and policy guidelines for elders, church courts, and

agencies for protecting against these sins and for responding to them;
(4) the possibility of changes to the Rules of Discipline that would

appropriately protect victims of these sins within a biblical process;
and

**Be it further resolved** that the committee shall be appointed by the

moderator, and shall include among its members or advisory members at
least two subject-matter experts and at least two women; and

**Be it further resolved** that the budget for the study committee be set at

$15,000/year and that funds be derived from gifts to the AC designated
for that purpose, with Northern New England Presbytery committed to
offer $1,000, and

**Be it further resolved** that the committee shall present their findings and

recommendations for adoption by the 48th General Assembly.

_Adopted by Northern New England Presbytery at its stated meeting,_
_February 2, 2019_
_Attested by TE C.S. Per Almquist, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 11 from Fellowship Presbytery (OC, AC, CDM, CC, RUF)

“Commend and Distribute RPCNA’s ‘Contemporary Perspectives
on Sexual Orientation: A Theological and Pastoral Analysis’”

**Whereas** it is the responsibility of the General Assembly to evaluate needs

for the most effective fulfillment of the Great Commission ( _BCO_ 141.5); and

**Whereas** the General Assembly has been given the power in general to

recommend measures for the promotion of charity, truth, and holiness
through all the churches under its care ( _BCO_ 14-6.k); and

**Whereas** the local Sessions are tasked with shepherding the flock in their

midst (1 Peter 5:2), and saving others by snatching them out of the fire,
showing mercy to others with fear, hating even the garment stained by
the flesh (Jude 23); and

<a id="ga47-p615"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=617 printed_page=615 -->

**Whereas** much of the flock in our midst has often been exposed to great

depths of evil through the ubiquitous presence of the internet, the
sexualization of entertainment, broadening sexual ethics in the court
system, and increasing globalization; and

**Whereas** some of the flock in our midst are hard-pressed with temptation

that manifests itself through same sex attraction or a confused sexual
identity (Rom 1:26); and

**Whereas** some elders, deacons, or sessions may feel overwhelmed or

underequipped to minister to those in their flock that struggle with these
unholy desires; and

**Whereas** the topic of sexual orientation needs clarification, and is as yet an

uncovered topic in PCA reports; and

**Whereas** a NAPARC member church, the Reformed Presbyterian Church

North America (RPCNA), has produced an excellent resource on this
issue that is both exegetically sound and pastorally tender entitled
“Contemporary Perspectives on Sexual Orientation: A Theological and
Pastoral Analysis”; and

**Whereas** at the 44 [th] General Assembly the Cooperative Ministries

Committee, though unable to make direct recommendations to the
Assembly, commended this RPCNA report to be read and studied by
teaching elders and Sessions ( _M44GA_, p. 334);

**Therefore, be it resolved** that the General Assembly receive “Contemporary

Perspectives on Sexual Orientation: A Theological and Pastoral
Analysis” (available on pages 86-131 at [http://rparchives.org/](http://rparchives.org/%20data/Minutes%20of%20Synod/2011%20Minutes.pdf)
[data/Minutes%20of%20Synod/2011%20Minutes.pdf) and distribute it](http://rparchives.org/%20data/Minutes%20of%20Synod/2011%20Minutes.pdf)
to the presbyteries and congregations of the denomination; and

**Be it further resolved** that the General Assembly direct the Committee on

Discipleship Ministries to publish the CPSO for sale and distribution,
along with the other actions of the Assembly related to human sexuality,
especially the actions of the 5 [th] General Assembly and the Joining and
Receiving, as the PCA’s statements on the Gospel and human sexuality;
and

**Be it further resolved** that the General Assembly direct the Committee on

Discipleship Ministries to assist sessions in the watching over their flock
and in the nourishing and guarding of the children of the church by
creating a resource or curriculum based on the CPSO report that could
be used with students that might be struggling with issues related to
sexual identity, same sex attraction, or gender confusion; and

**Be it further resolved** that the General Assembly direct Reformed

University Fellowship to participate in promoting the prosperity and
enlargement of the Church by incorporating CPSO in their annual
training; and

<a id="ga47-p616"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=618 printed_page=616 -->

**Be it further resolved** that the General Assembly direct Covenant College

to nourish and safeguard the young people of the church by making this
report available to faculty, staff, and students, as well as including the
content of this report in the life of the school in some fashion (perhaps
through RA training, chapel, or other venues); and

**Be it further resolved** that the General Assembly encourage Sessions and

elders to be active in shepherding those in their flock that struggle against
same sex attraction or sexual confusion so that elders might keep watch
over their flocks as ones who will give an account, and that we all might
win a full reward (Heb. 13:17; 2 John 8).

_Adopted by Fellowship Presbytery at its stated meeting, January_ _26, 2019_
_Attested by TE Robert F. Sprinkle Jr., stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 12 from Northern New England Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _RAO_ 8-4 regarding Floor Nominations”

**Whereas**, subjection to the brethren is an oath taken by both Teaching and

Ruling Elders in the PCA ( _BCO_ 21-5.6; 24-6.5) and such subjection
begins with those brothers closest in relationship to said officer; and

**Whereas**, our form of government seeks to give deference to and maintain

the authority of lower courts; and

**Whereas**, floor nomination of officers to fill open positions on the Permanent

Committees, Agencies, and the Standing Judicial Commission of the
General Assembly are, by definition, not made through such lower
courts; and

**Whereas**, the rise of celebrity officers within Christ’s Church is a worrisome

trend that can lead to polarization and division within Christ’s body
(1 Corinthians 1:10-14); and floor nominations can serve as an avenue
for the furtherance of this trend; and

**Whereas**, the undertaking of service within the Agencies and Committees of

the General Assembly is one to be considered with deliberation and
prayer; and

**Whereas**, the Nominating Committee is entrusted with the important task of

evaluating and nominating

**Therefore be it resolved**, Northern New England Presbytery hereby

overtures the 47th General Assembly to restrict floor nominations at the
General Assembly for the Assembly Committees and Agencies to those
whose names were submitted to the Nominating Committee according
to _RAO_ 8-4.d and e, by adding a final sentence to _RAO_ 8-4.i as indicated
below. [Addition indicated by underlining.]

<a id="ga47-p617"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=619 printed_page=617 -->

_RAO_ 8-4

i. Additional nominations may be submitted either (1) in
writing or (2) electronically, in either case on forms
supplied by the Stated Clerk, which shall include consent
of the nominee to serve, if elected, and a brief statement
regarding the nominee. In the event that a nomination is
submitted electronically, the nominator should be aware
that responsibility for such nomination rests with the
nominator and that non- delivery is the sole responsibility
of the nominator. Because the nominator has sole

responsibility for delivery of a nomination, the nominator
should obtain a receipt verifying successful electronic
delivery of the nomination. The nominee is to give consent
to only one nomination. In the event that the nominee is
nominated for more than one position, the nominee may
indicate to which nomination the nominee consents. The

deadline for these nominations is the close of the afternoon
session of the first day of the Assembly. The Clerk’s office
shall issue a supplement to the Assembly’s Nominating
Committee report during the second day’s business
sessions. No floor nomination will be received unless the
nominee’s name was previously submitted to the
Nominating Committee according to _RAO_ 8-4.d and e,
excepting circumstances where there is no nominee from
the Nominating Committee.

_Adopted by Northern New England Presbytery at its stated meeting,_
_February 2, 2019_
_Attested by TE C.S. Per Almquist, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 13 from Southern New England Presbytery (to AC, OC)

“Form Ad Interim Committee on Domestic Abuse and Sexual Assault”

(Version 3)

**Whereas**, the sins of domestic abuse and sexual assault are expressly

forbidden (2 Timothy 3:2-5; Jude 7; Exodus 21:15; Deuteronomy 22:2527; also _WLC_ 135 and 139); and

**Whereas**, these sins are especially heinous because they are “against the

express letter of the law,” “break many commandments,” “admit of no
reparation,” and often involve various other aggravations ( _WLC_ 151); and

<a id="ga47-p618"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=620 printed_page=618 -->

[6]

**Whereas**, these sins are grievously prevalent in our society; and

**Whereas**, these sins often produce in the victims a complex response of

denial along with immense fear and shame that persist with varying
severity throughout life (e.g., 2 Samuel 13:1-22); and

**Whereas**, it is the responsibility of the elders of the church to exercise their

authority, severally and jointly, to shepherd those who are under their
care by guarding against such sinful behavior as much as possible, by
wisely caring for those victimized by the sins of others, and by
appropriately disciplining those guilty of such sins toward a full
repentance; and

**Whereas,** these sins have been a concerning subject of attention not only in

other denominations but even in the proceedings of the 46th General
Assembly; and

**Whereas**, the 42nd General Assembly previously exhorted the PCA and its

courts to respond boldly against one form of these sins, namely the
sexual abuse of children; and

**Whereas**, no standardized resources for pastoral care on these sins and their

complex consequences exist for officers, church courts, and agencies in
the PCA; and

**Whereas**, no clear guidelines for policy or practice with regard to these sins

exist for the officers, church courts, and agencies in the PCA that would
protect individuals and congregations; and

**Whereas**, the Rules of Discipline make no consideration for the unique

heinousness of these sins and their practical effect on victims;

**Therefore, be it resolved** that Southern New England Presbytery hereby

overtures the 47 [th] General Assembly of the PCA to form a committee to
study:
(1) the nature of domestic abuse and sexual assault;
(2) the pastoral resources available for the care of survivors of these

sins;

6 For example, the National Domestic Violence Hotline reports: “More than 1 in 3 women

(35.6%) and more than 1 in 4 men (28.5%) in the United States have experienced rape,
physical violence and/or stalking by an intimate partner in their lifetime” (available at
[http://www.thehotline.org/resources/statistics/,](http://www.thehotline.org/resources/statistics/) accessed 6/15/18). For further sobering
statistics on sexual violence see the National Center for Injury Prevention and Controls report
entitled _Sexual Violence Surveillance: Uniform Definitions and Recommended Data_
_Elements_ [(Atlanta: Center for Disease Control, 2014; available online athttp://www.cdc.gov/](http://www.cdc.gov/%20violenceprevention/pdf/sv_surveillance_definitionsl-2009-a.pdf)
[violenceprevention/pdf/sv_surveillance_definitionsl-2009-a.pdf).](http://www.cdc.gov/%20violenceprevention/pdf/sv_surveillance_definitionsl-2009-a.pdf)

<a id="ga47-p619"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=621 printed_page=619 -->

(3) the best practices and policy guidelines for elders, church courts, and

agencies for protecting against these sins and for responding to
them;
(4) the possibility of changes to the Rules of Discipline that would

appropriately protect victims of these sins within a biblical process;
and

(5) the possible need for repentance as a church; and

**Be it further resolved** that the committee shall be appointed by the

moderator, and shall include among its members or advisory members
at least two subject-matter experts and at least two women; and

**Be it further resolved** that the budget for the study committee be set at

$15,000/year and that funds be derived from gifts to the AC designated
for that purpose, with Southern New England Presbytery committed to
offer $3,000; and

**Be it further resolved** that the committee shall present their findings and

recommendations for adoption by the 48th General Assembly.

_Adopted by Southern New England Presbytery at its stated meeting,_
_January 19, 2019_
_Attested by /s/ TE Ron Heald, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 14 from Southern New England Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 14-1.10, 14-1.11, and 14-1.12 to Allow Non-ordained
Persons to serve on Committees and Boards” (Version 2)

**Whereas**, vigorous participation of women in the life of the early church is

attested by the New Testament: they helped select (Acts 1:14) and train
(Acts 18:26) leaders of the church, were prophets (Acts 21:8-9), served
the church through deeds of mercy in vital ways (Acts 9:36–41), were
instrumental in the planting of churches (Acts 16:12–15; 17:4,12) and
other missionary endeavors (Acts 18:1f), and were acknowledged as
important leaders for the church (Rom. 16:3-4); and

**Whereas**, the Presbyterian Church in America believes Christ has called his

church to be a “spiritual commonwealth” ( _BCO_ 3-1), in which members
elect godly men to be ordained as elders who are given the power of
jurisdiction as they act jointly in the courts of the church ( _BCO_ 3-2);
and

**Whereas**, a robustly complementarian perspective affirms the oversight of

these elders and the proper, vital and full expression of the gifts given
by Christ to men and women for his service; and

<a id="ga47-p620"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=622 printed_page=620 -->

**Whereas**, as part of the calling of church leaders to “equip the saints for the

work of ministry for building up the body of Christ” (Eph. 4:12),
sessions throughout the PCA identify gifted men and women to serve on
committees of the church, such as missions, building, and pastoral search
committees; and

**Whereas**, in contrast with a commission in the PCA, which is "authorized

to deliberate and conclude the business referred to it," committees are
appointed by courts “to examine, consider and report” to their
constituting court concerning the matters that court delegates to them
(BCO 15-1) and thus do not exercise governing authority; and

**Whereas**, in accordance with this principle, Permanent Committees

established by the General Assembly are “to serve and not to direct any
Church judicatories. They are not to establish policy, but rather execute
policy established by the General Assembly” ( _BCO_ 14-1.7); and

**Whereas**, these considerations suggest there is no impediment to an

unordained member in good standing in the PCA serving on a Permanent
Committee or Agency of the General Assembly established in _BCO_ 141.12; and

**Whereas**, the 45th General Assembly adopted the recommendation of The

Ad Interim Committee on Women Serving in the Ministry of the Church
that stated “sessions, presbyteries and the General Assembly strive to
develop, recognize, and utilize the gifts, skills, knowledge, and wisdom
of godly women in the local, regional, and national church, and
particularly consider overtures that would allow qualified women to
serve on appropriate committees and agencies within the church”; and

**Whereas**, the 46th General Assembly answered in the negative an overture

to allow non- ordained members on General Assembly Agency Boards
on the grounds that _BCO_ 14-1.11 had to be amended prior to amendment
of the PCA Bylaws, which governs the Agencies;

**Therefore be it resolved** to amend _The Book of Church Orde_ r by deleting

14-1.10; by amending 14-1.11 as follows (and renumbering as 14-1.10);
by adding a new 14-1.11; and by amending 14-1.12 as follows:

[Proposed additions underlined and deletions noted by strike out.]

_BCO_ 14-1

10. ~~The committees are to be established on the basis of an~~

~~equal number between teaching and ruling elders.~~

~~11.~~ 10. A Nominating Committee shall be comprised of one

representative elected by each Presbytery in the following
manner. Each Presbytery shall be assigned to a class by the

<a id="ga47-p621"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=623 printed_page=621 -->

stated clerk based on its date of formation. The members

shall serve in classes of three year terms, alternating
between ruling and teaching elders. When necessary,
unexpired terms shall be filled by an elder of the same
class, teaching or ruling.
This committee is to present all nominations for which
it is responsible to the next meeting of the Assembly from
a slate of ~~men~~ candidates nominated by the Presbyteries.
Presbyteries shall utilize the nominating forms provided by
the stated clerk for their nominations. Each presbytery may
present one teaching elder, ~~and~~ one ruling elder, and, when
applicable, one candidate who is not an elder to fill
openings that are designated for members at large for each

committee or agency. Any non ordained person nominated
must meet the qualifications outlined in _BCO_ 14-1.11.

In addition to nominees for expired terms, the
Committee shall nominate for each permanent Committee
one ruling, ~~and~~ one teaching el ~~d~~ er and a member at large
candidate as alternates to fill any vacancies that may occur
during the year. Each alternate should attend each meeting
and fill any vacancy necessary to meet a quorum. In
addition to the new nominees from the Presbyteries,
alternates not assuming any vacancies during a year will be
automatically considered by the Nominating Committee as
candidates for nomination to that same committee.

11. To be qualified for nomination and election to serve on a

Permanent Committee or Agency as a member at large, a
non-ordained candidate must be a member in good
standing of a Church in the Presbyterian Church in
America and must receive an endorsement from their

Session. The endorsement will consist of testimony
regarding the candidate’s Christian character, knowledge of
the Constitution and promise of faithful service to the
General Assembly.

12. The Assembly permanent committees are the
Administrative Committee of the General Assembly,
Committee on Discipleship Ministries, Committee on
Mission to North America, Committee on Mission to the
World, and Committee on Reformed University Fellowship.

<a id="ga47-p622"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=624 printed_page=622 -->

The Administrative Committee of the General

Assembly (AC) shall consist of twenty (20) members:
a. Eleven members, six elders and five members who

are not elders, shall be in classes elected through the
standard nomination and election procedure,
b. One member from each of the following program

committees or agencies:
1. Committee on Discipleship Ministries (CDM)
2. Covenant College (CC)
3. Covenant Theological Seminary (CTS)
4. Mission to North America (MNA)
5. Mission to the World (MTW)
6. PCA Foundation (PCAF)
7. PCA Retirement & Benefits, Inc. (RBI)
8. Reformed University Fellowship (RUF)
9. Ridge Haven Conference Center (RH)
The eleven elected members ~~at large~~ shall serve a
term of four years. The chairman of the Administrative
Committee shall be one of its elected ordained members ~~at~~

~~large.~~
Each program committee and agency shall
designate its member each year at the last meeting of the
committee or board before the meeting of the General
Assembly. The chief administrative officers of the
program committees and agencies may attend any meeting
of the Administrative Committee. They shall be entitled to
the privilege of the floor but shall not have a vote and must
be excluded when an executive session is called.

Committee on Discipleship Ministries, Committee
on Mission to North America, Committee on Mission to
the World, and Committee on Reformed University
Fellowship shall consist of fifteen (15) ~~men~~ members
divided into five classes of three ~~menm~~ embers. Three

classes will have one TE, one RE and one member at large.
One class shall have one TE and two members at large and
one class shall have one RE and two members at large.
~~each, with two men Being TEs and one RE or two men~~
~~being REs and one TE on alternate years,~~ Each class shall
be elected to serve five-year terms. Committee on
Discipleship Ministries, Committee on Mission to North
America, Committee on Mission to the World, and

<a id="ga47-p623"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=625 printed_page=623 -->

Committee on Reformed University Fellowship shall have
one ruling elder, ~~and~~ one teaching elder and one member
at large as alternates to fill any vacancy that may occur
during the year. A quorum for each Committees shall be
eight (8) members, with a majority of those present being
elders. The Chairman of each Committee shall be an elder.

Persons who have served for a full term, or for at least
two years of a partial term, on one of the Assembly’s
permanent committees or agencies shall not be eligible for
re- election to an Assembly committee until one year has
lapsed. (Exceptions may be permitted in agency bylaws
approved by the Assembly).

**So that** _**BCO**_ **14-1.10, 14-1.11, and 14-1.12 as amended would read** :

_BCO_ 14-1

10. A Nominating Committee shall be comprised of one

representative elected by each Presbytery in the following
manner. Each Presbytery shall be assigned to a class by the
stated clerk based on its date of formation. The members

shall serve in classes of three year terms, alternating
between ruling and teaching elders. When necessary,
unexpired terms shall be filled by an elder of the same
class, teaching or ruling.

This committee is to present all nominations for which
it is responsible to the next meeting of the Assembly from
a slate of candidates nominated by the Presbyteries.
Presbyteries shall utilize the nominating forms provided by
the stated clerk for their nominations. Each presbytery may
present one teaching elder, one ruling elder, and, when
applicable, one candidate who is not an elder to fill
openings that are designated for members at large for each
committee or agency. Any non-ordained person nominated
must meet the qualifications outlined in BCO 14-1.11.
In addition to nominees for expired terms, the
Committee shall nominate for each permanent Committee
one ruling, one teaching elder and a member at large
candidate as alternates to fill any vacancies that may occur
during the year. Each alternate should attend each meeting
and fill any vacancy necessary to meet a quorum. In

<a id="ga47-p624"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=626 printed_page=624 -->

addition to the new nominees from the Presbyteries,
alternates not assuming any vacancies during a year will be
automatically considered by the Nominating Committee as
candidates for nomination to that same committee.

11. To be qualified for nomination and election to serve on a

Permanent Committee or Agency as a member at large, a
non-ordained candidate must be a member in good
standing of a Church in the Presbyterian Church in
America and must receive an endorsement from their

Session. The endorsement will consist of testimony
regarding the candidate’s Christian character, knowledge
of the Constitution and promise of faithful service to the
General Assembly.

12. The Assembly permanent committees are the
Administrative Committee of the General Assembly,
Committee on Discipleship Ministries, Committee on
Mission to North America, Committee on Mission to the
World, and Committee on Reformed University Fellowship.
The Administrative Committee of the General

Assembly (AC) shall consist of twenty (20) members:
a. Eleven members, six elders and five members who are

not elders, shall be in classes elected through the
standard nomination and election procedure,
b. One member from each of the following program

committees or agencies:
1. Committee on Discipleship Ministries (CDM)
2. Covenant College (CC)
3. Covenant Theological Seminary (CTS)
4. Mission to North America (MNA)
5 Mission to the World (MTW)
6. PCA Foundation (PCAF)
7. PCA Retirement & Benefits, Inc. (RBI)
8. Reformed University Fellowship (RUF)
9. Ridge Haven Conference Center (RH)
The eleven elected members shall serve a term of four

years. The chairman of the Administrative Committee
shall be one of its elected ordained members.

Each program committee and agency shall designate
its member each year at the last meeting of the committee

<a id="ga47-p625"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=627 printed_page=625 -->

or board before the meeting of the General Assembly. The
chief administrative officers of the program committees
and agencies may attend any meeting of the Administrative
Committee. They shall be entitled to the privilege of the
floor but shall not have a vote and must be excluded when

an executive session is called.

Committee on Discipleship Ministries, Committee on
Mission to North America, Committee on Mission to the
World, and Committee on Reformed University
Fellowship shall consist of fifteen (15) members divided
into five classes of three members. Three classes will have

one TE, one RE and one member at large. One class shall
have one TE and two members at large and one class shall
have one RE and two members at large. Each class shall be
elected to serve five-year terms. Committee on Discipleship
Ministries, Committee on Mission to North America,
Committee on Mission to the World, and Committee on
Reformed University Fellowship shall have one ruling
elder, one teaching elder and one member at large as
alternates to fill any vacancy that may occur during the
year. A quorum for each Committees shall be eight (8)
members, with a majority of those present being elders.
The Chairman of each Committee shall be an elder.

Persons who have served for a full term, or for at least
two years of a partial term, on one of the Assembly’s
permanent committees or agencies shall not be eligible for
re- election to an Assembly committee until one year has
lapsed. (Exceptions may be permitted in agency bylaws
approved by the Assembly).

**Further, be it resolved** that the Stated Clerk shall be responsible for

developing the nomination forms necessary to implement this process
and shall be responsible for converting the composition of each class as
each class term expires.

_Adopted by Southern New England Presbytery at its stated meeting, January_
_19, 2019_
_Attested by /s/ TE Ron Heald, stated clerk_

<a id="ga47-p626"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=628 printed_page=626 -->

##### OVERTURE 15 from West Hudson Presbytery (CCB, OC)

“Amend _RAO_ 11-5 to Prevent Confusion and Possible
Contradictory Actions on Overtures by Multiple
Committees of Commissioners”

**Whereas**, Presbyteries (ordinarily) and other bodies (in some circumstances)

request action by the General Assembly on specific matters by means of
an Overture ( _RAO_ 11-4);

**Whereas**, an Overture may contain subject matter that could appear pertinent

to more than one Committee of Commissioners;

**Whereas**, currently _RAO_ 11-5 states that “the Stated Clerk shall refer all

overtures requesting amendment of the _Book of Church Order_ or the
_Rules of Assembly Operations_ to the Committee on Constitutional
Business for its advice to the Overtures Committee”;

**Whereas**, the term “shall” in the _BCO_ delineates an obligatory rather than a

permissible action, requiring the Stated Clerk to refer such overtures to
the Committee on Constitutional Business, which then shall forward
such overtures (along with CCB’s advice) to the Overtures Committee;

**Whereas**, if an Overture requesting amendment to the _BCO_ or the _RAO_ was

also referred to another Committee of Commissioners (CoC), the result
could be confusion as to which Committee’s recommendation, whether
Overtures or another CoC, should be taken up by the Assembly; and

**Whereas**, it is possible that in such instance contradictory recommendations

could be brought to the Assembly by two (or more) CoCs;

**Therefore, be it resolved,** that the General Assembly of the Presbyterian

Church in America amend _RAO_ 11-5 with the following additions
(additions are underlined):

**11-5.** Upon receipt the Stated Clerk shall refer all overtures
requesting amendment of the _Book of Church Order_ or the
_Rules of Assembly Operations_ to the Committee on
Constitutional Business for its advice to the Overtures
Committee. Upon receipt, the Stated Clerk shall forward all
overtures concerning presbytery boundaries or a new presbytery
to the permanent Committee on Mission to North America.
Any overture, other than an overture proposing amendment to
the Constitution (which shall be referred to the Committee on
Constitutional Business for its advice to the Overtures
Committee), having to do with the nature or responsibilities of
a permanent Committee or Agency shall be referred by the
Clerk to the appropriate permanent Committee or Agency or
ad interim committee. All other overtures shall be referred to

<a id="ga47-p627"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=629 printed_page=627 -->

the Overtures Committee. No overture shall be referred for

consideration and recommendation to more than one
Committee of Commissioners, permanent Committee or
Agency or ad interim committee; an overture, however, may
be referred for informational purposes only to another
Committee of Commissioners, permanent Committee or
Agency or ad interim committee. All overtures shall be
published in the _Commissioner Handbook_ with reference for
consideration indicated.

_Adopted by West Hudson Presbytery at its stated meeting, February 7, 2019_
_Attested by /s/ TE Christopher M. Diebold, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 16 from Georgia Foothills Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend the _RAO_ to Provide for a Moderator-Elect and Vice-Moderator
by Revision of _RAO_ 1-4, Addition of _RAO_ 1-6, 1-7,
and 1-8, and Revision of _RAO_ 2-1, 2-3”

**Whereas**, the role of Moderator of the General Assembly is a complicated

responsibility. it will be helpful fora man to have a year’s notice in order
to prepare for his duties, and

**Whereas**, some PCA presbyteries have a Moderator-In-Nomination rule in

their Standing Rules, and

**Whereas**, other Presbyterian denominations, such as the Associate

Reformed Presbyterian Church and the Evangelical Presbyterian Church
elect a Moderator-Elect or Vice-Moderator, and

**Whereas**, the Assembly subsequent to the election of a Moderator-Elect or

Vice-Moderator still would have the option of electing another man as
Moderator, and

**Whereas**, since we use electronic voting rather than voting cards or paper

ballots, it is no longer necessary to specify that paper ballots be used, and

**Whereas**, it is customary that the Moderator yield the chair at times. The

revision to _RAO_ 2-1 specifies who the Moderator _pro tempore_ may be,
and

**Whereas**, under _Robert’s Rules of Order_ the term “privileges of the floor”

only means that one is allowed to be present during a meeting and does
not mean that one has the right to speak ( _RONR_ [11 [th] ed.] p. 29, footnote).
The original purpose of _RAO_ 2-3 was to give a former Moderator of the
General Assembly the right to participate in debate. One must be a
commissioner in order to vote.

<a id="ga47-p628"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=630 printed_page=628 -->

**Therefore, be it resolved** that Georgia Foothills Presbytery overtures the

Forty-seventh General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America
to amend the _Rules of Assembly Operations_ by revision of _RAO_ 1-4; by
addition of _RAO_ 1-6, 1-7, and 1-8; and by revision of _RAO_ 2-1, 2-3 to
provide for a Moderator-Elect and a Vice-Moderator as follows:

**ARTICLE I. ORGANIZATION OF A GENERAL**

**ASSEMBLY’S MEETING**

**1-4.** If more than one commissioner is nominated, election
shall be by secret ballot, ~~on ballots provided by the Stated~~
~~Clerk.~~ ~~Tellers appointed by the Stated Clerk shall gather and~~
~~count the ballots, and report the tabulation to the Stated Clerk.~~
If no nominee receives a majority of the votes cast, a second
ballot shall be called on the two nominees who received the
highest number of votes on the first ballot. The Moderator
shall declare an election when a nominee receives a majority
of the votes cast by the commissioners present and voting.

**1-5.** No change.

**1-6.** After the Moderator has been elected, he shall preside over
the election of a Moderator-elect. There shall be only one
nominating speech not to exceed five minutes for each
nominee. No seconding speeches shall be permitted.
**1-7.** If more than one commissioner is nominated, election
shall be by secret ballot. If no nominee receives a majority of
the votes cast, a second ballot shall be called on the two
nominees who received the highest number of votes on the first
ballot. The Moderator shall declare an election when a
nominee receives a majority of the votes cast by the
commissioners present and voting.

**1-8.** The Moderator-Elect shall be placed in nomination for
Moderator at the subsequent General Assembly. Additional
nominations may be made from the floor.

**ARTICLE II. THE MODERATOR**

**2-1.** The Moderator shall preside at all sessions of the
Assembly except when he may invite the Moderator-Elect
(Vice-Moderator), a former Moderator, or another commissioner
to act temporarily as the presiding officer.

<a id="ga47-p629"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=631 printed_page=629 -->

**2-2.** No change.

**2-3.** Any former Moderator attending a General Assembly
shall have the privileges of the floor, may participate in debate,
and, if he is a commissioner, may vote.

_Approved by Georgia Foothills Presbytery at its stated meeting, January 26,_
_2019_
_Attested by /s/ TE Don Aldin, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 17 from Houston Metro Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 32-8 to Allow for Video Testimony by Witnesses”

**Whereas,** an important aspect of any case of process is the gathering and

presenting of evidence, including testimony produced by witnesses;

**Whereas**, testimony by witnesses before the court can be inconvenient due

to geographic distance or other circumstances;

**Whereas**, it is important that witnesses be examined with an opportunity to

be cross-examined by both parties ( _cf._ _BCO_ 32-13); and

**Whereas**, technology has made available the possibility of having a virtual

presence with video and audio interaction even when a person is
geographically distant from a meeting or court room, and the courts of
the civil magistrate allow for video testimony by witnesses;

**Therefore, be it resolved**, that the General Assembly of the Presbyterian

Church in America amend _BCO_ 32-8 with the following (additions are
underlined, deletions are struck through):

**32-8** . When the offense with which an accused person stands
charged took place at a distance, ~~and~~ or it is inconvenient for
the witnesses to appear before the court having jurisdiction,
that court may either (a) appoint a commission of its body, or
(b) request the coordinate court contiguous to the place where
the facts occurred, to take the testimony for it, or (c) have the
testimony taken by videoconference, which shall employ
technical means that ensure that all persons participating in the
meeting can see and hear each other at the same time, and
which allows for live cross-examination by both parties. The
accused shall always have reasonable notice of the time and
place of the meeting of this commission or coordinate court.

_Adopted by Houston Metro Presbytery at its stated meeting, January 18, 2019_
_Attested by /s/ TE Lou Veiga, stated clerk_

<a id="ga47-p630"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=632 printed_page=630 -->

##### OVERTURE 18 from Houston Metro Presbytery (CCB, OC)

“Amend _RAO_ 11-5 to Prevent Confusion and Possible
Contradictory Actions on Overtures by Multiple Committees
of Commissioners”

**Whereas,** Presbyteries (ordinarily) and other bodies (in some circumstances)

request action by the General Assembly on specific matters by means of
an Overture ( _RAO_ 11-4);

**Whereas**, an Overture may contain subject matter that could appear pertinent

to more than one Committee of Commissioners;

**Whereas**, currently _RAO_ 11-5 states that “the Stated Clerk shall refer all

overtures requesting amendment of the _Book of Church Order_ or the
_Rules of Assembly Operations_ to the Committee on Constitutional
Business for its advice to the Overtures Committee”;

**Whereas**, the term “shall” in the _BCO_ delineates an obligatory rather than a

permissible action, requiring the Stated Clerk to refer such overtures to
