the Committee on Constitutional Business, which then shall forward
such overtures (along with CCB’s advice) to the Overtures Committee;

**Whereas**, if an Overture requesting amendment to the _BCO_ or the _RAO_ was

also referred to another Committee of Commissioners (CoC), the result
could be confusion as to which Committee’s recommendation, whether
Overtures or another CoC, should be taken up by the Assembly; and

**Whereas**, it is possible that in such instance contradictory recommendations

could be brought to the Assembly by two (or more) CoCs;

**Therefore, be it resolved**, that the General Assembly of the Presbyterian

Church in America amend _RAO_ 11-5 with the following additions
(additions are underlined):

**11-5.** Upon receipt the Stated Clerk shall refer all overtures
requesting amendment of the _Book of Church Order_ or the
_Rules of Assembly Operations_ to the Committee on
Constitutional Business for its advice to the Overtures
Committee. Upon receipt, the Stated Clerk shall forward all
overtures concerning presbytery boundaries or a new
presbytery to the permanent Committee on Mission to North
America. Any overture, other than an overture proposing
amendment to the Constitution (which shall be referred to the
Committee on Constitutional Business for its advice to the
Overtures Committee), having to do with the nature or
responsibilities of a permanent Committee or Agency shall be
referred by the Clerk to the appropriate permanent Committee
or agency ora d interim committee. All other overtures shall
be referred to the Overtures Committee. No overture shall be

referred for consideration and recommendation to more than

<a id="ga47-p631"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=633 printed_page=631 -->

one Committee of Commissioners, permanent Committee or
Agency or ad interim committee; an overture, however, may
be referred for informational purposes only to another
Committee of Commissioners, permanent Committee or
Agency or ad interim committee. All overtures shall be
published in the _Commissioner Handbook_ with reference for
consideration indicated.

_Adopted by Houston Metro Presbytery at its stated meeting, January 18, 2019_
_Attested by /s/ TE Lou Veiga, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 19 from Rocky Mountain Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 14-1.10, 14-1.11, and 14-1.12 to Allow
Non-ordained Persons to serve on Committees and Boards”

[Editorial Note: This overture is identical to Overture 8 from Ohio Valley
Presbytery.]

**Whereas**, the Presbyterian Church in America believes Christ has called his

church to be a “spiritual commonwealth” ( _BCO_ 3-1), in which members
elect godly men to be ordained as elders who are given the power of
jurisdiction as they act jointly in the courts of the church ( _BCO_ 3-2); and

**Whereas**, a robustly complementarian perspective affirms the oversight of

these elders and the proper, vital, and full expression of the gifts given
by Christ to men and women for his service; and

**Whereas**, vigorous participation of women in the life of the early church is

attested by the New Testament: they helped select (Acts 1:14) and train
(Acts 18:26) leaders of the church, prophesied (Acts 21:8-9), served the
church through deeds of mercy in vital ways (Acts 9:36–41), were
instrumental in the planting of churches (Acts 16:12–15; 17:4,12) and
other missionary endeavors (Acts 18:1f), and were acknowledged as
important leaders for the church (Rom. 16:3-4); and

**Whereas**, as part of the calling of church leaders to “equip the saints for the

work of ministry for building up the body of Christ” (Eph. 4:12), sessions
throughout the PCA identify gifted men and women to serve on
committees of the church, such as missions, building, and pastoral search
committees; and

**Whereas**, in contrast with a commission in the PCA, which is "authorized to

deliberate and conclude the business referred to it," committees are
appointed by courts “to examine, consider and report” to their

<a id="ga47-p632"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=634 printed_page=632 -->

constituting court concerning the matters that court delegates to them
( _BCO_ 15-1) and thus do not exercise governing authority; and

**Whereas**, in accordance with this principle, Permanent Committees

established by the General Assembly are “to serve and not to direct any
Church judicatories. They are not to establish policy, but rather execute
policy established by the General Assembly” ( _BCO_ 14-1.7); and

**Whereas**, these considerations suggest there is no impediment to an

unordained member in good standing in the PCA serving on a Permanent
Committee of the General Assembly established in _BCO_ 14-1.12; and

**Whereas**, the 45th General Assembly adopted the recommendation of The

Ad Interim Committee on Women Serving in the Ministry of the Church
that stated, “sessions, presbyteries and the General Assembly strive to
develop, recognize, and utilize the gifts, skills, knowledge, and wisdom
of godly women in the local, regional, and national church, and
particularly consider overtures that would allow qualified women to
serve on appropriate committees and agencies within the church”;

**Therefore be it resolved** to amend _The Book of_ Church _Order_ by deleting

14-1.10; by amending 14-1.11 as follows (and renumbering as 14-1.10);
by adding a new 14-1.11; and by amending 14-1.12 as follows:

[Proposed additions underlined and deletions noted by strike out.]

_**BCO**_ **14-1**

10. ~~The committees are to be established on the basis of an~~

~~equal number between teaching and ruling elders.~~

~~11.~~ 10. A Nominating Committee shall be comprised of one

representative elected by each Presbytery in the following
manner. Each Presbytery shall be assigned to a class by the
stated clerk based on its date of formation. The members

shall serve in classes of three year terms, alternating
between ruling and teaching elders. When necessary,
unexpired terms shall be filled by an elder of the same
class, teaching or ruling.
This committee is to present all nominations for which
it is responsible to the next meeting of the Assembly from
a slate of ~~men~~ candidates nominated by the Presbyteries.
Presbyteries shall utilize the nominating forms provided by
the stated clerk for their nominations. Each presbytery may
present one teaching elder, ~~and~~ one ruling elder, and, when
applicable, one candidate who is not an elder to fill
openings that are designated for members at large for each

<a id="ga47-p633"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=635 printed_page=633 -->

committee or agency. Any non ordained person nominated
must meet the qualifications outlined in _BCO_ 14-1.11.
In addition to nominees for expired terms, the
Committee shall nominate for each permanent Committee
one ruling, ~~and~~ one teaching elder and a member-at-large
candidate as alternates to fill any vacancies that may occur
during the year. Each alternate should attend each meeting
and fill any vacancy necessary to meet a quorum. In
addition to the new nominees from the Presbyteries,
alternates not assuming any vacancies during a year will be
automatically considered by the Nominating Committee as
candidates for nomination to that same committee.

11. To be qualified for nomination and election to serve on a

Permanent Committee or Agency as a member at large, a
non-ordained candidate must be a member in good
standing of a Church in the Presbyterian Church in
America and must receive an endorsement from their

Session. The endorsement will consist of testimony
regarding the candidate’s Christian character, knowledge
of the Constitution, and promise of faithful service to the
General Assembly.

12. The Assembly permanent committees are the
Administrative Committee of the General Assembly,
Committee on Discipleship Ministries, Committee on
Mission to North America, Committee on Mission to the
World, and Committee on Reformed University Fellowship.
The Administrative Committee of the General

Assembly (AC) shall consist of twenty (20) members:
a. Eleven members, six elders and five members who are

not elders, shall be in classes elected through the
standard nomination and election procedure,
b. One member from each of the following program

committees or agencies:

1. Committee on Discipleship Ministries (CDM)
2. Covenant College (CC)
3. Covenant Theological Seminary (CTS)
4. Mission to North America (MNA)
5. Mission to the World (MTW)
6. PCA Foundation (PCAF)

<a id="ga47-p634"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=636 printed_page=634 -->

7. PCA Retirement & Benefits, Inc. (RBI)
8. Reformed University Fellowship (RUF)
9. Ridge Haven Conference Center (RH)
The eleven elected members ~~at large~~ shall serve a
term of four years. The chairman of the Administrative
Committee shall be one of its elected ordained
members ~~at large.~~
Each program committee and agency shall
designate its member each year at the last meeting of
the committee or board before the meeting of the
General Assembly. The chief administrative officers
of the program committees and agencies may attend
any meeting of the Administrative Committee. They
shall be entitled to the privilege of the floor but shall
not have a vote and must be excluded when an

executive session is called.
Committee on Discipleship Ministries, Committee
on Mission to North America, Committee on Mission
to the World, and Committee on Reformed University
Fellowship shall consist of fifteen (15) ~~men~~ members
divided into five classes of three ~~men~~ members ~~each,~~
~~with two men Being TEs and one RE or two men being~~
~~REs and one TE on alternate years.~~ Three classes will
have one TE, one RE and one member at large. One
class shall have one TE and two members at large and
one class shall have one RE and two members at
large. Each class shall be elected to serve five-year
terms. Committee on Discipleship Ministries,
Committee on Mission to North America, Committee
on Mission to the World, and Committee on Reformed
University Fellowship shall have one ruling elder, and
one teaching elder and one member at large as
alternates to fill any vacancy that may occur during the
year. A quorum for each Committees shall be eight (8)
members, with a majority of those present being elders.
The Chairman of each Committee shall be an elder.

Persons who have served for a full term, or for at
least two years of a partial term, on one of the
Assembly’s permanent committees or agencies shall
not be eligible for re-election to an Assembly
committee until one year has lapsed. (Exceptions may
be permitted in agency bylaws approved by the
Assembly).

<a id="ga47-p635"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=637 printed_page=635 -->

So that _BCO_ 14-1.10, 14-1.11, and 14-1.12 as amended would read:

10. A Nominating Committee shall be comprised of one

representative elected by each Presbytery in the following
manner. Each Presbytery shall be assigned to a class by the
stated clerk based on its date of formation. The members

shall serve in classes of three year terms, alternating
between ruling and teaching elders. When necessary,
unexpired terms shall be filled by an elder of the same
class, teaching or ruling.
This committee is to present all nominations for which
it is responsible to the next meeting of the Assembly from
a slate of candidates nominated by the Presbyteries.
Presbyteries shall utilize the nominating forms provided by
the stated clerk for their nominations. Each presbytery may
present one teaching elder, one ruling elder, and, when
applicable, one candidate who is not an elder to fill
openings that are designated for members at large for each
committee or agency. Any non-ordained person
nominated must meet the qualifications outlined in _BCO_
14-1.11.

In addition to nominees for expired terms, the
Committee shall nominate for each permanent Committee
one ruling, one teaching elder and a member-at largecandidate as alternates to fill any vacancies that may occur
during the year. Each alternate should attend each meeting
and fill any vacancy necessary to meet a quorum. In
addition to the new nominees from the Presbyteries,
alternates not assuming any vacancies during a year will be
automatically considered by the Nominating Committee as
candidates for nomination to that same committee.

11. To be qualified for nomination and election to serve on a

Permanent Committee or Agency as a member at large, a
non-ordained candidate must be a member in good
standing of a Church in the Presbyterian Church in
America and must receive an endorsement from their

Session. The endorsement will consist of testimony
regarding the candidate’s Christian character, knowledge of
the Constitution, and promise of faithful service to the
General Assembly.

<a id="ga47-p636"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=638 printed_page=636 -->

12. The Assembly permanent committees are the Administrative

Committee of the General Assembly, Committee on
Discipleship Ministries, Committee on Mission to North
America, Committee on Mission to the World, and
Committee on Reformed University Fellowship.
The Administrative Committee of the General

Assembly (AC) shall consist of twenty (20) members:
a. Eleven members, six elders and five members who are

not elders, shall be in classes elected through the
standard nomination and election procedure,
b. One member from each of the following program

committees or agencies:

1. Committee on Discipleship Ministries (CDM)
2. Covenant College (CC)
3. Covenant Theological Seminary (CTS)
4. Mission to North America (MNA)
5. Mission to the World (MTW)
6. PCA Foundation (PCAF)
7. PCA Retirement & Benefits, Inc. (RBI)
8. Reformed University Fellowship (RUF)
9. Ridge Haven Conference Center (RH)
The eleven elected members shall serve a term of

four years. The chairman of the Administrative
Committee shall be one of its elected ordained

members.
Each program committee and agency shall
designate its member each year at the last meeting of
the committee or board before the meeting of the
General Assembly. The chief administrative officers
of the program committees and agencies may attend
any meeting of the Administrative Committee. They
shall be entitled to the privilege of the floor but shall
not have a vote and must be excluded when an

executive session is called.
Committee on Discipleship Ministries, Committee
on Mission to North America, Committee on Mission
to the World, and Committee on Reformed University
Fellowship shall consist of fifteen (15) members
divided into five classes of three members. Three
classes will have one TE, one RE and one member at
large. One class shall have one TE and two members
at large and one class shall have one RE and two

<a id="ga47-p637"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=639 printed_page=637 -->

members at large. Each class shall be elected to serve
five-year terms. Committee on Discipleship Ministries,
Committee on Mission to North America, Committee
on Mission to the World, and Committee on Reformed
University Fellowship shall have one ruling elder, one
teaching elder and one member at large as alternates to
fill any vacancy that may occur during the year. A
quorum for each Committees shall be eight (8)
members, with a majority of those present being elders.
The Chairman of each Committee shall be an elder.
Persons who have served for a full term, or for at
least two years of a partial term, on one of the
Assembly’s permanent committees or agencies shall
not be eligible for re-election to an Assembly
committee until one year has lapsed. (Exceptions may
be permitted in agency bylaws approved by the
Assembly).

**Further, be it resolved** that the Stated Clerk shall be responsible for

developing the nomination forms necessary to implement this process
and shall be responsible for converting the composition of each class as
each class term expires.

_Adopted by Rocky Mountain Presbytery at its stated meeting, January 24, 2019_
_Attested by TE Milan Norgauer, stated clerk._

##### OVERTURE 20 from Nashville Presbytery (to AC, OC)

“Establish Study Committee on Domestic Abuse,” (Version 4)

**Whereas**, the sins of domestic abuse and sexual assault are expressly

forbidden (2 Timothy 3:2-5; Jude 7; Exodus 21:15; Deuteronomy 22:2527; also _WLC_ 135 and 139); and

**Whereas**, these sins are especially heinous because they are “against the

express letter of the law,” “break many commandments,” “admit of no
reparation,” and often involve various other aggravations ( _WLC_ 151);
and

**Whereas**, these sins are grievously prevalent in our society; [7] and

7 For example, the National Domestic Violence Hotline reports: “More than 1 in 3 women

(35.6%) and more than 1 in 4 men (28.5%) in the United States have experienced rape,
physical violence and/or stalking by an intimate partner in their lifetime” (available at
http://www.thehotline.org/resources/statistics/, accessed 6/15/18). For further sobering

<a id="ga47-p638"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=640 printed_page=638 -->

**Whereas**, these sins often produce in the victims a complex response of

denial along with immense fear and shame that persist with varying
severity throughout life (e.g., 2 Samuel 13:1-22); and

**Whereas**, it is the responsibility of the elders of the church to exercise their

authority, severally and jointly, to shepherd those who are under their
care by guarding against such sinful behavior as much as possible, by
wisely caring for those victimized by the sins of others, and by
appropriately disciplining those guilty of such sins toward a full
repentance; and

**Whereas,** these sins have been a concerning subject of attention not only in

other denominations but even in the proceedings of the 46th General
Assembly; and

**Whereas**, the 42nd General Assembly previously exhorted the PCA and its

courts to respond boldly against one form of these sins, namely the
sexual abuse of children; and

**Whereas**, no standardized resources for pastoral care on these sins and their

complex consequences exist for officers, church courts, and agencies in
the PCA; and

**Whereas**, no clear guidelines for policy or practice with regard to these sins

exist for the officers, church courts, and agencies in the PCA that would
protect individuals and congregations; and

**Whereas**, the Rules of Discipline make no consideration for the unique

heinousness of these sins and their practical effect on victims;

**Therefore, be it resolved** that Nashville Presbytery hereby overtures the

47th General Assembly of the PCA to form a committee to study:

(1) the nature of domestic abuse and sexual assault;
(2) the pastoral resources available for the care of survivors of these sins;
(3) the best practices and policy guidelines for elders, church courts, and

agencies for protecting against these sins and for responding to them;
(4) the possibility of changes to the Rules of Discipline that would

appropriately protect victims of these sins within a biblical process;
and

(5) the possible need for repentance as a church; and

statistics on sexual violence see the National Center for Injury Prevention and Controls
report entitled _Sexual Violence Surveillance: Uniform Definitions and Recommended Data_
_Elements_ (Atlanta: Center for Disease Control, 2014; available online at
[http://www.cdc.gov/violenceprevention/pdf/sv_surveillance_ definitionsl -2009-a.pdf](http://www.cdc.gov/violenceprevention/pdf/sv_surveillance_%20definitionsl%20-2009-a.pdf) ).

<a id="ga47-p639"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=641 printed_page=639 -->

**Be it further resolved** that the committee shall be appointed by the

moderator, and shall include among its members or advisory members at
least two subject-matter experts and at least two women; and

**Be it further resolved** that the budget for the study committee be set at

$15,000/year and that funds be derived from gifts to the AC designated
for that purpose, with General Assembly appealing to the various
Presbyteries to fund this initiative via such gifts.

**Be it further resolved** that the committee shall present their findings and

recommendations for adoption by the 48th General Assembly.

_Adopted by Nashville Presbytery at its stated meeting, November 13, 2018_
_Attested by /s/ TE R. Neil Spence, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 21 from Nashville Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _Book of Church Orde_ r 14-1.10., 14-1.11., and 14-1.12”
(Version 3)

**Whereas**, the Presbyterian Church in America believes Christ has called his

church to be a “spiritual commonwealth” ( _BCO_ 3-1), in which members
elect godly men to be ordained as elders who are given the power of
jurisdiction as they act jointly in the courts of the church ( _BCO_ 3-2); and

**Whereas**, a robustly complementarian perspective affirms the oversight of

these elders and the proper, vital and full expression of the gifts given by
Christ to men and women for his service; and

**Whereas** ereas, vigorous participation of women in the life of the early

church is attested by the New Testament: they helped select (Acts 1:14)
and train (Acts 18:26) leaders of the church, prophesied (Acts 21:8-9),
served the church through deeds of mercy in vital ways (Acts 9:36–41),
were instrumental in the planting of churches (Acts 16:12–15; 17:4,12)
and other missionary endeavors (Acts 18:1f), and were acknowledged as
important leaders for the church (Rom. 16:3-4); and

**Whereas**, as part of the calling of church leaders to “equip the saints for the

work of ministry for building up the body of Christ” (Eph. 4:12), sessions
throughout the PCA identify gifted men and women to serve on
committees of the church, such as missions, building, and pastoral search
committees; and

**Whereas**, in contrast with a commission in the PCA, which is "authorized to

deliberate and conclude the business referred to it," committees are
appointed by courts “to examine, consider and report” to their
constituting court concerning the matters that court delegates to them
( _BCO_ 15-1) and thus do not exercise governing authority; and

<a id="ga47-p640"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=642 printed_page=640 -->

**Whereas**, in accordance with this principle, Permanent Committees

established by the General Assembly are “to serve and not to direct any
Church judicatories. They are not to establish policy, but rather execute
policy established by the General Assembly” ( _BCO_ 14-1.7); and

**Whereas**, these considerations suggest there is no impediment to an

unordained member in good standing in the PCA serving on a Permanent
Committee of the General Assembly established in _BCO_ 14-1.12; and

**Whereas**, the 45th General Assembly adopted the recommendation of The

Ad Interim Committee on Women Serving in the Ministry of the Church
that stated “sessions, presbyteries and the General Assembly strive to
develop, recognize, and utilize the gifts, skills, knowledge, and wisdom
of godly women in the local, regional, and national church, and
particularly consider overtures that would allow qualified women to
serve on appropriate committees and agencies within the church”;

**Therefore be it resolved** to amend _The Book of Church Order_ by deleting

14-1.10; by amending 14-1.11 as follows (and renumbering as 14-1.10);
by adding a new 14-1.11; and by amending 14-1.12 as follows:

[Proposed additions underlined and deletions noted by strike out.]

10. ~~The committees are to be established on the basis of an~~

~~equal number between teaching and ruling elders.~~

~~11.~~ 10. A Nominating Committee shall be comprised of one
representative elected by each Presbytery in the following
manner. Each Presbytery shall be assigned to a class by the
Stated Clerk based on its date of formation. The members shall

serve in classes of three year terms, alternating between ruling
and teaching elders. When necessary, unexpired terms shall be
filled by an elder of the same class, teaching or ruling. This
committee is to present all nominations for which it is
responsible to the next meeting of the Assembly from a slate
of ~~men~~ candidates nominated by the Presbyteries. Presbyteries
shall utilize the nominating forms provided by the Stated Clerk
for their nominations. Each presbytery may present one
teaching elder, ~~and~~ one ruling elder, and, when applicable, one
candidate who is not an elder to fill openings that are
designated for members at large for each committee or agency.

Any non ordained person nominated must meet the
qualifications outlined in _BCO_ 14-1.11.
In addition to nominees for expired terms, the Committee
shall nominate for each permanent Committee one ruling, ~~and~~

<a id="ga47-p641"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=643 printed_page=641 -->

one teaching elder and a member at large candidate as
alternates to fill any vacancies that may occur during the year.
Each alternate should attend each meeting and fill any vacancy
necessary to meet a quorum. In addition to the new nominees
from the Presbyteries, alternates not assuming any vacancies
during a year will be automatically considered by the
Nominating Committee as candidates for nomination to that
same committee.

11. To be qualified for nomination and election to serve on a

Permanent Committee or Agency as a member at large, a non
ordained candidate must be a member in good standing of a
Church in the Presbyterian Church in America and must
receive an endorsement from their Session. The endorsement
will consist of testimony regarding the candidate’s Christian
character, knowledge of and adherence to the Constitution, and
promise of faithful service to the General Assembly.

12. The Assembly permanent committees are the Administrative
Committee of the General Assembly, Committee on
Discipleship Ministries, Committee on Mission to North
America, Committee on Mission to the World, and Committee
on Reformed University Fellowship.
The Administrative Committee of the General Assembly
(AC) shall consist of twenty (20) members:

a. Eleven members, six elders and five members who

are not elders, shall be in classes elected through the
standard nomination and election procedure,
b. One member from each of the following program

committees or agencies:
1. Committee on Discipleship Ministries (CDM)
2. Covenant College (CC)
3. Covenant Theological Seminary (CTS)
4. Mission to North America (MNA)
5. Mission to the World (MTW)
6. PCA Foundation (PCAF)
7. PCA Retirement & Benefits, Inc. (RBI)
8. Reformed University Fellowship (RUF)
9. Ridge Haven Conference Center (RH)

<a id="ga47-p642"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=644 printed_page=642 -->

The eleven elected members ~~at large~~ shall serve a term of
four years. The chairman of the Administrative Committee
shall be one of its elected ordained members ~~at large.~~
Each program committee and agency shall designate its
member each year at the last meeting of the committee or board
before the meeting of the General Assembly. The chief
administrative officers of the program committees and
agencies may attend any meeting of the Administrative
Committee. They shall be entitled to the privilege of the floor
but shall not have a vote and must be excluded when an

executive session is called.

Committee on Discipleship Ministries, Committee on
Mission to North America, Committee on Mission to the
World, and Committee on Reformed University Fellowship
shall consist of fifteen (15) ~~men~~ members divided into five
classes of three ~~men~~ members ~~each, with two men Being TEs~~
~~and one RE or two men being REs and one TE on alternate~~
~~years.~~ Three classes will have one TE, one RE and one member
at large. One class shall have one TE and two members at large
and one class shall have one RE and two members at large.
Each class shall be elected to serve five-year terms. Committee
on Discipleship Ministries, Committee on Mission to North
America, Committee on Mission to the World, and Committee
on Reformed University Fellowship shall have one ruling
elder, ~~and~~ one teaching elder, and one member at large as
alternates to fill any vacancy that may occur during the year.
A quorum for each Committee shall be eight (8) members, with
a majority of those present being elders. The Chairman of each
Committee shall be an elder.

Persons who have served for a full term, or for at least two
years of a partial term, on one of the Assembly’s permanent
committees or agencies shall not be eligible for reelection to an
Assembly committee until one year has lapsed. (Exceptions
may be permitted in agency bylaws approved by the
Assembly).

So that _BCO_ 14-1.10, 14-1.11, and 14-1.12 as amended would

read:

10. A Nominating Committee shall be comprised of one
representative elected by each Presbytery in the following
manner. Each Presbytery shall be assigned to a class by the

<a id="ga47-p643"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=645 printed_page=643 -->

Stated Clerk based on its date of formation. The members shall

serve in classes of three year terms, alternating between ruling
and teaching elders. When necessary, unexpired terms shall be
filled by an elder of the same class, teaching or ruling.
This committee is to present all nominations for which it is
responsible to the next meeting of the Assembly from a slate
of candidates nominated by the Presbyteries. Presbyteries shall
utilize the nominating forms provided by the Stated Clerk for
their nominations. Each presbytery may present one teaching
elder, one ruling elder, and, when applicable, one candidate
who is not an elder to fill openings that are designated for
members at large for each committee or agency. Any nonordained person nominated must meet the qualifications
outlined in _BCO_ 14-1.11.

In addition to nominees for expired terms, the Committee
shall nominate for each permanent Committee one ruling, one
teaching elder and a member at large candidate as alternates to
fill any vacancies that may occur during the year. Each
alternate should attend each meeting and fill any vacancy
necessary to meet a quorum. In addition to the new nominees
from the Presbyteries, alternates not assuming any vacancies
during a year will be automatically considered by the
Nominating Committee as candidates for nomination to that
same committee.

11. To be qualified for nomination and election to serve on a
Permanent Committee or Agency as a member at large, anon ordained candidate must be a member in good standing of a
Church in the Presbyterian Church in America and must
receive an endorsement from their Session. The endorsement
will consist of testimony regarding the candidate’s Christian
character, knowledge of and adherence to the Constitution, and
promise of faithful service to the General Assembly.

12. The Assembly permanent committees are the Administrative
Committee of the General Assembly, Committee on
Discipleship Ministries, Committee on Mission to North
America, Committee on Mission to the World, and Committee on
Reformed University Fellowship.
The Administrative Committee of the General Assembly
(AC) shall consist of twenty (20) members:

<a id="ga47-p644"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=646 printed_page=644 -->

a. Eleven members, six elders and five members who are

not elders, shall be in classes elected through the
standard nomination and election procedure,
b. One member from each of the following program

committees or agencies:
1. Committee on Discipleship Ministries (CDM)
2. Covenant College (CC)
3. Covenant Theological Seminary (CTS)
4. Mission to North America (MNA)
5. Mission to the World (MTW)
6. PCA Foundation (PCAF)
7. PCA Retirement & Benefits, Inc. (RBI)
8. Reformed University Fellowship (RUF)
9. Ridge Haven Conference Center (RH)
The eleven elected members shall serve a term of four
years. The chairman of the Administrative Committee shall be
one of its elected ordained members.
Each program committee and agency shall designate its
member each year at the last meeting of the committee or board
before the meeting of the General Assembly. The chief
administrative officers of the program committees and
agencies may attend any meeting of the Administrative
Committee. They shall be entitled to the privilege of the floor
but shall not have a vote and must be excluded when an

executive session is called.
Committee on Discipleship Ministries, Committee on
Mission to North America, Committee on Mission to the
World, and Committee on Reformed University Fellowship
shall consist of fifteen (15) members divided into five classes
of three ~~m~~ embers. Three classes will have one TE, one RE and
one member at large. One class shall have one TE and two
members at large and one class shall have one RE and two
members at large. Each class shall be elected to serve five-year
terms. Committee on Discipleship Ministries, Committee on
Mission to North America, Committee on Mission to the
World, and Committee on Reformed University Fellowship
shall have one ruling elder, one teaching elder and one member
at large as alternates to fill any vacancy that may occur during
the year. A quorum for each Committee shall be eight (8)
members, with a majority of those present being elders. The
Chairman of each Committee shall be an elder.
Persons who have served for a full term, or for at least two
years of a partial term, on one of the Assembly’s permanent

<a id="ga47-p645"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=647 printed_page=645 -->

committees or agencies shall not be eligible for reelection to an
Assembly committee until one year has lapsed. (Exceptions
may be permitted in agency bylaws approved by the
Assembly).

**Further, be it resolved** that the Stated Clerk shall be responsible for

developing the nomination forms necessary to implement this process
and shall be responsible for converting the composition of each class as
each class term expires.

_Adopted by Nashville Presbytery at its stated meeting, November 13, 2018_
_Attested by /s/ TE R. Neil Spence, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 22 from Philadelphia Metro West Presbytery (to OC)

“Urge Assembly to Adopt Overture 4 Regarding Nashville Statement”

**Be it resolved that** Philadelphia Metro West Presbytery requests the 2019

PCA General Assembly to adopt Overture 4 from Calvary Presbytery:
“Declare the Council on Biblical Manhood & Womanhood’s ‘Nashville
Statement’ on Biblical Sexuality as a Biblically Faithful Declaration"
(Exhibit A, attached).

_Adopted by Philadelphia Metro West Presbytery at its stated meeting,_
_February 23, 2019_
_Attested by /s/ RE Paul Rich, stated clerk_

**EXHIBIT A**

##### OVERTURE 4 from Calvary Presbytery (to OC)

“Declare the Council on Biblical Manhood & Womanhood’s ‘Nashville
Statement’ on Biblical Sexuality as a Biblically Faithful Declaration”

**Whereas** the Council on Biblical Manhood & Womanhood’s "Nashville

Statement" declares:

<a id="ga47-p646"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=648 printed_page=646 -->

**Article 1**
WE AFFIRM that God has designed marriage to be a
covenantal, sexual, procreative, lifelong union of one man and
one woman, as husband and wife, and is meant to signify the
covenant love between Christ and his bride the church.
WE DENY that God has designed marriage to be a
homosexual, polygamous, or polyamorous relationship. We
also deny that marriage is a mere human contract rather than a
covenant made before God.

**Article 2**
WE AFFIRM that God’s revealed will for all people is chastity
outside of marriage and fidelity within marriage.
WE DENY that any affections, desires, or commitments ever
justify sexual intercourse before or outside marriage; nor do
they justify any form of sexual immorality.

**Article 3**

WE AFFIRM that God created Adam and Eve, the first human
beings, in his own image, equal before God as persons, and
distinct as male and female.
WE DENY that the divinely ordained differences between
male and female render them unequal in dignity or worth.

**Article 4**
WE AFFIRM that divinely ordained differences between male
and female reflect God’s original creation design and are meant
for human good and human flourishing.
WE DENY that such differences are a result of the Fall or are
a tragedy to be overcome.

**Article 5**

WE AFFIRM that the differences between male and female
reproductive structures are integral to God’s design for selfconception as male or female.
WE DENY that physical anomalies or psychological
conditions nullify the God-appointed link between biological
sex and self-conception as male or female.

**Article 6**
WE AFFIRM that those born with a physical disorder of sex
development are created in the image of God and have dignity
and worth equal to all other image-bearers. They are
acknowledged by our Lord Jesus in his words about “eunuchs
who were born that way from their mother's womb.” With all
others they are welcome as faithful followers of Jesus Christ
and should embrace their biological sex insofar as it may be
known.

<a id="ga47-p647"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=649 printed_page=647 -->

WE DENY that ambiguities related to a person’s biological sex
render one incapable of living a fruitful life in joyful obedience
to Christ.

**Article 7**
WE AFFIRM that self-conception as male or female should be
defined by God’s holy purposes in creation and redemption as
revealed in Scripture.
WE DENY that adopting a homosexual or transgender selfconception is consistent with God’s holy purposes in creation
and redemption.

**Article 8**
WE AFFIRM that people who experience sexual attraction for
the same sex may live a rich and fruitful life pleasing to God
through faith in Jesus Christ, as they, like all Christians, walk
in purity of life.
WE DENY that sexual attraction for the same sex is part of the
natural goodness of God’s original creation, or that it puts a
person outside the hope of the gospel.

**Article 9**
WE AFFIRM that sin distorts sexual desires by directing them
away from the marriage covenant and toward sexual
immorality— a distortion that includes both heterosexual and
homosexual immorality.
WE DENY that an enduring pattern of desire for sexual
immorality justifies sexually immoral behavior.

**Article 10**
WE AFFIRM that it is sinful to approve of homosexual
immorality or transgenderism and that such approval
constitutes an essential departure from Christian faithfulness
and witness.
WE DENY that the approval of homosexual immorality or
transgenderism is a matter of moral indifference about which
otherwise faithful Christians should agree to disagree.

**Article 11**
WE AFFIRM our duty to speak the truth in love at all times,
including when we speak to or about one another as male or
female.
WE DENY any obligation to speak in such ways that dishonor
God’s design of his image bearers as male and female.

**Article 12**
WE AFFIRM that the grace of God in Christ gives both
merciful pardon and transforming power, and that this pardon

<a id="ga47-p648"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=650 printed_page=648 -->

and power enable a follower of Jesus to put to death sinful
desires and to walk in a manner worthy of the Lord.
WE DENY that the grace of God in Christ is insufficient to
forgive all sexual sins and to give power for holiness to every
believer who feels drawn into sexual sin.

**Article 13**
WE AFFIRM that the grace of God in Christ enables sinners to
forsake transgender self- conceptions and by divine
forbearance to accept the God-ordained link between one’s
biological sex and one’s self-conception as male or female.
WE DENY that the grace of God in Christ sanctions selfconceptions that are at odds with God’s revealed will.

**Article 14**

WE AFFIRM that Christ Jesus has come into the world to save
sinners and that through Christ’s death and resurrection
forgiveness of sins and eternal life are available to every person
who repents of sin and trusts in Christ alone as Savior,
WE DENY that the Lord’s arm is too short to save or that any
sinner is beyond his reach.

**Therefore be it resolved** that the Calvary Presbytery hereby overture the

47th General Assembly and asks it to declare the Council on Biblical
Manhood & Womanhood’s "Nashville Statement" on biblical sexuality
as a biblically faithful declaration and refer the “Nashville Statement”
to the Committee on Discipleship Ministries for inclusion and
promotion among its denominational teaching materials.

**Grounds:**
“The Nashville Statement,” carefully worked out by the Council on Biblical
Manhood and Womanhood in 2017, is a timely statement that speaks into the
current confusion surrounding gender and sexuality within the evangelical
church (including the PCA) and the broader culture. Signed by many leaders
within the Reformed and evangelical world, it is a balanced and biblically
faithful attempt to “speak the truth in love” (Eph. 4:15) on one of the most
pivotal and controversial issues facing the church today.

Additionally, “The Nashville Statement” clearly reiterates the truth of
foundational passages of Scripture and our confessional understanding of
marriage, gender, and sexual ethics. It affirms God’s good purpose in
ordaining the binary nature of gender while bestowing His image on mankind
(Gen. 1:27), His design for marriage between one biological man and one
biological woman (Gen. 1:23, 24), and the sinfulness of homosexual and
transgender desire as well as conduct (Mat. 15:18, 19; I Cor. 6:9-11). In

<a id="ga47-p649"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=651 printed_page=649 -->

addition to affirming these historic, orthodox truths, the Nashville Statement
also lifts up the hope of the gospel to the sexual struggler, pointing the reader
to our Lord Jesus Christ as “the double cure,” saving us both from sin’s guilt
and power. Therefore, the PCA would do well to commend “The Nashville
Statement” and prepare it for distribution through Committee on
Discipleship Ministries.

_Adopted by Calvary Presbytery at its stated meeting, October 25, 2018._
_Attested by /s/ RE Melton L. Duncan, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 23 from Central Carolina Presbytery (to IRC)

“Withdraw from the National Association of Evangelicals”

**Whereas** the Presbyterian Church in America is a member of the National

Association of Evangelicals (NAE); and

**Whereas** the label “evangelical” has come to designate political distinctives

as much or more than theological commitments and distinctives; [8] and

**Whereas** Chapter 31 of the _Westminster Confession of Faith_ states that

“Synods and councils are to handle, or conclude nothing, but that which
is ecclesiastical: and are not to intermeddle with civil affairs which
concern the commonwealth, unless by way of humble petition in cases
extraordinary; or, by way of advice, for satisfaction of conscience, if they
be thereunto required by the civil magistrate.”; and

**Whereas** PCA _BCO_ 3-3 states that “The sole functions of the Church, as a

kingdom and government distinct from the civil commonwealth, are to
proclaim, to administer, and to enforce the law of Christ revealed in the
Scriptures.”; and

**Whereas** the Scriptures clearly teach that Christians should be stewards of

God’s creation, care for sojourners and aliens, and promote matters of
civil justice, and members of the PCA may have legitimate differences
of opinion on the most effective political policies to address these
matters; and

**Whereas** the NAE has stated that it intends not only to advocate for biblical

values and ethics in general, but that “we must advocate for political
policies;” [9] and

8 Benjamin Huskinson. “The Missing Link between Evangelicals and Trump.” The[American-Interest.com. (https://www.the-american-interest.com/2018/11/23/the-missing-](https://www.the-american-interest.com/2018/11/23/the-missing-link-between-evangelicals-and-trump/)
[link-between-evangelicals-and-trump/)](https://www.the-american-interest.com/2018/11/23/the-missing-link-between-evangelicals-and-trump/)
9 National Association of Evangelicals. “For the Health of a Nation: An Evangelical Call to
[Civic Responsibility.” NAE.net (https://www.nae.net/wp-content/uploads/2018/09/For-the-](https://www.nae.net/wp-content/uploads/2018/09/For-the-Health-of-the-Nation_spreads.pdf)
[Health-of-the-Nation_spreads.pdf)](https://www.nae.net/wp-content/uploads/2018/09/For-the-Health-of-the-Nation_spreads.pdf)

<a id="ga47-p650"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=652 printed_page=650 -->

**Whereas** the NAE has frequently intermeddled in civil affairs, by publicly

pushing for action on the environment [10], immigration [11], and has changed
its original stance on the death penalty [12] ; and

**Whereas** the NAE has recently passed a motion entitled “Fairness for All” [13]

which, in advocating for a political compromise regarding sexual
orientation, gender identity, and religious freedom, goes beyond Chapter
31 of the _Westminster Confession of Faith_ ; and

**Whereas** the NAE claims to speak for all of its members and to be their voice

in Washington; and

**Whereas** these are only a few of many examples of the NAE’s continuing

practice of intermeddling in civil affairs; and

**Whereas** our sister denomination the RPCNA earlier withdrew from the

NAE in 2009 citing “President Leith Anderson’s participation in the
meeting between Christians and Muslims where the document ‘Loving
God and Neighbor Together: A Christian Response to “A Common
Word Between Us and You”’ was approved and signed,” and noting that
“the document is clearly based on an unbiblical premise. It falsely
assumes that Christianity and Islam approach the same God, but in
different ways.” [14] ; and`

**Whereas** no other NAPARC denominations are members of the NAE; the

Southern Baptist Convention is not a member of the NAE; the Lutheran
Church-Missouri Synod is not a member of the NAE; no Anglican bodies
are members of the NAE; no predominantly African American
denominations are members of the NAE; no mainline denominations are
members of the NAE; the Roman Catholic Church is not a member of
the NAE; and no Orthodox communions are members of the NAE; and

**Whereas** the Presbyterian Church in America, as such, has no need of the

benefits of membership in the NAE, especially of having a voice in
Washington championing political concerns;

10 [“NAE Releases Poverty, Creation Care Document” (https://www.nae.net/nae-releases-](https://www.nae.net/nae-releases-poverty-creation-care-document/)
[poverty-creation-care-document/)](https://www.nae.net/nae-releases-poverty-creation-care-document/)
11 “Evangelicals Push For Immigration Reform At Capitol Hill”

- [(https://www.huffingtonpost.com/2014/04/29/evangelicals](https://www.huffingtonpost.com/2014/04/29/evangelicals-immigration-reform_n_5235516.html) immigration
[reform_n_5235516.html)](https://www.huffingtonpost.com/2014/04/29/evangelicals-immigration-reform_n_5235516.html)
12 “The National Association of Evangelicals has changed its position on the death penalty”
[(https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/acts-of-faith/wp/2015/10/19/the-national-](https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/acts-of-faith/wp/2015/10/19/the-national-association-of-evangelicals-has-changed-its-position-on-the-death-penalty/)
[association-of-evangelicals-has-changed-its-position-on-the-death-penalty/)](https://www.washingtonpost.com/news/acts-of-faith/wp/2015/10/19/the-national-association-of-evangelicals-has-changed-its-position-on-the-death-penalty/)
13 “Boards back SOGI compromise”

[(https://world.wng.org/2018/12/boards_back_sogi_compromise/) “Fairness for All](https://world.wng.org/2018/12/boards_back_sogi_compromise/)
[Motion” (https://world.wng.org/sites/default/files/assets/NAEBoardResolution_0.pdf)](https://world.wng.org/sites/default/files/assets/NAEBoardResolution_0.pdf)

14 RPCNA IRC NAE Withdrawal Statement (permission granted to cite by the RPCNA

Interchurch Committee)

<a id="ga47-p651"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=653 printed_page=651 -->

**Therefore,** Central Carolina Presbytery hereby overtures the 47 [th] General

Assembly to withdraw the membership of the PCA in the NAE at the
soonest possible date.

_Adopted by Central Carolina Presbytery at its stated meeting, February 23, 2019_
_Attested by /s/ RE Flynt Jones, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 24 from Philadelphia Metro West Presbytery (to AC, CDM,

“Add Constitutional Precedence Statement to MNA, MTW, RUF
Policy Manuals” (Revised) CC, CTS, PCAF, PCA-RBI, RH)

[Note: An earlier version of this overture appeared online in early March.
At its May 4 meeting Philadelphia Metro West Presbytery revised the
first “Whereas” clause to clarify the Committees and Agencies involved.
There is no other change to the original overture.]

**Whereas** the Presbyterian Church in America (PCA) currently has five

Permanent Committees (Administrative Committee, Committee on
Discipleship Ministries, Mission to North America, Mission to the
World, and Reformed University Fellowship) and five Agencies
(Covenant College, Covenant Seminary, PCA Foundation, PCA Retirement
& Benefits, Inc., and Ridge Haven), and may in the future have other or
additional Permanent Committees and Agencies (all such current and
future Permanent Committees and Agencies, collectively, the
“Permanent Committees and Agencies”) [ _BCO_ 14.1.12; _RAO_ 14.1]; and

**Whereas** it is necessary for the Permanent Committees and Agencies to

function at all times under the authority of the Constitution (as defined
in _BCO_ Preface III, and which includes the _Westminster Confession of_
_Faith,_ the _Larger Catechism_ and _Shorter Catechism,_ and the _Book of_
_Church Order_, collectively, the “Constitution”); [ _BCO_ 14.1.7–8; 14.6;
Article V.G of the Corporate Bylaws]; and

**Whereas** it is standard operating procedure for organizations to write policy

manuals; and

**Whereas** the Permanent Committees and Agencies rightly and necessarily

have at times produced their respective policy manuals; and

**Whereas** it is essential for every policy manual of the Permanent

Committees and Agencies to remain subject to the Constitution; and

**Whereas** it is possible for unanticipated conflicts, potential conflicts, or

ambiguities to arise between Permanent Committee and Agency policy
manuals and the Constitution; and

<a id="ga47-p652"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=654 printed_page=652 -->

**Whereas** it is a well-recognized best practice for policy manuals of

organizations to explicitly state their subordination to organizational
constitutional documents and by-laws;

**Therefore, it is resolved** that the Philadelphia Metro West Presbytery hereby

overture the 47 [th] General Assembly to add the following statement to the
preface of all policy manuals of the Permanent Committees and
Agencies: “At every point, this entire manual is subordinate to the entire
Constitution of the Presbyterian Church in America—the _Westminster_
_Standards_ and the _Book of Church Order_ . If there exists any conflict
between the PCA’s constitutional documents and the content and
stipulations of this manual, the Constitution carries final authority.”
**Further resolved**, that any policy manual produced or amended by any of

the Permanent Committees and Agencies shall, before becoming
operational, be approved by its respective board and submitted to the
General Assembly for ratification through the usual process of the
reports of the Permanent Committees and Agencies.
**Further resolved**, that each of the Permanent Committees and Agencies

report back on the implementation of these actions to their respective
Committee of Commissioners at the 48 [th] General Assembly.

**Rationale** _**:**_

In order to carry out their responsibilities faithfully, Permanent Committees
and Agencies must be able to develop and propose policies to the General
Assembly for ratification. _BCO_ 14.1.7 indicates that “The Assembly's
committees are to serve and not to direct any Church judicatories. They are
not to establish policy, but rather execute policy established by the General
Assembly.” The only effective method to ensure Permanent Committee and
Agency policies yield to the PCA constitution, and the only way to ensure
that constitutional due process for staff of these permanent Committees and
Agencies in relation to written policies, is (1) to give the PCA constitution
explicitly stated priority in all policy manuals, and (2) to make policy
changes according to _BCO_ 14.1.7, which grants the General Assembly full
visibility into proposed policies (and any wording changes to policies) in
order to exercise its constitutionally-appointed powers of ratification.

The action of the 5 [th] General Assembly, for example, delivers a clear process
in relation to the Committee on Mission to the World (CMTW). This GA
action (1977, p. 72, 5–64, IV, 9) differentiated between the MTW policy
manual and the MTW handbook, when it determined “the policy manual to
be revised only by General Assembly procedures while changes in the
handbooks (which unavoidably need continual changes to meet changing

<a id="ga47-p653"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=655 printed_page=653 -->

situations with regard to such things as insurance, annuity, travel, etc.) be
reported annually to the MTW Committee of Commissioners (CoC) at the
General Assembly for their approval.” Usual process for policy changes then
requires that changes/additions be submitted by CMTW to the CoC for
ratification by the General Assembly, with a CMTW minute to this effect in
each instance. The CoC should then include these as recommendations in

their report to GA (including any minority reports).

In 2014, the 42 [nd] General Assembly of the PCA passed Overture 6, “Child
Protection in the PCA.” This overture directed the Permanent Agencies and
Committees “to review their policies, procedures and practices in the area of
child protection, including their response to child sexual abuse disclosures,
their faithfulness in reporting child sexual abuse to duly appointed proper
representatives of the God-ordained civil authorities, in accordance with
local laws, their care for survivors of child sexual abuse, and their future
plans to help educate the PCA on child sexual abuse, and all other areas of
response consistent with Scripture and the Constitution of the PCA and report
to the 43 [rd] General Assembly through the Administrative Committee, after it
has referred the matter to and received a report from the Cooperative
Ministries Committee.” We trust that the Permanent Agencies and
Committees have attended to these Overture 6 mandates, but to date no such
policies or revisions to policy manuals have been reviewed by the General
Assembly. A more recent example appeared in 2018, when MTW created
and implemented a sexual harassment policy, which has yet to receive
General Assembly ratification.

_Adopted by Philadelphia Metro West Presbytery at its stated meeting,_
_February 23, 2019_
_Attested by /s/ RE Paul Rich, stated clerk_
_Revision adopted by Philadelphia Metro West Presbytery, May 4, 2019_
_Attested by /s/ RE Paul Rich, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 25 from the Suncoast Florida Presbytery (to CDM)

“Support Christian Education”

**Whereas** the Presbyterian Church in America has seen fit to establish

Covenant College and Covenant Theological Seminary to educate
students in accordance with a Biblical worldview, including studies in
covenantal and Reformed theology, as well as studies touching upon our
Lord’s Great Commission and the extension of His Kingdom; and

<a id="ga47-p654"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=656 printed_page=654 -->

**Whereas** the Scriptures command faithful Biblical instruction be given to

God’s covenant children as well, those little ones being brought up in the
context of God’s covenant people and in accordance with their union
with Christ (Deut 6:7-15; Prov. 22:6; Mt. 19:14, 15; Eph. 6:4), making a
distinction between their education and that of unbelievers; and

**Whereas** the wisdom of God being expressed through His people reveals

itself to be “pure, then peaceable, gentle, open to reason, full of mercy
and good fruits, impartial, and sincere…” (James 3:17); and

**Whereas** a considerable number of parents in our churches, increasing in

their knowledge of God’s Word and works (Ps. 78:4-7), desire an
educational context for their covenant children consistent with God’s

Word, a context which encourages them to rejoice in Him as Creator,
Redeemer, and Lord over all; a context which encourages them to
exercise dominion as His image-bearers for His honor, one which will
enable them to be informed by and conformed to His Truth (John 17:17);
and

**Whereas** a considerable number of materialistic/secular world view ideas

(such as those touching upon gender identity issues, new definitions of
marriage and family, sexual boundaries, questions of who has ultimate
authority over the children, etc.) become increasingly politicized and
passed into law; and

**Whereas** Christian families are experiencing an increase of tension in the

public school arena as school policies, approved practices, classroom
instruction, discussion, and textbooks oppose and in some instances,
vilify, their Biblical convictions; indeed, sometimes resulting in legal
action on the part of the state (more recently in reference to gender
identity issues); and

**Whereas** parents of baptized children make vows to bring the child up in the

nurture and admonition of the Lord ( _BCO_ 56-5); and

**Whereas** the congregation also makes a vow to assist these parents in the

aforesaid endeavor ( _BCO_ 56-5); and

**Whereas** the elders and deacons of Christ’s church work together in

promoting the spiritual well-being of church members (including the
covenant children) encouraging, praying, and supporting if necessary
(and possible) what tends to their spiritual growth and good. ( _BCO_ 6-1,
3; _BCO_ 7-2; _BCO_ 8-3; _BCO_ 9-1; _BCO_ 12-5);

**Therefore** **be it resolved** that the Presbyteries, Sessions, and Diaconates of

the Presbyterian Church in America, be encouraged to embrace and
provide a supportive posture for Christian education in our churches and
communities by:

<a id="ga47-p655"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=657 printed_page=655 -->

a. Teaching on the nature, purpose, and tasks involved in bringing

covenant children up in the nurture and admonition of the Lord
(Ephesians 6:4).
b. Faithfully addressing the challenges Christian families face who

enroll their covenant children in public educational settings, seeking
to provide resources and support which will equip their students to
be salt and light, and to remain faithful to the truth as expressed in
Christ and His Word.

c. Considering/dialoguing with members in our covenant communities

regarding the privileges and benefits of a wholly Christian education
in which the primary goal is the development of a mind and heart
consistently conformed to the worldview of the Bible as it touches
upon all aspects of living in union with Christ- in God’s amazing
world under Christ’s reign (Genesis 1:1; Psalm 2:6, 11, 12; Psalm
19:1; Psalm 24; Romans 12:1, 2).
d. By offering, whenever possible, financial, spiritual and instructional

assistance helpful to those families expressing a desire to pursue a
wholly Christian education for their children.
e. By encouraging the establishment of sound Christian schools in our

churches and communities through prayer and support as our Lord
provides.

_Adopted by Suncoast Florida Presbytery at its stated meeting, February 12, 2019_
_Attested by /s/ TE David Stewart,_ _stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 26 from Tennessee Valley Presbytery (to AC, OC)

“Establish Study Committee on Domestic Abuse” (Version 5)

**Whereas**, the sins of domestic abuse and sexual assault are expressly

forbidden (2 Timothy 3:2-5; Jude 7; Exodus 21:15; Deuteronomy 22:2527; also _WLC_ 135 and 139); and

**Whereas**, these sins are especially heinous because they are “against the

express letter of the law,” “break many commandments,” “admit of no
reparation,” and often involve various other aggravations ( _WLC_ 151);
and

**Whereas**, these sins are grievously prevalent in our society; and

**Whereas**, these sins often produce in the victims a complex response of

denial along with immense fear and shame that persist with varying
severity throughout life (e.g., 2 Samuel 13:1-22); and

<a id="ga47-p656"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=658 printed_page=656 -->

**Whereas**, it is the responsibility of the elders of the church to exercise their

authority, severally and jointly, to shepherd those who are under their
care by guarding against such sinful behavior as much as possible, by
wisely caring for those victimized by the sins of others, and by
appropriately disciplining those guilty of such sins toward a full
repentance; and

**Whereas,** these sins have been a concerning subject of attention not only in

other denominations but even in the proceedings of the 46th General
Assembly; and

**Whereas**, the 42nd General Assembly previously exhorted the PCA and its

courts to respond boldly against one form of these sins, namely the
sexual abuse of children; and

**Whereas**, no standardized resources for pastoral care on these sins and their

complex consequences exist for officers, church courts, and agencies in
the PCA; and

**Whereas**, no clear guidelines for policy or practice with regard to these sins

exist for the officers, church courts, and agencies in the PCA that would
protect individuals and congregations; and

**Whereas**, the Rules of Discipline make no consideration for the unique

heinousness of these sins and their practical effect on victims;

**Therefore, be it resolved** to hereby overture the 47th General Assembly of

the PCA to form a committee to study:
(1) the nature of domestic abuse and sexual assault;
(2) pastoral resources available for the care of survivors of these sins;
(3) pastoral resources available on the perpetrators of these sins;
(4) the best practices and policy guidelines for elders, church courts, and

agencies for protecting against these sins and for responding to them;
(5) the possibility of changes to the Rules of Discipline that would

appropriately protect victims of these sins within a biblical process;
and
(6) the possible need for repentance as a church; and

**Be it further resolved** that the committee shall be appointed by the moderator,

and shall include among its members or advisory members at least two
subject-matter experts and at least two women; and

**Be it further resolved** that the budget for the study committee be set at

$15,000/year and that funds be derived from gifts to the AC designated
for that purpose; and

**Be it further resolved** that the committee shall present their findings and

recommendations for adoption by the 48th General Assembly.

_Adopted by Tennessee Valley Presbytery at its stated meeting, January 12, 2019_
_Attested by /s/ TE Jacob A. Bennett_

<a id="ga47-p657"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=659 printed_page=657 -->

##### OVERTURE 27 from Westminster Presbytery (to AC)

“Study Possibility of Remote Voting at General Assembly”

**Whereas**, the Presbyterian Church in America was founded as a “grass
roots” denomination with power and authority flowing from the bottom
up; and

**Whereas**, the expense of sending a commissioner to the General Assembly

has become prohibitive for many small churches in the PCA; and

**Whereas**, many commissioners would attend the General Assembly, yet are

unable because of health restrictions; and

**Whereas**, the General Assembly meetings now have a small representation

by ruling elders; and

**Whereas**, it is in accordance with the founding principles of the PCA to have

parity of the teaching and ruling elders in authority; and

**Whereas**, it is in accordance with the founding principles of the PCA and

our _BCO_ to have parity of teaching and ruling elders on General
Assembly committees ( _BCO_ 14-1; Principles for the Organization of the
Assembly, item 10) and to have at least parity of teaching and ruling
elders at General Assembly court meetings ( _BCO_ 14-2); and

**Whereas**, the technology exists to have commissioners vote on issues of the

General Assembly from a distance while they watch the proceedings of
the General Assembly online; and

**Whereas**, the General Assembly as presently structured would continue with

the exception of a distance voting commissioner;

**Therefore**, Westminster Presbytery overtures the 47 [th] General Assembly to

instruct the PCA Administrative Committee to investigate and report at
the 48 [th] general assembly a plan to create adv c (Distance Voting
Commissioner) which would entail the ability of regularly elected
commissioners to vote electronically at a distance from the site of the
General Assembly.

_Adopted by Westminster Presbytery at its stated meeting, March 9, 2019_
_Attested by /s/ TE Mark J. Blalack, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 28 from Westminster Presbytery (to OC)

“Affirmations and Denials regarding Homosexuality **”**

**Whereas** a controversy has arisen in the Presbyterian Church in America

over certain teachings espoused by Revoice, an organization that is
promoting LGBT+ flourishing in various PCA churches, it has become
necessary to clarify and reaffirm what the Bible teaches concerning
homosexuality and gender;

<a id="ga47-p658"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=660 printed_page=658 -->

**Therefore be it resolved** that Westminster Presbytery ask the 47 [th] General

Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to adopt the following
12 articles that clarify and affirm the Bible’s teaching about homosexuality
and gender.

**Article 1**
WE AFFIRM that homosexual attraction, homosexual orientation, and
homosexual acts are against nature and sinful. (Rom. 1:26-27)
WE DENY that homosexual attraction is part of the original creation order,
since God created man in His image male and female for one another. (Gen.
1:27-28; Gen. 2:18, 21-24; Mt. 19:4-6)

**Article 2**
WE AFFIRM that homosexual attraction is a vile, degrading, and
dishonorable passion, and those who condone homosexuality invite the
further judgment of God. (Rom. 1:26-32)
WE DENY that unrepentant homosexuals will inherit the kingdom of God.
(1 Cor. 6:9-10)

**Article 3**

WE AFFIRM that Jesus condemned both sinful sexual attractions and sinful
sexual actions. (Mt. 5:27-28)
WE DENY that scripture permits homosexual attraction and orientation.
(Rom. 1:26-27)

**Article 4**
WE AFFIRM that those with homosexual attractions, incestuous attractions,
and bestial attractions are ”sexual minorities” in the church.
WE DENY that there is any virtue in being identified as a “sexual minority,”
because all of these dispositions are sinful. (Lev. 18:6, 22-23; 20:13, 15-16)

**Article 5**
WE AFFIRM that God by His grace can change an unnatural sexual
orientation. (1 Cor. 6:11; 1 Jn. 1:9)
WE DENY that unnatural sexual orientations are fixed, permanent, and
unchangeable. (Col. 1:21-23)

**Article 6**
WE AFFIRM that many people having a homosexual orientation do not
desire this orientation.
WE DENY that unwanted vile passions are natural, neutral, or good. (Rom.
7:21-25)

**Article 7**
WE AFFIRM that Christians ought to abhor homosexual sin (Lev. 18:22).
Abhorrence of all sin should be cultivated. Those who love the Lord hate
evil. (Ps. 97:10)
WE DENY that revulsion and abhorrence of homosexual sin is sinful. (Rom.
12:9; 1 Cor. 13:4-7)

<a id="ga47-p659"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=661 printed_page=659 -->

**Article 8**
WE AFFIRM that people living in homosexual sin should be shown kindness
and lovingly encouraged to turn from their sin to serve Christ who alone can
deliver them from sin and its shame. (Mk. 2:16-17; James 5:19-20)
WE DENY that it is loving or kind to try to diminish or remove the shame
of homosexual attraction and orientation, since God calls it shameful. (Rom.
1:26-27; Jer. 6:14-15)

**Article 9**

WE AFFIRM that repentant sinners turning from all unnatural attractions
and trusting in Christ should be welcomed into the church as members in
good standing. (1 Tim. 1:15-16)
WE DENY that followers of Christ who struggle with homosexual attraction
should be identified as “LGBT+ Christians,” because it dishonors Christ to
identify with sinful attractions and orientations. (2 Cor. 5:16-17; 1 Cor. 6:911)

**Article 10**

WE AFFIRM that homosexual attraction should be mortified by the power
of the Spirit. (Col. 3:1-7; Rom. 8:12-14; 1 Thess. 4:3-5)
WE DENY that continuing homosexual attraction or failure to attain
orientation change is evidence of God’s approval of these unnatural and vile
passions. (Rom. 7:21-25; Rom. 1:26-27)

**Article 11**

WE AFFIRM that those struggling with unlawful sexual attractions of any
kind should be encouraged to seek the help, prayer, and counsel of mature
believers, elders, and pastors. (James 5:16; Heb. 3:12-13; Heb. 10:24-25)
WE DENY that those struggling with homosexual attraction and orientation
should suffer in silence. (Gal. 6:1-2)

**Article 12**

WE AFFIRM that our inward self-conception and outward gender identity
must conform to the sex God assigned us at conception, and thus we are to
live in obedience to his call evident in our reproductive organs. (Dt. 22:5)
WE DENY that physical anomalies or psychological conditions nullify the
union God has decreed between biological sex and self-conception and
gender identity.

_Adopted by Westminster Presbytery at its stated meeting, March 9, 2019_
_Attested by /s/ TE Mark J. Blalack, stated clerk_

**OVERTURE 29** -= **WITHDRAWN**

<a id="ga47-p660"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=662 printed_page=660 -->

##### OVERTURE 30 from Illiana Presbytery (to AC, OC)

“Establish Study Committee on Same-sex Orientation and
Gender Identity”

**Whereas,** the Fifth PCA General Assembly in 1977 affirmed “the Biblical

position for our denomination which states that: (1) The act of
homosexuality is a sin according to God’s Word; (2) Churches should
actively seek to lead the homosexual person to confession and
repentance that he might find justification and sanctification in Jesus
Christ, according to 1 Corinthians 6:11; and, (3) In light of the Biblical
view of its sinfulness, a practicing homosexual continuing in this sin
would not be a fit candidate for ordination or membership in the
Presbyterian Church in America”; and

**Whereas,** the PCA General Assembly has affirmed this position on

subsequent occasions, including 1996 and 1999; and

**Whereas,** the Revoice Conference held at Memorial Presbyterian Church

(PCA) in St. Louis, MO, has promoted questions and concerns about the
PCA’s position on sexual orientation and gender identity that are not
addressed in the affirmation of the Fifth General Assembly; and

**Whereas,** the peace and purity of Christ’s church is under assault by the

acceptance of cultural norms and misleadings concerning sexual
orientation and identity;

**Therefore be it resolved** that Illiana Presbytery hereby overtures the 47 [th]

General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America to form a study
committee comprised of at least four Teaching and four Ruling Elders
appointed by the Moderator, to study questions of same-sex orientation
and gender identity and to develop a PCA position statement on these

matters.

_Adopted by Illiana Presbytery at its stated meeting, February 9, 2019_
_Attested by /s/ TE J. Dawson Miller_ _, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 31 from Eastern Pennsylvania Presbytery (to AC, OC)

“Form an Ad Interim Committee to Address Matters Related to
Domestic Abuse, Domestic Oppression, and Sexual Assault”
(Version 6)

**Be it resolved:**

1. That the 47th General Assembly create a seven-member Ad Interim

Committee (AIC) tasked to accomplish the following.

<a id="ga47-p661"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=663 printed_page=661 -->

a. Prepare an annotated bibliography of recommended pastoral

resources available on the nature of domestic abuse, domestic
oppression, [15] and sexual assault, as well as pastoral resources for the
care of victims of these sins;
b. Explore these matters from an historical perspective, giving special

consideration of how they have been conceived of and addressed
within the Reformed tradition and provide a summary of their
findings.
c. Describe "best practices" and policy guidelines that could be helpful

for elders, Sessions, Presbyteries, and agencies for protecting against
these sins and for responding to them;
d. If the Committee deems it prudent, it may include in the report any

revisions or additions to _BCO_ Rules of Discipline that might more
appropriately protect victims of these sins within a biblical process,
which changes may be issued as an overture to a subsequent General
Assembly by any Presbytery that would wish to do so;
e. If the Committee deems it prudent, it may include in the report any

statement the Committee might recommend for the GA to consider
adopting regarding any PCA repentance related to these matters,
which statement may be issued as an overture to a subsequent
General Assembly by any Presbytery that would wish to do so.
2. The 47th GA authorizes the Moderator of the 47th GA to appoint the

seven voting members of this Committee. ( _RAO_ 9-4)
a. These members shall be PCA ministers or PCA ruling elders, and

the Committee shall include at least two of each. (Presbyteries,
Sessions, and individuals may contact the Moderator with suggested
names.)
b. The 47th GA authorizes the Moderator to make all of his

appointments after the 47th GA adjourns. ( _RAO_ 9-4)
3. The 47th GA authorizes the Moderator to appoint others to serve the

Committee as advisors, regardless of gender or denominational
affiliation, within the confines of its budget. The 47th GA recommends
the Moderator appoint at least two women who are subject-matter
experts and voting members.

15 Domestic oppression is an ongoing pattern of intimidating and domineering behavior

employed by one family member to control one or more other family members. A
superior/inferior, hierarchical power differential is established, maintained, and increased
more and more as time goes on... domestic oppressors systematically tyrannize, intimidate,
threaten, emotionally coerce, dehumanize, objectify, demean, degrade, manipulate, and
bully at least one other person within the family home—usually their spouse. Source:
[Rev. Warren Lamb, https://pastorlamb.com/2018/05/08/domestic-oppression-time-to-talk-](https://pastorlamb.com/2018/05/08/domestic-oppression-time-to-talk-about-i)
[about-i](https://pastorlamb.com/2018/05/08/domestic-oppression-time-to-talk-about-i)

<a id="ga47-p662"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=664 printed_page=662 -->

4. Budget and Funding – The AIC budget shall be $15,000, all of which shall

come from contributions to the AC designated for the AIC. (The Pacific
Northwest Presbytery will commit to contribute $1,000 to that end.)
5. Reporting

The Committee shall either report its findings on 1.a (bibliography) and
1.c (best practices) directly to the 48th GA, or at that time request an
extension ( _RAO_ 9-4).

**Rationale** :

1. The sins of domestic abuse and sexual assault are expressly forbidden by

Scripture and the Westminster Standards. (For example, see 2 Timothy
3:2-5; Jude 7; Exodus 21:15; Deuteronomy 22:25-27; also, _WLC_ 135 and
139.)
2. These sins are especially heinous as they are “against the express letter

of the law,” “break many commandments,” “admit of no reparation,” and
often involve various other aggravations ( _WLC_ 151).
3. Sadly, these sins are prevalent in our society, and in the church. [16]

4. These sins often produce in the victims a complex response of denial

along with immense fear and shame that persist with varying severity
throughout life (e.g., 2 Samuel 13:1-22).
5. It is the responsibility of the elders of the Church to exercise their

authority, severally and jointly, to shepherd those who are under their
care by guarding against such sinful behavior as much as possible, by
wisely caring for those victimized by the sins of others, and by
appropriately disciplining those guilty of such sins, with an aim toward
a full repentance.
6. these sins have been a concern and a subject of attention not only in

other denominations but even in the proceedings of the 46th General
Assembly.
7. In 2014, the 42nd General Assembly (Houston, TX) exhorted the PCA

and its courts to respond boldly against one form of these sins, namely
the sexual abuse of children.

16 For example, the National Domestic Violence Hotline reports: “More than 1 in 3 women

(35.6%) and more than 1 in 4 men (28.5%) in the United States have experienced rape,
physical violence and/or stalking by an intimate partner in their lifetime.” (Available at
www.thehotline.org/resources/statistics/, accessed 6/15/18). For further statistics on sexual
violence see the National Center for Injury Prevention and Controls Report titled _Sexual_
_Violence Surveillance: Uniform Definitions and Recommended Data Elements_ (Atlanta: CDC,
2014; available at [www.cdc.gov/violenceprevention/pdf/sv_surveillance_definitionsl-2009-](http://www.cdc.gov/violenceprevention/pdf/sv_surveillance_definitionsl-2009-a.pdf)
[a.pdf](http://www.cdc.gov/violenceprevention/pdf/sv_surveillance_definitionsl-2009-a.pdf) ).

<a id="ga47-p663"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=665 printed_page=663 -->

8. Many PCA ministers, ruling elders, and candidates report a desire to be

resourced in order to provide better pastoral care on these sins and their
resulting complex consequences.
9. Many PCA ministers, ruling elders, and candidates report a desire to

learn of "best practices" regarding church and presbytery policies in this
area. For example, some ministers and elders are unaware of the different
levels of background checks that can be utilized in screening. Some do
not know what their State's laws are regarding mandatory reporters or in
what situations those laws may or may not apply.
10. Our _BCO_ Part II Rules of Discipline might benefit from additions or

revisions that address these matters. For example, the following _BCO_
provisions might benefit from review and revision, at least in the context
of a minor who accuses an adult of sexual misconduct:

_BCO_ 35-1....The accused party may be allowed, but shall not
be compelled to testify; but the accuser shall be required to
testify, on the demand of the accused. ...

_BCO_ 32-13. In order that the trial may be fair and impartial, the
witnesses shall be examined in the presence of the accused, or
at least after he shall have received due citation to attend.

Witnesses may be cross-examined by both parties, and any
questions asked must be pertinent to the issue.
_BCO_ 32-20. Process, in case of scandal, shall commence within
the space of one year after the offense was committed, unless
it has recently become flagrant….

_BCO_ 35-4. no witness afterwards to be examined, unless a
member of the court, shall be present during the examination
of another witness on the same case, if either party object.

11. Reporting Recommendations through the Overtures Committee

In the past, Ad Interim Committees have customarily reported
recommendations directly to the GA floor. But going through the OC is
a more prudent and efficient path for any AIC recommendations on _BCO_
changes and/or any recommended statement for GA adoption.
Furthermore, _RAO_ 12-1 stipulates " _all_ recommendations proposing
amendment to the Constitution _shall_ be referred to the Overtures

Committee for their review and recommendation to the General

Assembly." And this would also be the most prudent and efficient path
for any _BCO_ recommendation from this AIC.

<a id="ga47-p664"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=666 printed_page=664 -->

The _RAO_ section on OC reports to GA provides a wiser method for
handling all recommendations from the AIC:

_RAO_ 15-8.c. "A recommendation [from the OC] shall be
