considered under the standard rules governing debate, but
Subsidiary Motions ( _RRO_ VI) to _Postpone Indefinitely_, to
_Amend_, to _Commit,_ and to _Limit Debate_ ; and Incidental
Motions ( _RRO_ VIII) to _Divide a Question_, to _Consider_
_Seriatim_, and _Constitutional Inquiries_, shall **not** be permitted.
A motion to _Recommit_ shall be permitted, but a motion to
_Recommit With Instructions_ shall not be permitted."

If the 2019 Dallas GA Overtures Committee, and then the Dallas GA, want
recommendations from this particular AIC to go through the 2020
Birmingham OC first, that is certainly the constitutional prerogative of the
commissioners in Dallas.

_Adopted by Eastern Pennsylvania Presbytery at its stated meeting, April 9, 2019_
_Attested by TE Mel Farrar, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 32 from Tennessee Valley Presbytery (CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 14-1.10, _14-_ 1.11, and 14-1.12 to Allow Non-ordained
Persons to Serve on Committees and Boards (Version 4)

**Whereas**, vigorous participation of women in the life of the early church is

attested by the New Testament: they helped select (Acts 1:14) and train
(Acts 18:26) leaders of the church, were prophets (Acts 21:8-9), served
the church through deeds of mercy in vital ways (Acts 9:36–41), were
instrumental in the planting of churches (Acts 16:12–15; 17:4,12) and
other missionary endeavors (Acts 18:1f), and were acknowledged as
important leaders for the church (Rom. 16:3-4); and

**Whereas**, the Presbyterian Church in America believes Christ has called his

church to be a “spiritual commonwealth” ( _BCO_ 3-1), in which members
elect godly men to be ordained as elders who are given the power of
jurisdiction as they act jointly in the courts of the church ( _BCO_ 3-2); and

**Whereas**, a robustly complementarian perspective affirms the oversight of

these elders and the proper, vital and full expression of the gifts given by
Christ to men and women for his service; and

**Whereas**, as part of the calling of church leaders to “equip the saints for the

work of ministry for building up the body of Christ” (Eph. 4:12), many
sessions throughout the PCA identify gifted men and women to serve on

<a id="ga47-p665"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=667 printed_page=665 -->

committees of the church, such as missions, building, and pastoral search
committees; and

**Whereas**, in contrast with a commission in the PCA, which is "authorized to

deliberate and conclude the business referred to it," committees are
appointed by courts “to examine, consider and report” to their
constituting court concerning the matters that court delegates to them
( _BCO_ 15-1) and thus do not exercise governing authority; and

**Whereas**, in accordance with this principle, Permanent Committees

established by the General Assembly are “to serve and not to direct any
Church judicatories. They are not to establish policy, but rather execute
policy established by the General Assembly” ( _BCO_ 14-1.7); and

**Whereas** neither the NT evidence nor the constitutional evidence cited above

seem to have been given adequate consideration by the 17th GA in their
denial of O.24 asking for a study committee to determine whether
"women could be included on some or all of the permanent committees
and agencies of the GA," which was the precedent cited for rejecting the
overtures presented last year to open participation on Boards and
Permanent Committees to non-ordained members; and

**Whereas**, these considerations suggest there is no impediment to an

unordained member in good standing in the PCA serving on a Permanent
Committee or Agency of the General Assembly established in _BCO_ 141.12; and

**Whereas**, the 45th General Assembly adopted the recommendation of The

Ad Interim Committee on Women Serving in the Ministry of the Church
that stated, “sessions, presbyteries and the General Assembly strive to
develop, recognize, and utilize the gifts, skills, knowledge, and wisdom
of godly women in the local, regional, and national church, and
particularly consider overtures that would allow qualified women to
serve on appropriate committees and agencies within the church”; and

**Whereas**, the 46th General Assembly answered in the negative an overture

to allow non-ordained members on General Assembly Agency Boards
on the grounds that _BCO_ 14-1.11 had to be amended prior to amendment
of the PCA Bylaws, which governs the Agencies,

**Therefore be it resolved** to amend _The Book of Church Orde_ r 14-1.10, 14
1.11, and 14-1.12 as follows: [Proposed additions underlined and
deletions noted by strike out.]

_**BCO**_ **14-1.**

~~10. The committees are to be established on the basis of an~~

~~equal number between teaching and ruling elders.~~

<a id="ga47-p666"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=668 printed_page=666 -->

~~11.1~~ 0. A Nominating Committee shall be comprised of one
representative elected by each Presbytery in the following
manner. Each Presbytery shall be assigned to a class by the
stated clerk based on its date of formation. The members shall

serve in classes of three year terms, alternating between ruling
and teaching elders. When necessary, unexpired terms shall be
filled by an elder of the same class, teaching or ruling.
This committee is to present all nominations for which it is
responsible to the next meeting of the Assembly from a slate
of ~~men~~ candidates nominated by the Presbyteries. Presbyteries
shall utilize the nominating forms provided by the stated clerk
for their nominations. Each presbytery may present one
teaching elder, ~~and~~ one ruling elder, and, when applicable, one
non-elder candidate to fill openings that are designated for

members at large for each committee or agency. Any non
ordained person nominated must meet the qualifications
outlined in _BCO_ 14-1.11.

In addition to nominees for expired terms, the Committee
shall nominate for each permanent Committee one ruling, ~~and~~
one teaching elder and a member at large candidate as alternates
to fill any vacancies that may occur during the year. Each
alternate should attend each meeting and fill any vacancy
necessary to meet a quorum. In addition to the new nominees
from the Presbyteries, alternates not assuming any vacancies
during a year will be automatically considered by the
Nominating Committee as candidates for nomination to that
same committee.

11. To be qualified for nomination and election to serve on a

Permanent Committee or Agency as a member at large, a non
ordained candidate must be a member in good standing of a
Church in the Presbyterian Church in America and must
receive an endorsement from their Session. The endorsement
will consist of testimony regarding the candidate’s Christian
character, knowledge of and adherence to the Constitution and
promise of faithful service to the General Assembly.

12. The Assembly permanent committees are the Administrative
Committee of the General Assembly, Committee on Discipleship
Ministries, Committee on Mission to North America, Committee

<a id="ga47-p667"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=669 printed_page=667 -->

on Mission to the World, and Committee on Reformed
University Fellowship.
The Administrative Committee of the General Assembly
(AC) shall consist of twenty (20) members:

a. Eleven members, six elders and five members who

are not elders, shall be in classes elected through the
standard nomination and election procedure,
b. One member from each of the following program

committees or agencies:
1. Committee on Discipleship Ministries (CDM)
2. Covenant College (CC)
3. Covenant Theological Seminary (CTS)
4. Mission to North America (MNA)
5. Mission to the World (MTW)
6. PCA Foundation (PCAF)
7. PCA Retirement & Benefits, Inc. (RBI)
8. Reformed University Fellowship (RUF)
9. Ridge Haven Conference Center (RH)
The eleven elected members ~~at large~~ shall serve a term of
four years. The chairman of the Administrative Committee
shall be one of its elected ordained members ~~at large~~ .
Each program committee and agency shall designate its
member each year at the last meeting of the committee or board
before the meeting of the General Assembly. The chief
administrative officers of the program committees and
agencies may attend any meeting of the Administrative
Committee. They shall be entitled to the privilege of the floor
but shall not have a vote and must be excluded when an

executive session is called.

Committee on Discipleship Ministries, Committee on
Mission to North America, Committee on Mission to the
World, and Committee on Reformed University Fellowship
shall consist of fifteen (15) ~~men~~ members divided into five
classes of three ~~men m~~ embers. Three classes will have one TE,
one RE and one member at large. One class shall have one TE
and two members at large and one class shall have one RE and
two members at large. ~~each, with two men Being TEs and one~~
~~RE or two men being REs and one TE on alternate years,~~ Each
class shall be elected to serve five-year terms. Committee ~~s~~ on
Discipleship Ministries, Committee on Mission to North
America, Committee on Mission to the World, and Committee

<a id="ga47-p668"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=670 printed_page=668 -->

on Reformed University Fellowship shall have one ruling
elder, ~~and~~ one teaching elder and one member at large as
alternates to fill any vacancy that may occur during the year.
A quorum for each Committees shall be eight (8) members,
with a majority of those present being elders. The Chairman
of each Committee shall be an elder.

Persons who have served for a full term, or for at least two
years of a partial term, on one of the Assembly’s permanent
committees or agencies shall not be eligible for re-election to
an Assembly committee until one year has elapsed.
(Exceptions may be permitted in agency bylaws approved by
the Assembly).

So that _BCO_ 14-1.10, 14-1.11, and 14-1.12 as amended would read:

_**BCO**_ **14-1**

10. A Nominating Committee shall be comprised of one
representative elected by each Presbytery in the following
manner. Each Presbytery shall be assigned to a class by the
stated clerk based on its date of formation. The members shall

serve in classes of three year terms, alternating between ruling
and teaching elders. When necessary, unexpired terms shall be
filled by an elder of the same class, teaching or ruling.
This committee is to present all nominations for which it is
responsible to the next meeting of the Assembly from a slate
of candidates nominated by the Presbyteries. Presbyteries shall
utilize the nominating forms provided by the stated clerk for
their nominations. Each presbytery may present one teaching
elder, one ruling elder, and, when applicable, one non-elder to
fill openings that are designated for members at large for each
committee or agency. Any non-ordained person nominated
must meet the qualifications outlined in _BCO_ 14-1.11.
In addition to nominees for expired terms, the Committee
shall nominate for each permanent Committee one ruling, one
teaching elder and a member at large candidate as alternates to
fill any vacancies that may occur during the year. Each
alternate should attend each meeting and fill any vacancy
necessary to meet a quorum. In addition to the new nominees
from the Presbyteries, alternates not assuming any vacancies
during a year will be automatically considered by the

<a id="ga47-p669"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=671 printed_page=669 -->

Nominating Committee as candidates for nomination to that
same committee.

11. To be qualified for nomination and election to serve on a
Permanent Committee or Agency as a member at large, anon ordained candidate must be a member in good standing of a
Church in the Presbyterian Church in America and must
receive an endorsement from their Session. The endorsement
will consist of testimony regarding the candidate’s Christian
character, knowledge of and adherence to the Constitution and
promise of faithful service to the General Assembly.

12. The Assembly permanent committees are the Administrative
Committee of the General Assembly, Committee on Discipleship
Ministries, Committee on Mission to North America, Committee
on Mission to the World, and Committee on Reformed
University Fellowship.
The Administrative Committee of the General Assembly
(AC) shall consist of twenty (20) members:

a. Eleven members, six elders and five members who are

not elders, shall be in classes elected through the
standard nomination and election procedure,
b. One member from each of the following program

committees or agencies:
1. Committee on Discipleship Ministries (CDM)
2. Covenant College (CC)
3. Covenant Theological Seminary (CTS)
4. Mission to North America (MNA)
5. Mission to the World (MTW)
6. PCA Foundation (PCAF)
7. PCA Retirement & Benefits, Inc. (RBI)
8. Reformed University Fellowship (RUF)
9. Ridge Haven Conference Center (RH)
The eleven elected members shall serve a term of four

years. The chairman of the Administrative Committee shall be
one of its elected ordained members.

Each program committee and agency shall designate its
member each year at the last meeting of the committee or board
before the meeting of the General Assembly. The chief
administrative officers of the program committees and
agencies may attend any meeting of the Administrative

<a id="ga47-p670"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=672 printed_page=670 -->

Committee. They shall be entitled to the privilege of the floor
but shall not have a vote and must be excluded when an

executive session is called.

Committee on Discipleship Ministries, Committee on
Mission to North America, Committee on Mission to the
World, and Committee on Reformed University Fellowship
shall consist of fifteen (15) members divided into five classes
of three ~~m~~ embers. Three classes will have one TE, one RE and
one member at large. One class shall have one TE and two
members at large and one class shall have one RE and two
members at large. Each class shall be elected to serve five-year
terms. Committee on Discipleship Ministries, Committee on
Mission to North America, Committee on Mission to the
World, and Committee on Reformed University Fellowship
shall have one ruling elder, one teaching elder and one member
at large as alternates to fill any vacancy that may occur during
the year. A quorum for each Committees shall be eight (8)
members, with a majority of those present being elders. The
Chairman of each Committee shall be an elder.

Persons who have served for a full term, or for at least two
years of a partial term, on one of the Assembly’s permanent
committees or agencies shall not be eligible for re-election to
an Assembly committee until one year has lapsed. (Exceptions
may be permitted in agency bylaws approved by the
Assembly).

**Further, be it resolved** that the Stated Clerk shall be responsible for

developing the nomination forms necessary to implement this process
and shall be responsible for converting the composition of each class as
each class term expires.

_Approved by Tennessee Valley Presbytery at its stated meeting, April 13, 2019_
_Attested by /s/ TE Jacob A. Bennett, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 33 from Tennessee Valley Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 57-5 to Add Vow Regarding Trinity"

**Whereas**, there is but one only, living, and true God, who exists as three

persons—God the Father, God the Son, and God the Holy Spirit—of one
substance, power, and eternity (WCF 2; WLC 9; WSC 6); and

<a id="ga47-p671"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=673 printed_page=671 -->

**Whereas**, the doctrine of the Trinity is foundational, essential, and vital to

the Christian faith; indeed, presbyteries have been instructed to give due
and serious consideration to “Appendix W,” which includes the
affirmation: “the confession of the Trinity is the heartbeat of the
Christian religion” (as adopted by the 45 [th] General Assembly, _Minutes,_
29 and 624); and

**Whereas**, the visible church is made up of those who profess faith in the

Lord Jesus Christ ( _BCO_ 2-1), and such profession of faith is a profession
of the whole Christ, who is both truly God and truly man; and

**Whereas**, we live ina pluralistic age, where there is confusion over the

essential being of God, illustrated by the fact that the vast majority of
self-professing evangelicals in America affirm that “Jesus is the first and
greatest being created by God” (Ligonier Ministries and LifeWay
Research, “The State of Theology” 2018); and

**Whereas**, the doctrine of the Trinity has been and is currently under assault

by the unbelieving world, by cults, by unitarians, and by various
religions; and

**Whereas**, Mormons, Jehovah’s Witnesses, and other non-Christian faiths

could affirm our current membership vows as stated; and

**Whereas**, the organization of a particular church in the Presbyterian Church

in America includes the pronouncement and declaration of its
organization in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy
Spirit ( _BCO_ 5-8); and

**Whereas**, when unbaptized persons apply for admission into the Church,

they shall make a public profession of their faith, in the presence of the
congregation, and thereupon be baptized in the name of the Father, and
of the Son, and of the Holy Spirit ( _BCO_ 56-6; 57-3), as shall the children
of believing parents; and

**Whereas**, “every Christian Church, or union or association of particular

churches, is entitled to declare the terms of admission into its communion
and the qualifications of its ministers and members” (Preface to the _BCO_
II.2); and

**Whereas,** at least three fellow NAPARC churches include a membership

vow requiring affirmation of the Holy Trinity; and

**Whereas**, the current membership vows of the Presbyterian Church in

America reference “God” ( _BCO_ 57-5), but do not require faith in God as
He exists in three persons;

**Therefore, be it resolved** to amend the _Book of Church Order_ 57-5 by

adding the following question to the membership vows [as question 2,
thereby renumbering questions 2–5 as questions 3–6]:

<a id="ga47-p672"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=674 printed_page=672 -->

**2. Do you believe in the one, true, and living God who**

**—**
**exists in three persons** **God the Father, God the Son,**
**and God the Holy Spirit—who are of the same**
**substance, equal in power and glory?**

So that the membership vows in _Book of Church Order_ 57-5 would read:

**1.** **Do you acknowledge yourselves to be sinners in the**
**sight of God, justly deserving His displeasure, and**
**without hope save in His sovereign mercy?**
**2.** **Do you believe in the one, true, and living God who**

**—**
**exists in three persons** **God the Father, God the**
**Son, and God the Holy Spirit—who are of the same**
**substance, equal in power and glory?**
~~**2.**~~ **3.** **Do you believe in the Lord Jesus Christ as the Son**
**of God, and Savior of sinners, and do you receive**
**and rest upon Him alone for salvation as He is**
**offered in the Gospel?**
~~**3.**~~ **4.** **Do you now resolve and promise, in humble**
**reliance upon the grace of the Holy Spirit, that you**
**will endeavor to live as becomes the followers of**

**Christ?**
~~**4.**~~ **5.** **Do you promise to support the Church in its**
**worship and work to the best of your ability?**
~~**5.**~~ **6.** **Do you submit yourselves to the government and**
**discipline of the Church, and promise to study its**
**purity and peace?**

_Adopted by the Tennessee Valley Presbytery at its stated meeting, April 13, 2019_
_Attested by TE Jacob A. Bennett, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 34 from Philadelphia Presbytery ( BCO amendments to

“Amend _BCO_ 42-4; 43-2; and 43-3 as well as CCB, OC;
_OMSJC_ 18.10 b. to Clarify Filing Requirements _OMSJC_ amendment
and Allow Electronic Filings” to SJC)

**Whereas,** the _Operating Manual of the Standing Judicial Commission_

( _OMSJC_ ) explicitly prohibits filings by electronic means, as set forth in
_OMSJC_ 18.10.b:

<a id="ga47-p673"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=675 printed_page=673 -->

Documents required or permitted to be filed by a party shall be
filed with the Stated Clerk. Such filing shall not be timely
unless the documents are received in the office of the Stated

Clerk within the time fixed for such filing, except that papers
shall be deemed filed on the day of mailing if sent by certified,
registered, priority, or express mail of the United States Postal
Service or any delivery service where verifying receipt is
utilized. **Neither facsimiles nor E-mail will be allowed for**
**purposes of filing.** Interested parties should be aware that
responsibility for such filings rests with them and that delays
in delivery or non-delivery are the sole responsibility of the
filing party.” (emphasis added)
and

**Whereas,** it is unspecified in _BCO_ 42-4, _BCO_ 43-2, and _BCO_ 43-3 whether

facsimiles or email may be allowed for the purposes of filing written
notice of an appeal or complaint; and

**Whereas,** the provisions in _The_ _Book of Church Order_ related to filings were

created before the advent of any generally available and reliable
electronic communications;

**Whereas,** filings by electronic means are permitted, and in some instances,

mandated, in civil and criminal courts in the United States;

**Whereas,** Church courts and individuals may be uncertain or confused about

whether electronic filings are valid;

**Whereas,** Appendix H of the _BCO_ suggests that a Presbytery Commission

could accept electronic filings:

At its discretion and with the consent of both parties, the
Commission may decide to accept email, fax or similar means
for filings after establishing procedures to confirm the date that
a document is filed and received.

**Whereas,** procedural errors in filing often result from the current

requirement of simultaneous filings with the lower and higher court, and
courts have greater expertise in such matters than individuals;

**Whereas,** it is in the interest of procedural justice to preserve a uniform

process of filing between lower courts and higher courts of the
Presbyterian Church in America so that its members might not have their
constitutional rights of appeal or complaint denied them by obscurity and
unclarity in the _BCO_, and incongruence of procedures between lower
and higher courts;

<a id="ga47-p674"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=676 printed_page=674 -->

**Therefore,** the Philadelphia Presbytery overtures to amend _BCO_ 42-4, _BCO_

43-2, and _BCO_ 43-3 and _OMSJC_ 18.10.b as follows: [Proposed additions
underlined and deletions noted by strike out.]

**42-4.** Notice of appeal may be given the court before its
adjournment. Written notice of appeal, ~~with~~ including supporting
reasons (an “Appeal Notice”), shall be filed by the appellant
with ~~both the clerk of the lower court and~~ the clerk of the higher
court, within thirty (30) days of notification of the last court’s
decision. With respect to the notice of appeal and the
notification of the last court’s decision, the following
provisions shall apply:

a. An Appeal Notice shall not be timely unless received by

the clerk within the time fixed for such Appeal Notice,
except that an Appeal Notice shall be deemed filed on the
day of mailing if sent by certified, registered, priority, or
express mail of the United States Postal Service or any
delivery service where verifying receipt is utilized. Filing
an Appeal Notice by facsimile or e-mail will be allowed
and shall be deemed to have occurred on the day of
confirmed receipt. Clerks of courts shall (1) make
reasonable efforts to publicize addresses (both physical
and electronic) to which Appeal Notices may be sent and
(2) acknowledge as soon as practical the receipt of a
facsimile or e-mail filing of an Appeal Notice. Interested
parties should be aware that responsibility for such filings

rests with them and that delays in delivery or non delivery
are the sole responsibility of the filing party and that the
filing party bears the burden of proving that a facsimile or
e-mail filing was timely made.
b. Notification of the last court’s decision shall be deemed to

have occurred on the day of mailing (if certified, registered
or express mail of a national postal service or any private
service where verifying receipt is utilized), the day of hand
delivery, or the day of confirmed receipt in the case of email or facsimile. Furthermore, compliance with such
requirements shall be deemed to have been fulfilled if a
party cannot be located after diligent inquiry or if a party
refuses to accept delivery. No attempt should be made to
circularize the courts to which appeal is being made by
either party before the case is heard.

<a id="ga47-p675"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=677 printed_page=675 -->

c. Within ten (10) days of receiving the Appeal Notice, the

higher court shall send a copy of the Appeal Notice to the
lower court, together with a request for the Record of the
Case (as set forth in _BCO_ 42-5).

**43-2.** A complaint shall first be made to the court whose act or
decision is alleged to be in error. Written notice of complaint,
~~with~~ including supporting reasons (a “Complaint Notice”),
shall be filed with the clerk of the court within sixty (60) days
following the meeting of the court. A Complaint Notice shall
not be timely unless received by the clerk within the time fixed
for the Complaint Notice, except that a Complaint Notice shall
be deemed filed on the day of mailing if sent by certified,
registered, priority, or express mail of the United States Postal
Service or any delivery service where verifying receipt is
utilized. Filing a Complaint Notice by facsimile or e-mail will
be allowed and shall be deemed to have occurred on the day of
confirmed receipt. Clerks of courts shall (1) make reasonable
efforts to publicize addresses (both physical and electronic) to
which Complaint Notices may be sent and (2) acknowledge as

soon as practical the receipt of a facsimile or email filing of a
Complaint Notice. Interested parties should be aware that
responsibility for such filings rests with them and that delays

in delivery or non delivery are the sole responsibility of the
filing party and that the filing party bears the burden of proving
that a facsimile or e-mail filing was timely made. The court
shall consider the complaint at its next stated meeting, or at a
called meeting prior to its next stated meeting. No attempt
should be made to circularize the court to which complaint is
being made by either party.

**43-3.** If, after considering a complaint, the court alleged to be
delinquent or in error is of the opinion that it has not erred, and
denies the complaint, the complainant may take that complaint
to the next higher court. If the lower court fails to consider the
complaint against it by or at its next stated meeting, the
complainant may take that complaint to the next higher court.
~~Written~~ A Complaint Notice ~~notice thereof~~ shall be filed with
~~both the clerk of the lower court and~~ the clerk of the higher
court within thirty (30) days of notification of the last court’s
decision. With respect to the complaint to the next higher court

<a id="ga47-p676"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=678 printed_page=676 -->

and the notification of the last court’s decision, the following
provisions shall apply:

a. a complaint notice to a higher courts hall include a

copy of the complaint that was filed with the lower
court. A Complaint Notice shall not be timely unless
received by the clerk within the time fixed for the
Complaint Notice, except that the Complaint Notice
shall be deemed filed on the day of mailing if sent by
certified, registered, priority, or express mail of the
United States Postal Service or any delivery service
where verifying receipt is utilized. Filing a Complaint
Notice by facsimile or e-mail will be allowed and shall
be deemed to have occurred on the day of confirmed
receipt. Clerks of courts shall (1) make reasonable
efforts to publicize addresses (both physical and
electronic) to which Complaint Notices may be sent
and (2) acknowledge as soon as practical the receipt of
a facsimile or e-mail filing of a Complaint Notice.
Interested parties should be aware that responsibility
for such filings rests with them and that delays in

delivery or non delivery are the sole responsibility of
the filing party and that the filing party bears the

burden of proving that a facsimile or email filing was
timely made.
b. Notification of the last court’s decision shall be

deemed to have occurred on the day of mailing (if
certified, registered or express mail of a national postal
service or any private service where verifying receipt
is utilized), the day of hand delivery, or the day of
confirmed receipt in the case of e-mail or facsimile.
Furthermore, compliance with such requirements shall
be deemed to have been fulfilled if a party cannot be
located after diligent inquiry or if a party refuses to
accept delivery. No attempt should be made to
circularize the courts to which appeal is being made by
either party before the case is heard.
c. Within ten (10) days of receiving the Complaint

Notice, the higher court shall send a copy of the
Complaint Notice to the lower court, together with a
request for the response of the lower court, if any, and

<a id="ga47-p677"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=679 printed_page=677 -->

any papers bearing on the complaint (as set forth in
_BCO_ 43-6).

**OMSJC 18.10**

b. Documents required or permitted to be filed by a party

shall be filed with the Stated Clerk. Such filing shall
not be timely unless the documents are received in the
office of the Stated Clerk within the time fixed for such
filing, except that papers shall be deemed filed on the
day of mailing if sent by certified, registered, priority,
or express mail of the United States Postal Service or
any delivery service where verifying receipt is utilized.
Filing by facsimile or e-mail will be allowed and shall
be deemed to have occurred on the day of confirmed
receipt. ~~Neither facsimiles nor E-mail will be allowed~~
~~for purposes of filing.~~ Interested parties should be
aware that responsibility for such filings rests with
them and that delays in delivery or non-delivery are the
sole responsibility of the filing party and that the filing

party bears the burden of proving that a facsimile ore
mail filing was timely made.”

_Adopted by Philadelphia Presbytery at its stated meeting, March 16, 2019_
_Attested by /s/ TE Greg Hobaugh, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 35 from Savannah River Presbytery (to OC)

“Affirm the PCA’s Commitment to the Sanctity of Biblical
Sexual Relationships”

**Whereas,** the Scripture is infallible and inerrant, the only rule of faith and

practice, and the only touchstone of truth; and

**Whereas**, the Constitution of the Presbyterian Church in America “is subject

to and subordinate to the Scriptures of the Old and New Testaments;”
and

**Whereas,** every officer (teaching elders, ruling elders, and deacons) in the

Presbyterian Church in America vows that they “believe the Scriptures
of the Old and New Testaments, as originally given, to be the inerrant
Word of God, the only infallible rule of faith and practice;” and

**Whereas**, all church courts in the Presbyterian Church in America have the

power “to bear testimony against error in doctrine and immorality,” “to
require obedience to the laws of Christ,” and “to cut off the contumacious
and impenitent from the congregation of believers” ( _BCO_ 11-2); and

<a id="ga47-p678"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=680 printed_page=678 -->

**Whereas**, every PCA Presbytery has power “to condemn erroneous opinions

which injure the purity and peace of the Church” ( _BCO_ 13-9f); and

**Whereas**, the Revoice Conference, held at Memorial Presbyterian Church in

St. Louis, Missouri on July 26-28, 2018, was for the purpose of
“Supporting, encouraging, and empowering gay, lesbian, same-sex
attracted and other LGBT+ Christians;” and

**Whereas**, some within the Presbyterian Church in America are claiming that

same-sex attraction is not necessarily a sin but can be a disposition given
to some: “that such a disposition of sinful desires are God given for the
acceptance of all believers and the Church, and for the promotion of the
Glory of God;” or “that individuals with sinful homosexual feelings of
same-sex attraction have no control over these feelings and/or
attractions; that though they may be unwanted, they are helpless to avoid
and are in no need of repentance;” or who answer affirmatively: “Can a
person claim to be born with same-sex attraction in the same manner as
the man born blind?” or are in agreement with: “I believe my same-sex
attractions are broken, but I do not believe they are sinful. It is not sinful
for me to be attracted to another man;” or asserting that same-sex
attraction and orientation are permissible under the Gospel as long as the
person remains celibate; even though these assertions are contrary to
what Jesus said that any and all lust in the heart is a sin: “I say to you
that everyone who looks at a woman with lust for her has already
committed adultery with her in his heart” (Matthew 5:28); and

**Whereas,** the Scripture teaches that same-sex attraction is an evidence that

God has given over such people to “the lusts of their hearts to impurity,
so that their bodies would be dishonored among them” and “to degrading
passions” in which “women exchanged the natural function for that
which is unnatural” and “the men abandoned the natural function of the
woman and burned in their desire toward one another” because they
“exchanged the truth of God for a lie” and “did not see fit to acknowledge
God any longer” (Romans 1:24-28); and

**Whereas,** homosexuality is a sin from which God delivered some of the

Corinthian Christians (“such **were** some of you…): “Or do you not know
that the unrighteous will not inherit the kingdom of God? Do not be
deceived; neither fornicators, nor idolaters, nor adulterers, nor
effeminate, nor homosexuals, nor thieves, nor the covetous, nor
drunkards, nor revilers, nor swindlers, will inherit the kingdom of God.
Such were some of you; but you were washed, but you were sanctified,
but you were justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ and in the
Spirit of our God” (1 Corinthians 6:9-11); and continues to deliver
believers from this and other sins; and

<a id="ga47-p679"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=681 printed_page=679 -->

**Whereas,** same-sex attraction is lust in the very same manner that

heterosexual attraction is lust, and both are violations of the seventh
commandment as the Larger Catechism teaches:

Question 138: What are the duties required in the seventh
commandment?

Answer: The duties required in the seventh commandment are,
chastity in body, mind, affections, words, and behavior; and the
preservation of it in ourselves and others; watchfulness over
the eyes and all the senses; temperance, keeping of chaste
company, modesty in apparel; marriage by those that have not
the gift of continency, conjugal love, and cohabitation; diligent
labor in our callings; shunning all occasions of uncleanness,
and resisting temptations thereunto.

Question 139: What are the sins forbidden in the seventh
commandment?

Answer: The sins forbidden in the seventh commandment,
besides the neglect of the duties required, are, adultery,
fornication, rape, incest, sodomy, and all unnatural lusts; all
unclean imaginations, thoughts, purposes, and affections; all
corrupt or filthy communications, or listening thereunto;
wanton looks, impudent or light behavior, immodest apparel;
prohibiting of lawful, and dispensing with unlawful marriages;
allowing, tolerating, keeping of stews, and resorting to them;
entangling vows of single life, undue delay of marriage; having
more wives or husbands than one at the same time; unjust
divorce, or desertion; idleness, gluttony, drunkenness, unchaste
company; lascivious songs, books, pictures, dancings, stage
plays; and all other provocations to, or acts of uncleanness,
either in ourselves or others.

**Therefore**, be it resolved, that in light of the fact that this current issue is

once again confronting the PCA, the 47 [th] General Assembly take the
following actions:

**That** the 47 [th] General Assembly reaffirm the action of the 5 [th] General
Assembly:

Whereas God's Word teaches in the Old Testament that

homosexuality is a sin, labeling it an “abomination" in such

<a id="ga47-p680"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=682 printed_page=680 -->

passages as Leviticus 18:22 and 20:13, and clearly viewing
it as sin in the story of Sodom in Genesis 19:1-11; and

Whereas the New Testament reaffirms the sinfulness of this

“unnatural'' practice in such passages as Romans 1:26, 27
and Jude 7, even stating in 1 Corinthians 6:9-11 that
homosexuals will not inherit the kingdom of heaven;

Therefore, be it resolved that the Fifth General Assembly of the

Presbyterian Church in America affirm the Biblical
position for our denomination which states that:
1. The act of homosexuality is a sin according to God’s

Word;
2. Churches should actively seek to lead the homosexual

person to confession and repentance that he might
find justification and sanctification in Jesus Christ,
according to I Corinthians 6:11; and
3. In light of the Biblical view of its sinfulness, a

practicing homosexual continuing in this sin would
not be a fit candidate for ordination or membership
in the Presbyterian Church in America.
[(http://pcahistory.org/ga/5th_pcaga_1977.pdf p. 68).](http://pcahistory.org/ga/5th_pcaga_1977.pdf%20p.%2068)

**Further**, that the 47 [th] General Assembly reaffirm the declaration of the

21 [st] PCA General Assembly:

Therefore, the 21 [st] General Assembly of the Presbyterian
Church in America makes the following declaration: The
Bible, which is our infallible rule of faith and practice,
commands us to express redemptive love and compassion
to all persons. It further commands us to condemn sexual
behavior which deviates from God's revealed standard as

sinful and destructive. The Bible calls all who engage in
these behaviors to repentance by turning to God and
proving their repentance by their deeds. The chaplains of
the Presbyterian Church in America are committed to
providing a compassionate, redemptive, and caring
ministry to military service members, their families, and
other groups among whom they are called to minister
regardless of sexual behavior or the diseases with which
their constituents may be afflicted. Therefore, Presbyterian
Church in America chaplains endorsed by the Presbyterian
and Reformed Joint Commission on Chaplains and

<a id="ga47-p681"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=683 printed_page=681 -->

Military Personnel will continue to preach and minister in
light of the whole counsel of God on these matters.
[(http://pcahistory.org/ga/21st_pcaga_1993.pdf](http://pcahistory.org/ga/21st_pcaga_1993.pdf) pp. 94-95)

**And further**, that the 47 [th] General Assembly reaffirm the principles

regarding the biblical teachings on homosexuality contained in the
"A Declaration of Conscience Addressed to the President of the

United States of America" adopted by the 21st PCA General
[Assembly (http://pcahistory.org/ga/21st_pcaga_1993.pdf, pp. 129-](http://pcahistory.org/ga/21st_pcaga_1993.pdf)
132). The letter said in part:

Mr. President, in this Declaration the General Assembly of
the Presbyterian Church in America humbly declares its
conscience concerning the moral legitimacy of
homosexuality. God has spoken without equivocation
through His Word declaring homosexuality to be a
perversion of His created order, His moral law, and the
foundations of society….

In particular, we feel compelled of conscience to speak
because of the slanderous way in which truth has been
portrayed, as if opposition to homosexual practice is
merely a matter of prejudice against a minority. Please be
informed that to act on this basis is to misjudge the issue
entirely. This is a question of moral principle, striking at
the very root of God's authority, man's created nature, and
the structure and preservation of human society. We do not
act out of a hateful prejudice which rejects the rights of
minority peoples and seeks to exclude them from our
communities. On the contrary, we affirm and rejoice in the
God-created differences among the peoples of the world
and we condemn prejudice as contrary to the heart of the
gospel by which we live: that Jesus Christ is by His grace
making of various peoples one community of love and
fellowship. As His disciples we are called to judge, not by
appearances, but to judge righteous judgment (John 7:24).
The God we serve has made it plain that with Him there is
no partiality, and that in this we must be like Him (Romans
2:11, James 2:1-9). As citizens we support the recognition
and protection of the civil rights of all peoples.

<a id="ga47-p682"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=684 printed_page=682 -->

And yet in perfect consistency with this commitment we
stand resolutely opposed to homosexual practice as
incompatible with the temporal good of our nation and the
eternal good of its people. As a part of the Church of Jesus
Christ, the Presbyterian Church in America has a primary
concern for the spiritual well-being of women and men
created in the image of God. Responsibility to such a
calling will thrust us into irreconcilable conflict with any
government policy ostensibly approving a way of life
under its sphere of responsibility which is contrary to the
eternal good of its citizens. Further, such approval would
be a grievous violation of the government's own Godordained calling (Romans 13:1-4)….

together a man and a woman, united outwardly in the
institution of marriage, united inwardly in love and
affection, and united physically in sexual relations, are the
means of transmitting and nurturing life, and that life,
growing and flourishing in families, is the necessary
foundation of all society. homosexuality is a violation of
these creation ordinances: it is a perversion of human
nature, the gift of sexuality and the social order….

Here nature itself, the land personified, is portrayed as
revolted by homosexuality as a perversion of God's
purposes. Lest this be thought of as an outmoded and
unenlightened perspective, the New Testament affirms this
same truth with even greater clarity when it excludes, not
merely from the land of Israel, but from the eternal
Kingdom of God, those who pursue homosexual
practice….

Please note: in condemning homosexual practice we claim
no self-righteousness. The Bible we cite also teaches that
all particular sins flow from one rebellious disposition of
heart, a disposition of heart that belongs to all (Ephesians
2:1- 3). If we have been preserved from this perversion, it
is only by God's mercy. And that mercy in Jesus Christ is
so broad and free that may extend even to those caught up
in homosexual practice, freeing them from its bondage. In
the same passage we cited above the Apostle reminds the
Corinthian believers, "And such were some of you; but you

<a id="ga47-p683"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=685 printed_page=683 -->

were washed, but you were sanctified, but you were
justified in the name of the Lord Jesus Christ, and in the
Spirit of our God."….

To conclude, while condemning homosexual practice, we
affirm our duty to love and do good to all, even those who
are pursuing this perversion. But for us such love includes
the responsibility to speak the truth in love. The truth is
that our nation will suffer grievously if accommodated to
this perversion. More profoundly, however, the truth is that
those given over to homosexual practice will face the
judgment of God. We understand the gospel to teach that
there is no hope for such persons until they see this truth,
embrace it, and turn away from their perversion through
the power of Jesus Christ. Thus love requires that we bear
witness to their hopeless condition in order that they might
find hope. Love forbids our silence in the face of the eternal
consequences of believing a lie.

**And further**, that the 47 [th] General Assembly reaffirm the action of the
24 [th] General Assembly:

We affirm the Bible's teaching that promotion of
homosexual conduct and relationships by any society,
including action by the governments to sanction and
legitimize homosexual relationships by the legalization of
homosexual marriages, is an abominable sin calling for
God's judgment upon any such society (Lev. 18:22 and
Rom. 1:18-32). We also reaffirm our belief in the
transforming power of God's grace and our commitment to
'speaking the truth in love' to those in the homosexual
community.
[(http://pcahistory.org/ga/ 24th_pcaga_1996.pdf, p. 315)](http://pcahistory.org/ga/%2024th_pcaga_1996.pdf)

**And further resolve**, to remind all PCA officers and churches:

1. That all persons are born sinners justly deserving God’s

wrath and condemnation, and that outside of Christ all
sinners are enslaved to corruption which distorts their
hearts and their understanding of God’s created order.
2. That homosexuality is a sin according to God’s Word,

both in thought, attraction, and behavior.
3. That churches should actively seek to lead all sinners,

including those enslaved in homosexuality, to repentance

<a id="ga47-p684"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=686 printed_page=684 -->

of their sin and to embrace Jesus Christ by faith as he is
offered in the gospel, so they may find justification,
sanctification, and their identity in Jesus Christ alone.
4. That long as those enslaved in homosexuality continue in

this practice of sin, they are not be considered candidates
for church membership or ordination in the Presbyterian
Church in America.

_Adopted by Savannah River Presbytery at its stated meeting, April 16, 2019_
_Attested by /s/ RE William Hatcher, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 36 from Savannah River Presbytery (to CCB, OC)

“Amend _BCO_ 40-4 and 40-5 To Clarify the Meaning of
‘Credible Report’”

**Whereas,** there has been various interpretations regarding the meaning of

“credible report” in _BCO_ 40-5; and

**Whereas**, these various interpretations have contended either that (1) a

credible report may arise from any credible source presented to the court
having appellate jurisdiction with respect to the court next below; or (2)
a credible report may arise only upon referral of a matter from the
General Assembly according to _RAO_ 16-10.c; and

**Whereas**, _BCO_ 40-4 allows that: “Courts may sometimes entirely neglect to

perform their duty, by which neglect heretical opinions or corrupt
practices may be allowed to gain ground; or offenders of a very gross
character may be suffered to escape; or some circumstances in their
proceedings of very great irregularity may not be distinctly recorded by
them.” and

**Whereas**, _BCO_ 40-4 also states that: “If, therefore, the next higher court be

well advised that any such neglect or irregularity has occurred on the part
of the lower court, it is incumbent on it to take cognizance of the same,
and to examine, deliberate and judge in the whole matter as completely
as if it had been recorded, and thus brought up by review of its records.”
and

**Whereas**, consequently when a court next below neglects to perform its duty

under the Constitution; and further, fails to record its actions distinctly,
the remedy is for the court with appellate jurisdiction to “be well advised
that any such neglect or irregularity has occurred;” and

**Whereas**, since it is not clearly defined by what means the appellate court

may “be well advised that any such neglect or irregularity has occurred;”
and

<a id="ga47-p685"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=687 printed_page=685 -->

**Whereas**, when the lower court fails to distinctly record its actions as

required, and further does not inform members who may be affected and
harmed by these actions with notice in a timely manner, it results in
members losing their right to file an Appeal ( _BCO_ 42) or a Complaint
( _BCO_ 43) in a timely manner; and

**Whereas**, courts can fail in the proper exercise of their responsibility to

distinctly record their actions in such a manner that it is not discerned or
discovered by the higher court during the process of the regular review
of the lower court’s records, the higher court during its review of records
is not able to determine or judge the validity of the record; and

**Whereas**, _BCO_ 40-4 and _BCO_ 40-5 grant the higher court the right to review

the record again once the higher court has received credible reports of
“any important delinquency or grossly unconstitutional proceedings of
such court;” and these credible reports can come from any credible
source, which then requires the higher court to review “any important
delinquency or grossly unconstitutional proceedings of such court.”

**Therefore**, to clarify the meaning of “credible reports” this overture

recommends that _BCO_ 40-4 and _BCO_ 40-5 be amended with the
following (additional wording underlined):

40-4. Courts may sometimes entirely neglect to perform their
duty, by which neglect heretical opinions or corrupt practices
may be allowed to gain ground; or offenders of a very gross
character may be suffered to escape; or some circumstances in
their proceedings of very great irregularity may not be
distinctly recorded by them. In any of these cases their records
will by no means exhibit to the higher court a full view of their
proceedings. If, therefore, the next higher court be well
advised, through a credible report as defined in _BCO_ 40-5, that
any such neglect or irregularity has occurred on the part of the
lower court, it is incumbent on it to take cognizance of the
same, and to examine, deliberate and judge in the whole matter
as completely as if it had been recorded, and thus brought up
by review of its records.

40-5. When any court having appellate jurisdiction shall
receive a credible report, with respect to the court next below
of any important delinquency or grossly unconstitutional
proceedings of such court, the first step shall be to cite the court
alleged to have offended to appear before the court having
appellate jurisdiction, or its commission, by representative or

<a id="ga47-p686"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=688 printed_page=686 -->

in writing, at a specified time and place, and to show what the
lower court has done or failed to do in the case in question. A
credible report may arise either from a review of the records of
the lower court as required under _BCO_ 40-1; or from other
credible means that make known to the court having appellate
jurisdiction of the court next below of any important
delinquency or grossly unconstitutional proceedings; and/or
where the right of members to file a timely Appeal ( _BCO_ 42)
or Complaint ( _BCO_ 43) was abridged by a lower court’s failure
to conduct proper proceedings or to provide timely notices.
When a court acts on any credible report, the parties shall be
the lower court and the court having appellate jurisdiction.

The court thus issuing the citation may reverse or redress the
proceedings of the court below in other than judicial cases; or
it may censure the delinquent court; or it may remit the whole
matter to the delinquent court with an injunction to take it up
and dispose of it in a constitutional manner; or it may stay all
further proceedings in the case; as circumstances may require.

As amended the provision would read:

40-4. Courts may sometimes entirely neglect to perform their
duty, by which neglect heretical opinions or corrupt practices
may be allowed to gain ground; or offenders of a very gross
character may be suffered to escape; or some circumstances in
their proceedings of very great irregularity may not be
distinctly recorded by them. In any of these cases their records
will by no means exhibit to the higher court a full view of their
proceedings. If, therefore, the next higher court be well
advised, through a credible report as defined in _BCO_ 40-5, that
any such neglect or irregularity has occurred on the part of the
lower court, it is incumbent on it to take cognizance of the
same, and to examine, deliberate and judge in the whole matter
as completely as if it had been recorded, and thus brought up
by review of its records.

40-5. When any court having appellate jurisdiction shall
receive a credible report, with respect to the court next below
of any important delinquency or grossly unconstitutional
proceedings of such court, the first step shall be to cite the court

<a id="ga47-p687"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=689 printed_page=687 -->

alleged to have offended to appear before the court having
appellate jurisdiction, or its commission, by representative or
in writing, at a specified time and place, and to show what the
lower court has done or failed to do in the case in question. A
credible report may arise either from a review of the records of
the lower court as required under _BCO_ 40-1; or from other
credible means that make known to the court having appellate
jurisdiction of the court next below of any important
delinquency or grossly unconstitutional proceedings; and/or
where the right of members to file a timely Appeal ( _BCO_ 42)
or Complaint ( _BCO_ 43) was abridged by a lower court’s failure
to conduct proper proceedings or to provide timely notices. In
these cases, the parties shall be the lower court and the court
having appellate jurisdiction.

The court thus issuing the citation may reverse or redress the
proceedings of the court below in other than judicial cases; or
it may censure the delinquent court; or it may remit the whole
matter to the delinquent court with an injunction to take it up
and dispose of it in a constitutional manner; or it may stay all
further proceedings in the case; as circumstances may require.

_Adopted by Savannah River Presbytery at its stated meeting, April 16, 2019_
_Attested by /s/ RE William Hatcher, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 37 from James River Presbytery (to OC)

“Assembly Statement on Same-sex Attraction”

**Whereas**, there is tremendous cultural pressure in the United States in these

days concerning homosexuality and sexual sin; and

**Whereas**, there are many people inside and outside the church who struggle

with same-sex attraction and gender confusion; and

**Whereas**, the Revoice organization held a conference in 2018 which

discussed how to improve the way the church ministers to people who
struggle with same-sex attraction, encouraging them to uphold the
Biblical sexual ethic; and

**Whereas**, we acknowledge and affirm that Revoice showed love to people

struggling with these sins; and

**Whereas**, we acknowledge and affirm that the Church has not always shown

love to people who struggle with these sins; and

<a id="ga47-p688"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=690 printed_page=688 -->

**Whereas**, the Presbyterian Church in America, as part of its duty under the

Great Commission, desires to better minister to people who struggle with
same-sex attraction;

**Whereas**, the Church commends the faithfulness of Christians struggling

with same-sex attraction who are seeking to remain faithful to a Biblical
sexual ethic;

**Therefore**, the 47th General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in

America affirms that:
1. That all persons are born sinners justly deserving God’s wrath and

condemnation and inherit both the guilt of our first parents as well
as corruption. (Rom 5:12)
2. That outside of Christ, men and women are enslaved to corruption

which enslaves the thinking of all persons and distorts God’s created
order. (Eph 2:1-8)
3. That Christ has purchased not only the faith of his saints on the cross

by which they are made just in God’s sight but has also broken the
power of sin/corruption to enslave His sheep. (Rom 8:1-11)
4. That Christ unites sinners to Himself, though they be corrupt through

and through, but he does not leave them in corruption but subdues
their corruption and produces holiness in them. (Rom 5:12-22, 6:110; Col. 2:15)
5. That corruption draws all toward sinful desires whether they be

anger, disobedience, pride, hatred, lusts, or unnatural attractions.
(Eph 2:1-3).
6. That the Scriptures consistently exhort believers to reckon

themselves dead to sin/corruption and alive to Christ (Rom 6:5-11,
1 Cor 6:9-11)
7. That “sexual orientation” is a modern invention describing men and

women as being on a spectrum of sexual attraction or desire, and is
not a Biblical category.
8. That the use of terms such as “Gay Christian,” “Same Sex Attracted

Christian,” “Alcoholic Christian,” “Lustful Christian,” “Greedy
Christian,” or any other corrupt desire is not consistent with our
union with Christ. Such descriptors of our fallenness must not be
used as an identity marker but must rather be humbly submitted to
Christ who provides our primary identity as Christians. (1 Cor. 6:9-11)
9. That the pull toward same-sex lust is not morally neutral but arises

from our fallen nature, and with all other temptations must be
mortified by the power which Christ supplies. (Rom. 1:18-27)
10. That Christians will struggle all their lives with sin and that the

Church should never promise healing from sin in this life but the

<a id="ga47-p689"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=691 printed_page=689 -->

Spirit’s strength to kill unholy desires and to grow holy desires.
(Rom 8:9-11)
11. That no temptation has overtaken any of us except that which is

common to man and the Church must, therefore, be a place where
we encourage one another in the battle against indwelling sin.
(1 Cor 10:13)
12. That while there is a place for conferences and support groups, the

Church is a haven for sinners and we should find solidarity in this
common battle with sin not only with others who struggle exactly
the same but primarily among those in our local congregation who
share a common Savior. (Hebrews 10:25, Gal. 6:10)

_Adopted by James River Presbytery at its stated meeting, April 13, 2019_
_Attested by /s/ RE Jeremy L. Pryor, stated clerk_

##### OVERTURE 38 from Georgia Foothills Presbytery (to AC, OC)

“Establish a Study Committee on Domestic Violence and Sexual
Assault” (Version 7)

**Whereas**, the sins of domestic abuse and sexual assault are expressly

forbidden (2 Timothy 3:2-5; Jude 7; Exodus 21:15; Deuteronomy 22:2527; also _WLC_ 135 and 139); and

**Whereas**, these sins are especially heinous because they are “against the

express letter of the law,” “break many commandments,” “admit of no
reparation,” and often involve various other aggravations ( _WLC_ 151);
and

**Whereas**, these sins are grievously prevalent in our society; [17] and

**Whereas**, these sins often produce in the victims a complex response of

denial along with immense fear and shame that persist with varying
severity throughout life (e.g., 2 Samuel 13:1-22); and

**Whereas**, it is the responsibility of the elders of the church to exercise their

authority, severally and jointly, to shepherd those who are under their

17 For example, the National Domestic Violence Hotline reports: “More than 1 in 3 women

(35.6%) and more than 1 in 4 men (28.5%) in the United States have experienced rape,
physical violence and/or stalking by an intimate partner in their lifetime” (available at
http://www.thehotline.org/resources/statistics/, accessed 6/15/18). For further sobering
statistics on sexual violence see the National Center for Injury Prevention and Controls
report entitled _Sexual Violence Surveillance: Uniform Definitions and Recommended Data_
_Elements_ (Atlanta: Center for Disease Control, 2014; available online at
[http://www.cdc.gov/violenceprevention/pdf/sv_ surveillance_definitionsl-2009-a.pdf).](http://www.cdc.gov/violenceprevention/pdf/sv_%20surveillance_definitionsl-2009-a.pdf)

<a id="ga47-p690"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=692 printed_page=690 -->

care by guarding against such sinful behavior as much as possible, by
wisely caring for those victimized by the sins of others, and by
appropriately disciplining those guilty of such sins toward a full
repentance; and

**Whereas,** these sins have been a concerning subject of attention not only in

other denominations but even in the proceedings of the 46th General
Assembly; and

**Whereas**, the 42nd General Assembly previously exhorted the PCA and its

courts to respond boldly against one form of these sins, namely the
sexual abuse of children; and

**Whereas**, no standardized resources for pastoral care on these sins and their

complex consequences exist for officers, church courts, and agencies in
the PCA; and

**Whereas**, no clear guidelines for policy or practice with regard to these sins

exist for the officers, church courts, and agencies in the PCA that would
protect individuals and congregations; and

**Whereas**, the Rules of Discipline make no consideration for the unique

heinousness of these sins and their practical effect on victims;

**Therefore, be it resolved** that the Georgia Foothills Presbytery hereby

overtures the 47th General Assembly of the PCA to form a committee to
study:
(1) the nature of domestic abuse and sexual assault;
(2) the pastoral resources available for the care of survivors of these sins;
(3) the best practices and policy guidelines for elders, church courts, and

agencies for protecting against these sins and for responding to them;
(4) the possibility of changes to the Rules of Discipline that would

appropriately protect victims of these sins within a biblical process;
and

(5) the possible need for repentance as a church; and

**Be it further resolved** that the committee shall be appointed by the

moderator, and shall include among its members or advisory members at
least two subject-matter experts and at least two women; and

**Be it further resolved** that the budget for the study committee be set at

$15,000/year and that funds be derived from gifts to the AC designated
for that purpose, with the Georgia Foothills Presbytery committed to
offer $1,000; and

**Be it further resolved** that the committee shall present their findings and

recommendations for adoption by the 48th General Assembly.

_Adopted by Georgia Foothills Presbytery at its stated meeting, April 9, 2019_
_Attested by /s/ TE Don Aldin, stated clerk_

<a id="ga47-p691"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=693 printed_page=691 -->

##### OVERTURE 39 from Southern New England Presbytery (to OC)

“Re-commend the Previous Statements and Study Reports of
the PCA in Regard to Homosexuality”

**Whereas**, the Fifth General Assembly of the PCA in 1977 affirmed “the

Biblical position for our denomination which states that: (1) The act of
homosexuality is a sin according to God’s Word; (2) Churches should
actively seek to lead the homosexual person to confession and
repentance that he might find justification and sanctification in Jesus
Christ, according to 1 Corinthians 6:11; and, (3) In light of the Biblical
view of its sinfulness, a practicing homosexual continuing in this sin
would not be a fit candidate for ordination or membership in the
Presbyterian Church in America.” **(** _Minutes of the 5th_ _General Assembly_
_of the PCA_, 1977, pp. 67-68); and

**Whereas**, the PCA General Assembly has reaffirmed its position on the

issue of homosexuality on subsequent occasions, including 1996, 1999,
and 2018; and

**Whereas**, the 158th General Synod of the RPCES adopted a study report on

homosexuality and commended their report, entitled “Pastoral Care for
the Repentant Homosexual,” to their sessions and congregations as an
aid for their ministry to those struggling with homosexuality” (158th
General Synod _Minutes_, 4 July 1980, pp. 43-50; _Documents of Synod_,
pp. 301-8 http://www.pcahistory.org/findingaids/rpces/docsynod/301.html);
and

**Whereas**, the 9th General Assembly of the PCA in 1981 adopted the Ad

Interim Committee report on Joining and Receiving, which included the
statement: “In receiving these denominations, the Presbyterian Church
in America recognizes the history of the respective denominations as
part of her total history and receives their historical documents as
valuable and significant material which will be used in the perfecting of
the church” [ _Minutes of the 9th_ _General Assembly of the PCA_, p. 305].

**Therefore, be it resolved**, that the Southern New England Presbytery

overture the 47th General Assembly of the PCA to re-commend the
previous statements and study reports of the PCA, including our study
report adopted by the 158th General Synod of the RPCES on
homosexuality entitled “Pastoral Care for the Repentant Homosexual,”
with the deletion of Sections III and IV, to our sessions and
congregations as an aid for their ministry to those struggling with
homosexuality.” [158th General Synod _Minutes_, 4 July 1980, pp. 43-50;
_Documents of Synod_, pp. 301-308. [http://www.pcahistory.org/](http://www.pcahistory.org/%20findingaids/%20rpces/docsynod/301.html%5d.)
[findingaids/ rpces/docsynod/301.html].](http://www.pcahistory.org/%20findingaids/%20rpces/docsynod/301.html%5d.)

_Adopted by Southern New England Presbytery at its stated meeting, April 27, 2019_
_Attested by /s/ TE Ron Heald, stated clerk_

<a id="ga47-p692"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=694 printed_page=692 -->

##### OVERTURE 40 from Northwest Georgia Presbytery (to OC)

“Overture for Sessions to Acknowledge and Support Women Leaders
without Delay or More Divisiveness”

**Whereas**, the PCA has devoted many hours of debate, study, and discussion

to the role of women in the church—and such does not seem to be

yielding progress or resolution but rather much distraction from mission,
along with much quarreling and divisiveness (1 Cor. 6:7); and

**Whereas**, a recent _Ad Interim_ Committee recommended commissioning

women to serve other women and their ministry in the local church; and

**Whereas**, each local church may and should use the gifts of women as that

local church sees fit, under the authority and norms of Scripture—and
the Resolution below requires no higher court voting or constitutional
amendment; and

**Whereas**, the PCA has an institution already in existence that could launch

and nurture many gifts for ministry by our godly sisters, with no required
_BCO_ changes or time-consuming debates; and

**Whereas**, most of our female members do not desire ordination as an

aspiration or prerequisite to carry out gospel ministry; and

**Whereas**, rather than dividing and quarreling over this matter in the years to

come; and

**Whereas**, _BCO_ 9-7 presently allows Sessions to recruit godly women in

ministries in the local church under the authority and norms of Scripture;
and

**Whereas**, Paul differentiates between the Deacons and The Women in 1

Timothy 3:8-12; and

**Whereas**, the PCA could devote itself more to mission, evangelism, unity,

and edification by implementing the Resolution below, rather than
dividing over this issue in the courts of the church;

**Therefore, be it resolved** that those churches wishing to acknowledge and

support Women leaders be authorized to select and appoint the officers
of their Women in the Church [previously known as WIC, or more
recently “Women’s Ministry”] with the laying on of hands to serve in
the local church and assist the deacons in caring for those in need and
distress. The manner of appointment or election of these officers shall be
the discretion of each Session.

_Adopted by Northwest Georgia Presbytery at its stated meeting, April 2, 2019_
_Attested by TE Paul Legree Finch Jr., stated clerk_

<a id="ga47-p693"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=695 printed_page=693 -->

##### OVERTURE 41 from Heritage Presbytery (to MTW)

“Direct CMTW to Amend its Guidelines to Implement CMTW’s
‘Statement on Valuing Women in MTW,’ so that only Ordained
Elders Will Be Allowed to Serve in the Roles of Team Leader,
Regional Director, and International Director within MTW.”

**Whereas** on January 9, 2019, the Committee on Mission To the World

(CMTW) adopted a “Statement on Valuing Women in MTW”; and

**Whereas** MTW subsequently proposed guidelines to implement the

“Statement on Valuing Women in MTW” that were distributed to
missionaries on January 11, 2019; and

**Whereas** the MTW guidelines affirm “that women may serve in leadership

roles within MTW, recognizing that only ordained elders exercise
ecclesiastical authority over church plants or churches” [See the attached
Statement with Guidelines, page 3, paragraph 3.]; and

**Whereas** the MTW guidelines also affirm “there has been no MTW policy

regarding women serving in these leadership roles…” [See the attached
Statement with Guidelines, page 4, paragraph 1.]; and

**Whereas** the MTW guidelines initiate a new policy that allows women to

serve in authority over men within MTW in the roles of team leader,
regional director, and international director [See the attached Statement
with Guidelines, page 8, #3.]; and

**Whereas** Heritage Presbytery and other presbyteries of the Presbyterian

Church in America (PCA) are charged to provide spiritual oversight for
ordained elders who come from within our bounds, but who serve under
MTW [See _BCO_ 8-3 &13-2.];

**Therefore be it resolved** that Heritage Presbytery hereby overture the 47th

General Assembly of the PCA to direct CMTW to ensure that MTW
amend its guidelines to implement CMTW’s “Statement on Valuing
Women in MTW,” so that only ordained elders will be allowed to serve
in the roles of team leader, regional director, and international director
within MTW.

**Rationale:**
While CMTW’s intention of “supporting and equipping our sisters in Christ
as they exercise their gifts and abilities throughout MTW’s global ministry…”

[see the attached Statement with Guidelines, p. 698] is commendable, the
MTW guidelines actually hinder some of our women from serving in MTW
by creating an untenable crisis of conscience for them. Therefore, the MTW
guidelines discourage some of the very sisters in Christ that CMTW’s
“Statement on Valuing Women in MTW” intends to support in their
missionary service.

<a id="ga47-p694"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=696 printed_page=694 -->

Secondly, them tw guidelines allow a woman to hold a position of authority
within MTW in which she cannot exercise ecclesiastical authority over a
man. And the MTW guidelines concede this would require an ordained man
to fulfill some of her position’s responsibilities. [See the attached Statement
with Guidelines, p. 700, #3.] But an obvious consequence of such an
awkward arrangement would be questions about what a woman in such a
position is and is not allowed to do. For example, MTW’s Policy Manual
currently indicates the team leader position has very broad authority,
including “spiritual care” over those on the missionary team. Yet the MTW
guidelines leave the team leader position potentially open to non-elders.
Therefore, the MTW guidelines are unwise because they can lead to placing
women in difficult positions, while causing unnecessary confusion and
conflict within a missionary team.

Lastly, the MTW guidelines cite the Report of the Ad Interim Committee on
Women Serving in the Ministry of the Church to the 45th General Assembly
of the PCA, as a primary reason MTW is allowing women to have authority
over men within MTW – even potentially over ordained elders. [See the
attached Statement with Guidelines, p. 698.] But the 46th General Assembly
rejected two overtures that would have allowed women to serve on PCA
agency boards. [See Overtures 13 and 26.]

_Adopted by Heritage Presbytery at its stated meeting, May 14, 2019_
_Attested by /s/ RE Conrad W Judy Jr., Stated Clerk_

**Attachment**

**Statement on Valuing Women in MTW**
Adopted by the Committee on Mission to the World

January 9, 2019

Statement Adopted by Committee on Mission to the World:

MTW values all women as created in the image of God. Under Scripture's
authority and in our doctrinal view of ordination, we affirm that female
disciples of Christ are co-heirs of the kingdom, co-laborers in the work of the
gospel, gifted by the Spirit as members of the body of Christ and called to
use these gifts for the building up of the Church and for the extension of the
Kingdom of God throughout the world.

Furthermore, the MTW community deeply needs the gifts and glory of God
uniquely expressed in women. Therefore, MTW is committed to supporting

<a id="ga47-p695"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=697 printed_page=695 -->

and equipping our sisters in Christ as they exercise their gifts and abilities
throughout MTW's global ministry as together we show and tell the gospel
of God's grace.

In keeping with this statement, we affirm that women may serve in leadership
roles within MTW, recognizing that only ordained elders exercise
ecclesiastical authority over church plants or churches.

**Reasons for Creation of the Statement:**
First, the growing desire from our denomination to encourage women to use
their gifts for the service of the church are reflected in the recommendation
passed at the 45 [th] General Assembly of the PCA, stating “that sessions,
presbyteries, and the General Assembly strive to develop, recognize, and
utilize the gifts, skills, knowledge and wisdom of godly women in the local,
regional, and national church …” The spirit of this recommendation is to
actively look for ways in which women can utilize their gifts in the local and
larger church context.

Second, women have been serving in various leadership capacities in MTW
on different fields, in the home office, and in specific roles. In each of these
contexts, those who made these appointments ensured that their leadership is
in accord with our complementarian view of men and women’s roles in the
church. The problem that emerged, however, was when women leaders were
invited to global leadership meetings, questions arose from other MTW
leaders and missionaries as to whether women should attend those meetings,
and about the legitimacy of female leadership roles in light of passages such
as 1 Timothy 2:12. What has complicated the matter is the fact that there has
been no MTW policy regarding women serving in these leadership roles, nor
has there been an organization-wide discussion on these issues. What has
resulted is that faithful women serving in leadership are put in difficult
positions, having to defend their leadership positions and presence at
leadership meetings.

It is in this context that we have initiated a discussion on women serving in
leadership in MTW, and on deploying the God-given gifting of our MTW
women, for the service of the Church. Field leadership has participated in
this discussion in various ways, including writing of papers expressing a
variety of opinion submitted to Committee on Mission to the World
(CMTW). These discussions and papers were used by CMTW in their
deliberations as they sought how to best articulate a policy for MTW on
women serving in MTW.

<a id="ga47-p696"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=698 printed_page=696 -->

**Purpose of the Statement:**
_“So God created man in His own image, in the image of God he created him;_
_male and female he created them. And God blessed them.”_ **Genesis 1:27-**
**28a**

_“In accordance with Scripture and the Reformed Confession, (is) the idea_

_that a human being does not bear or have the image of God, but that he or_
_she is the image of God”_ –
Herman Bavinck, _Reformed Dogmatics: God and Creation_

CMTW’s statement calls our missional community to value all women, and
to recognize and deploy women’s gifting in building the Church and
extending the Kingdom of Jesus Christ around the world. In addition, our
General Assembly through the Ad Interim Committee report has encouraged
us to consider applicable recommendations from their report on “Women
Serving in the Ministry of the Church,” amended and adopted by the 45 [th]
General Assembly of the PCA.

One aspect of the CMTW statement focuses on women in leadership roles in
MTW. This leadership affirmation is part of CMTW’s encouragement to our
missionary community to be “supporting and equipping our sisters in Christ
as they exercise their gifts and abilities throughout MTW’s global ministry.”
As part of any discussion on leadership, we also affirm that all MTW
leadership roles are based on a biblical model of servant leadership that does
not seek to wield power over others, whether leadership is exercised by a
man or a woman. MTW further recognizes that application of this portion of
the statement regarding women serving in leadership roles in MTW may lead
to specific and local questions of implementation, because of our shared
desire to ensure that ecclesiastical authority over churches or church plants
is reserved for men. Therefore, the Senior Leadership of MTW has
endeavored to provide a list of considerations as a framework for thinking
through application of this portion of the statement.

**Recommendations from the 45** **[th]** **PCA General Assembly** [18] **:**

These three recommendations from the adopted Recommendations from the
Ad Interim Committee report are those that have particular applicability to
our work in Mission to the World.

18 From the _Report of the Ad Interim Committee on Women serving in the Ministry of the_
_Church, to the 45_ _[th]_ _General Assembly of the Presbyterian Church in America_, as amended
and adopted.

<a id="ga47-p697"></a>
<!-- PAGE ga=47 pdf_page=699 printed_page=697 -->

**1. That sessions, presbyteries, and the General Assembly recognize**

**that, from the founding of the PCA, there has been a variety of views**
**and practices regarding the ways in which women may serve the**
**Lord and the church within scriptural and constitutional**
**parameters, without ordination, and that such mutual respect for**
**said views and practices continues.**

Rationale: The Scriptures and the constitution of the PCA state that
offices of the church are only open to qualified and ordained men. But
the committee also recognizes that the Scriptures and the constitution of
the PCA give freedom to its churches with regard to varying views and
practices by its churches in the ways in which women may serve the Lord
and the church. This freedom of view and practice has also been part of
the PCA’s history…

Therefore, the committee recommends that ruling and teaching elders
continue this recognition of freedom within PCA churches to utilize
women’s gifts within the scriptural and constitutional parameters,
without ordination. … this freedom of views and practices within PCA
churches where Scripture and the constitution allow for freedom is a
strength of the denomination and should be valued by the whole church.

**2. That sessions, presbyteries and the General Assembly strive to**

**develop, recognize, and utilize the gifts, skills, knowledge, and**
**wisdom of godly women in the local, regional, and national church,**
**and particularly consider overtures that would allow qualified**
**women to serve on appropriate committees and agencies within the**
**church.**

Rationale: The Scriptures teach that the Holy Spirit has given gifts to the
church for the glory of God and the edification of the church (Rom. 12:38; 1 Cor. 12:1-11; Eph. 4:11- 16). Churches should strive to identify,
develop, recognize, and utilize the gifts, skills, knowledge, and wisdom
of all our members. … The committee further encourages PCA churches
to utilize the gifts, skills, and knowledge of women by particularly and
especially promoting and recruiting qualified women to serve.

**3. That sessions, presbyteries and the General Assembly consider how**

**they can affirm and include underprivileged and underrepresented**
